# Igros Kodesh

## Volume 14

## 4746

4746

B”H, between Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur, 5717

Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the National Administration of the Tzeirei Agudas Yisrael Youth Organization in the Holy Land, may it be rebuilt and established,

May Hashem protect them and may they live,

Peace and blessing!

In response to your notification regarding the Ninth National Conference, which will take place, G-d willing, during the upcoming days of Chol Hamoed Sukkos.

I was pleased to read in your letter that you feel the responsibility incumbent upon the Charedi youth to protect and save the children of Israel who are in danger of spiritual annihilation, Heaven forbid, G-d save us. May it be the will of Hashem that you translate this sense of responsibility from the world of thought and speech into the world of actual deed.

I have already spoken regarding the role of the Charedi youth during these days of the world’s conception in general, and specifically in the Holy Land, the King’s palace, the land upon which the eyes of Hashem your G-d are from the beginning of the year until the end of the year. I emphasize once again, for the sake of added urgency and priority:

The great fortune that Divine Providence has assisted you in being among those whose portion is the Torah of Hashem, and the special quality that Hashem has imbued in the youth—that they possess the greatest energy—these two things place upon you a special responsibility to be among the first, both in doing and in causing others to do, to provide spiritual tzedakah to those who are poor in knowledge and poor in deeds, and to give with a generous eye and an open hand.

During these days of the month of Tishrei—the month in which it is the duty and privilege of every person in Israel to "renew his deeds and improve his deeds"—you must utilize this auspicious time to gird yourselves with greater strength and greater intensity:

(a) In your work with yourselves: To awaken with a true awakening of teshuvah, acceptance of the yoke of the Kingdom of Heaven, and acceptance of the yoke of Torah with yiras shamayim and the performance of mitzvos with joy.

(b) In your work with others: To bring close those who need to be brought close and to strengthen those who need strengthening—in the work of teshuvah, the study of Torah, and the performance of mitzvos. Specifically, to conduct a broad campaign, in all appropriate ways, so that every boy and girl, every young man and young woman in Israel, is educated with a proper education, in the spirit of our ancestors, without compromises.

"And the Holy One, blessed be He, said to Israel: If you improve your deeds, I will rise from the Throne of Judgment and sit upon the Throne of Mercy, and I will transform the attribute of judgment for you into the attribute of mercy."

With respect and with a blessing for success in abundance, and with a blessing for a *chasimah v’gmar chasimah tovah* in both material and spiritual matters.

#### ***4746***

Most of the letters are from the secretariat's copy, and some are from a photocopy of the original letter. We have not marked them with a footnote unless the source is different or it has already been printed.

The letter before us was printed in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 21, p. 346, and was completed according to the secretariat's copy.

## 4747

4747

B.H., 4 Tishrei, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and learned man of many deeds, a man of energy and character,
R’ Moshe Zalman, shlita,

Greetings and blessings!

I have just received your letter from Monday, and I was pleased to read therein that you have found your entire family, shichyu, healthy and whole. I was also pleased to hear that a meeting was held and it was decided to begin the Beis Rivkah school immediately, as was discussed here. That discussion took place during a Chassidishe farbrengen, at which time there is a special *hemshech* and blessing; therefore, I hope that in this, too, one will see extraordinary success. Since the holy Torah tells us that the Ribono shel Olam does not remain a debtor, this will certainly bring an added blessing and success to those who are involved in this, and especially to those who stand at the helm.

Since I, too, have entered into this partnership, and although I am certain that there will be no lack of funds in Australia itself—where Beis Rivkah will bring the primary light—I nevertheless wish to have a share in the financial aspect as well. I am therefore enclosing my contribution check for the founding and strengthening of the Beis Rivkah Lubavitch school in Australia. May it be His will that I merit that this money should further contribute to the aforementioned success.

With blessings for a *chasimah* and *gmar chasimah tovah* for you and your entire family, shichyu, and especially for your son, Mr. David, and his wife, shichyu, who are surely also taking **a major** part in the founding and maintenance of Beis Rivkah, as was discussed while they were here in Brooklyn.

And with blessings for health, long life, and true *nachas*—which is Jewish *nachas*—from children, grandchildren, and their children, shichyu. May Hashem Yisbarach help you that for many more years, we should receive good news from you regarding your personal matters, as well as regarding general matters.

Awaiting good tidings soon.

#### ***4747***

**Discussed here:** See above, Vol. 13, letter 4765.

## 4748

B"H, 4 Tishrei, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

Your letter from Erev Rosh Hashanah has just been received. As you requested, I will mention you at the holy resting place of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, regarding the matters you wrote about in your letter. May it be His will that you be able to share good news regarding this.

On this occasion, I would like to urge you regarding the letter I received stating that you have been invited to serve as a *moreh hora'ah* in Yisrael. There are those who suspect you of evading this invitation. It is understood that this is not right at all, for our Torah is a *Toras Chaim*, and a *moreh hora'ah* in Yisrael is the one who interprets and makes known what is written within it. Through this, there is an increase in what is necessary for all parties involved in clarifying such matters. Elaborating on this simple point is unnecessary; you can certainly explain your arguments and detail your situation to the Rabbi, and our Torah is a *Toras Chesed*. May it be His will that you share good news regarding this as well.

With blessings for a *chasimah v'gmar chasimah tovah*.

## 4789

B"H, 4 Tishrei, 5717
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the esteemed and distinguished individual, occupied with communal needs,
Rabbi Moshe HaKohen, may he live

Greetings and blessings!

I have just received your letter, a *pidyon nefesh* from Erev Rosh Hashanah, which will be read at a time of favor at the holy resting place of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, *ztzukulleh"h, navgam, zya*, Nasi of Israel, who will surely arouse great mercy regarding the contents of the *pidyon nefesh*.

May it be the will of Hashem that very soon you will be able to share good news regarding all of this, as well as regarding the matters of the *beis knesses*—which, through *hashgacha pratis*, you are the manager of. Regarding these matters, both general and specific, it is said, "Judges and officers shall you appoint in all your gates" (*Devarim 16:18*), which refers also to the "gates" of the body, as hinted at in the *Shelah* at the end of *Parshas Shoftim*, and as explained at length in the *maamar* enclosed here. This is also the theme of the *parsha* in its conclusion: "In order that you may live and inherit the land," for all matters should be with *chayus* and in the manner of an inheritance, regarding which the *Gemara* states that it has no interruption.

My letter with blessings for the year was surely received in due time.

With blessings for a *chasimah v'gmar chasimah tovah*,

**M. Schneerson**

## 4450

4450

B"H, 4 Tishrei, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished individual, who is full of accomplishments and whose work is the work of Heaven,
R' Yosef, may he live and be well,

Greetings and blessings!

Your letter from Erev Rosh Hashanah was received. Surely, in the meantime, you have received my letter with blessings for the year. May it be His will that very soon you will be able to share good news regarding the content of your letter, as well as regarding the establishment of the Talmud Torah in the new location.

It is my hope that, since you did not mention your health, the situation has certainly improved, and may it be His will that it improve even further.

At the time, I requested that the shluchim from here visit you, especially while they are in Chicago. It is a established principle that a shaliach fulfills his mission, and since I hope that your health will permit it, surely the result will be that you will visit here during the joyous days of Shemini Atzeres and Simchas Torah, which are approaching, and we will participate together in a farbrengen. It is well known what our Rebbe the Alter Rebbe said: "What a Chassidishe farbrengen can accomplish, even the angel Michael cannot accomplish."

With blessings for a *chasimah v'gmar chasimah tovah*.

## 4,751

B"H, 5 Tishrei, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

Your *pidyon nefesh* has just been received, along with the check enclosed within it.

It is well known that while every Jew is obligated in the entire Torah and all its *mitzvos*, there are specific matters to which one is **uniquely** connected. Regarding *yeshiva* students, this is the matter of Torah study itself, studying Torah with others, and influencing others so that they, too, will increase their own Torah study. You do not write anything about this, and you will surely fill this in at the next opportunity. Remember well that the conduct during the days of Tishrei affects the entire year, as it is the general month [that sets the tone for the year].

May it be the will of Hashem that you utilize this month as is required, both in the study of *nigleh* and in the study of Chassidus and the dissemination of the *ma'ayanos*. When you truly desire it, all the imagined difficulties that exist in this regard will vanish. I will await good news regarding all of the above.

With the blessing of *chasimah v'gmar chasimah tovah*,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

**Secretary**

## 4,752

B"H, 5 Tishrei, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter from the end of Elul, in which you ask for an explanation of what was written in my letter regarding the need to write with more detail, without hints, etc.

The intention here is simple: when one writes to another, the goal is for the recipient to be informed about a certain event or situation—and in this case, for the recipient to also take action regarding it. Therefore, the letter should not be phrased in a way that leaves several doubts regarding various details, as is obvious. It is already well-known that the Torah teaches us to speak in clean language (e.g., "the animal that is not pure") when recounting an event, but not when speaking in terms of instruction or law, since concealing a matter can lead to a distortion of the matter, and the rest is understood.

It is surprising that reports regarding your activities in spreading the wellsprings are being received from others, while in your letter there is nothing regarding this. Surely you will rectify this at the next opportunity.

With the blessing of *chasimah v’gmar chatimah tovah*,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

**Secretary**

## 4753

4753

B"H, 6 Tishrei, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter from Erev Rosh Hashanah:

It has already been said regarding these days, "Seek Hashem when He is found, call upon Him when He is near." It is understood that the knowledge of how to "seek Hashem" can come only from Hashem Himself, for "My thoughts are not your thoughts, and My ways are not your ways." In His great kindness, He has instructed us in this through His holy Torah—a *Toras Chaim*, meaning a Torah that provides instructions for life, and in its entirety, a way of life according to the Torah and the *Shulchan Aruch*. Only in this manner can a Jewish person be truly happy, both materially and spiritually.

From this, it is understood that a young couple after their wedding—which is the beginning of their lives together—must, all the more so, base their lives specifically on the foundations of Torah and *mitzvos*. It is impossible, and indeed contrary to reason, to wait to conduct one’s life in this way until every detail is understood by one’s own intellect, for in the meantime, time passes. To what can this be compared? To a sick person in need of salvation who insists that he must first study the science of medicine himself, and until he completes his studies, he will not engage in the treatment advised by an expert physician. If this is true regarding the needs of the body, how much more so is it true regarding the needs of the soul and the fulfillment of its requirements, in which the Sages of Israel in every generation have instructed us. From this, it is simple regarding your own lives: that although it should be done with "ways of pleasantness," yet with the necessary firmness, you must—together and each of you individually—prioritize *naaseh* (we will do) before *nishma* (we will understand) in fulfilling the commands of the Creator, may He be blessed. The resolve and the prioritization of *naaseh* will also hasten the arrival of *nishma*.

Anyone who reads the history of the Jewish people sees that all those sects and circles that did not accept upon themselves the entire Torah and *mitzvos* in their entirety, and who deviated from the path of Torah—whether by much or even by a little—eventually withered, separated themselves from the borders of Israel entirely, and are disappearing. Some of them have no remnant or survivor left. Those who have withstood all the trials and eras and continue to exist are the keepers of the Torah and *mitzvos*. Even though in every generation and era, due to our many sins, there are those who deviate from the path of Torah because of an inability to withstand the temptation of the *yetzer* and to restrain their desires, after a certain time they either return in *teshuvah* or, G-d forbid, go from bad to worse, as the *Mishnah* says, "One *aveirah* drags along another *aveirah*." Surely, any further elaboration on this is superfluous.

It is certainly unnecessary to emphasize that in a matter concerning the soul and the entire future, one does not take into account what the mockers on the outside will say. You know that this is the foundation for many years, for decades of life in the future. Especially since a significant portion of the mockers are such because they have been bitterly disappointed in their own ideals and are jealous when they see a man or woman standing firm in their faith and at peace within their own souls.

It is understood that everything said above is directed to you and your wife, *tichyeh*, equally, for each one of you—a married couple—is called "half a body," and together you have the ability to build a complete and perfect home if you only truly desire it, for the matter depends on nothing but your own will.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above, and may it be soon, and for a *chasimah v'gmar chasimah tovah*.

## 4754

B"H, 6 Tishrei, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and distinguished, engaged in communal needs, R' Shmuel Yosef, may he live and be well,

Shalom u'Bracha,

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter dated 21 Elul, along with the *pidyon nefesh* enclosed therein. In the meantime, you have surely received my letter with blessings for the year. May it be the will of Hashem that the blessings of every Jew for his fellow be fulfilled in their entirety, in a way that is good, visible, and revealed.

It gave me pleasure to read in your letter that several people have come to speak with you regarding matters of Yiddishkeit and Chassidus without any effort on your part. Regarding what you write—that you do not know how to conduct yourself—my words have already been stated in many places: your conduct must be through "ways of pleasantness," yet with the appropriate firmness. The point is that although we must draw every Jew near, as written in *Tanya* Chapter 32 and in many other places, this *kiruv* also includes bringing them under the wings of the *Shechinah* in a manner suited to their understanding, standing, and situation. This sometimes necessitates not demanding with full force that they transform from one extreme to the other in a single moment. However, an explanation is necessary on the spot (so that they do not learn from this that there are *mitzvos* that can be compromised, or that they only apply to a certain circle), for there is one Torah for us all. Rather, due to the weakness of their spiritual health, we speak with them now about a specific *mitzvah*, but they must know—they must know—that there are many others, and that they are obligated in them as well. And this is simple. You will surely see good fruit from your labor in this, in accordance with the instruction of our *Nasi*—that *ta'amulah* (outreach) never returns empty.

With blessings for a *Chasimah v'Gmar Chasimah Tovah*.

## 4755

4755

B"H, 6 Tishrei, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the Administration of Tzeirei Agudas Chabad in Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established,
Tel Aviv Branch

Greetings and Blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter regarding the activities of 18 Elul. I look forward to receiving further good news, detailed as necessary, regarding the utilization of the month of Tishrei—the seventh month, which is "satiated" with everything, and "blessed" from the beginning—as well as regarding the content of my letters concerning the utilization of the impressions left by the visit of the shluchim, shlita. May Hashem grant you success in all of the above.

With blessings for a *chasimah v’gmar chatimah tovah*.

## 4456

B"H, 6 Tishrei, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

...Regarding what you wrote about a *tikkun* and so on, this has already been discussed with my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, and in general, it follows the lines explained in *Kuntres HaTefillah*: that through beautifying matters that are not as essential, one becomes susceptible to compromising on matters that are obligatory... And if only it were clearly known whether the students... are putting on *tefillin* every day (naturally, those who are already obligated in this), or if there is a shortage of several pairs of *tefillin*, and consequently, a deficiency in the aforementioned... For through this, one would add light to the souls of many Jews, and their portion in the world; surely the Creator of the world and its Guide will grant His reward by increasing that light many times over in one’s own portion. It seems that the conduct there follows what my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, said regarding the United States: that people become suddenly enthusiastic and fired up, but after a short time, they are already occupied with a different matter...

With blessings for a *chasimah v’gmar chatimah tovah* and for good tidings regarding all that was mentioned.

## 4757

B"H, 6 Tishrei, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

...Regarding what you wrote about the thoughts of your wife, may she live, and her worries concerning the fulfillment of your hearts' desires for the good: you already know that the less one thinks about such things, the greater the advantage. May Hashem grant you success in all of the above for the best.

With blessings for a *chasimah v'gmar chatimah tovah*.

## 4758

B"H, 7 Tishrei, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished individual, who engages in communal needs with great accomplishment and energy, Rabbi Moshe, may he live:

Peace and blessing!

It was a pleasure to receive your letter and its enclosure, following the reports I receive from time to time from anash in Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established, who meet with you and inform me of your activities. May it be the will of the Ribono shel Olam that our generation finally merits to utilize its time, talents, and strengths not for fighting against evil, but for strengthening the good and increasing the good. Even though, due to our many sins, the current situation is as our Sages of blessed memory described regarding the signs of the footsteps of Moshiach—"there is no day whose curse is not greater than the day before," etc.—I have already stated that, like all of Torah, this statement of our Sages is also an **instruction** regarding our thoughts, speech, and actions. The meaning of this instruction is that we must **increase** the blessing every single day, and not be satisfied with yesterday’s status quo. For "G-d has made one thing corresponding to the other," and it is an explicit verse that the good blessing and life are our Torah and mitzvos...

As we stand at the beginning of a new year, may it be the will of the Ribono shel Olam that all matters be renewed for good and for blessing, in a way that is good, visible, and revealed.

With respect and with the blessing of a *chasimah v’gmar chasimah tovah*, and with thanks for the aforementioned.

#### ***4758***

**Rabbi Moshe:** Porush, Jerusalem.

## 4559

B"H, 8 Tishrei, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and learned, engaged in communal needs,
R' Avraham Tzvi, may he live, HaKohen

Greetings and blessings!

It was with pleasure that I received your letter of 4 Tishrei, with the good news that you have merited to teach the Torah of the words of the Living G-d in public. Furthermore, you did so on a single day last year, which is considered as a full year, and you thereby brought merit to the many by teaching in a place of prayer, in accordance with the words of our Sages regarding the verse, "Hashem loves the gates of Zion." You continued this study from the end of the year into the beginning of the year, as you continued the study on Rosh Hashanah. Surely, from here on, this will become a fixed practice, such that at the very least, three days will not pass without studying the inner dimension of the Torah. The magnitude of this matter cannot be sufficiently described. May it be the will of Hashem that we immediately merit the promise of the *Ra'aya Mehemna*, through which the Jewish people will emerge from exile with mercy.

With the blessing of *Chasimah v'Gmar Chasimah Tovah*.

## 4760

B”H, 8 Tishrei, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter, in which you write regarding Rosh Hashanah, etc.:

You should memorize fourteen chapters of the Tanya, and it would be best to complete them during the month of Tishrei. On every weekday (including Chol Hamoed), before morning davening, you should set aside a few coins for tzedakah, and be careful with the three daily shiurim of Chitas — all this, bli neder (and surely you are careful with tevilas Ezra in any case).

May Hashem bless you with a *chasimah v’gmar chasimah tovah* for a good and successful year, in revealed and manifest goodness.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

**Secretary**

## 4751

4751

B"H, 11 Tishrei, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and accomplished, R' Zev, may he live:

Shalom u'Bracha!

In response to your letter, in which you write of the commencement of the studies, which took place in a good and auspicious hour.

May it be the will of Hashem that things proceed in the aforementioned—especially regarding spiritual matters—in accordance with the verse, "And I will walk in a broad place." It is already a well-known saying of our Rebbeim: "As one sets out, so it proceeds." May you succeed in raising students who are Chassidim, G-d-fearing, and scholars.

May your reward be doubled from Heaven, together with your wife, may she live, to see much Chassidishe nachas from your children, may they live.

With blessings for a happy Sukkos holiday.

## 4763

4763

B"H, 11 Tishrei, 5717
Brooklyn.

Blessings and peace!

I acknowledge receipt of your letter from the 4th of Tishrei, in which you write of your activities in your surroundings to increase the light of Yiddishkeit and its matters there. Surely you will not be satisfied with the current dimensions of your work, but will add to it, in accordance with the directive of our Torah, the Torah of life, to "increase in holiness," and there is nothing that can stand before the will.

Regarding your request for advice on what to occupy yourself with, it is understood that this depends on the conditions of the place and the nature of the person involved. There is a vast field for the aforementioned avodah in many areas, and since the instruction is that "your own affairs take precedence," you should occupy yourself first and foremost with the matters of the Chabad vineyard and those connected to it. Surely, when you contemplate the situation, you will find many matters in which you can be active regarding the N'shei U'Bnos Chabad organization and the Chabad educational institutions... May it be His will that you act with energy and also succeed.

Regarding what you write about establishing a permanent residence, etc. — it requires consideration whether the time has already come to discuss this, at a time when there are still several matters to be addressed prior to that. It is understood that when this is discussed, they will certainly take into account the matters about which you write. Although, in general, I am somewhat puzzled by a point in your letter, namely what you write regarding the longing of your parents, may they live, should you be at a distance. While it is certainly true that this is the case, it is also true that parents forgo their longing provided that the arrangement between them is more suitable. Ultimately, they are not satisfied if one forgoes the quality of an arrangement just to minimize the longing, especially since a sacrifice that continues for several months, and possibly even years, is something to be concerned about; it may eventually awaken in the heart of the one who sacrificed a feeling of resentment toward the one for whose sake they sacrificed their own affairs and gains. Only few are the exceptions to this rule.

And we conclude with good. In light of the announcement of your upcoming marriage in these days, may it be His will that it be in a good and auspicious hour for the building of a Chassidishe home, from which the surroundings will be influenced with Chassidishe light and warmth, and to the great nachas of the parents on both sides.

With blessings of Mazal Tov.

## 4764

B"H, 11 Tishrei, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and learned, engaged in communal needs,
R' Binyamin Eliyahu, may he live and be well,

Greetings and blessings!

...I do not know the weather conditions in Paris, but if it is suitable, it is worthwhile to make an effort to organize Sukkos gatherings for the students of the Talmud Torah and the like, similar to what is done here. They should hold these gatherings in close proximity to the Sukkah, recite the blessings with them over the Arba Minim, etc., and expenses in such matters are actually income...

With blessings for a Gmar Chasima Tova and for outstanding success in all matters, both general and specific.

## 4765

B"H, 12 Tishrei, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, G-d-fearing, and learned, who engages in communal needs, Moreinu HaRav Shneur Zalman, may he live:

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from Erev Yom Kippur and the one preceding it, along with the enclosures therein. Surely, the response to the *pidyon nefesh* has already been received.

I have requested that they contact you by telephone to inquire about the possibility of arranging a *Simchas Beis HaShoeivah* in Chicago on the night of the third day of *Chol HaMoed Sukkos*. If there is benefit in this, one of the guests from out of state who is visiting here could come as a "new face" for this *farbrengen*, and he would also speak. This is in response to the complaints from some time ago, and from the *anash* of Chicago in particular, that they "take but do not give." Even though it is possible that the guest who speaks might also want to "take" (this depends on whether the guest is a representative of an institution or not), it is clear that this would be secondary. The reason I write "the night of the third day" is because the night of the second day is the *farbrengen* of the *Merkaz L'Inyonei Chinuch* here, and the night of the fourth day is the night of *Hoshana Rabbah*...

With blessings for a joyous holiday and for good tidings, in a spirit of true and inner joy.

## 4756

B"H, 12 Tishrei, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

...It is surprising that you do not mention that you are preparing to hold a farbrengen at the camp to share some of the points that were discussed. This relates to the virtue of Bnei Yisrael, who are on the level of kings; the coronation of the souls on Rosh Hashanah is in the manner of the King of Kings, etc. Avodah in such a way—in addition to being more pleasant—is, in most cases, more successful. Although, one must be careful regarding the "expansion of holiness" so that the matter of the "Philistines" does not enter, as explained in Torah Or, at the beginning of Parshas Beshalach. Surely you also remember points from the farbrengen of Shabbos Nitzavim-Vayelech, etc.

With blessings for good tidings regarding all that was mentioned.

#### ***4756***

Published in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 14, p. 379.

**From what was discussed:** In the sicha of Rosh Hashanah.

## 4757

B"H, 12 Tishrei, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished R' Dovber, may he live:

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 8 Tishrei and the one preceding it, along with the *pidyon nefesh* enclosed therein: It is surprising that you do not mention receiving my letter of New Year’s blessings, which, by all accounts, should have arrived some time ago.

...It is also surprising that you do not mention your conduct during the days of Rosh Hashanah and *Shabbos Shuvah*, as these were certainly opportunities to utilize those days for various matters.

Regarding what you conclude in your letter about "distance," etc.: It is understood that this is not the case at all. On the contrary, it is the greatest form of *kiruv* (closeness) when one is entrusted with the most important work—work of which it is said that it is a *mitzvah* specifically for a "great one." The very thought (*hava amina*) that this implies distance is surprising.

With blessings for a *chag sameach* and for good news, and with details regarding all that has been mentioned.

#### ***4757***

**R' Dovber:** Baumgarten.

**Distance:** Referring to his *shlichus* to Argentina.

## 4768

B”H, 13 Tishrei, 5717
Brooklyn

Greetings and blessings!

In my conversation with your father, the Rav, shlita, I was most astonished to learn of your conduct regarding eating and drinking, which is not as it should be. This is despite the fact that it is tangibly evident that a lack of order in this area causes physical weakness, which immediately leads to weakness in the study of Torah and the fulfillment of mitzvos. The demand of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of all yeshiva students is already well-known: that they should conduct themselves with an orderly routine regarding eating, drinking, sleep, and so on. It is my hope that, at the very least, upon receiving this letter, the aforementioned matters will be set in order.

The saying of my revered ancestor, the Tzemach Tzedek, is already well-known: that it is better to eat in order to be able to daven afterward, rather than the opposite—davening in order to be able to eat afterward. In the weakness of our current generations, we permit even eating food before davening (naturally, after reciting the short Krias Shema) for those who finish their davening a significant time after waking from their sleep. One should not be stringent in such a case, since this stringency leads to leniency in avodas Hashem.

I await good news regarding all of the above.

## 4759

B"H, 13 Tishrei, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 7 Tishrei, I was pleased to read in your letter about your success as a counselor in instilling a spirit of *yiras shamayim* in those you educate. Surely you are not satisfied with the achievements attained thus far and are adding to them, in accordance with the directive that we must always ascend in matters of holiness.

Regarding what you wrote concerning the *Bnos Chabad*—that there are no other girls of your age there, etc.—my wonderment has grown even further. Surely there are many girls from *anash*, and even those not from *anash* who are *chareidi* girls, who should be organizing themselves within the framework of *Bnos Chabad*. It is possible that even here, the primary obstacle is that someone needs to be the first to conduct the outreach. Since you come from the home of a Rav and were raised in a Chassidishe home, you are also among the candidates who are suited to take this first step.

It is understood that you have permission to share the content of these lines with all the young women and girls of your age. May it be the will [of Hashem] that you all together develop and spread the *Bnos Chabad* framework in a way that you will fulfill your role in the area of *hafotzas hamaayanos chutzah*. As is known, the saying of our *Alter Rebbe* is that Chassidus is a matter for all *Bnei Yisrael*, and not just for one sect, one circle, or one specific type of person, and these matters are well-explained.

With blessings for success in all of the above, for good news, and for a happy holiday,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

**Secretary**

## 4049

4049

B”H, 13 Tishrei, 5717

Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter which you began writing on the 21st of Elul—surely you have meanwhile received my letter with blessings for the year, and may it be the will of Hashem that all the blessings of the children of Israel for one another be fulfilled in their entirety...

Regarding what you wrote about the reaction concerning the girls' organization: the saying of the wise is well known, which my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, brought in his holy sichos: "For every hatred there is a remedy, except for hatred that comes from jealousy." She prophesied and did not know what she was prophesying; I mean that she revealed the inner reason for the reaction by saying, as you quote in your letter, "Chabad has taken to spreading itself forcefully, and therefore..." May it be the will of Hashem that they return from this path while there is still time, before:

1. They instill in the students an undesirable attitude toward the Baal Shem Tov and his disciples, our Alter Rebbe and his disciples, whose Torah is the Torah of the living G-d.

It is simple that this should not, Heaven forbid, cause us to cease the dissemination of the wellsprings of the Baal Shem Tov until they reach the outermost reaches. Certainly, this can be done without entering into conflicts, Heaven forbid, for there is no spare time for this, nor is there any necessity for it, for the truth will ultimately prevail. It is only a pity about the delays that the aforementioned are causing in this, and it is a great pity for those who are mistaken and do not see the truth—that it is good. They are not content with the fact that they themselves do not merit to benefit from this, but they even prevent another man or woman of Israel from strengthening themselves in the path of Torah and mitzvos through the *neshamta d’oraisa*—in the language of the holy Zohar, this is the inner dimension of the Torah, its concepts and its guidance. The pity is even greater because, for the time being, they say in their hearts that they are whole and innocent, at the very same time that they do not incorporate any of the matters of the inner dimension of the Torah into their lives. And when Divine Providence informs them about these matters from several sides and in several ways, they rise up against it for various strange reasons. May Hashem have mercy. It is already known what the disciples of Rabbeinu Yonah wrote, brought in the Shulchan Aruch of our Alter Rebbe, Siman 613, that one who blesses himself in his heart, saying that he has not committed a sin, his teshuvah must be greater and his offering more costly; see there.

Regarding what you wrote about your financial situation: it has already been said to every person, "Victory is through many counselors." It would be appropriate for you to speak with the friends you mention in your letter... and draft a plan similar to the one you write about—namely, to summarize your debts up to now, and most importantly, to arrange the methods of expenditure from here on in a way that corresponds to your income, while paying off the accumulated debts little by little, and categorizing the debts. That is to say, which are the most urgent, which can be paid after some time, etc. Once this plan exists, my hope is that you will be able to make an effort and seek ways to bring it from potential into action, or at least a significant part of it.

With blessings for success in your holy work to increase light within the children of Israel, and through this, in the entire world, as explained in Tanya, Chapter 37, through your work in education on the purity of holiness.

## 4741

B"H, 13 Tishrei, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of Friday, in which you write regarding one of your relatives whose fiancée is sometimes pleased with his conversation with her and sometimes is not.

Surely the reason is that the fiancé needs to be more careful in matters of *tznius* and *kedusha*. Furthermore, it is appropriate that his *tefillin* be checked, that every weekday before *davening* *Shacharis* he should set aside a coin for *tzedakah*, and that every day—even on *Shabbos*—he should recite at least one *siman* of *Tehillim* after *davening*. May the *Ribono shel Olam* grant him success and bring good news regarding all of the above.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

**Secretary**

## 4712

4712

B.H., 13 Tishrei, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

Regarding what you wrote in your letter concerning your state of mind, etc. — it is known that there are several types of matters: some whose existence is external to the person, some whose existence is, at the very least, within the person, and some whose entire existence consists only of the thoughts regarding them. And although, on the face of it, at the moment of thinking, there is no practical difference regarding the content of the matter’s existence, in truth, this is not so. For there is always within a person a faculty that examines his feelings, their veracity, and their intensity. The results of this examination affect the person even when this faculty is in a state of concealment, or in the term currently in common usage (here a word in Russian is written) — since it is a concealment that exists in reality. It is understood that the thoughts you write about are of the third category, and the nullification of existence of this type is easier. Furthermore, it often happens automatically, through mere distraction—distraction caused by an external reason, or even distraction caused by the person’s own decision.

Surely what is stated above is known to you as well, but sometimes when things are spoken or written, and heard from others, the matter becomes clearer. It is my hope that this will be the case here as well. Enclosed is a copy of my general letter for this Rosh Hashanah, in which you will also find my perspective on several points that you touched upon in your letter, either by way of allusion or more than an allusion, which also serve as a cause for the thoughts you write about.

To use the language of Chazal, *maaseh rav*—which is a strong proof and evidence—there are hundreds and thousands who were in the state of mind you write about some time ago, and this state of mind was nullified completely, without leaving any impression whatsoever (and from this, the end of the matter is simple, even regarding those who still retain remnants of this state of mind). However, according to the way of the world, no one makes announcements about all of these, because the nature of the world is to publicize, and even more so, only the minority of the minority, and even less than that, which deviates from the norm.

Now, all that has been said above applies if one were in need of debate and explanation regarding the matter. But upon contemplation—even a slight contemplation—that each one of us must fulfill his role in His world, i.e., to increase light by upholding and disseminating a way of life consistent with the instruction of our Torah, the Torah of life—dissemination in his portion of the world, and certainly in his immediate environment—there remains no free time to dwell on thoughts about oneself of the type mentioned in your letter. And although in the first steps it is not easy to exchange thoughts about oneself for thoughts about the role of acting in the world of the Ribono shel Olam, from time to time this exchange becomes easier, especially when one does so with joy. The foundation for this joy is according to what the Rambam writes at the end of Hilchos Lulav.

The *pidyon nefesh* enclosed in your letter will be read at an *eis ratzon* at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, *ztzukulleh"h, navgam, zya*.

It goes without saying that you can continue to write to me in the future regarding personal matters. We have already been promised by our Rebbe HaZakein that this is not a contradiction to engaging in communal matters. Just as the entire world and all that fills it is one complete *komah*, so too is a single individual a complete world. This applies even to matters that are, for him, *gashmius*—for the Chassidic adage is known: "Someone else’s *gashmius* is my *ruchnius*." Especially since the *gashmius* of a Jewish person is also holy, for their role is to be "one nation in the land," to find the unity within worldly matters and to reveal it.

With blessings for a *chag sameach*—and as explained in the Midrash and Yalkut Shimoni, a "triple joy"—and with good tidings regarding all that was mentioned.

## 4743

18 Tishrei, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter: The statements of our Sages of blessed memory are well known regarding how much one must exert effort in searching for a shidduch, like a person searching for their lost object, and even more so. While it is understood that this must be done in accordance with the ways of Torah and yiras shamayim, it must simultaneously be pursued with vigor through friends and relatives. Regarding what you write—that you wish to know in advance who your destined partner is—you must perform the hishtadlus through natural means. According to the guidance of our Torah, the Torah of life, the Creator of the world and its Guide, who oversees each and every person with hashgacha pratis, will lead you on the correct and good path.

As you requested, you and your father, may he live, will be mentioned at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

Surely you are aware of the three daily shiurim that are incumbent upon every person, instituted by my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory: 1) Reciting the daily portion of Tehillim—as divided by the days of the month—each day after davening Shacharis. 2) Studying the Chumash portion with Rashi’s commentary for the weekly parsha—on Sunday, from the beginning of the parsha until the second aliyah; on Monday, from the second aliyah until the third, and so on. 3) Studying the daily Tanya shiur, as the Tanya is divided for the days of the year. In any event, you should maintain these from now on.

With blessings.

Due to the holiness of the festival, the Rebbe shlita has not signed this personally; I am signing on his behalf,

**The Secretary**

P.S.
Regarding what you write about the Jew from Bulgaria who says he is waiting to see miracles and wonders and then, etc.—tell him that if he looks at his past, he will see many miracles and wonders therein, provided he does not wish to deceive and delude himself. Why should he test the Creator of the world and its Guide, that He should bring him, Heaven forbid, once again into a straits and a situation of danger in order to save him? It is understood that the fears he would experience until the salvation, at a time when he has already been in such a situation and was saved, are merely the yetzer hara enticing him so that he will not fulfill the mitzvos of the Creator of the world.

May it be the will that your words come from the heart, for then they will enter the heart of the aforementioned person, and he will walk on the straight path—the path of Torah, our Torah, the Torah of life—for his own good, not only in material matters but also in spiritual ones. The very fact that I am writing about a Jew whom I have never seen, who was in a situation of danger in the past and was saved, should be enough for him to begin, immediately upon receiving this information, to put on tefillin, eat kosher, observe Shabbos, etc. May it be the will that you bring good news regarding this.

## 4744

B"H, 18 Tishrei, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 13 Tishrei.

At a time of favor, I will mention you at the holy resting place of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, for whatever you may need, as per your letter. May it be His will that you bring good tidings regarding each of them, and also in matters of inner goodness.

That is to say, in matters of Torah and mitzvos, for to each and every one was given the **entire** Torah, as is hinted at immediately at the giving of our Torah, when the Giver of the Torah said, "I am Hashem your G-d"—using the singular form for each and every one who was present at that standing. As is explained in the teachings of our Sages, all the souls of all generations were present at the time of the giving of our Torah.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

**Secretary**

P.S.
Enclosed is the *Kovetz* for Lubavitch that was recently published. Surely you will share its contents with the many, and the merit of the many depends on you.

## 4,009

B"H, 18 Tishrei, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and learned, engaged in communal needs,
R' Michael Yehuda Aryeh Leib, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter from Motzaei Yom Kippur:

Regarding what you wrote about washing hands in the morning on Yom Kippur, see my notes to the *Reshimot* of Eichah in *Likutei Minhagim*, at the end of the volume in *Kovetz* 3 (14), p. 190.

In the text of *Avinu Malkeinu*, it is obvious that it must be *Zechor* with a *cholam*, as it is the language of the verse. This has already been noted in the *Kuntres HaSiddur*, and it has also been corrected in several editions of the *Siddur Torah Or*.

In the text of the *Yehi Ratzon* of the Kohanim, one should say it as it is printed in the *Siddur Torah Or*, although one who says it according to the text of *Tehillas Hashem* should not be discouraged.

With blessings.

Due to the holiness of the festival, the Rebbe shlita has not signed personally, and I am signing on his behalf.

***

4,009

Published in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 9, p. 286, and completed according to the secretariat's copy.

**R' Michael Yehuda Aryeh Leib:** A Kohen. Kfar Chabad. Additional letters to him appear above in Vol. 10, p. 359.

See below, 4,845.

**According to the text of "Tehillas Hashem":** In several of its editions, it says, "that there should be no stumbling block in it."

## 4796

B"H, 18 Tishrei, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, man of action and energy,
R' Dovid, may he live,

Peace and blessing!

Your letter of 9 Tishrei of this year has been received, and I trust that in the meantime you have received my response to your previous letters.

I will reiterate the main point: It is understood that the administration of the Reshet must be the authority over all matters of the Reshet. However, as much as possible, this must be done in a way that does not provoke or cause friction. Therefore, it is understood that one should not compromise on the appointment of teachers, their selection, and the determination of their place of work, nor on the methods of payment. Yet, it is imperative to conduct this in the most pleasant manner possible. I am sure that in the meantime, several of the details you wrote about have been resolved, and you will share good news with me regarding this.

Awaiting good news regarding all the above, and regarding the improvement of the health of your entire family, may they live.

Due to the holiness of the festival, the Rebbe shlita has not signed, and I sign on his behalf,

**The Secretary**

#### 4796

**R' Dovid, may he live:** Chanzin. Petach Tikvah. Additional letters to him — see above 4362, and the references in the notes there.

## 4757

4757

B"H, 18 Tishrei, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from the eve of Yom Kippur. May it be the will of Hashem that you only report good news, both regarding your personal matters and communal affairs.

It gave me pleasure to read what you wrote regarding the activities of your daughter, *tichyeh*, in her work within the field of Chabad education. May it be the will of Hashem that she be met with outstanding success, and may the Almighty grant you and your wife, *tichyeh*, the merit to derive from her an abundance of Chassidishe *nachas* in all matters. In accordance with the well-known *maamar*—which was explained during the *farbrengen* here—that in the footsteps of Moshiach "a daughter will rise against her mother, and the young will shame the old," may both the mother and the daughter increase their activities in Chabad matters, and may these, too, be met with success.

With blessings,

Due to the holiness of the *moed*, the Rebbe, *shlita*, did not sign personally, and I sign on his behalf,

**The Secretary**

## 4748

B.H., 19 Tishrei, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 13 Tishrei, in which you write regarding the question posed to me in the name of [Name], and the answer I gave to it. You detail and explain that [Name]’s conduct in accordance with that answer is liable to lead to complications in the affairs of the yeshiva and, G-d forbid, also cause damage regarding the position in which the aforementioned serves in holiness.

It is understood and simple that the answer to questions such as these is in accordance with what is stated in the Tosefta, end of Chapter 2 of Demai: "If you asked such a thing..." That is to say, my opinion and answer were conditional upon the matters mentioned in the letter being accurate. Even now, taking into account everything stated in your letter, it does not touch upon or relate to the aforementioned opinion, and I ask for your forgiveness. May Hashem grant you the merit to strengthen the matters of the education of the children of Israel in purity and holiness, and to develop and expand them. For if in all times and in all places education was one of the pillars upon which the house of Israel stands, how much more so in our generation and in the land upon which the eyes of Hashem your G-d are always, the palace of the King of Kings, the Holy One, Blessed be He.

On this occasion, I would like to inquire about the study of *halachos* that are necessary for daily life. Although in previous generations there was no necessity to include this as part of the yeshiva curriculum, since the students knew these things in their homes through the education of their parents, unfortunately, this is not the situation at all today. All matters of Yiddishkeit must be provided to the yeshiva students, even the most simple matters. After all, this is the main thing, as it is written: "Fear G-d and keep His mitzvos, for this is the whole man." Similarly, regarding the study that leads to the fear and love of Hashem within the students, this is the study of Chassidus and the like. I thank you in advance for writing to me in detail regarding the aforementioned.

With blessings.

Due to the holiness of the festival, the Rebbe shlita did not sign, and I am signing on his behalf,

**The Secretary**

## 4479

19 Tishrei, 5717
Brooklyn, N.Y.

Rabbi, G-d-fearing, engaged in communal affairs, R' Shlomo Zalman, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter from Erev Sukkos.

Regarding what you write—that you have observed the custom of Anash that when there is only one *lechem mishneh* and one person is making the blessing to exempt the others, the other members of the household recite the *Hamotzi* blessing themselves—and you raise a difficulty based on what is written in the *Shulchan Aruch* of our Rebbe the Alter Rebbe, chapter 174, paragraph 4, and in the *Acharonim* there, that the participants are indeed exempted by the *Hamotzi* blessing of the one reciting it.

Indeed, this is the case: they can be exempted by the *Hamotzi* blessing, as it is well known that even regarding *birchas hanehenin* (blessings on pleasures), one can exempt others from their obligation if he is also partaking. Nevertheless, the aforementioned custom is correct. Since, in order to be exempted by this blessing, both the one reciting it and the one listening must have the intention to fulfill the obligation, and it is forbidden to interrupt between hearing the blessing and eating, etc.—and while in the case of *Kiddush*, *Havdalah*, and the like, the listeners are already accustomed to being exempted by the one reciting, and therefore even in a general situation, the matter follows the majority who intend to be exempted by hearing it—the case of *Hamotzi* is different. Especially when there is a large crowd—such as with a *kos shel bracha* at a *seudah*, a *farbrengen*, and the like—where, due to the length of time between hearing the blessing and the drinking of the cup by all the listeners, it is difficult to be careful about avoiding an interruption. Therefore, it is proper to maintain that specific custom, i.e., that each and every person recites the *birchas hanehenin* for himself.

Regarding what you wrote—that it appears in the *Acharonim* that if they do this, each reciting the blessing for himself, they do not fulfill the obligation of *lechem mishneh*—I do not know of a source for this. On the contrary, it is explicitly written in *Eshel Avraham* by the holy Gaon of Buchach to the aforementioned chapter that one fulfills the obligation of *lechem mishneh* even if he recites the *Hamotzi* blessing for himself. Furthermore, this applies even if the listener has not yet washed his hands. This is also cited in *Orchos Chaim* there.

Regarding what you wrote about a *Kollel* for Yeshiva students—to remain in the "tent of Torah" even after their marriage, and the custom of the Yeshivos of old, where they would marry at a relatively advanced age, from thirty and up:

The early authorities have already cried out against this practice of delaying marriage in a "generation of orphans," as is explained in the *Rishonim* and *Acharonim*. See also the *Hilchos Talmud Torah* of our Rebbe the Alter Rebbe, chapter 3, regarding the study of Torah specifically in a state of purity.

And it was never the custom in Chabad Yeshivos to delay marriage until the aforementioned age, G-d forbid.

Regarding what you wrote about establishing a *Kollel*: It is a known ruling of our holy Torah that a *mitzvah* that cannot be performed by others takes precedence over all other matters (*Moed Katan* 9b). In recent generations, communal work in the field of the Rabbinate, in the field of education on the basis of purity, and the like, is a matter of *pikuach nefesh* (saving lives) and actual rescue of souls.

To seat tens of students who are qualified for this to engage in Torah study all day—at a time when hundreds and thousands are crying out for help, even if in a "still, small voice" that is not heard, as they are drowning in the turbulent waters flooding the streets, and unfortunately have begun to penetrate the homes as well—it is difficult to understand who permitted this. Even if this arrangement is for the select few—for those for whom Yeshiva study in a *Kollel* is necessary in order to act properly afterward for the salvation of the children of Israel—this is not a path for the masses. If only a generation ago they had paid more and more attention to saving the children of Israel from assimilation and from distancing themselves from the path of Torah and *mitzvos*, and had placed this work of saving souls upon the shoulders of the Yeshiva students immediately upon their departure from the walls of the Yeshiva, there would now be tens of thousands more within the walls of the Yeshivos, within the walls of the synagogues, and within the walls of the *batei medrash*. There is no need to elaborate on a matter so distressing that it is alarming, for it seems that some of the heads of the Yeshivos still hold to this approach, saying: "I have saved my own soul." Our Sages have already elaborated on this in many places, among them the holy *Zohar*, Vol. I, 67b, 106b, regarding the conduct of Noach at the time the whole world was drowning in the Flood—and even then, Noach was different, as that was before the giving of the Torah and the commandment to "love your fellow as yourself."

Consider for yourself: if the matter concerned the son of the greatest Yeshiva head, or his only daughter, whom one must bring closer to Hashem and His Torah, would he also lock himself in a room within a room and rely on a miracle, just so he could engage in Torah for a few more hours every single day? It is self-evident.

Regarding the select few and those qualified of the aforementioned type, the practice has indeed existed among Anash for generations to engage in Torah even after marriage, in a manner where "Torah is their profession." But one should not make this a method for the masses based on the aforementioned conduct of "I have saved my own soul."

Regarding what you wrote about relocating to South America and the like, it does not seem right to me, as regarding the education of young children in those countries about which you write, the atmosphere is not at all suitable at this time.

With blessings for good tidings regarding the above and for the health of your entire household, may they live—soon.

Because of the holiness of the festival, the Rebbe shlita has not signed, and I sign on his behalf.

**The Secretary**

## 4780

B”H, 20 Tishrei, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter: The ruling of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, the Tzemach Tzedek, regarding this matter is already known—which he also found for this teshuvah in his own writings—that one should take upon himself an additional shiur in Toras HaChassidus, and increase his efforts to draw others near to the study of Toras HaChassidus and to walking in its guidance and conduct. Surely, even without this, you are accustomed to setting aside a few coins for tzedakah each weekday before davening Shacharis. May it be the will of Hashem that all the above be with success, and in your future letters you will bring good news regarding how much you have succeeded in adding to the ranks of our brethren, the Chassidim and G-d-fearing individuals. The statement of our Sages is already known: "Whoever teaches the son of his fellow Torah, etc."

With blessings,

Due to the holiness of the festival, the Rebbe shlita has not signed personally, and I am signing on his behalf.

**The Secretary**

#### ***4780***

**Your letter:** Regarding a teshuvah for an impure occurrence on the night of the holy day.

**The ruling:** Shaar HaTeshuvah, Chapter 62.

## 4,881

4,881

B”H, 20 Tishrei, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings,

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from the 12th of Tishrei. It is surprising that there have been no letters from you during all this time, for it is a known ruling of our holy Torah that every single day must have its own work accomplished. When one knows that they must report to someone regarding this, it adds vigor and strength to increase one’s activities, in accordance with the statement of our Sages: “If Reuven had known [that the Torah would write about him that he returned to save Yosef], he would have carried him on his shoulders to his father.” Indeed, even in this letter of yours, you write with extreme brevity.

It is surely unnecessary to arouse you regarding the teaching of our Sages on the verse, “When you see the naked, you shall cover him, etc., and do not hide yourself from your own flesh.” The intent regarding “naked” is one who is naked of Torah and mitzvos, as is found in Tanna D’bei Eliyahu Rabbah. In all of this, the verse concludes, “and do not hide yourself from your own flesh,” which refers to the three pillars of Torah, avodah (this is the avodah of tefillah), and gemilus chasadim, including regarding oneself. Further elaboration for one such as you on this matter is unnecessary, and may it be His will that you bring good tidings in this as well.

With blessings,

Due to the holiness of the festival, the Rebbe has not signed personally, and I am signing on his behalf.

**The Secretary**

## 4782

B"H, 26 Tishrei, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 14 Tishrei, in which you write that you considered the transfer from one yeshiva to another to be a simple matter, and therefore did not consult the yeshiva administration about it.

It is well known that the directive of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, regarding students of Yeshivas Tomchei Temimim, and students of Chabad yeshivas in general, is that all their affairs must be conducted through the yeshiva administration—that is to say, according to their guidance and after consulting them. This is because the intent and purpose of these yeshivas is not merely for the private matters of the students, or for a few specific issues, but to make the student, in all respects, fit for the will of our Nesi'im, the founders and directors of the Chabad yeshivas. This is similar to what is explained in Chassidic discourse in many places regarding the statement, "Moshe, the faithful shepherd, is the King of Israel, 'from where shall I have meat?'" Because of his great level and stature, he was not personally connected to matters of physical flesh, yet because he was the shepherd of all the Children of Israel, **all** of their needs had to be fulfilled specifically through him; and the rest is understood.

Regarding the practical course of action: you must now turn to the administration of Yeshivas Tomchei Temimim in Lod regarding the aforementioned question (and it is understood that you may present all your reasons and arguments), and act according to the instructions you receive thereafter. However, since you are already in Jerusalem—may it be rebuilt and established—do not change your location now until you receive a response from the aforementioned administration. May it be His will that you increase in the acceptance of the yoke of Heaven and the acceptance of the yoke of Torah and mitzvos, as commanded by our holy Torah and as explained and illuminated in the teachings of Chassidus, and that you also increase in the study of Torah and the performance of its mitzvos with hiddur.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe**

## 4783

4783

B.H., 26 Tishrei, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

I was pleased to receive your letter in which you express that "surely, with the help and assistance, etc., my prayers have been accepted."

This brings to mind the well-known story regarding the great Tzaddik, Gaon, and Chassid, one of the foremost disciples of the Maggid, Rabbi Levi Yitzchak of Berditchev, ztzkll"h, nbg"m zy"a, who made a declaration during the recitation of one of the prayers of the Yomim Noraim.

It is a known principle that in cases of doubt regarding a blessing, we rule leniently. However, since Hashem Yisbarach has provided a clear ruling in His holy Torah that we are to recite a blessing with His Name and Kingship—"King who pardons and forgives our iniquities and the iniquities of His people, the House of Israel"—it is a certainty that there can be no doubt or even a "doubt of a doubt" regarding this.

As is the case with many matters, Chassidic teaching provides a broad and deep explanation, and this subject is even more understandable through the content of Chapter 11 of Iggeres HaTeshuvah by our Alter Rebbe.

From there, it is also understood that what was said above applies not only on Yom Kippur, as might appear from the simple reading of the aforementioned story, but also every single day; for we say at that time, "Gracious One who abundantly forgives"—the word "abundantly" is significant.

Although, as is understood, Yom Kippur provides a general pardon and forgiveness for the entire year, in the manner that "the essence of the day atones," while the daily prayer is primarily connected only to that specific day—in addition to what is explained in Iggeres HaTeshuvah there, "except that Yom Kippur atones, etc.," see there.

May Hashem Yisbarach help you that for many years to come you should fulfill the command, "Honor Hashem with your throat" and "Honor Hashem with your wealth," and may this be for long, good years together with your wife, shichyu.

With blessings.

## 4784

B”H, 26 Tishrei, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed and distinguished partners of the factory...

Peace and blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your note, which will be read at the holy resting place of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, the Nasi of Israel. In accordance with the statements of our Sages regarding the tzaddikim who are the shepherds of Israel, even after their souls ascend on high, they continue to serve in holiness, arousing Divine mercy for whatever is needed by their flock, and in general for all those connected to them, both in material and spiritual matters.

It is surely unnecessary to remind you that the more you increase your involvement in matters of Torah and mitzvos, the more the blessing of the Creator and Guide of the world will increase in fulfilling your heart’s desires for the good. As it is written, “If you walk in My statutes” (which is explained in the Sifrei, cited by Rashi, to mean that you should be toiling in Torah), “and keep My commandments... then I will give your rains in their season,” etc.

I am confident, as I said when you visited me, that the expansion of your vessel of livelihood—the aforementioned factory—will also increase the blessing of Hashem that is poured into this vessel, provided that you also increase and widen the channels that connect the source of blessing with the vessel below, namely, through the fulfillment of the mitzvos of Hashem. May it be His will that very soon the verse, “Open your mouth wide and I will fill it,” will be fulfilled in you.

Awaiting good tidings in all the aforementioned.

## 4785

B"H, 26 Tishrei, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed and distinguished individual, occupied with communal needs, R' Chaim Zusia, may he live:

Peace and blessing!

I acknowledge receipt of your letters, which you began writing on the 26th of Elul and the 13th of Tishrei (which were received together), as well as those from Motzaei Shabbos Chol Hamoed Sukkos and the third day of Chol Hamoed. I thank you for writing, albeit briefly, regarding these matters, though the gaps between your letters and their brevity are surprising.

It is surprising that you do not write in greater detail regarding the distribution of the seforim. It is explained in many places in Chassidus, and we see it tangibly, that when one writes in a general manner, it does not add energy to the activities. This is because, when describing a situation in general terms, the description does not necessarily change even if the activities were to be increased many times over, or conversely, if they were to be decreased many times over. This is not the case when one knows in advance that after a short time one will write in detail—as mentioned above—that such-and-such a number of copies were sold by such-and-such a person, and so on. This changes the entire approach from the outset...

With blessing,

**M. Schneerson**

P.S.

It is surprising that you do not mention the mivtza of the Arba Minim. Although this may not be applicable in Kfar Chabad, it has been practiced in recent years in Jerusalem (may it be rebuilt and established) and in Tel Aviv, etc. If so, an increase in this area is also necessary, in accordance with the directive: "We increase in matters of holiness."

Due to a certain reason, the sending of this letter was delayed, and in the meantime, your letter from Motzaei Simchas Torah was received. Surely you have already received my response to the Vaad of the Kfar, which also contains answers to several of the matters in the aforementioned letters.

## 4785

4785

B”H, 27 Tishrei, 5717
Brooklyn.

The Kfar Chabad Committee
May Hashem preserve them and grant them life.

Peace and Blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your reports from the meetings of 26 Elul, 8 Tishrei, 12 Tishrei, and Chol Hamoed Sukkos. Likewise, the letters of 13 Tishrei and 4 Chol Hamoed Sukkos. I look forward to further updates. May it be Hashem’s will that they be good tidings.

...It is understood that what you write regarding your decision on the mikveh does not seem right to me at all. In particular, the statement that preparing the plans will require two months is incomprehensible! (Especially since there has already been a commotion about this for half a year or more). Likewise, the fact that suddenly and unexpectedly the construction costs have risen from twenty thousand to thirty-five thousand, and I fear they will increase even further. Also, it is not understood why you do not know if it is possible to build "half a mikveh," for we are not talking about half, but rather that for the time being, one will be built instead of two.

It is understood that my intention in the above is not to lodge complaints, but rather, as I wrote in my previous letter, the winter days are approaching, and the mikveh for women is used in the evening and at night. Efforts must be made to complete it as soon as possible, naturally without compromising not only the kashrus of the mikveh, but also its convenience...

Awaiting and expecting good, joyous, and detailed tidings.

#### ***4785***

From a copy.

**Regarding the mikveh:** See above, Vol. 13, 4284, and the references in the notes there.

## 4787

4787

B"H, 27 Tishrei, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished, etc., Menachem, may he live,
known as Dr. Harari,

Shalom u’Bracha!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter dated 13 Tishrei, in which you also write regarding your proposal to the Chabad institutions in Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established, to take upon yourself the dental care of the children.

It is understood that from here it is difficult to decide and respond regarding a matter that depends on local conditions, housing, etc. I am certain that the management of the institutions will take the necessary interest in what you have stated and will enter into a discussion with you regarding this matter.

It is certainly superfluous for me to express my thanks for your willingness in this matter, which indicates your interest and participation in the affairs of Chabad in Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established. May it be His will that, as you expressed in your letter, as is fitting for a scion of a Chabad family, you will develop and be as is fitting for a Chabad man. This is the purpose of the vow: to become a tree, and a tree produces fruit fitting for the roots from which the scion sprouted. Now is the time for the mobilization of all Chabad people—the scions, the roots, and the trees—to expand the affairs of Chabad in Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established, in both quantity and quality.

With respect and blessing for good tidings in all the above.

#### ***4787***

**Menachem... Harari:** Another letter to him—see Vol. 13, 4519.

## 4788

4788

By the Grace of Hashem, 27 Tishrei, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, who toils in communal affairs, R’ Shimon, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter of 14 Tishrei: It seems that your previous letter was already answered, so it is surprising that you write that you did not receive a response. It is possible that the letter was delayed in transit.

Regarding what you wrote about the haircut for your son, may he live, who will turn three years old on 14 Shevat: You ask whether you should delay the haircut until Lag B’Omer, as it appears from your letter that it is the custom of Chassidim in Eretz Yisrael to do so.

It is well known how much our Sages valued the importance of custom. Nevertheless, regarding what you write—that there are those who move the haircut earlier to Lag B’Omer even though he has not yet turned three—this does not seem correct to me at all. Even if one were to say that a haircut in Meron is more significant than the haircut at three years old, one could potentially delay the haircut, but not move it earlier. As for delaying it until Lag B’Omer: even if the delay is only for a few days, it should be done, but regarding a delay of several months, it requires study. This is because the haircut is calculated as a matter of *chinuch* in holiness. According to the precise language of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, regarding the leaving of *peios* and the beginning of *chinuch*—which has been sanctified for many years and generations—it surely draws down an *hemshech* from Above upon the one being educated. When the haircut is delayed, this *hemshech* also arrives only after a time, and its effect is missing during the intervening weeks and months.

Regarding what you wrote about his entry into *cheder*—whether to wait until the day he turns three or to move it earlier—this depends on the manner of his *chinuch* now. If, at home, you are already careful with him regarding certain matters, as is the custom in many Jewish homes, seemingly there is no reason to rush his entry into *cheder* until the aforementioned day. However, if through his entry into *cheder* there will be an increase in the aforementioned conduct, then it is not so much a matter of learning as it is a matter of *chinuch*, and it is no worse than *chinuch* at home, which is not tied to the aforementioned date.

There are those who are careful about learning before three years of age, based on what is written, "Three years... shall be forbidden to you," etc. However, I have not seen *anash* being careful about this. At most, one should distinguish between learning by heart and learning in writing. See the precise language of the Alter Rebbe in *Hilchos Talmud Torah*, beginning of Chapter 1; based on this, what was noted in the *Biurei HaGra* on *Shulchan Aruch, Yoreh Deah*, section 245, is also simply resolved.

Regarding what you wrote about whether to continue in the business of *tefillin* and *mezuzos*: This depends on whether you are earning a profit commensurate with the time you invest in it, and also whether there is a matter of a more profitable livelihood.

Surely you will provide a detailed report regarding the activities of the month of Tishrei. May it be Hashem’s will that they be full and overflowing, for we have entered a complete year, as our Sages say, including the leap month.

With blessing.

## 4789

B"H, 28 Tishrei, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and upright, whose work is the work of Heaven,
R' Chaim Meir, may he live,

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter—which lacks the date of writing—in which you write regarding the question of the local doctor: See also Sanhedrin, end of Chapter 1, and specifically according to the commentary of the Rambam (Hilchos Deos, end of Chapter 4). It is understood that in such matters, everyone must consult together, as with any issue affecting the many, the collective of Kfar Chabad. In any case, may it be the will [of Hashem] that the residents of the village experience the form of healing of "the Healer of all flesh and the Worker of wonders," as He says in His Torah, the Torah of life: "I am Hashem your Healer"—and therefore—"none of the diseases... will I place upon you," which is the most sublime form of healing.

It is surprising that you do not mention in this letter the status of your holy work in proofreading STaM, which I trust you are continuing.

On this occasion, I am interested to know if you are clearly familiar with the form of the letters as practiced among Chabad Chassidim according to the Alter Rebbe. If so, from whom did you receive this tradition, what is the authority of the one who transmitted it, and any further details regarding this are commendable.

With blessing,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

***

**R' Chaim Meir:** Gerlik. Kfar Chabad. Additional letters to him—above 4652, and in the references in the notes there.

## 4490

4490

B"H, 28 Tishrei, 5717

Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and man of action, R' Moshe, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from the eve of the second day [of the holiday]. My mind is put at ease, just as you have put my mind at ease, regarding your remaining at your holy post to be with the students and those being educated during the days of Shemini Atzeres and Simchas Torah, and to explain these days in speech, and surely also through conduct appropriate to the matters of these days. As is explained in many places in Chassidus, the great preciousness of these times—the "time of our rejoicing"—is that "Hashem will rejoice in His works, and Yisroel will rejoice in their Maker." May it be the will of Hashem that the influence of these days should extend throughout all the days of the coming year, to reveal the hidden powers within the students, in addition to the activities performed through their revealed powers. May they grow to be a name, a praise, and a glory for the institution in which they are being educated and for their educators. It is a known ruling of our holy Torah that those who strive in this take the primary share...

With blessing.

#### ***4490***

Printed in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 21, p. 593, and completed according to the secretariat's copy.

**R' Moshe, may he live:** Labartov, Pittsburgh. Additional letters to him: above, Vol. 11, 3734; below, 4813; 5088; 5290.

## 4791

4791

B.H., 28 Tishrei, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter in which you describe the relationship with A.

A. etc.

Unfortunately, you are not an exception in this matter; a similar situation exists in dozens upon dozens of homes. As experience shows, the best way to improve this is **not** to pay any attention to it, and certainly not to exacerbate it further through harsh and sharp expressions.
