It is easier to influence the situation itself than it seems beforehand, for one sees in a very short time that it brings good results. This is especially true since, in many cases, the true inner cause for such states of mind is the difficulties or constraints in *parnassah*. In such times, according to human nature, this is felt more in relationships with those close to us than with those who are distant or complete strangers, and there are very few exceptions to this. When one looks closely at the good that Hashem Yisborach has bestowed upon you in recent years—true good, and good for many, many years—it strengthens one’s *bitachon* and certainty that Hashem Yisborach will surely continue to show *chasadim* in the future as well. You yourself will come to the realization of how small and trivial those unpleasant incidents were in comparison to the *chesed* of Hashem Yisborach.

With blessings to hear good news from you very soon regarding the above,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

**Secretary**

## 4,792

B"H, Rosh Chodesh Menachem Av, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 26 Tishrei, in which you write the arguments of the opposing side regarding the approach of not "clogging" the minds of children with external matters—and in the language of Chazal and the Sages, on the contrary, to be "like oxen for the yoke, like donkeys for the burden" of holy studies—it is astonishing that these arguments have no basis in reason (but rather, as it seems, in desire; yet desire bribes and misleads the intellect, especially the human intellect of the *nefesh habahamit*). It is understood that you are correct in your conduct, but you must strive with all your might to ensure they are not left unoccupied by any matter; rather, you should engage them in holy studies, reviewing their lessons, and the like. May Hashem grant you the merit and success to *durchbrechen dem aiz* (break through the ice) that seemingly has settled on this matter in your camp, and may He transform the cold into the warmth and light of *kedushah*, and into the warmth and light of *Chassidus*.

With blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned.

## 4793

4793

Rosh Chodesh Menachem Av, 5717

...Regarding what you wrote about reciting *Retzei* and *Yaaleh V’yavo*—since the Simchas Torah meal extended into Shabbos, and what was discussed regarding this during the *farbrengen*.

This is based on what is explained in *Shulchan Aruch Orach Chayim*, end of section 188; see there in the *Shulchan Aruch* of *Rabbeinu HaZaken*.

The conclusion, the practical instruction given by *Kvod Kedushas Moreinu V’Rabbeinu* [the Rebbe Rayatz]—may his merit shield us—some years ago, after his arrival here when the calendar configuration was the same as this year, was: those who ate a *kezayis* both during the day and after nightfall (naturally, by spreading a cloth and making *Kiddush*) should recite *Retzei* and then *Yaaleh V’yavo*, according to the order...

#### ***4793***

Printed in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 14, p. 211.

**Some years ago**: See *Sefer HaSichos 5704*, p. 40.

## 4794

4794

B"H, 2nd of Menachem Av, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

Following a long pause and silence, I received your letter from Monday, in which you write regarding the founding of the Talmud Torah in the Ninth Camp, etc., and that the matter was handed over to the activists of Chinuch Atzmai. You note that for various reasons, several people from the Ninth Camp do not agree to this now, and you suggest that the aforementioned matter be transferred to the Chabad activists.

In general, it is for the Chabad activists in Eretz Yisrael—may it be built and established—to decide on this matter in accordance with local conditions, financial resources, and, primarily, the principle that Chabad has adopted in its communal work: not to compete and not to engage in politics, etc. Specifically regarding Chinuch Atzmai, it has been the practice for many years that Chabad does not encroach upon the domain of Chinuch Atzmai. After all, there is enough work in Eretz Yisrael—may it be built and established—to rescue [souls] from the hands of the leftists, etc.

With blessings.

## 4795

4795

B”H, 2nd of Mar-Cheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and learned individual, engaged in communal needs, R’ Dovid, may he live:

Peace and blessing!

I acknowledge receipt of your letter from the 26th of Tishrei. May Hashem help that from now on, you should report only good news regarding all the matters you wrote about, and that things should go from good to even better.

Regarding what you wrote about your stay at the hotel and the content of the studies there—it is a pity and a source of distress that you did not carry out your good intention to teach them Chassidus (Da”Ch). On the contrary, the fact that the leader of the speakers said that the audience is not accustomed to this is a clear indication that you are the one to be the first to reveal this part of Torah to them. The leader of the speakers prophesied without knowing what he was prophesying, as unfortunately happens quite often with people.

That which appears to the leader of the speakers as an argument—that people are not accustomed to such studies—has already been answered and explained at length, according to the level, in the *Kuntres Limud HaChassidus*.

It goes without saying that I do not intend to distress you with what has been said, but rather to draw your attention to look out for such opportunities in the future. In our days, it is not enough to wait for such an opportunity; one must seek it out. As is known from the *sicha* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, regarding the precise phrasing of "engaged in Torah" and "engaged in a mitzvah"—it must be like a business engagement in material matters, where one does not wait until someone comes and suggests a business deal, but rather one seeks out the buyer and persuades him, etc., and the matter is understood.

The *pidyon nefesh* that was in your letter will be read at an *eis ratzon* at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, may his merit protect us.

With blessings for good tidings.

#### 4795

R’ Dovid: Tannenhaus. Of Bathhurst. Additional letters to him—above, Vol. 13, 4701. And as noted in the footnotes there.

## 4796

4796

By the Grace of G-d, 2nd of Mar-Cheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the esteemed and distinguished, etc., Rabbi Y., may he live,

Shalom u’Bracha!

In response to your letter of the 28th of Tishrei, in which you write that you are preparing to travel, in a good and auspicious hour, to our Holy Land, may it be rebuilt and established, and you ask if the rumor is true that it is forbidden to travel on Israeli ships that continue their voyage on Shabbos Kodesh.

As I have written at length elsewhere, the manner in which ships are operated in our times leaves no room for doubt.

Traveling on Shabbos on Israeli ships is an absolute prohibition. Furthermore, it constitutes a public desecration of Shabbos, performed with a high hand. It also involves a staggering Chillul Hashem in the eyes of all the nations. All of the above applies even if the ship begins its voyage at the start of the week.

Regarding what you write—that by traveling on the aforementioned ship, our Holy Land will gain a number of dollars that will be used for its sustenance and development:

—It seems you are unaware that this is one of the arguments used by those who wish to have work performed, G-d forbid, on Shabbos in all the factories and fields in Eretz Yisrael. By doing so, they would earn far more money than through the ships—

We have nothing to rely upon but the declaration of our Torah, which teaches that through the desecration of Shabbos, not only is there no increase in sustenance and building, but on the contrary—"people are diminished and the roads become desolate" (Shabbos 33a). Furthermore, our Sages of blessed memory said, "Jerusalem was destroyed only because they desecrated the Shabbos therein" (ibid. 119b).

If you desire the existence of Eretz Yisrael—and you are surely mourning and pained by its destruction—it is self-evident that you should not contribute to its destruction through actions such as these.

May it be the will of the Ribono shel Olam that in his days and in ours, the statement of our Sages of blessed memory will be fulfilled in practice (Yerushalmi, Taanis, Chapter 1, end of Halacha 4): "If Yisrael were to observe one Shabbos properly, the son of Dovid would immediately come." And as added in Shemos Rabbah (end of Chapter 25): "Why? Because it is equivalent to all the mitzvos."

With blessings to hear good tidings regarding the above,

**M. Schneerson**

In the Bavli (Shabbos 118b) it states "two Shabbosos properly." This does not contradict the statement in the Yerushalmi Taanis mentioned above, as explained by the Alter Rebbe in his book Likutei Torah, Parshas Behar, in the discourse entitled "Es Shabbosoi Tishmoru." See there.

## 4797

4797

B"H, 2nd of Mar-Cheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and learned, who engages in communal needs and accomplishes much,
R' Schneur Zalman, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

... It is surprising that you are so brief regarding the *farbrengens* of the month of Tishrei, for this is a matter of bringing the *Maor* (the Luminary) close to the *nitzotz* (the spark), to the point where all the sparks are awakened, as is evident in a tangible way. Consequently, decisions regarding the future are certainly being made, etc., etc.

Surely a detailed report has already arrived from the guests and the *shluchim*, may they live, and may it be the will of Hashem that you will be able to share good news with one another.

With blessings.

#### ***4797***

**R' Schneur Zalman:** Gerlik. The Rabbi of Kfar Chabad. Additional letters to him: above, Vol. 13, 4281, and in the references noted there. Below, 4974.

## 4798

4798

B"H, 2nd of Mar-Cheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

...It is truly a wonder to see a situation such as this, where every possible reasoning comes to mind except for the most simple one — *folgen* (to obey). To our great sorrow, he is not the only one in this regard; there are many among *anash*, and even *fun shpitz Chabad* (from the top echelons of Chabad), who, "at times," when written to about a matter that they perceive as new, immediately occupy their minds with various ways and means, except for the most simple path: to fulfill what is written exactly as it is, without *peshatlach* (clever interpretations) and without *dreidlech* (twists and turns)...

With blessings for good tidings.

## 4,799

B"H, 2nd of Mar-Cheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

...I am taking the opportunity of this letter, written on the first day that Tachanun is recited after several weeks, to record something I recall from a Chassidic story: that the Chassid, the great Gaon and Tzaddik, Rabbi Hillel of Paritch, of blessed memory, would not recite *Al Naharos Bavel* before Birchas Hamazon even on this day. He would say that Tachanun itself is a "guest," and in honor of the guest, one should recite *Shir Hamaalos* (Tehillim 126).

As with every Chassidic story, there is a moral lesson to be drawn from this as well: that even the recitation of Tachanun—that is, the confession of *Ashamnu* and so on—brings joy to one who serves Hashem. This is because he contemplates the magnitude of the kindness of the Ribono shel Olam, who grants a person the ability to rectify the past. This applies not only to one’s conduct for the future, but it actually uproots the past actions from their very essence, until they reach the level known in the words of our Sages, where one’s willful sins are transformed into merits.

With blessings for health and for success in the education of the sons and daughters of Yisrael in the purity of holiness.

## 4800

B"H, 2nd of Mar-Cheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of the 26th of Tishrei, in which you write regarding the manner of speech, etc. — surely you are familiar with the story related by my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of saintly memory, regarding the chossid Rabbi Ze'ev Dov Kazevnikov. Therefore, you should study with diligence and persistence in general, and in particular the words of the living G-d, which is the Torah of Chassidus. This will hasten your recovery through the success of your medical treatment.

Regarding your notification of your birthday: surely you followed the custom of Anash on one's birthday. May it be the will of Hashem that this be a year of success for you in the study of Nigleh and Chassidus, in the performance of mitzvos with hiddur, and a good beginning in the avodah of tefillah. May it also be His will that you influence your friends in all of the above.

With blessings.

#### ***4800***

**Regarding the story:** See Sefer HaSichos 5700, p. 32.

## 4841

4841

B"H, 2nd of Mar-Cheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of the 25th of Tishrei:

1) The shluchim are certainly correct that letters do not burden me. May we merit, speedily and in our days, that the content of every person’s letter to another be joyous, both materially and spiritually, and in a way that is good, visible, and revealed.

2) Regarding what you wrote in connection with the incident in the shul, may Hashem spare us, concerning the thought of purchasing a Sefer Torah:

While the magnitude of such a matter is understood, in the current situation—where institutions of chinuch al taharas hakodesh are in a dire financial state, and many of those who study Torah and carry its banner are in extremely difficult material circumstances, etc., etc.—the opinion of the Rav shlita is correct: that the tzedakah should be for the support of Torah institutions and those who study Torah. And this is simple to understand.

You have surely received detailed regards from the visiting shluchim shiy who were here. May it be the will of Hashem that you receive good news from one another, only good.

With blessings.

#### ***4841***

**The incident:** That a Sefer Torah fell to the ground. See also the following letter.

## 4802

4802

B"H, 3rd of Mar-Cheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and learned, engaged in communal needs,
R' Alexander Sender, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from the 27th of Tishrei. May it be the will of Hashem that from here on, the children of Israel will only share good news with one another in all matters, and may the horn of Torah be raised higher and higher, together with the horn of the people of Israel, with the flourishing of the horn of Dovid Malka Meshicha, as explained in the words of our Sages.

With blessings for good news, 

In the *Me'asef Lechol Hamachanos*, section 4, paragraph 4, the books that discuss the falling of a Sefer Torah, *l'o aleinu* and *l'o aleinu*, and how one should conduct oneself, are noted. In the *Shulchan Aruch* of the Alter Rebbe, it is mentioned—in general terms—in *Orach Chaim*, end of section 44.

Regarding what you wrote about whether one may continue to keep a *pasul* Sefer Torah that cannot be repaired for the purpose of *Hakafos*:

It is difficult to answer. On the face of it, the law is clear in *Shulchan Aruch*, *Yoreh Deah* 279, that even for thirty days it is forbidden to keep it. However, in several *batei knesses* I have seen them keeping them as mentioned for some time, and no one protests (except that they mark them by wrapping the *mapah* around the *me'il*). And the basis for this requires further study (*tzarich iyun gadol*). Perhaps regarding this, too, it can be said that the spirit of the Sages is not pleased with this.

#### ***4802***

Printed in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 21, p. 539, and completed according to the secretariat's copy.

**R' Alexander Sender:** Yudasin, Tel Aviv. Additional letters to him: above, Vol. 13, 4348, and in the references in the notes there. Below, 4924, 5046, 5183, 5208.

**To keep the Sefer Torah:** In continuation of this—below, letter 5046.

## 4403

B"H, 3rd of Mar-Cheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of the 28th of Tishrei, together with the *pidyon nefesh* which will be read at an auspicious time at the holy *tziyun* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, *ztzukulleh"h, nbg"m, zy"a*.

In it, you write regarding your hernia, and that for some time you have been feeling pains, and that even after treatment, they have not passed entirely.

It is known in such cases that the truss worn for a hernia must be adjusted from time to time to accommodate changes in the body—whether one has gained a little weight or the opposite, and the like. It is highly likely that your pain also stems from the truss not being properly fitted. Therefore, it is advisable that a specialist in this field examine the truss and adjust it as necessary.

You surely know of and observe the three *shiurim* that are equal for every soul, established by my father-in-law, the Rebbe—in *Chumash*, *Tehillim*, and *Tanya*. In any case, you should observe them from this point forward. Furthermore, you should recite each day the chapter of *Tehillim* that corresponds to your age (starting from *bar mitzvah*, one begins with chapter 14, and each subsequent year, the following chapter). It is also appropriate to study *Mishnayos* consistently—at least one or two *mishnayos* every day. It would be even better to memorize a few chapters of *Mishnayos* and at least one chapter of *Tanya*.

With blessings for long life and good years, and for *yiddishe*, *chassidishe nachas* together with your wife from all your children, *shichyu*.

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,**

**Secretary**

## 4804

4804

B"H, 3rd of Mar-Cheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

...It seems to me that I have already told you, or written to you at some point, that if only Anash would get into the habit of sharing good news when they have it, it would reduce the need for news of this nature to be sent here from Above. Although even in the days of our Rebbe HaZaken they already complained about the practice of remaining silent regarding good news, etc., in matters such as these, that does not serve as a justification, nor even as a precedent for those who come after, since our Rebbes already protested strongly against that practice back then.

Consistent with what is stated above, in this letter of yours as well, you do not mention anything regarding the utilization of the month of Tishrei for the strengthening and dissemination of matters of Yiddishkeit. I would like to hope that there was no lack in practice, even if there was a lack in the reporting.

With blessings for good news.

#### ***4804***

**In the days of our Rebbe HaZaken:** See his Igros Kodesh, Letter 90. See also below, Letter 4874, and in many other places.

## 4554

B"H, 3rd of MarCheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of Wednesday, in which you inquire about the commendable custom of many among the Jewish people to provide the public with the opportunity to fulfill the mitzvah of taking the Arba Minim. As our Sages of blessed memory state in Vayikra Rabbah, Chapter 30, this is the very sign that we have emerged victorious for a good and sweet year in all matters.

You ask whether, when standing in the street amidst a multitude of passersby, one must be concerned that, in a full gaze, there may be a *tefach* of exposed skin on a woman—such as hair or a leg, etc.—which would result in a Torah prohibition being transgressed while reciting the blessing, whereas the blessing and the taking of the Arba Minim, even on the subsequent days, is only a Rabbinic obligation.

It is understandable that I was extremely astonished by such a question—that one should **certainly** nullify a Rabbinic mitzvah because of the **possibility** that the person reciting the blessing might, at that moment, encounter the aforementioned situation directly in front of them, etc. This is not the place to elaborate on the details of the laws, which in many instances permit [the situation] even if one simply closes one's eyes, and certainly when one turns their face away, etc., as is explained and cited in the later authorities in Shulchan Aruch, Orach Chayim, Siman 75. See also the Responsa of the Tzemach Tzedek, Even HaEzer, Siman 139, and in Shaar HaMiluim there, Siman 45, and in his Chiddushim on the Shas for Maseches Berachos, Chapter 3.

Fortunate is the portion of all those who provide the **public** with the opportunity to fulfill the aforementioned mitzvah. As is known, the interpretation of our Sages of blessed memory regarding the allusions therein is that the Arba Minim include all Jewish people, without exception. Just as it is impossible to fulfill the mitzvah at all without the Esrog, which possesses both taste and fragrance, so too is it literally impossible to fulfill this mitzvah at all without the Aravah, which possesses neither taste nor fragrance.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,**

**Secretary**

## 4406

4406

B"H, 3rd of Mar-Cheshvan, 5717

Brooklyn.

To the esteemed and distinguished individual, engaged in communal needs, our teacher, Rabbi Avraham, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

...I was pleased to read in your letter that during our time of rejoicing [Sukkos], you gathered and discussed the expansion of Yeshivas Oholei Yosef Yitzchok Lubavitch in Melbourne—the expansion of the building, which will surely also lead to an increase in the number of students and the expansion of matters of study. The magnitude of the merit of all those who participate and are involved in this is beyond measure, and the measure of the Ribono shel Olam is "measure for measure," only that it is many times over, with special added nachas from sons and daughters and grandchildren.

Regarding what you write about exchanging the check sent from here: it is understood that I agree to any arrangement by which the Yeshiva will profit, and may it be Hashem's will that the exchange also serves as a segulah for the matter about which you write.

Please convey my regards to your father, your brother, your wife, and all the members of your family, may they live.

Awaiting good tidings.

#### ***4406***

**Rabbi Avraham:** Feiglin, Melbourne.

**Exchanging the check sent from here:** He wanted to keep the check signed by the Rebbe shlita for himself and give an equivalent sum of money to the Yeshiva in its place. See also the following letter.

## 4407

B"H, 3rd of MarCheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished individual, a man of many accomplishments, involved in communal needs, a man of character, R' Moshe Zalman, may he live and be well.

Shalom u'Bracha!

I was pleased to receive your letter from the 28th of Tishrei, as well as the letter from your son, Avraham, may he live and be well. As I have already written, anything that brings benefit to the Yeshiva Oholei Yosef Yitzchok Lubavitch, I am, needless to say, in agreement with and pleased by. A benefit in matters of the Yeshiva is always, in the final analysis, a spiritual benefit; for when funds are added, students are added, and there are more hours of learning and the like. May Hashem Yisbarach help your son, may he live and be well, that this should bring him, within the entirety of Klal Yisrael, everything good for which he hopes.

You do not mention in your letter the development and progress of Beis Rivkah in particular, and the affairs of Chabad in general, but I am certain that you and your children and grandchildren are doing everything required in this regard, and we have the promise of our holy Rebbes that it will be successful.

Especially as we are now coming from the days of Sukkos, which are called the time of **our rejoicing** (*zman simchaseinu*). Chassidus interprets the plural form used here to mean that Hashem rejoices with Yidden, and Yidden rejoice with Hashem, and we have been instructed to carry this with us from these days throughout the entire year.

With regards to all members of the family, and with success in all that was mentioned, and for good tidings.

## 4808

4808

B"H, 4th of Menachem Av, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and distinguished, occupied with communal needs, R' Dovid, may he live,
Shochet u'Bodek

Greetings and blessings!

...I am writing regarding the present matter — as these days, the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and distinguished, a man of many accomplishments, R' Azriel Zelig Slonim, may he live, director of Kolel Chabad in the Holy Land, may it be rebuilt and established, will be visiting your camp to attend to matters of the Kolel. It is surely unnecessary to elaborate on the importance of Kolel Chabad, as all of our holy Nesi'im, beginning with our Rebbe, the author of the Tanya (the authority on the hidden aspects of Torah) and the Shulchan Aruch (the authority on the revealed aspects of Torah), and continuing until the Nasi of our generation, have cherished and held this charity dear, and have repeatedly encouraged and urged support for it.

I am certain that you will do everything in your power to ensure the success of his visit, and the merit of the many and the charity of the Holy Land, may it be rebuilt and established, will depend upon you and assist you.

With blessings for good tidings, in good health, and with much nachas from all your offspring, may they live.

#### ***4808***

**R' Dovid:** Patshnik. Another letter to him — above, Vol. 8, 2327.

## 4809

4809

B"H, 4 Menachem Av, 5717

Brooklyn, N.Y.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your notification that a daughter has been born to you, mazal tov.

May it be the will of Hashem that you raise her, together with your wife—may she live and be well—for Torah, for the chuppah, and for maasim tovim, in comfort and abundance.

It is known from my father-in-law, the Rebbe [Rashab], of blessed memory, that our custom is to say "for **Torah**, for the chuppah, and for maasim tovim" even upon the birth of a daughter, based on the words of our Sages (Berachos 17a): "By what merit do women earn [a share in the World to Come]? By sending their children to learn Torah... by waiting for their husbands... and by guarding [their children]..."

Regarding the timing of the naming of a daughter: We heard the instruction of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, based on the precedent of the Mitteler Rebbe, to do so at the earliest possible Torah reading. For various customs regarding this, see the sefer *Taamei HaMinhagim*, Part 4, 78, and the sefer *Kesser Shem Tov* by Rabbi Gagin, Part 1.

With blessings of mazal tov.

4410

B"H, 5 Menachem Av, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, the esteemed Chassid, G-d-fearing and learned, engaged in communal needs, etc.,
R' Shlomo Yosef, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

It is surprising that for a very long time there have been no letters or news from you. Yet in the meantime, the month of Tishrei has passed, with its Yomim Tovim and their associated matters—which provide ample opportunity to exert oneself in the field of Chabad, both internally and externally.

It seems that the matter of the ships has been silenced entirely, despite the fact that the opposing side has increased their activities beyond what was practiced in the summer—for now, the sailings have been scheduled to take place on the day of and on Erev Shabbos! And even though, in my opinion, sailing on Motzaei Shabbos is also absolutely forbidden... anyone who observes the history of the persecutions against Torah and mitzvos will realize that whenever a persecution or decree against a specific mitzvah intensifies, the beginning and the opening for it was the weakening in the observance of that very mitzvah among those who are G-d-fearing.

I hope that the arrangement for the payment of the shluchim’s expenses has already been implemented, as you discussed with our friend [Shazar] at the time, especially considering what I wrote in my previous letter—that the repayment of an old debt is somewhat in doubt, and the wise will understand. Thank you for informing me of the details regarding this.

With blessing,

**M. Schneerson**

Rabbi Valgelernter told me during our farewell conversation before his return to Eretz Yisrael—may it be rebuilt and established—that he would endeavor to at least obtain a prohibition from the Chief Rabbinate directed at the ship owners.

Surely you remember that I protested the matter of shipping frozen meat to Eretz Yisrael—may it be rebuilt and established—before it has been salted, which caused you to wonder, etc. What I feared has now come to pass: although they have only been doing this officially from here for a few months, the breach has already spread here, and they are doing this in the slaughterhouses in American commerce, claiming that the Chief Rabbinate permits it. It is understood that here, the extreme freezing, etc., does not affect the health of the **body** as much. (And the same applies in Eretz Yisrael—may it be rebuilt and established—after the meat is received there.)

Regarding what I wrote previously concerning the concern about the title "President," I recently came across the volume *HaTorah VeHaMedinah*, volumes 5-6, which contains an article by Rabbi Fogelman. See there.

## 4411

B"H, 5th of MarCheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of the 23rd of Tishrei, in which you write regarding your work in the field of girls' education on the basis of *taharas hakodesh*, and regarding the bitter situation that there is no continuation for the education of these students in your institution, and therefore some of them are being swept away by the current, etc. This has raised a question in your mind as to whether you are permitted to continue to engage as an educator in such an education, since you are assuming responsibility for the students.

It is surprising that there is even room for doubt in this matter. For, according to your own estimation, a large number of the students remain in the camp of those who are *chareidim* to the word of Hashem—and even one *nefesh* of Yisrael is an entire world. Furthermore, there is no doubt that even for those few who go out to a foreign camp, a deep impression remains from the years of traditional education they received—an impression that acts not only regarding thought, but also in action, as is clearly evident. Although it is obvious that one must necessarily seek ways to arrange for the older students to be in a *chareidi* environment—and the magnitude of this matter cannot be overstated—especially since the prospects for saving these students are great, given that they spent several years in a traditional environment. I do not doubt that you have already thought about this problem and the manner of its solution; I would be interested to know your conclusions on this, which surely, at the very least, some of them should be implemented in practice. It is a known statement of our Sages: "Do not consider anything impossible." We have seen in our time many events that there was no room in the intellect to believe would ever come to pass, and the period of extraordinary conduct and events that exceed what was anticipated in advance has not ended at all, as is evident even in daily life. And we have been promised that the measure of good is greater than the measure of retribution.

Fortunate is his portion, and the portion of all those who engage in education on the basis of *taharas hakodesh*, who in this period stand in the breach to save *nefashos Yisrael* from the unusual winds and the raging waters—a salvation that is not only for the life of the World to Come, but also for the life of this world.

Certainly, in the program of the institution that you manage, an important place is given to the study of a Jewish worldview, and primarily to the study of the *halachos* required in daily life. As we saw from the experiences in the country of Russia, the study of *Chassidus* and its concepts fortifies the student and their home to a much greater degree, even in a situation where the study of *mussar* and the like did not hold up. And although we must be strong in the *bitachon* that there will not be, *chas v'shalom*, persecutions in such a manner again, even in that country—and certainly in other countries—the trial of wealth and freedom and the lack of a yoke is sometimes much harder to withstand than the trial through poverty and persecution.

With blessings for success in your holy work and for good tidings in all that has been mentioned.

## 4412

B"H, 5th of Menachem Av, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed and distinguished, etc., R' Refael, may he live,

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter—which lacks the date of its writing—

1) Regarding what we discussed concerning *kavanah* in *tefillah*: that there are two types—the one required throughout the entire *tefillah*, and the one that is mandatory only for the first verse of *Krias Shema*, the first blessing, and *Shemoneh Esrei*. You raised a question regarding the first type: whether it is required for the entire duration of the *tefillah*.

This is similar to the laws of *lishmah* in every area, where it must be present at the beginning of the matter, and the assumption is that what follows continues from that beginning. See also what our Alter Rebbe writes in *Tanya*, end of Chapter 41 (58b). It is also worth noting the general principle regarding this in the *Kuntres Acharon* there (154b).

2) Regarding what you wrote in connection with the explanation of the *Kesef Mishneh* on the language of the Rambam, end of Chapter 6 of *Hilchos Avos HaTumah*, that the purity of the *mikveh* is specifically at the moment one emerges from the *mikveh*—and thank you for transcribing the words of the Gaon of Rogatchov on this from the *Tosefta*. I, too, have room to remark that the very logic mentioned—that the purity is specifically upon emerging from the *mikveh*—is a great innovation and also puzzling. Were it not for the fact that the *Kesef Mishneh* stated it, one would have several points to raise against it, as is discussed at length in the book *Shem MiShimon*, First Edition, by R' S. Pollak (Satmar, 5692), *Yoreh Deah*, Siman 13.

It was pleasant for me to read in your letter that you relate to Chabad, which strengthens my hope that you have fixed times for studying the teachings of Chabad, or that you will, at the very least, establish such times from here on. Certainly, your friends and acquaintances among *anash* in Jerusalem, the Holy City, may it be rebuilt speedily, will assist you in this if any assistance is needed.

With blessing.

#### ***4412***

Printed in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 21, p. 355.

## 4413

4413

B”H, 6 MarCheshvan, 5717

Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter, in which you write regarding the *takanah* of reciting *Tehillim* after *davening*, asking why it should not be recited before *davening*.

These are two separate *takanos* and two different *segulos* and matters. Reciting *Tehillim* before *davening* is a *hachanah* for *davening* and is related to *Tikkun Chatzos*. Reciting *Tehillim* after *davening* serves as a conclusion to the *davening* and a beginning to the *seder hayom* that follows *davening*. The *takanah* of reciting *Tehillim* specifically after *davening* dates back to the *kadmonim*, and in our generation, it was renewed by my father-in-law, the Rebbe, *ztzuklalleh-h, navgam, zi”a*. As explained in many of his letters—specifically in the *Kovetz Michtavim* booklet on *Tehillim*—and as is also printed in the *Tehillim Ohel Yosef Yitzchak*, from which the greatness of this matter is understood, it is the *zechus* and holy obligation of everyone who is able to do so to see to it that this *takanah* is implemented in every place they possibly can, *uzechus harabbim talui bo*.

I hope that you yourself observe the *takanah* of reciting *Tehillim* after *davening*, as well as the other *takanos*: studying every day a *parsha* of *Chumash* with the commentary of *Rashi* from the *sedra* of the week—Sunday from the beginning of the *sedra* until *parshas sheni*, Monday from *parshas sheni* until *shlishi*, and so on—and also the *takanah* of studying the daily *shiur Tanya*, as the *Tanya* is divided according to the days of the year, as explained in the aforementioned letters. In any case, you should begin doing so from now on, *v’Hashem yatzlichu*.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

**Secretary**

#### ***4413***

**R’ Refael:** Cohen, Jerusalem.

## 4414

B”H, 6 MarCheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

I have received your letter, in which you write regarding your family relations.

In general, one sees from experience in such cases that occur during the first few years after the wedding, that the best way to smooth things over and establish peace for many years to come—to the satisfaction of both parties—is by **not** paying attention to the disagreement, which is certainly only a temporary matter. The less attention one pays to it, and the more each side continues with their own positive conduct, the sooner it resolves and smooths itself out.

It goes without saying that I will try to do what I can in an appropriate manner, but I hope that you, too, will act from your side as written above. It is well known what is written: "As water reflects a face to a face, so does the heart of man to man" (Mishlei 27:19). This is especially true when it concerns a man and his wife. Since our Sages have said that "a woman’s tears are easily shed" (Bava Metzia 59a), one must be careful in one’s conduct toward her, that it should be gentle and in ways of pleasantness. May Hashem Yisbarach grant success so that very soon you will be able to report a change for the better for both of you, and that you may both raise your son, *sheyichyeh*, to Torah, to the *chuppah*, and to good deeds.

With blessings, and awaiting good news.

#### ***4414***

**These are:** See also the collection of letters on Tehillim, p. 211, in the footnote.

## 4456

4456

B"H, 6 Marcheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the avreich, Yitzchak Tzvi, may he live.

Shalom and blessing!

In response to your letter of 29 Tishrei:

At an auspicious time, I will mention all those you wrote about at the holy resting place of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. May it be the will of Hashem that your brother, Chananya Yosef, may he live, will accept upon himself the full yoke of Heaven as he enters the age of the yoke of mitzvos, and in good health, may he grow to be a Chassid, a G-d-fearing person, and a lamdan.

Regarding your observation concerning the instruction of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe (in the *Hayom Yom* calendar), regarding the commencement of laying tefillin—that it does not state exactly how long one should lay them without a blessing, and what the reason is for starting without a blessing in the first place:

It can be said, simply, that it is until one becomes thoroughly accustomed to being precise in laying them so that it is in accordance with the mitzvah, for otherwise, it would be a *bracha l'vatalah*. Since not everyone is the same in this regard, a precise time for laying them without a blessing was not established.

It is surprising that you do not mention in your letter the activities of the month of Tishrei, and in particular, within the framework of Tzeirei Agudas Chabad.

With blessing,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,**

**A. Quint**

**Secretary**

## 4816

4816

B"H, 6 Marcheshvan, 5717

Brooklyn.

To the esteemed and distinguished, etc., R' Nachum, may he live,

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter from Motzaei Shabbos, in which you ask for my opinion on whether you should endeavor to transfer a youth vocational training center for maritime trades and fishing to the framework of those who are G-d-fearing. However, you do not provide any details regarding this, neither concerning the spiritual aspects nor the material ones. This is especially pertinent given that such an institution surely requires experts, who are few in number, and on the other hand, it certainly involves specific expenses for an institution of this nature.

You are surely aware of the wild state of Yiddishkeit among sailors—wild even in comparison to the most dismal situations in similar circles, but in other professions. Although there are several reasons for this, one of them is that the captain is the sole ruler of the ship or vessel. Furthermore, this aforementioned cause is difficult to remove through a G-d-fearing captain, as for the time being, such individuals are not readily available. This is particularly true given that there are other factors involved as well, which must also be taken into account when deliberating on taking on an institution such as this. It is understood that most of the aforementioned contradictions do not apply to fishing, but some of them are relevant there as well.

With blessing,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,**

**Secretary**

#### ***4816***

A portion was printed in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 11, p. 200, and it was completed according to a photocopy of the letter.

**The avreich Yitzchak Zvi, may he live:** Eisenbach, Jerusalem. Another letter to him—above, Vol. 13, 4551.

## 4417

4417

B"H, 6 Cheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

I received with pleasure your letter dated Erev Shabbos Kodesh, with the news that you have been accepted as Rav in [city name]. As it appears, it will be possible for you to conduct the rabbinate without any outside influence, Heaven forbid.

May it be the will of Hashem that the desire of Hashem shall succeed in your hand to uphold and disseminate matters of Yiddishkeit in your camp, going from strength to strength. As is known, the words of our Alter Rebbe regarding the essence of the title *Mara D'Asra* are that, in truth, the Rav is the "master of the house" of the location—meaning, with the authority of *semicha* and the *Shulchan Aruch*. And even though a person is close to himself and "a person is not trusted regarding himself," it is known—as explained in many places in the *sichos* of our Rebbeim—that in the teachings of Chassidus one can find many pieces of advice and perspectives on how to nullify the counsel of the *yetzer* and his schemes to deceive a person.

Furthermore, you have additional assistance in that your wife, *tichyeh*, will certainly be able to assist you in this aforementioned *avodah* within her own sphere, based on the trials she has already passed and overcome. The general point is that even while using ways of pleasantness and ways of peace, one must lead the congregation with the appropriate firmness; there is no need for compromises at all. On the contrary, by offering a compromise, one loses the trust of the *baalei batim* in the Rav, whereas the path of truth achieves the opposite.

Although you do not specify the content of the *shiurim* taught in public, there is certainly included in this the study of *halachos* that are necessary for daily life, since, after all, "this is the whole of man."

With blessings for success in your aforementioned *avodah* in holiness, with health in spirituality and health in physicality.

#### ***4417***

**R' Nachum** Vinersky, Jerusalem.

## 4418

4418

B"H, 7 MarCheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed and G-d-fearing, who engages in the study of Torah and performs mitzvos, etc., our teacher R. Naftali, may he live, a shochet.

Peace and blessing!

You are surely aware of what my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, stated—and which is also found in some of his maamarim—that every year (or more precisely, at Rosh Hashanah), a Jewish person must undertake an increase in matters of Torah and mitzvos. You are surely doing so, and this increase should also include an increase in the study of Chassidus, which is the Torah of the Baal Shem Tov. May Hashem grant you success and enable you to bring good tidings regarding all of the above.

With blessing.

#### 4418

**The words of Rabbeinu HaZaken:** See also above, Vol. 4, Letter 885; Vol. 7, Letter 1885; Vol. 10, Letter 3135.

**Compromises:** See regarding this above, Vol. 13, Letter 4463, and the references in the footnotes there; below, Letter 5196.

## 4419

4419

B"H, 9 Cheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To Professor Avraham Yitzchak HaKohen, may he live and be well,

Shalom u’Bracha!

I received your letter before your departure, but I did not reply as I did not know when you would return, and thus the response has been delayed until now. Since we will soon meet face-to-face, I will be brief here, addressing the points in your letter:

Regarding your comment on what I wrote concerning the prohibition of intermarriage for non-Jews, noting that there are opinions that it is included in the prohibition of "You shall not intermarry" on the grounds that "he will turn away your son," etc.

In the case at hand, the discussion concerns the source of what is written in the Quran—that is, the writings of someone who was an *am ha'aretz* in matters of Judaism, whose knowledge was acquired in fragments from others, as evidenced by the contradictions found in the Quran. From this, it is understood that it is highly probable that the sources for his knowledge and conclusions must be simple and easily accessible. Therefore, where it can be said that the foundation is a explicit passage in *Devarim* or similarly in *Malachi*, as I noted in my letter, it is not clear why we should force the interpretation that he delved into the opinions of the *Rishonim* or even the *Amora'im* and decided in favor of one side, and the like.

Regarding what you write concerning the fast of Ramadan, I will admit, without shame, that it is news to me that it lasts forty days, for until now I had assumed that just as the month is thirty days, the fast is as well.

Under separate cover, I am sending you copies of what we have published recently, and I am certain you will find them of interest as well.

With respect and blessing.

#### ***4419***

**R' Naftali, may he live,** Unik, Montreal. Another letter to him — see above, Vol. 11, 3747.

## 4820

4820

B”H, 9 Marcheshvan, 5717

Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from the 5th of Cheshvan, along with its enclosure, following a silence and a break of the longest duration. It is evident from this letter of yours that, aside from the matter of [omitted], you are not involved in the affairs of Chabad. It is understood that such conduct—even though, to my regret, several of anash in Eretz Yisrael, may it be built and established, hold to it—has no place at all, not only in the intellect of holiness but even in healthy human reason. Although I understand all the excuses regarding the matter—that so-and-so is not cooperating, etc., etc.—as explained in several sichos, such excuses may have a place regarding reward and punishment, but it is simple that they add nothing regarding the matters themselves, their maintenance, and certainly not their development. It is also obvious that even though the matter of [omitted] is most exalted, and one who is occupied with a mitzvah is exempt [from other mitzvos], etc., there is no doubt that there are many hours in the day when one is free from the occupation of this mitzvah, and one searches for and occupies oneself with various matters, and "my own vineyard I have not kept."

May it be the will of Hashem that these words will not be idle talk or like a voice calling in the wilderness, and that in the end, they will achieve their effect.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

**Secretary**

## 4421

B"H, 9 Marcheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of the 2nd of Marcheshvan, in which you write regarding the health of your daughter, she should live and be well, concerning her eyes and the opinion of the doctors.

First and foremost, before deciding on a trip, the standard procedure in such matters is for the attending physician to describe what is known of her condition and the treatments administered, and to send this information to the doctor one wishes to consult—in this case, the doctor in Boston. It is also heard here that many from Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established, consult with expert physicians in Switzerland. It would be appropriate to do the same regarding them, as one can certainly find connections in Switzerland through acquaintances in Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established.

The matter itself, as you describe it in your letter, is somewhat unclear, as the degree of pressure in the eyes is easily determined; therefore, it is puzzling how there can be any doubt in the matter. Furthermore, if the ailment occurred suddenly, there is room to suggest that it is related to shock (psychological trauma). Your letter does not mention at all whether this has been investigated, and there are specific treatments for this. Although this is a simple concern, I nevertheless found it necessary to raise this point as well.

At a time of favor, you should mention her at the holy resting place of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. May it be His will that you bring good news regarding all that has been mentioned.

With blessings for good tidings.

## 4822

B"H, 10 Mar-Cheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of the 5th of Mar-Cheshvan, in which you describe your study schedule and conclude by noting that there are matters which are "difficult," citing as an example the language of the Rebbe himself in *Torah Or*, *Parshas Chayei Sarah*.

It is surely known to you that an explanation of this has been printed by the Tzemach Tzedek (printed as an addendum to the Kehot edition of *Torah Or*). In general, regarding matters such as these which are "difficult" in their phrasing, it is understood that one should not skip over them. Rather, one should study them at least superficially, and in a case where even a superficial understanding is not achieved, one should at least recite the words. As with all matters, one can find an allusion to this in the *nigleh* of Torah as well, namely the statement of our Sages in *Megillah* (18a): "Are we... do we know...? Rather, the mitzvah is the reading..."

With blessings to increase in the study of Torah and the performance of *mitzvos* with *hiddur*, in good health.

***

**4822**

**Avraham Yitzchak [name]:** Katz. Another letter to him appears above in Vol. 12, 4190. See there for the context of the present letter.

**Regarding the eye:** That it fell to the ground and its vision was interrupted.

## 4823

4823

B"H, 10 Mar-Cheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of the 5th of Cheshvan, in which you write about what happened to you during childbirth, may Hashem spare us.

You should clarify whether, during your shidduch, there was any slight to the honor of a son or daughter of Yisrael—meaning, were there conditions made with another person, and the like, and then you retracted them without receiving proper forgiveness, etc.? If this is the case, you must now arrange to ask for their forgiveness, or do so in the presence of ten Jews. Likewise, it is understood and simple that you must be meticulous in observing the laws and customs of Taharas Hamishpacha, and also have the husband’s (may he live and be well) tefillin checked, as well as the mezuzos in your home. In addition to the fact that you surely follow the custom of kosher daughters of Yisrael to set aside money for tzedakah before lighting candles every Erev Shabbos and Erev Yom Tov, you should also set aside a few coins for tzedakah every weekday of Monday and Thursday. It is proper that your heart be confident in the Creator of the world and its Guide, who will fulfill the requests of your hearts for the good, in health and joy.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,**

**Secretary**

## 4824

B"H, 10 MarCheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter dated the 5th of MarCheshvan, in which you write regarding the curriculum in the Yeshiva concerning the study of the Zohar and the writings of the Arizal, and you conclude that you are studying according to the path of [the Rebbe], who illuminated for us in this generation, etc.

Now, without intending to detract from the honor, essence, or matters of anyone—for it is understood and simple—it is most astonishing to study the wisdom of Kabbalah according to new interpretations and new methods, after the alarming things that have been said about this in the holy Zohar, and at greater length and with greater clarity in the introduction of Rav Chaim Vital to *Shaar HaHakdamos*. This is a *kal v'chomer* (an argument a fortiori): If in the earlier generations, of which the introduction of Rav Chaim Vital speaks, there was such a strong warning that the study of Kabbalah must be specifically as transmitted from person to person, etc., until [the source], then how much more so is this true in our generation.

I am even more surprised that at a time when the Baal Shem Tov—to whom the word of Hashem was revealed and who learned from his teacher Achiya HaShiloni, the teacher of Eliyahu HaNavi—transmitted this gift to his students, and from them to the students of their students, and their teachings spread, and the teachings of Kabbalah and Chassidus became publicized with broad explanations and firmly established interpretations, from where do these ideas come to explain matters with other interpretations and in a different manner? Especially when the words of our aforementioned Rebbeim illuminate and explain, and by their light we shall walk to greet Moshiach Tzidkeinu. As is known, the response to the Baal Shem Tov [was] "when your fountains spread outward." And even if you were to say that regarding oneself, each person can explain matters in his own words, suited to his own intellect and understanding (though even this requires further study), there is certainly no place for this regarding teaching others. This is similar to the statement of the Mishnah: "I heard from the many... you heard from the individual" (*Eduyos*, end of chapter 5), and the matter is understood.

My words have already been said, relying on what I heard many times from my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, and it is also brought in many places in the teachings of Chassidus: Fortunate is the portion of anyone who strives and labors for the dissemination of the teachings of the Baal Shem Tov, his students, and the students of his students—their teachings exactly as they are, with the interpretations and explanations according to their path specifically. Such a person will walk his path securely and takes the primary share, for the merit of the many depends upon him.

#### 4824

**Regarding the birth:** That the infant did not survive.

4825*

**Libah Chaya:** Rimler, Brooklyn.

## 4425

4425

B"H, 11 Menachem Av, 5717
Brooklyn.

To Liba, may she live,

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your notification that you have accepted a position as a kindergarten teacher for the education of Jewish girls based on the purity of holiness.

May it be Hashem’s will that this appointment be in a good and auspicious hour, to educate those under your influence and instruction upon the foundations of our Torah—the Torah of life, the words of the living G-d—so that they may grow to be a name, a praise, and a glory.

It is surely unnecessary to emphasize the magnitude of the matter of engraving the laws and customs of Israel into the memories of Jewish children. Even though at the age of young children this does not come through formal study, we see tangibly that with small children, habituation in the laws and customs of Israel is, on the contrary, more formative than the study of these laws and customs themselves.

This is especially true if, when fulfilling these laws and customs, one adds a story or an explanation appropriate to the children’s understanding, which gives them a taste of the law and custom.

I am certain that you have fixed times for your own learning, not only to review what you have already learned, but also to add to it—especially since Divine Providence has placed you in such a lofty and responsible position as the one mentioned: traditional Jewish education.

With blessings for success in all of the above and for good tidings in this regard,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

## 4826

B"H, 11 Mar-Cheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, G-d-fearing, learned, and diligent, engaged in the study of Torah and the performance of many deeds,
Rabbi Menashe, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter of the 4th of Mar-Cheshvan, continuing our discussion regarding what you wrote in *Chochmas Shlomo* concerning whether a wife’s maintenance (*mezonos*) is a Torah obligation or Rabbinic, and your transcription of his language and the ensuing back-and-forth on the matter.

There are several points to note regarding what you wrote:

1) It is obvious that for those who hold that a master is obligated in the maintenance of his student’s wife by Torah law, it follows that a wife’s maintenance is by Torah law. This very derivation proves that the maintenance of his children is also by Torah law, since a master is obligated in the maintenance of his children. You have set aside his words, where he strains to explain that this refers to minor children; surely, for *Chochmas Shlomo* as well, this must be the case.

2) Regarding your response to the difficulty: that even if we were to say that a master is obligated in the maintenance of his student’s wife, it is still merely a custom—for if so, many derivations would be nullified, and you wish to explain that all these derivations are for a Rabbinic obligation.

I am surprised that you would attribute to me the error of saying that all these *hilchasa gavrata* (Berachos 31a) are by Torah law, especially since they are derived from the Prophets and Writings (*Nach*). The same applies to the covering of the head for a mourner and the like.

However, it is simple that there is no practical difference whether we derive a Torah obligation or a Rabbinic one, since the point is whether a custom mentioned in the *Tanach* constitutes an instruction that we are obligated to follow, or if it might be merely a narrative and remain optional. If you hold by the latter view, it is understood that just as one cannot derive a Torah obligation from customs mentioned in the Torah, so too one cannot derive an obligation from the words of the Prophets and Rabbinic sources regarding customs mentioned in the words of the Prophets. This is simple—and that is why I raised the difficulty as I did.

3) A comment in passing, though it does not touch upon the essence of the discussion: regarding your assertion that since *tefillah* is Rabbinic, it must be said that all these *gavrata* are also Rabbinic—in my humble opinion, the two are not dependent on one another. It is possible that *tefillah* itself could even be optional, yet when one prays before the King of Kings, the Holy One, blessed be He, several parameters would be placed upon him, even by Torah law—parameters related to the honor of the Place, the love of Hashem and His awe, "G-d is in heaven and you are on earth, therefore let your words be few," and so on. In my opinion, this is also simple.

4) Regarding whether mourning is by Torah law or Rabbinic, the opinions of the *Acharonim* and *Rishonim* are collected in *Sdei Chemed*, *Klallim*, *Maareches Alef*, subsection 350, and cited there, as well as in *Pe'as HaSadeh* there.

5) Regarding customs mentioned in the *Tanach* and their authority—whether this makes them an obligation or merely a narrative—this is explained in several details in the book *Toras Neviim* by Maharatz Chayes; see there.

With blessing.

## 4827

4827

B"H, 12 MarCheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn.

To my dear friend, the chossid, G-d-fearing, and learned man, occupied with communal needs,
R' Yehuda Leib, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

I hereby confirm receipt of your letter from the 7th of MarCheshvan, the day in the Holy Land (may it be rebuilt and established) when the recitation of "tal umatar" [dew and rain] for a blessing begins (see Likutei Torah, Ha'azinu, that tal umatar represents the general flow of influence from Above, brought about either through *is'arusa d'le'eila* [arousal from Above] or *is'arusa d'לתתא* [arousal from below]; see there).

In it, you write regarding the farbrengen during the time of our rejoicing in the seventh month, which is saturated with all goodness. May it be the will [of Hashem] that it should have a practical effect upon the entire year, such that all undesirable matters should be in a state of "decreasing and going," and all matters of holiness should be in a state of "increasing and going." Happy is the portion of all those who exert themselves in this with the exertion of soul and the exertion of body, as instructed by our holy Torah, for the merit of the many assists them, and they take the lead.

Please convey my regards to all his colleagues in this holy work mentioned above, and [wishing them] success in the task of each one of us in *tuch"y* [tishmerem v'tichyem - may He guard and keep them]. "And Yaakov went on his way"—a going that is incomparable, which comes about specifically through the descent of the soul into the body, a descent that is for the sake of an ascent, even higher than the level of "they arose in thought," as is explained in many places.

With blessings.

#### ***4827***

**R' Yehuda Leib:** Slonim, Jerusalem. Additional letters to him—above, Vol. 13, 4436, and in the references noted there.

## 4828

4828

B"H, 12 Mar-Cheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of the 7th of Mar-Cheshvan. Surely, even though you do not mention it in this letter, you are making an effort to ensure that your like-minded friends, when they meet with your wife—may she live and be well—will explain to her the matters that are unclear to her regarding our Torah in general and Chassidus in particular. Often, words heard from a stranger are received better than those heard from relatives, friends, or even one’s husband. It is certainly unnecessary to remind you that in all matters of Torah and mitzvos, including the matter of influencing the mistress of the home—for "his wife is as himself"—one must exert effort, as our Sages of blessed memory famously said: "If one says, 'I have not labored and yet I have found,' do not believe him; 'I have labored and have not found,' do not believe him." One of the details of this is to be a living example of all the matters one speaks about to the person one wishes to influence. May Hashem grant you success in all the above.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

**Secretary**

## 4829

B"H, 12 Mar-Cheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 7 Mar-Cheshvan, in which you write regarding your state of mind and that you perceive changes in it, etc.

It is surprising that you find this surprising, after the statement of our Sages, of blessed memory—which is also explained at length in Tanya—that "one nation shall be stronger than the other," and at times this one rises, etc. I await a detailed proposal from you regarding the coming months, at which time I will be able to consider it and express my opinion. Certainly, until then, you will continue with your studies just as you have until now.

With blessings for good tidings in all that was mentioned.

## 4430

B”H, 12 Menachem Av, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

It is surprising that there has been no news from you at all, particularly regarding the utilization of your talents in upholding and disseminating Yiddishkeit. This is the spiritual task of every single person in Israel in our generation—those of us who remain as brands plucked from the fire. It is even more surprising that, apparently, your son—may he live and be well—is not only failing to draw closer to the matters to which he should be drawing closer, but on the contrary: instead of using the strength of mind he inherited from his fathers and forefathers in accordance with their wishes, he uses it to demonstrate that he is not influenced by any true friends and is capable of walking specifically in other paths, even if they are crooked. Despite the fact that in recent times he has seen several miracles from Divine Providence regarding himself, he is ashamed to reveal his true and inner connection to everything that is holy to us. It seems the reason is, "Perhaps someone will say that he is being dragged along by the rock from which he was hewn." Yet, in such a situation, if he were to hear the appropriate words from you—with the appropriate strength and in the appropriate manner—it would certainly encourage him to reveal openly what he possesses internally—what he received as an inheritance, as mentioned above: the Chassidic point that permeates all of a person’s faculties, which remains in a state of concealment until the person himself brings it from concealment into the open.

I do not know the details of the relationship and the connection between you, but now is not the time to be concerned with all of that regarding a matter that concerns the soul of the son and the manner in which his life will be arranged in the future.

With blessings for good tidings in this matter, and also regarding the other matters mentioned in my letter due to the opportunity,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,**

**Secretary**

## 4431

4431

By the Grace of Hashem, 12 Menachem Av, 5717
Brooklyn

To the Administration of Tzeirei Agudas Chabad in...
May Hashem keep them alive.

Peace and Blessing!

Your very long silence is both puzzling and distressing. After investigating from various angles, the reason appears to be a lack of proper activity, and it is obvious that there has been no increase in "routine" activities.

Regarding what I wrote above—that the situation is "distressing"—this truly does not suffice or fully capture the matter. If the youth, who are full of energy and for the time being are free from the burdens of earning a livelihood, need to be awakened, encouraged, and prodded, and even after all that it does not produce results, then what will be regarding those who already have the "millstone around their necks"? And if this is the case for those who have already merited to see the light of the Torah of Chassidus and its concepts to some degree—where it is discussed and explained thoroughly regarding the virtue of *zerizus* (diligence) and *hiddur* (beautification) in all matters of Torah and *mitzvos*—and [where it is explained] that the matter of *zerizus* and *hiddur* is not merely an "addition," but rather it is precisely through this that one sees whether the person is a servant of the King of Kings, the Holy One, Blessed be He, or someone who thinks he stands on his own authority, merely asking what his obligation is so that he can perform it, viewing the matters as a burden and therefore disposing of them in a way that he believes he has already fulfilled his duty and returns to his independence—it is understood that such a mode of *avodah* requires encouragement, prodding, and the giving of *yashar koach*, etc., and even this sometimes succeeds and sometimes does not.

It is understood how, according to the mindset of those who conduct themselves in such a way, the behavior of those who have not yet merited the light of the Torah of Chassidus and the empowerment from the *Nesi'im* of Chassidus—beginning from the Baal Shem Tov until my father-in-law, the Rebbe, *ztzukulleh*—will be.

...And it is also understood and simple that if throughout all the days of the year it is required of every individual within the Jewish people that the days be filled with content and not be "empty days," then how much more so regarding the days of the month of Tishrei, the seventh month, which is "sworn" with all good and is also a general good for all the days of the year. If one does not utilize these days—and utilize them fully—the consequences of this for the duration of the year are understood.

It is true that I received clippings from the newspaper regarding the *Daled Minim* campaign, but aside from the fact that it appears this was done on a small scale and not in an organized manner as required, it does not exhaust the *avodah* that Divine Providence has privileged Tzeirei Agudas Chabad to bring to fruition. And not only does it not exhaust it, it is only a small part of it (even though, like all *mitzvos*, this *mitzvah* is also comprised of all the *mitzvos*, and from it comes an addition of strength to all other *mitzvos*, both the lighter ones and the more stringent ones)...

And is this, according to the opinion of the administration of Tzeirei Agudas Chabad, the content of the command that "Yaakov went on his way"? And by "going" at such a pace, when will we reach the matters that Divine Providence has placed at our disposal and demands of us to work upon? *Avodah* (work), from the root *avod* (tanning) hides—through which one arrives at the matter of being a "servant of Hashem," and from there to a higher level of "serving Hashem," until the level of the "faithful servant"...

With blessings for good news regarding all that has been said, through appropriate *hisbonenus* (contemplation) on the role of the Jewish person in general upon the face of the earth, and in particular on the role of the members of the community of Chassidim, for whom our holy *Nasi* gives his life—not only during his lifetime in this world, but also after his soul ascends on high. As is explained in the letters of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, and in many places, and as is also hinted at in the words of our Sages (Sotah 13b, end; and see there in *Tosafos* at the end of the chapter) regarding the first redeemer being the final redeemer, Moshe *Ra'iya Mehemna* (the Faithful Shepherd): "Moshe did not die... just as he was there... so too here," and similarly in *Bamidbar Rabbah* and *Tanchuma* (Chukas) on the verse, "He gathered the heads of the people, etc. of Israel," which indicates that a faithful shepherd is connected to his flock even after his passing.

Awaiting good news.

## 4432

4432

B"H, 12 Menachem Av, 5717

Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of the *pidyon nefesh* regarding [Name] *shlita*. It is surely unnecessary to urge you to seek appropriate methods and suitable ways to assist the aforementioned, so that he may emerge from this spiritual impasse in which he has entangled himself due to shame before those who mock him among the youth who are devoid of Torah and *mitzvos*. Because of his stubbornness—utilizing his intellect in the wrong place—he is ashamed to admit that he has made a mistake in this. As is the nature of many people in such situations, he wants to demonstrate that, on the contrary, he will take yet another step and another step along his path, which is utterly undesirable. I am confident that through contemplation, and perhaps also through consultation with those who are familiar with his nature, you will find several paths and methods, and may Hashem grant you success. It is known from our Sages that one who sustains a single soul of Israel is as if he has sustained an entire world. This is especially true in a case such as this, where the ancestors of the aforementioned are interceding on his behalf, demanding above and below his rescue from the crooked paths upon which he has walked in recent years.

Awaiting good news.

#### ***4432***

**Topic:** Impasse.

## 4433

B”H, 13 Menachem Av, 5717
Brooklyn

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and refined, occupied with communal needs, R’ Mordechai Dov, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

In response to the notification of your safe arrival at your destination: surely you remember what we discussed before your departure, that your activities should be conducted with a bright countenance and with joy. Through this, there will be an increase not only in the matter of “spiritual sowing,” but also in the matter of “gathering physicality.”

Regarding your question in your letter as to how to conduct oneself in giving tzedakah so that it aligns with what is stated in Iggeres HaKodesh concerning the virtue of the mitzvah of tzedakah when performed many times: This matter is explained in *Levushei Serad*, which was cited in this regard by the Rebbe the Tzemach Tzedek and has now been printed in a maamar explaining the teaching of our Sages that "every single coin...". Since the book (*Levushei Serad*) is not readily available, I have copied in the margin what is written there. Furthermore, it is obvious that the Alter Rebbe—and the source cited in Iggeres HaKodesh and the aforementioned [maamar]—do not intend to diminish [the amount given], but rather to add to it. That is to say, when one has a specific sum to give, the intention is not that they should delay giving the entire sum in order to ensure the act is performed many times. Rather, it is only to add that one should not wait to give a coin until several coins have accumulated, for there is a virtue in repeating the act of giving tzedakah many times. And this is simple.

With blessings for success.

*Levushei Serad*, Siman 103:
"Every person is obligated to hold fast to the trait of tzedakah and to have a special pocket for tzedakah. The authors explained that it is better to give a little at a time than to give a large amount all at once. Therefore, it is appropriate for a person to place one coin into the tzedakah pocket each time, and to do so every hour, all according to his capacity and means, until what he gives throughout the day adds up to a large sum, and all should be done for the sake of Heaven (see the following paragraph). When someone who needs to receive tzedakah happens to come along, he should distribute from the pocket as explained (in Shulchan Aruch, Siman 251, Seif 9, and see Knesses HaGedolah, Siman 7)."

***

 R’ Mordechai Dov Talishevsky, Brooklyn. Another letter to him appears in Vol. 13, 4185.

## 4434

B"H, 13 Menachem Av, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter, in which you write regarding the shidduch that you made in recent weeks, and for which the time of the wedding has already been set in a good and auspicious hour, and now rumors have reached you concerning the health of someone in the family of the person in question.

You should continue with the preparations for the wedding, which has been set for the coming month of Kislev, and may it take place at its proper time, for the building of a home in Israel, an everlasting edifice founded upon the principles of Torah and mitzvos. It is understood that the primary preparations for a wedding are preparations in one's spiritual state, through an increase in the study of our holy Torah—the Torah of life—with diligence and perseverance, and an increase in the beauty and care with which one performs mitzvos. You should completely dismiss these rumors from your mind. The Creator of the world and its Guide, through your perseverance in connection and dveikus with Him—as stated in the holy Zohar, "Three bonds are linked one to another: Yisrael is linked to the Torah, and the Torah is linked to the Kudsha Brich Hu"—will guide you in what is good for you, both materially and spiritually.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**
**Secretary**

#### 4434

**Regarding health:** That he is not entirely settled in his mind.

## 4435

4435

B"H, 14 Menachem Av, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings,

In response to your letter from Erev Shabbos Kodesh, in which you write that you wish to hear about the matter of fear of Hashem, as there are those who are distant from it, etc.

Heaven forbid to say of a Yid that he is distant from fear of Hashem. After all, the verse explicitly states, "For this mitzvah... is not distant from you... for the matter is very close to you" (see there in the commentary of the Ramban that this refers to the mitzvah of teshuvah). This has already been explained at length and with great clarity in the holy Tanya, how the matter is close to you—and not just close, but "very" close. See there at the beginning of Chapter 17 and the beginning of Chapter 18, where the discussion concerns ahavah, and **dichilu** (fear) is included within it as well. See also Likutei Torah on the maamar beginning "Ki Hamitzvah."

It would be appropriate to have your tefillin checked, as well as the mezuzos in your home, to ensure they are all kosher according to the law. Furthermore, you should establish a daily study session in the teachings of Chassidus, in addition to the daily shiur in the holy Tanya, which is appropriate for every soul, as part of the well-known three daily shiurim of Chumash, Tehillim, and Tanya.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,**

**Secretary**

#### ***4435***

Published in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 21, p. 531.

## 4436

4436

B"H, 14 Adar I, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter.

It is my hope that you will safeguard your time, filling it with the content of Torah and mitzvos—both the revealed Torah and the teachings of Chassidus—and with the beautification of the performance of mitzvos in general, and specifically in the avodah of tefillah, which is one of the foundations and essential principles in this regard (as is explained in many places in Chassidus, including Likutei Torah, Parshas Balak 70d, and at length in Kuntres HaTefillah). It appears that you have not yet begun this, and yet the time before the wedding is more precious than anything, for the preparation at that time influences one’s entire life, and this is understood.

Certainly, the strength is granted from Above so that, with will, exertion, and appropriate effort, you will succeed in all the aforementioned. I look forward to receiving good news from you regarding all of the above.

With blessings.

#### ***4436***

Published in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 19, p. 513.

## 4437

B"H, 14 Cheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the esteemed and distinguished individual, who engages in communal needs, etc.,
R' Shmuel, may he live,

Peace and blessing!

It was a pleasant surprise to receive your letter of 14 Tishrei, and it was even more pleasant to see your interest in Kfar Chabad, which surely leads to actions that assist the village in its stabilization and development.

I read your proposal in this letter with special attention. As is evident from your letter itself—from what is written and what is implied between the lines—you too recognize the difficulties involved, especially in Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established, where every matter is interpreted in seventy different and strange ways, if only to stamp it with a partisan seal. It seems that word of this proposal has already reached several circles and has triggered reactions of the very kind that you, too, wish to negate the basis of.

It is understood that after all this, I am considering the proposal, and I will also enter into communication with Anash, may they live, in the village.
