It is my hope that you will continue your practical interest in the affairs of the village and in the affairs of Chabad in Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established, in general. And it goes without saying that regarding your concluding remark that you approached me without being asked, etc.—there is no place for an apology for this; on the contrary.

With respect and blessing.

Regarding your mention in your letter of the example of Rabbi Meir, who found a pomegranate, ate its inside, and discarded its peel: it is well-known among Chassidim that the Rebbe, my father-in-law, said that every Jewish person must be the "master of the house" (baal habayis) over the portion of the world that has been given to him through Divine Providence to clarify, refine, and illuminate. He added an explanation that the concept of a "master of the house" is that **every** item found in his estate is utilized for the benefit of the estate.

The above is based on one of the foundations of Chassidic teaching: that in every thing and every matter there is a Divine spark, and therefore, for every thing and every matter, the command applies: "In all your ways, know Him."

In light of the above, I am confident that even the "peel" will not be thrown away, but rather that it, too, will be utilized to increase light over darkness and spirituality over physicality, increasing the form over the matter (it is understood that the above does not apply to the common terminology regarding the three [impure] kelipos, in which there is no good at all; this is not the case with the peel of a pomegranate, which, although not fit for eating, can be utilized in several good and useful ways. And so it is also in the analogy).

#### 4437

**R' Shmuel:** Rappaport, auditor of the Jewish Agency.

4439

## 4440

4440

B"H, 17 Menachem Av, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter, which is divided into two parts: the first part actually answers the question posed in the second part, the content of which is that you find it difficult to be victorious in the *milchemes hayetzer* (battle against the *yetzer hara*).

The answer to this is found in the first part of your letter, where for all the things you were supposed to do, you found reasons why you cannot do them now—all sorts of strange and varied excuses. Yet, the things you *could* have done, you do not mention at all, as if the statement of the *Mishnah*, "I was created to serve my Maker," is, *chas v'shalom*, not applicable to you... or that to serve *Hashem Yisbarach* there, you occupy yourself with business and the like. May *Hashem Yisbarach* help you remember that you are a *tamim* (a wholehearted, sincere person), and the first [thing] for a *tamim* is to be a "lamp to illuminate"—with the *ner mitzvah*, *Torah* light, and the *maor* (luminary) of *Torah*, which is the *Toras HaChassidus*. **All** other matters depend on this and come **after** this. If you will truly contemplate this, it will affect you in truth, and then you will see what you need to do and how to do it. It is a pity for every day that passes without occupying yourself with your [true] matters, *v'charmi sheli lo natarti* (and my own vineyard I have not kept).

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

## 4441

B"H, 17 MarCheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 25 Tishrei, in which you write that you may be required to teach in your class books that are contrary to our Torah and its mitzvos. Some argue that if these subjects were taught by others, the students would not identify the negative aspects of these authors' words, but would instead prefer them; whereas if you teach them, the loss would be outweighed by the gain.

However, we see clearly that when a young man or woman is introduced to new ideas and one subsequently attempts to separate them from those ideas, it is certain that they will absorb the new material, but it is highly doubtful that they will accept the refutation and criticism. They will accept the material in its entirety. Furthermore, the opposing side will have a powerful argument: if these matters and books are to be rejected, why are they being taught in school, and by a charedi man or woman at that? There have already been instances in the past involving arguments such as the one mentioned, and those who accepted them suffered a loss; it is highly doubtful that there was even the slightest gain or benefit in the matter. This is, of course, in addition to the issue of what onlookers will say—those who will not concern themselves at all with the method of teaching or the conclusions of the teachers, but will only look at the curriculum. If a certain book is included in it and is taught by one who is G-d-fearing and committed to His Torah, this serves as a complete hechsher (endorsement) for the book, and the rest is understood.

Since you do not mention anything regarding your situation, I assume everything is in order and peaceful. May it be Hashem's will that you continue to share only good news regarding yourself and all those you write about.

It is understood that there is no need at all for the aforementioned reasons to know that many of the great halachic authorities hold that it is **forbidden** to teach the aforementioned material—it is like forbidden food, which one is forbidden to eat even if one intends to try to vomit it up afterward.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

**Secretary**

## 4442

4442

B"H, 17 Menachem Av, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 11 Menachem Av: At an auspicious time, I will mention all those you wrote about at the holy resting place of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

Regarding what you wrote about your wife’s health and the suggestion by some doctors to perform surgery: It is surprising that you do not mention whether other methods have been attempted, such as wearing support stockings, massage, injections, etc. It is understood that before deciding on surgery, these methods should be tried. If all the above have already been utilized without any benefit, then a consultation (consilium) should be held with two specialists in the field, and you should follow their instructions. The Torah has granted the physician permission to heal, and may it be Hashem’s will that you bring good news regarding all of the above.

Surely your wife maintains the good custom of virtuous Jewish women to set aside money for tzedakah before lighting the candles every Erev Shabbos and Erev Yom Tov. It would also be appropriate to have the mezuzos in your home checked. I trust that you are keeping the three daily study portions established by my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory—the well-known portions of Chumash, Tehillim, and Tanya—and in any case, you should keep them from this point forward.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

**A. Quint**

**Secretary**

## 4443

4443

By the Grace of Hashem, 17 Mar-Cheshvan, 5717

Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, learned, and distinguished, engaged in communal affairs, R' Boruch, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of Wednesday, Parshas Vayeira, and surely you have in the meantime received my response to your previous letter.

I am surprised by your writing that no one is in contact with you, at a time when, as I wrote to you in my previous letter, negotiations are taking place here with the *Hisachdus HaRabbanim*, and Rabbi Hollander is in contact and will also participate financially, although they do not say how much. Also, in my conversation with Rabbi Hollander, he spoke about traveling as an expert, etc., and surely you know all this from the primary source.

My intention in speaking with Rabbi Hecht, may he live, that he should telephone you, was as an addition to the above-mentioned, because being an excellent speaker in English, he makes a strong impression on various circles. And here, as in other places, the impression on the judge and on those present plays a large role regarding the verdict. I calculated that this should be kept in mind and that one should be prepared, so that when it needs to be decided, we can utilize it.

In the meantime, I have received information here that there are certain circles among the Conservatives who want to stir up the matter, reconsidering the outcome. I do not know how well-founded this rumor is or what the basis for it is, but it is understood that if the judge remains in a good mood, one should not miss the opportunity to obtain a decision that will also serve as a precedent for other places.

Regarding what you write about apologizing for your letter, it is self-evident that there is nothing to apologize for. I hope that for many long years you will continue to write, and may Hashem help that it should be with joyful content regarding communal matters and personal matters. And in the communal matters, there should also be included not only work for Yiddishkeit in general, but also activity in the conduct of Chassidus. As is the word of our Rebbe, the founder of Chassidus Chabad, who said that Chassidus is not a thing for Chabad alone or for a portion of Jews, but rather it is a thing for **all** Jews. And it is well known, the saying that a good thing that can be done today should not be put off until tomorrow.

Unfortunately, some still need someone to explain to them that this thing is good, and I hope that the one who will lead this mission in the vicinity of Mount Clemens will be R' Boruch Litvin, may he live. And since you write that *gedolei Yisrael* do not agree with you anyway, you will surely not be deterred if there are those who consider themselves *gedolei Yisrael* and will not agree with your advocacy for Chassidus; *l'fum tza'ara agra* (according to the effort is the reward).

With blessings for good tidings.

## 4444

B"H, 17 Menachem Av, 5717
Brooklyn

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and involved in communal needs,
R' Shmuel Yosef, may he live and be well,

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter from Erev Shabbos and the one preceding it:

It is understood that you are correct in most of what you wrote regarding propaganda among those who come with claims that they consider to be scientific, etc.

However, as I have seen with many of them, in the vast majority of cases, it is as I wrote in my previous letter: the reason comes after the desire, in order to justify it. This is along the lines of what I wrote in my letter to the conference of the Independent Education teachers, which you are surely aware of. And it is understood that despite the above, it would be worthwhile to act even for the sake of the minority of the minority. But the difficulty is that there are not enough hands—writers, printers, propaganda managers, etc.—even for the work that everyone agrees is for the majority, such as the work of organizing Anash in Eretz Yisrael (may it be built and established) and in the Diaspora (where you surely see the neglect in this area), organizing the forces of those active and capable of communal work, and much more...

With blessing,

#### ***4444***

**R' Shmuel Yosef:** Pindrik, Netanya. Additional letters to him appear above, 4354, and in the references noted in the footnotes there.

4445

Printed in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 19, p. 456.

**Maaseh Rav:** See also above, Vol. 13, letter 4350, and the references noted in the footnotes there. Below, 5101, 5165.

## 4445

B"H, 17 MarCheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of the 25th of Tishrei—it gave me pleasure to read that upon the arrival of the new year, you began to daven according to the clear nusach of the Alter Rebbe, the nusach of the Arizal. May it be the will of Hashem that your tefillah be accepted among the tefillos of Klal Yisrael. And just as the middle blessings begin with the request for man’s needs, "You graciously grant us wisdom, understanding, and knowledge (Chochma, Binah, Da'as)," may it be that through this, the change for the better will be evident in all other matters requested thereafter. May it be His will that all of this occurs without unnecessary concealments and hiddenness.

Regarding your question at the end of your letter, as to how you should conduct yourself on Shabbos and Yom Tov when davening in a shul that uses a different nusach:

The room for doubt exists only if you are to serve as the shaliach tzibur at the amud. However, it is simple that when you are davening for yourself, you should maintain your own nusach*, as is well known from the *maaseh rav* (authoritative precedent) regarding this in previous generations. It is understood that the same applies to the recitation of "V'shamru," etc.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

**Secretary**

## 4846

B"H, 17 Menachem Av, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed and distinguished, etc., R' Eliyahu, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter, in addition to the *pan* delivered by the esteemed and distinguished R' Dovber Baumgarten, may he live and be well.

As you requested, I will mention all those you wrote about at the holy *tziyun* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, in accordance with the content of your letter.

Certainly, since we have entered a new year—and as is the saying of the Alter Rebbe, author of the *Tanya* and *Shulchan Aruch*, that light is added to the world (see *Iggeres HaKodesh*, Epistle 14, at the end of the *Tanya*)—may you, your friends, and all the members of the holy community of *Shuva Yisrael*, may they live, increase in matters of Torah and *mitzvos*, both you and your wives and households, may they live. Through this, the Creator and Guide of the world will increase His blessings for each and every one of them in whatever they require. May it be His will that you bring good tidings regarding all that has been mentioned.

As you requested, enclosed is a *shekel* from one of the *pushkas* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, and may it be for success in all that has been mentioned.

With blessing.

## 4447

B"H, 17 Menachem Av, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 14 Menachem Av.

In my opinion, and after considering all the reasons you write, you should dedicate yourself entirely this year to limudei kodesh, and study with hatmadah and shekidah. I hope that with effort you will find a suitable chavrusa. It goes without saying that I do not mean this for the benefit of anyone else, but rather that this is for your own benefit. If you study this year with the proper shekidah, then at the end of the year we will be able to discuss the matters you touch upon at the end of your letter. It also goes without saying that there is no basis to reconsider your senses or to think that the study is foreign, etc., etc., for this is only a question of will. When one studies without confusion, without thinking about other matters (and everything is made beautiful **in its time**), and at the appropriate time, I am certain that you will settle in, and by utilizing your talents in such a way that you will be satisfied both materially and spiritually.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

## 4848

B"H, 18 MarCheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the Avreich, Moshe Aharon, may he live,

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 3 MarCheshvan and the one preceding it...

2) Regarding your question about the places in the holy Tanya where it says "etc." (*ko*) or "and so on" (*go*), and you are puzzled as to what is intended by these words.

In general, in such instances, it is understood and simple that the "etc." or "and so on" must pertain to the words preceding them and to that specific subject, and not to matters discussed earlier or further back. Furthermore, it is understood and simple that they are intended either to indicate the conclusion of a verse or a statement, or (regarding the word "etc.") the conclusion of a subject; but they are not a hint to a new subject, nor to a subject discussed elsewhere rather than in that immediate location.

3) Regarding what you wrote about the number 620 (*taryach*) "pillars of light," suggesting that it could perhaps be 613 (*taryag*) "pillars of light," etc.

The matter of 620—*Keter* (Crown) pillars of light—is a well-known concept in the books of Kabbalah, in the prayer of Rabbi Nechunya ben HaKaneh which is cited in these books and elsewhere, and there is no basis for the number 613 "pillars of light."

With blessings,

#### ***4848***

**The Avreich, Moshe Aharon, may he live:** Priluk. Bnei Brak.

## 4449

B"H, 18 Mar-Cheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letters from the 3rd and 13th of Cheshvan, in which you write regarding the birth of your son and his entry into the covenant of Avraham Avinu, and that his name in Yisrael is... [may he grow]—may it be the will of Hashem that you and your wife, may they live, raise him to Torah, chuppah, and maasim tovim, and may he grow to be a Chassid, a yirei shamayim, and a lamdan.

Regarding what you write about your wife’s, may she live, schedule in the coming months, as caring for the son requires several changes to her previous routine:

It is understood that this depends on details and minute details that are not in your letter. The point here is based on the words of our Sages, of blessed memory, that "idleness leads to..." [sin/boredom], and therefore one must endeavor that your wife, may she live, be occupied for at least a few hours a day. It is best if this is in matters of Chabad, which are a conduit and vessels for receiving the blessings of our Nasi, the holy Rebbe, may the merit of the tzaddik protect us. It is understood that this arrangement must be made in a way that does not interfere with the care, upbringing, and development of your son, shlita. Based on these two points, and through consultation with your friends among anash, you will surely be able to find a suitable solution for the above, and it will be good in body and soul.

With blessings.

## 4850

4850

B"H, 18 MarCheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and learned, etc., R' Yaakov Eliezer, may he live.

Shalom u'Bracha!

In response to your letter of 25 Tishrei: It is surprising that there has been such a long gap between your letters. It is even more surprising given that we have just entered a new year, a time that demands an intensification of activities. As the well-known saying goes, "As one sets out, so it goes." This is especially true now that a new matter has been added—the matter of Beis Rivkah—and yet, precisely now, anash are writing less.

Regarding your questions:

1) How to rectify what occurred on Simchas Torah, when among those who came to participate in the hakafos at Yeshivas Ohalei Yosef Yitzchok, there were some who arrived by car, etc.

The rectification for this is simple, and it is surprising that even this year it was not done: A few days before the aforementioned holiday, a notice should be printed in the newspapers (it would be best to include it along with the announcement regarding the times of the hakafos and the like), stating that the prohibition of traveling by car on Shabbos and Yom Tov is certainly simple and well-known, and that it is a contradiction in terms to participate in a Torah-related event in a manner that is the opposite of Torah.

2) Regarding calling up to the Torah those whose observance of Torah and mitzvos is not clear.

I have already been asked about this once before by the shul of Yeshivas Ohalei Yosef Yitzchok in Melbourne, and I answered then. That response remains in full force now: The decision in this matter depends on several details—both regarding the conduct of the individual who wishes to be called to the Torah, and regarding those present in the shul and the impression it makes upon them. As with all matters done in public, this too must be taken into account. Based on the clarification of the aforementioned, those who are on-site must make the decision. However, it is understood according to the words of our Sages, "Always let the right hand draw near and the left hand push away," that one must conduct oneself in such a manner regarding the question at hand as well—especially when there is doubt whether one should even push away with the left hand at all. After all, it has already been said, "Its ways are ways of pleasantness, and all its paths are peace."

With blessings for good news regarding all the above.

## 4451

B"H, 18 Menachem Av, 5717
Brooklyn

Blessings and peace!

In response to your letter of 11 Menachem Av, in which you write regarding the sequence of events concerning the *shidduch* and the current relations between you, and that amidst the arguments between you, it was decided to approach a *beis din* in order to, etc., and you conclude that you are currently on the front lines against him and the entire family.

It is understood that it is difficult to offer an opinion based on the claims of only one side. For regarding every person—even the most complete individual—there is a general rule: a person is biased toward himself. Since you have decided to approach a *beis din*—and surely your intention is to appear before rabbis who are masters of *hora'ah* and experienced—there will be an opportunity to present the claims of both sides and to hear the opinion of people who stand above the parties involved.

However, I am writing to note the following regarding your letter:

1) Regarding your writing that you are on the front lines against him and the family: there is room to say that this front is also against yourself. For family life is a question of life itself, and every hasty and incorrect step has consequences that affect both sides. Often, it affects the side that initiated the step even more than the other side. I do not mean to express an opinion on the essence of the problem or the manner of its resolution—as mentioned, I have only heard the claims of one side—but merely to express my concern that you seem to be trying to sharpen the relations so that the clarification and resolution will come as quickly as possible. Yet, in such matters, a quick resolution is not always good or beneficial.

2) And this is perhaps even more fundamental: regarding your writing that you are approaching the *beis din* in order to divorce—meaning that you see this as the only possible outcome—as mentioned, and surely you would agree with this, a person standing on the sidelines can judge much more objectively than those who are in the midst of the matter. Therefore, it is not correct to decide in advance to approach the *beis din* with a fixed goal, without asking for the opinion of the *beis din* on the matter itself.

3) What surprised me in your description of your actions and the conduct you have adopted is that although you are religious, received a religious education, etc., and your worldview is religious, you have nevertheless adopted conduct to the contrary, with the intention of using this to end the relations between you, etc. It is surely unnecessary to explain that conduct appropriate to the instructions of our Torah—*Toras Chaim*—is not a matter for negotiation or to be used for matters of pressure, etc. Rather, it is a value in itself and a fundamental matter in the relationship between a person and their Creator and Guide. Aligning one’s conduct with the instructions of the Giver of the Torah draws the blessings of the Giver of the Torah upon the one who acts accordingly. These blessings include sound judgment, clear understanding, and a correct assessment of the situation in which a person finds themselves, as well as decisions appropriate to their true happiness. This is as in the formula of the *tefillah* three times a day, where the request for a person’s needs begins with the prayer: "Grant us from You wisdom, understanding, and knowledge." By implication, one can infer the opposite: that weakening the connection between a person and their Creator through contrary conduct hinders all of the above and denies one a vital and essential matter at all times, and especially during a period when one wishes to resolve complicated and fundamental questions.

I am sending this letter of mine quickly, prior to answering letters that were received before yours, in the hope that these few lines—small in quantity but significant in the importance of the matters—will awaken you to reflect once again in accordance with the three points mentioned, and to change your perspective, and most importantly, your conduct in practice, in accordance with the instructions of our Torah, *Toras Chaim*. It would please me to receive confirmation of this hope from you.

With blessings that Hashem Yisbarach, who oversees each and every one with *hashgacha pratis*, will guide you on the good path before you, in true goodness.

On behalf of the Rebbe Shlita,
Secretary

4452

4452

B”H, 20 Marcheshvan, 5717

Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letters from Erev Shabbos Kodesh, the previous one, and from Thursday, in which you write regarding a *tikkun* for the known matter.

Regarding all matters of *tikkunim*, it is a simple principle that first and foremost there must be a firm commitment for the future regarding action—in the language of the Rambam, "that he will not return to his folly again," etc.

Concerning the *tikkun* for the past, the letters of the Torah—specifically the letters of the *Mishnayos* and the *Pnimiyus HaTorah*, meaning the *Toras HaChassidus*—must be engraved in one’s memory. In this way, even at times when one is not consciously thinking about them, they remain engraved in the faculty of memory, as is explained in many places, including in *Likutei Torah* by the Alter Rebbe, at the end of *Parshas Kedoshim*.

Therefore, it is proper that you be fluent by heart in several chapters of *Mishnayos* and several chapters of *Tanya*. In addition to the monthly *Tehillim* cycle—as *Tehillim* is divided according to the days of the month—which you should certainly be careful to recite each day after *davening* Shacharis, and the chapter of *Tehillim* corresponding to your age, you should recite additional chapters of *Tehillim* each day in order. The number of these chapters depends on the amount of your free time. You should also have a set *shiur* for *girsa* and *iyun* in *Nigleh* of Torah, and for *girsa* and *iyun* in *Pnimiyus HaTorah*; the duration of these *shiurim* also depends on your free time.

It is also known that it is a *segulah* to give a few coins to *tzedakah* each weekday before *davening* Shacharis.

Also known is the matter of exerting effort regarding the proper *chinuch* of Jewish boys and girls in an appropriate manner—that is, through effort of one’s body, soul, or finances.

Let it be known that regarding the matter of *taaniyos* (fasting) mentioned in many books, one should consult *Iggeres HaTeshuvah* by the Alter Rebbe, beginning of Chapter 3, which states: "Indeed, all this applies to a strong and healthy person... but... as in these generations of ours..." Especially since we see clearly that fasting affects one’s health, and thereby also affects one’s power of understanding, comprehension, and *hisbonenus*. If so, the loss outweighs the gain. Therefore, it is better to "fast" according to the adage of the Rebbe Maharash (printed in *Toldos Maharash*, page 72), that one should restrain his mouth from speaking undesirable matters, etc.—for this, too, is a form of *inu'i* (affliction). On the contrary, in the vast majority of cases, it is a greater *inu'i* than the *inu'i* of abstaining from food and drink, as this is clearly evident. My hope is that when you act according to the above, you will see tangible improvement soon, which will increase from time to time. It is understood that one should not be impressed by the *yetzer hara’s* battle against the above with various strange arguments, including those he clothes in the guise of *yiras shamayim*—that is, that instead of occupying yourself with the above, you should engage in seclusion, withdrawal, fasting, and self-affliction, etc. For, unfortunately, in our generation—a "fatherless" generation—we see the results of such conduct, which often leads to matters that are the opposite of the goal and intention, and the wise will understand.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above.

Regarding what you wrote with various opinions about your arrangements and means of livelihood—you should consult with your friends who are on-site and are also familiar with your talents. May Hashem grant them the correct insight on how to advise you for the best, in a revealed and good way.

## 4453

4453

B"H, 20 Marcheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of the 17th of Marcheshvan, along with the enclosure.

At an auspicious time, I will mention you, your wife—may she live and be well—and all those you wrote about, at the holy resting place of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

Regarding what you wrote—that you are in doubt about studying Gemara with an eleven-year-old girl: If it is only a matter of ease, you should arrange to study such *sugyos* that have relevance to practical matters. In that case, it falls into the category of studies for women, as explained in *Hilchos Talmud Torah* by our Alter Rebbe, at the end of Chapter 1; see there.

There are many such tractates and numerous *sugyos*, especially since she is approaching the age of accepting the yoke of *mitzvos*, at twelve years old.

It is surprising that in the *shiur* you study with Mr. [Name]—may he live and be well—there is no mention of studying Chassidus. Even if one were to say that the aforementioned does not feel the necessity of the matter, on the contrary, that is proof of the necessity of studying this with him, and *kal v'chomer* (it is understood).

Especially since my hope is that after he has already spent time within the walls of Tomchei Temimim, the situation is not as it seems—rather, he is surely making a calculation that since the time for studying together is limited, it should be utilized for the study he mentions, but not for the aforementioned. However, when you explain to him the concept of *kav chumtin* and the content of the matters explained in *Kuntres Eitz Chaim*, he will surely agree easily and will even request to dedicate some of their shared study time to the study of Chassidus. May Hashem grant you to share good news regarding all that has been said.

With blessings for success in your holy work, both you and your wife—may they live and be well—in good health.

May Hashem fulfill the days of your wife’s pregnancy—may she live and be well—properly and easily, and may she give birth to a healthy child in its proper time, properly and easily.

With blessings.

## 4454

4454

B"H, 20 Marcheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of the 14th of Cheshvan, in which you write regarding the state of mind of your wife, *tichyeh*, and that because of this she wishes to be examined by a doctor, while you do not see the need for such haste.

While my opinion is the same as yours—that one should not be overly concerned that she has not yet conceived—since your wife is affected by this, you should explain your reasoning to her. If she accepts it calmly and with a glad heart, that is good. However, if for any reason she remains troubled, you should fulfill her wish to be examined by a doctor. Regarding your concern because it would be a male doctor, I have not heard that there is a stricture regarding this in matters of medicine. At most, you should inquire whether there is a female doctor who is an expert in this field.

Regarding what you wrote concerning so-and-so, that he had his tefillin checked and the *parshiyos* were found to be kosher and *mehudar*, it is understood and simple that the checking of tefillin includes the *parshiyos* as well as the *batim* and the *retzuos*. Furthermore, although I am surprised by the matter, I have already seen **many times** in such cases that I was informed from Eretz Yisrael, *tizkeh l'mitzvos*, that tefillin were given to one *sofer* for checking and he declared them kosher, and subsequently, a second *sofer* found a missing letter or something similar—which is a *pasul* according to all opinions.

It is surprising that you do not mention in your letter anything about your activities in spreading the *maayanos* outward, nor about the activities of *Tzeirei Agudas Chabad* in general. I am sure you will fill this in at the earliest opportunity.

With blessings,

**M. Schneerson**

#### ***4454***

Part of this was printed in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 17, p. 501, and part on p. 589; it was completed based on a photocopy of the letter.

## 4855

4855

B”H, 21 MarCheshvan, 5717

Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 13 Cheshvan, in which you write that you have proposed to the administration of the Tomchei Temimim Yeshiva in Lod that changes be made to the curriculum, and you provide your reasons.

It is well known how much caution must be exercised regarding changes to a curriculum that has been in place for many years, and according to which hundreds and thousands of students have been educated—students who have proven the validity of the program. It is also well known how well-suited this program is to the inner goal of raising students who are *temimim* both at home and abroad, and of fortifying them firmly against the unusual winds currently blowing in the world. This applies to all students, and all the more so regarding students who excel in their studies within this program, i.e.,

That success comes to such a student more than is usual. After all, human beings are not subjects for experimentation to see whether a change will succeed and add something or not. In particular, changing the status quo is, in most cases, more likely to lead to loss than to gain, for by doing so, one connects the student not only to the subject matter being studied but also to a new environment—both in terms of books and people—whose influence is, to a certain extent, contrary to the worldview in which we wish to educate the students. Furthermore, when a change is introduced into the Yeshiva’s curriculum, it provides a *hechsher* (legitimization) for that study and the matters surrounding it, and the rest is understood.

Although I do not know him personally, my advice—and I hope it is accepted—is that for as long as it is possible for him to remain in the tent of Torah and complete *yirah*, he should study in the Yeshiva and endeavor not to confuse his heart or take time away for new and different matters. For this is not merely a question of losing a few hours of *limudei kodesh*; it is a question of the student entering a different world.

Regarding what you wrote concerning the necessity of certain knowledge due to the demands of the present world: to our regret, such matters are absorbed by the youth much more easily than *limudei kodesh*. Therefore, within a short time after they leave the walls of the Yeshiva, and after they have received sufficient immunization against harmful influences, it will be easy to acquire the matters truly necessary for settling down and for simple livelihood. May it be His will that you derive much true *nachas*—that is, traditional Jewish *nachas*—from all your descendants, may they live.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

**Secretary**

#### 4855

**Regarding the curriculum:** The study of the Hebrew language.

## 4856

B"H, 21 MarCheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and learned, involved in communal needs,
R' Yisroel Zusman, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

...Surely by the time this letter is received, you have already received regards from the guests who visited here, along with at least some points from the words of *hisorerus*. May it be the will of Hashem that they have their intended effect regarding your *avodah* throughout the entire year.

With blessings for good tidings in your personal matters and in general matters.

P.S.

Regarding what you wrote concerning the appropriateness of a *farbrengen* without hearing words of Torah — it is surprising that there is even a doubt about this, as it is an explicit *Mishnah* (*Avos*, Chapter 3), and especially in light of what is explained in *Chassidus* in many places regarding the concept that "when ten sit together, the *Shechinah* rests among them." While the *Shechinah* rests even if they are not speaking words of Torah, on the other hand, it is understood how necessary and precious every word of Torah is when heard at such a time of the resting of the *Shechinah*.

**And it is simple** that one should speak words of Torah, provided that this does not cause *machlokes* (or the cancellation of the *farbrengen*). See *Shulchan Aruch Admur HaZaken*, *Orach Chayim*, Section 159, end of subsection 23; and *Kuntres Acharon* there.

## 4457

4457

B”H, 21 Mar-Cheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of Thursday: It is surprising that there is such a long interval between your letters, and that you are so brief regarding matters where one is instructed to elaborate—namely, matters of Torah and mitzvos, and matters specifically concerning the camp of the Chassidim.

Regarding what you wrote with reasons and explanations for your involvement in the profession of *shochet* and your avoidance of working in "my vineyards" [the Rebbe’s institutions] as is required: I have already spoken of this in many places and to many of *anash*. The excuses offered in such cases—aside from the doubt as to how true they are, whether in their entirety or only in part—even if we were to assume that these excuses are one hundred percent correct, what does this accomplish regarding the inner and true task of every Jew, which is the Mishnah’s statement, "I was created to serve my Maker"? As the Tzemach Tzedek stated, we are "workers of the day," whose task is **to illuminate**. Even in a case of genuine *ones* (duress) according to Torah, the halachic ruling is known: we do not say "it is considered as if it were done." And although, according to what is explained regarding the level of a true *eved* (servant), it should not matter to him through whom the Master’s affairs are accomplished, as long as they are done properly, it is highly questionable whether any of us has reached such a level of *avodah*. The test for this is whether he is equally accommodating regarding physical matters and bodily concerns, or if he is only so regarding spiritual matters and the concerns of the *neshamah*...

With blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned.

## 4458

4458

B"H, 21 Mar-Cheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter, it is understood that my reference to certain seforim was intended to facilitate your work in gathering information and dealing with the matter about which you are writing.

Regarding what you wrote concerning the visit of Rabbi... shlita: surely you are aware that his visit was for the purpose of inspiring matters of Torah and mitzvos in the area, and for no other goal. Therefore, it is surprising that you did not seek to assist him in that aforementioned work, for you were on-site and certainly could have helped with the matter. As it appears from your letter, you did not even meet with him.

Regarding what you wrote about the doubt concerning the spelling of the name Akiva, and that they showed the Rebbe Rayatz in a dream the verse, "Light is sown for the righteous, and joy for the upright of heart" (Tehillim 97:11), which implies the spelling of Akiva with a hei: surely "it is not in heaven" (Devarim 30:12), and we do not pay heed to a heavenly voice (bas kol). This has already been discussed at length in this and similar matters in several places. The point here is that "it is not in heaven" applies to a halachic ruling, whereas it is different regarding the clarification of a factual reality. In such a case—to know how the name of Rabbi Akiva was spelled, with which the names Akiva are connected even in subsequent generations—and it is understood.

With blessings.

#### 4458

Printed in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 7, p. 349, and completed according to a copy.

** Regarding what you wrote about the doubt:** See also Igros Kodesh of the Rebbe Rayatz, Vol. 2, Letter 534.

## 4859

4859

B"H, 23 MarCheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the avreich David Tzvi, may he live,

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter, in which you ask regarding what our Alter Rebbe writes in Igeres HaTeshuvah, Chapter 1:

1) Regarding a positive mitzvah, that even if one has done teshuvah, the light is nonetheless missing.

The proof is written right there, as per our Sages of blessed memory regarding the verse "that which is crooked cannot be made straight," etc. It is understood that this refers to teshuvah that is not on the highest level, for there is a teshuvah that is effective even for this; see what is written in Tanya, end of Chapter 7.

2) Regarding a negative mitzvah, he writes that through teshuvah, the reality of the prohibition is not erased.

It is surprising that this is the basis for his words, for the matter is not so. See also the *Biur Heitev* in the Tzemach Tzedek’s *Derech Mitzvosecha*, in the mitzvah of Viduy and Teshuvah, through which the revealed law—that there must be the necessity of verbal confession—is also understood.

With blessing,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**
**Secretary**

***

**David Tzvi:** Merzbach, Be'er Yaakov.

## 4460

B"H, 23 Mar-Cheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the Kfar Chabad Committee
May Hashem preserve them and keep them alive.

Greetings and Blessings!

My letter, a response to your previous letter, was surely received in due time.

Regarding the matter that the time necessitates, I am writing because this year the festival of 19 Kislev falls on Friday, Erev Shabbos. Since in Kfar Chabad you also hold a public celebration in which guests from various places in Eretz Yisrael (may it be built and established) participate—and surely also from places where the travel to the village requires several hours—it should be seriously considered whether it is worthwhile to arrange the aforementioned celebration on Motzaei Shabbos, so as not to cause [a chillul Shabbos], etc. This is especially true since even if the celebration begins several hours into the night, there are always those who arrive early, and the matter is understood. If there is room for this concern, and even if there is only a doubt in the matter, it is worthwhile to schedule the celebration on Sunday (or Thursday). It is understood that this must be publicized in advance in an appropriate manner.

Since I have touched upon this, I will point out one more matter: I do not know the format of the celebration in previous years—whether there was a program or not. Since, thank Hashem, the celebration has been successful in previous years, it is appropriate that there be a program, as that would certainly allow for even greater utilization of the event. If a precise program with every minute detail is not possible, then at the very least, it should be planned in advance as much as possible, and individuals with authority should be appointed to discuss and oversee the details during the event itself.

May it be the will of Hashem that all of the above, as well as other efforts, serve as a prelude, preparation, and vessel for the fulfillment of our heart's desires, speedily and in our days, in the style of our Nasi, may the merit of the righteous protect us, that we be written and sealed for a good year in the study of Chassidus and the ways of Chassidus.

With blessings.

## 4861

4861

B”H, 24 Menachem Av, 5717

Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter from the 20th of Cheshvan and the previous one.

At an opportune time, I will mention you and your wife, may she live, at the holy resting place of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, for the fulfillment of your hearts’ desires for the good, in a revealed and revealed way. You should certainly strengthen your trust in the Creator of the world and its Guide; since this is the first mitzvah in the holy Torah—the Torah of life—and a great mitzvah, and the Holy One, blessed be He, does not deal tyrannically with His creatures, surely if you remain in hiskashrus and dveikus to the mitzvah, by living a life consistent with it and elevating yourselves through this—as our Sages, of blessed memory, said, "one elevates in matters of holiness"—you will be able to fulfill this mitzvah and do so with joy.

With blessings for good tidings.

#### ***4861***

In continuation of this—see below, letter 5136.

4462

B"H, 24 Marcheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and learned R' Yitzchak, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter of 14 Cheshvan (and the one preceding it), in which you ask:

1) Whether it is permissible to engage in medical treatment only for a sick person, or if it is also permissible to use those medicines that prevent illness—that is, those that fortify one’s health so that one does not become ill.

It is a daily occurrence that even the greatest of our people and their leaders do so; see also the Rambam, Hilchos Deios, Chapter 4. Furthermore, the verse explicitly states, "All the sickness... I will not place upon you, for I am Hashem, your healer," which implies that this, too, is included in the category of healing.

2) Regarding those substances that are injected into the body, is it permissible to use them? It seems your question concerns those that are made from non-kosher materials.

Here, too, it is a daily occurrence that G-d-fearing and observant people act in this manner without any hesitation, for it is permitted to derive benefit from things that are forbidden to eat but permitted for other forms of benefit.

3) How to conduct oneself regarding the Salk vaccine, which they are now beginning to inject into children.

This has already been practiced here for several months, including in the most observant circles. That is to say, there is no question here regarding kashrus or the like, though it is understood that one must ensure the vaccine comes from a reputable and reliable laboratory.

I trust you maintain your set times for study in both Nigleh and Chassidus, and that you increase them from time to time, in accordance with the well-known directive to "ascend in matters of holiness."

It is understood that blood transfusion is a separate matter, which has become common practice in recent times and is becoming increasingly widespread, where no scrutiny is made at all as to who is the donor. Logically, this would seem to cause more *timtum* (spiritual blockage) than what is mentioned in Shulchan Aruch, Yoreh Deah, end of Chapter 81, in the Rema. Perhaps the reason for the lack of scrutiny is that transfusions are often used for a patient who is in danger, or at the very least, seriously ill. Furthermore, it is a one-time occurrence, unlike the case in Yoreh Deah there.

- To note: The bloodletting mentioned in the Gemara is, in most instances, performed on a **healthy** person.

Regarding the laws of medicine, see Shulchan Aruch, Yoreh Deah, Chapters 155 and 336.

The study of the inner dimension of Torah in our time is, as explained at length elsewhere, through the study of the teachings of Chassidus. It is appropriate to begin with Tanya, Part III, then Part II, and subsequently *Kuntres Uma'ayan*, *Derech Mitzvotecha* by the Tzemach Tzedek, etc.

Regarding what you wrote about whom you should influence in your surroundings: the law is explicit in the Shulchan Aruch that even a poor person in Israel is obligated in the mitzvah of tzedakah, and the same applies to spiritual tzedakah.

The role of every individual is as the Mishnah states: "I was created to serve my Maker." The manner of this service is explained in the Shulchan Aruch. See Shulchan Aruch, Orach Chaim, Chapter 231. It is understood that it must be suited to one’s talents and one’s environment.

With blessing,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,
Secretary

## 4863

B"H, 24 Marcheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of Wednesday, with the *pidyon nefesh* enclosed, in which you write regarding the state of your health, which is not as it should be.

May it be His will that you throw yourself with the same intensity into improving your health in spiritual matters as you have labored until now in your livelihood, as you write in your letter; for then this will immediately influence the improvement of your physical health as well. It is understood that this does not preclude following the doctor’s instructions, for the Torah has given the physician permission to heal, and they are messengers of the *Rachmana*. However, one must be careful not to make the primary secondary and the secondary primary, as is understood. Especially since you had the merit of studying for several years at Tomchei Temimim, where the words of the *tzaddikim* and their actions endure forever and exert their influence through hidden powers, down to the revealed powers and the garments of the soul, which are thought, speech, and action.

You will surely inform me of the doctors’ further opinion following the examination you wrote about, and may it be His will that you receive good news soon.

You are surely keeping the three known *shiurim* in *Chumash*, *Tehillim*, and *Tanya*. From now on, you should also establish an additional *shiur* in the study of Chassidus, the *ilana d’chayei*. May it be His will that the promise, "He who fulfills the Torah... will eventually fulfill it," be realized for you soon, with physical and spiritual wealth.

With blessings for good tidings,

**M. Schneerson**

## 4864

4864

B"H, 24 Menachem Av, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished individual, occupied with the needs of the community, R' Yehuda Leib
Shy

Greetings and blessings!

I was pleased to receive the news, among the reports from the Yeshiva Achei Tmimim Lubavitch in Pittsburgh, that a dinner is being prepared, the proceeds of which will go to the Yeshiva and which is being held in your honor.

It is a well-known instruction of our Torah, the Torah of life, that it is a mitzvah to publicize those who perform mitzvos. This is especially true when the action of those who perform the mitzvah is in the field of *chinuch al taharas hakodesh*—Torah education—which is one of the pillars upon which the House of Israel stands. Particularly in our time, a time of double and redoubled darkness of the *galus*, it is of vital importance that the education of Jewish children be in the spirit of the *Torah Temimah*, with pure *yiras shamayim*, and with *chom chassidim* and light.

And fortunate is the portion of all those who are involved in this, to receive additional blessings from the Giver of the Torah in all that they and their families are in need of.

I hope that our brethren, the Children of Israel in Pittsburgh, who have known you for many years, will appreciate the importance of what has been said above and will participate in this dinner, and will also influence their friends to participate as well. As mentioned, you will also have a share in their portion.

Since you do not write to me with any details regarding your private matters, I hope that this is a sign that all is well and in peace, and may Hashem Yisbarach grant you the merit to report good tidings in this regard as well.

With blessings for success in all that is mentioned.

#### ***4864***

**R' Yehuda Leib:** Ungar, Pittsburgh. Another letter to him - above, Vol. 4, p. 127.

## 4465

B"H, 24 Menachem Av, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed and distinguished, etc., R' Avraham Yehuda Leib, may he live:

Peace and blessing!

I was pleased to receive your letter from the 20th of Menachem Av, the birthday of the Rebbe Rashab, on which day, as is known, one’s *mazal* is strengthened.

I read in it about the strengthening of the *shiur* in *Chassidus* and the addition that you will review a *maamar* during *Seudah Shlishis*, at the time of *Idna d'Rava d'Ravin* and *Eis Ratzon*. This is also the time to behold the radiance of *Ze'er Anpin* when one fulfills the invitation, "Draw near to Me and see My strength." Happy is the portion of all those who participate, and even more so those who exert themselves in such matters, for "in the light of the King's countenance is life." The Creator and Guide of the world will surely fulfill His promise at this time to nullify all the *klipos* of *Bei Rava* and to remove all wrath. And since from *Shabbos* all the days are blessed, you draw this down upon the entire week, as it is from *Shabbos* that it is drawn down below in a revealed manner.

With blessing.

#### ***4465***

**R' Avraham Yehuda Leib:** Klein, Jerusalem.

## 4866

B"H, 25 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished individual, of noble lineage, R' Shmuel Abba, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

Not long ago, I received the items delivered through our relative, the esteemed Chossid, man of many accomplishments and fine character, R' Azriel Zelig Slonim, may he live. These include a comb, a snuffbox, and a handkerchief, all from the Rebbe, the Rashab. Since the aforementioned does not know the precise details regarding the later years—and our Sages have said, "Whoever gives, gives with a generous eye"—you surely know the details regarding to whom these belong and the order of their provenance. Many thanks for this gift, and also for the effort taken in writing [the accompanying letter].

Since we have just entered a new year, and especially in light of the saying common in Poland, where you lived for several years, that the final sealing occurs in the month of Kislev during the days of Chanukah, may it be the will of Hashem that the renewal of fortune and the increase in the blessings of the Creator and Guide of the world exert their influence upon your daily life, in goodness that is visible and revealed, both materially and spiritually. And in accordance with what has been said many times, great joy breaks through barriers and removes all measurements and limitations in this.

With blessings for good tidings.

#### ***4866***

**R' Shmuel Abba:** Shapira. Additional letters to him appear above in Vol. 11, 2866, and in the references noted there.

## 4467

B"H, 25 MarCheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed and distinguished, etc., R' Shmuel Elazar, may he live:

Peace and blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter dated the 18th of MarCheshvan. You have surely already received my response to your previous letters. I also acknowledge receipt of the newspaper you sent; I trust you will continue this good practice in the future, and I thank you in advance.

As you requested, at an auspicious time, I will mention all those you wrote about at the holy resting place of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

It was a pleasure to receive your regards and the news regarding the farbrengens and the inspiration for good activities. May it be His will that you also share good news regarding the practical implementation of these resolutions.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above.

Regarding what you wrote about the custom in Eretz Yisrael (may it be rebuilt and established) to add the word **"Kaddisha"** (this [place]) in the Kaddish D'Rabbanan: one should investigate the origin and source of this custom. Especially since this addition is not found in the Kaddish D'Rabbanan in the version of prayer in the Rambam—in all the editions I have seen—nor in the version found at the end of the tractates of the Shas and Mishnayos, nor in the *Avodas Yisrael* or *Otzar HaTefillos* prayer books. Furthermore, it is somewhat difficult halachically, for the entire land is holy, not just "this place" specifically. One should note the lesson from *Shemos 3:5*. Moreover, every shul and beis medrash is a holy place (see *Sefer Yereim*, section 264). In the *Siddur HaRashash* printed in Jerusalem in 5676, this addition is not present.

#### 4467

**R' Shmuel Elazar:** Shaulson, Jerusalem. Additional letters to him appear above in Vol. 13, p. 294, and in the notes referenced there.

## 4468

B"H, 25 MarCheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn.

Mrs. Frida, may she live,

Blessings and peace!

...Regarding what you wrote about erecting a *matzeivah*, one should clarify the custom in Eretz Yisrael as to whether one waits a full year or not, and follow the local custom. If there is no fixed custom regarding this, or if there are differing customs, one should not delay the erection.

May the Creator of the world and its Guide lengthen your days and years in goodness, and may you bring tidings of only good regarding yourself, your children, and your children’s children, may they live.

With blessings.

***

**Mrs. Frida, may she live:** Silberstrom, Jerusalem. Another letter to her—see above, Vol. 13, 4441. See there for the content of the letter before us.

**Matzeivah:** See also below, letter 4499.

## 4869

B"H, 24 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, learned, and distinguished Rabbi Moshe, may he live:

Peace and blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of the book *Kiryat Sefer*, which you have published with the addition of *Oznayim LaKupah*. May your strength be fortified in disseminating the Torah of the *Rishonim*, who are "like angels and like men," as the *Shelah* explains: "man" (*adam*) is derived from the word "to resemble" (*adamah*) the Supernal, for the ancients were those who sacrificed their lives for the sanctification of the Name (*Berachos 20a*).

And great is the study that leads to action—a directive for action in the conduct of every person who trembles at the word of Hashem, even in these days. See there in Rashi, *s.v. b'nezikin*, that it now also speaks of one who degrades himself for the sanctification of the Name.

Out of affection for the matter, I perused the pages of the book shortly after receiving it, albeit in haste, and some notes are transcribed below.

With blessings for success in the work of strengthening and spreading true Yiddishkeit to raise the horn of *Yisrael Sava*.

P.S.

**At the beginning of the book's introduction:** "Which caused me to wander, etc." — Your Honor brings a proof from this — in the introduction, section 4 — regarding the time the book was written, proving from it that it was written at the time of "the expulsion of the Jews from France, (and even though) our teacher was not among the expelled, he speaks of the entire collective." However, according to this, what follows later in the author's introduction is not understood: "He gave my sons as refugees and my daughters into captivity, they were driven from clinging to the inheritance of the Land of Beauty, etc., for many days, etc." And several other expressions that follow.

In my opinion, and especially according to what Your Honor wrote that our teacher speaks of the entire collective, the author’s intent is to explain the reason why a Torah scroll corrected according to its law is not found, etc.—which is the cause for the necessity of such a book—that the situation came about because Israel went into exile and hearts were diminished, etc. And this is his intent in what he wrote, "Which G-d caused me to wander from my father's house," meaning the Land of Israel, as he wrote later, "the Land of Beauty," and all the above is resolved.

**Magen Avraham, Part 2:** That "for its sake" (*lishmah*) means for the sake of the Torah of **Israel**, and the mentions of the Name for the sake of the **sanctity** of the Name. And at the end of the part: "for the sake of the Torah of **Moshe**, etc." Now, it is logical to say that there is no practical difference between one who says "Torah of Moshe" (like the language of the *Rosh*) or "Torah of Israel" (like the language of the *Sefer Mitzvos Gadol*). And therefore he brought both expressions. But it requires study: 1) Whether it is the author's opinion that saying "Torah" alone is insufficient. 2) His source that one only mentions "sanctity" when mentioning the Name. Regarding these two points, one should look into *Tur Shulchan Aruch Orach Chaim 32* and *Yoreh Deah 274*, and the sources cited there.

**At the end:** The beginnings of *Matos-Masei* in the words of Yirmiyahu and the Shabbos before Tisha B'Av, etc., and in a place where they do not interrupt *Matos-Masei*, etc.

According to this, when they do not interrupt *Matos-Masei*, they only read two [Haftoros] of the three of rebuke, and on the Shabbos of Parshas Pinchas, even though it is during the Three Weeks, they read [a Haftorah] similar to it!

## 4870

4870

B"H, 25 MarCheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and refined, involved in communal needs,
R' Menachem Zev, may he live, Halevi

Shalom u’Bracha!

In response to your letter of the 18th of MarCheshvan and the one preceding it, along with the enclosures therein: It appears that I did not clarify my intention when I spoke with you regarding the compilation of Chabad customs, for I said to myself that it would be understood on its own.

My intention was along the lines of a compilation of the customs found in the *kuntreisim* and the complete *Machzor*, where only those customs that are novel or unique to *anash* are included, and primarily those matters that are not so well-known. For it is understood that for the time being, and without any prepared material yet, the time is not ripe for a more complete collection—which is what you seemingly intended in your plan—though I do not despair of this; it simply requires time and several preparations for the gathering of material.

Based on my aforementioned intention, many of the sections in the drafts you enclosed—such as those beginning with section 1, 2, etc.—have no place in such a compilation of customs. [An additional point regarding section 1, based on what is explained in *Kuntres HaTefillah* regarding the matter of *Tikkun Chatzos* in chapter 11 (end of page 24 and onwards): that although it requires study whether one should publicize what is stated in *Kuntres HaTefillah*, on the other hand, in a collection of customs regarding actual practice, one should not print the opposite.]...

With blessings.

***

**R' Menachem Zev, may he live:** Gringlas, Montreal. Additional letters to him: above, Vol. 13, 4285, and in the references noted there. Below, 4957, 5264.

**Compilation of Chabad Customs:** Published in the year 5726: *Sefer HaMinhagim - Chabad*.

## 4871

B"H, 26 Menachem Av, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and learned, engaged in communal needs, etc.,
R' Shlomo Yosef, may he live.

Shalom u'Bracha!

I have received your letter, which was delayed until Erev Shabbos (and certainly my letters have reached you in the meantime). I am hastening to reply in connection with the conclusion of your letter, in which you express hope to receive in these days from our friend Mr. Shazar, may he live, the amount according to his promise... And since you are receiving the sum in Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established, it should, of course, be utilized for matters in Eretz Yisrael, and what better way than for the matters of the Reshet, where the situation has been most pressing for some time. I am certain you will inform me regarding this, and I thank you in advance.

Some time ago, I asked whether it is true that there was an addition to the first volumes of the *Talmudic Encyclopedia*. Certainly, if this is correct, it should be sent here as well, and I thank you for this.

Regarding what you wrote about a concordance for the *Tanya*: I will tell you the truth—while in my opinion a subject index or the like is of the greatest benefit, I do not see the necessity for a concordance, nor in what way it would assist in the understanding of the concepts of Chassidus and the like.

It is my hope that you are planning activities to utilize the days of the festivals of the upcoming month of Kislev in the most effective manner.

May it be the will of Hashem that it be with success.

With blessings for good tidings,

**M. Schneerson**

#### ***4871***

## 4872

4872

B"H, 26 Menachem Av, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed and distinguished, etc., R' Yissachar Dov, may he live:

Peace and blessing!

I was pleased to read what you wrote regarding the increase in the number of students, may they live, and what you wrote regarding the lack of space. You should certainly speak about this once again with the esteemed Rav and Chossid, etc., R' B.E.G., may he live. It is understood, based on what is explained in many places in Chassidus in general, and specifically regarding the concept of "the burden is according to the camel," that since the education of the daughters of Yisrael is a necessity and a vital matter—as they are destined to build homes in Yisrael, and as their title is "the mainstay of the home"—you will surely find ways to bring this matter to fruition, and this is simple to understand. And since Divine Providence has privileged you to be among the primary workers in this, your reward from Heaven is assured, and you can be confident that all that you and your household require will be provided from His full, open, holy, and expansive hand.

With blessings for good tidings.

#### ***4872***

## 4873

4873

B"H, 26 Menachem Av, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and learned, occupied with communal needs,
R' Binyamin Eliyahu, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

...Regarding what you wrote about the difficulty with the *parshiyos* and *tefillin*, there is room for hope that in time, the school for *safrus* will be a self-sustaining institution. Even if only the *Melach* [Merkos L'Inyonei Chinuch] here were to procure all its requirements from the school for *safrus*, in addition to the needs of the students of the Oholei Torah and Tomchei Tmimim institutions, it is possible that this would suffice to absorb the entire output of both the Meknes and Paris institutions. And if there is any remainder, it would be possible to send them to all the locations where Chabad institutions are found, provided that the *parshiyos* and *batim* are as they should be. Therefore, I was pleased by your letter stating that you have already ordered all the necessary equipment in Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established. Surely, in the meantime, you are preparing some of the students who are suited for this—by studying the *halachos* and the like...
