## 4474

B”H, 26 Menachem Av, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and involved in communal needs, R’ Azriel [Chaikin]
May he live.

Greetings and blessings,

It was a pleasure for me to read in your letter about the increase in the number of students, may they live. Although it is understood that this is not enough, and you are certainly striving for further growth in both quantity and quality, regarding both the boys and the girls.

Regarding what you wrote about the Beis Rivkah for girls for the full day, the necessity of this matter is understood, even if the beginning is small [though it is understood that it must be arranged in a way that does not cause conflict, and there are certainly several ways to reassure those who fear that you wish to destroy their institution, especially as the Rav also writes that he supports establishing the institution]. It has already been said, “Though your beginning was small, yet your latter end shall greatly increase” (Iyov 8:7)—and according to the interpretation of the Baal Shem Tov, through this [small beginning] it will be—and your latter end shall greatly increase. It is known what the Rebbe, my father-in-law, of blessed memory, said: when one places a lantern on the street in the darkness of night, people gather around it, and this is well understood.

You will surely write in the future regularly and with the necessary detail, and may it be G-d’s will that the news be joyous in both material and spiritual matters.

You will surely utilize the days of 19 Kislev and Chanukah, which are approaching, as is required and appropriate for your holy work in the institutions of Ohalei Torah Lubavitch. May Hashem grant you success.

With blessings,

## 4475

4475

B"H, 26 Menachem Av, 5717

Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

Following the interval, your letter from the 21st of Cheshvan was received. Regarding what you wrote concerning the dispute with the collector [shiy], surely they will reach a compromise and the collector will continue his work. It is surprising that the aforementioned individual insists on his demand regarding the percentage, at a time when the matter concerns an educational institution based on the purity of holiness. It is understood according to the words of our Sages of blessed memory that more than the collector does for the institution, the institution does for the collector...

## 4876

4876

B"H, 26 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and involved in communal needs,
R' Yehoshua Shneur Zalman, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

After a long interval, I received your letter from the 17th of Cheshvan, in which you ask me to clarify my intent regarding new buildings and the purchase of houses, specifically the house belonging to Mrs. Hertz, may she live, which is approximately a half-hour walk from the Yeshiva building of Oholei Yosef Yitzchok Lubavitch in Melbourne.

It is understood that, in general, it is proper for a girls' school to be in closer proximity to a boys' school (though not in the same building; even if there were a separate entrance, it would only be permissible with great difficulty, because ultimately there would still be intermingling before and after classes. However, in a time of great need, it may be permitted. This is different from a situation where the schools are on different streets, meaning they would not see one another during those times, which is the ideal approach). The advantage of proximity is that there are many matters that fall under the category of "a lamp for one is a lamp for one hundred," such as administrative work, examinations, supervision, and so on. But it is understood that in a time of need, one must forgo even this, especially since there is certainly a telephone, which makes the distance in location less of an impediment.

Regarding what you wrote about additional grades for the high school, you yourself conclude that this will be on the agenda in three years' time. In general, your approach is correct—that is, to add only those grades in which the education required by state law is completed, as opposed to the grades that follow. As you wrote, this involves large expenses, especially since it is unknown how many will want to continue their studies when the law does not compel them to do so.

I am sure you are making plans on how to utilize the month of Kislev and its festivals, as is fitting, for the strengthening and dissemination of Chabad matters in Melbourne, and Chabad educational institutions in particular.

With blessings for good tidings.

#### ***4876***

**R' Yehoshua Shneur Zalman, may he live:** Serbranski, Melbourne. Additional letters to him: see above, Vol. 13, 4450, and the references in the notes there. See below, 4812, 4885, 5077.

## 4877

28 MarCheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the distinguished, G-d-fearing, learned, and esteemed R. Yosef Menachem, may he live:

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 13 MarCheshvan: May it be the will of the Healer of all flesh, Who performs wonders, that just as you bring news of the improvement in [your] health, so too may you continue to bring good tidings that the situation is progressing well toward a complete and speedy recovery. This is especially pertinent as we approach the month of Kislev, the month of miracles and the days of Chanukah, which mark the completion of the work of the Mishkan and the founding of the Second Beis Hamikdash. The concept of the Mishkan and Beis Hamikdash is as it is written, "And they shall make Me a sanctuary, and I will dwell among them"—within each and every Jew. This is the ultimate goal, and this goal is attainable in reality even during the time of Galus.

With blessings for good tidings,

The *Seder Olam* was received, sent by the distinguished, G-d-fearing, learned, and esteemed R. M. Weber, may he live—a man of character whose work is work for the sake of Heaven. It is the complete *Seder Olam Rabbah*, Volume 1, with the commentary *Yemos Olam*, *Seder Zemanim*, and *Mesoras HaSeder Olam*. Many thanks for the gift. Although there is not enough time to examine it in comparison to previous editions, I trust that the commentary contains what was promised in the introduction: that it would incorporate the explanations found in the notes and glosses of those who preceded him. This is no small matter, as it will spare people the need to search for earlier printings. Incidentally, the 5665 edition of *Seder Olam* was reprinted (via photography) in New York in 5712.

Per your request, and in accordance with my habit, I have noted in three or four places what appeared to me, at least, while glancing through the pages of the book in haste. The saying of our Sages regarding the verse "And Vahev in Suphah" is well known.

General Notes:

1) Several of the calculations are repeated in the introductory tables and in the commentary, including some simple calculations. [For example: Table 1, Introduction paragraph 2, *Yemos Olam* paragraph 1].

2) The matter of when the count begins is not explained at all: Is it from the Rosh Hashanah preceding the creation of man (the year of creation), the Rosh Hashanah after it (for man), or the Rosh Hashanah after that? (See Rambam, *Hilchos Kiddush HaChodesh* ch. 6, halacha 5 and the commentary there; *Hilchos Shemitah V'Yovel* ch. 10, halacha 2; and the books that deal with this subject at length). In any case, it should have been mentioned, as it also affects the calculations in *Seder Olam*. For example: According to the calculation that the year of creation—which is the one accepted in the calculations of *Ibbur* and the like—is when man was created in year 1 (after one year of the count had already passed) *L’Brias Olam*, and not as written in Table 1.

3) The matter of "truncated years" (*shanim mekutzo'os*) is not mentioned at all. Furthermore, it is not clarified in the numbers provided whether the intention is "in the 130th year" (for example), or after 130 years have passed. It is understood that the clarification of many calculations and contradictions depends on this. Even though in many places it is impossible to clarify this doubt from the verses or from our Sages, in several calculations the clarification does exist. At the very least, as mentioned above, this issue required a mention.

**Table Vav:** It is impossible that Moshe stood before Pharaoh in the year 2448, and that in this same year they offered the Korban Pesach, since according to *Seder Olam*, the plagues between these two events lasted twelve months. The error crept in due to the lack of clarification mentioned in (3) above. It must be said that Moshe’s standing before Pharaoh was at the *beginning* of his 80th year, i.e., in the year 2447.

**Ibid:** It is impossible that the passing of the three shepherds—Miriam, Aharon, and Moshe—occurred at the same moment *L’Brias Olam*, since Rosh Hashanah fell in between.

**Introduction, paragraph 1:** Whether the year of creation was in Nissan or Tishrei—this was not decided. See *Magen Avraham*, beginning of 592. It is also proven from the halachic ruling regarding *Birchas HaChamah* that it is in its cycle in Nissan, which leads to the practical halachic conclusion that the world was created in Nissan.

**Note:** a) There is a lone opinion that the world was created on the 1st of Tishrei (Nissan), and not on the 25th of the month preceding it (*Levush* 581; in *Sdei Chemed*, *Kelalim*, end of system 2, he found a supporter for both, and expressed wonder at both. See there). b) In *Shemen Sasson* on *Shaar HaKavanos*, ch. 4, paragraph 15, he reconciles the two opinions according to Kabbalah in a lengthy explanation.

**Chapter 1:** From the Flood until the Dispersion is 340 years, and the calculation is detailed in *Seder Zemanim*: Arpachshad was born two years *after* the Flood, etc. Now, the Flood lasted more than twelve months. In any case, it should have been mentioned, given that many have struggled to resolve the omission of this year.

**Chapter 10, at the end:** In *Seder Zemanim*, he explains the difference in the calculation (on the first day) regarding the time of eating the Manna after the death of Moshe. It can be explained simply: the Manna did not descend on Shabbos. According to one opinion, this day is also included in the calculation, since they ultimately ate from that same cake. According to the second opinion, he does not wish to count it, as it does not relate to the merit of Moshe, for on *every* Erev Shabbos, a double portion descended. In the book *Asarah Maamaros*, he reconciles the opinions regarding whether Moshe died on Shabbos or Erev Shabbos. What is written in [paragraph] 150, I did not merit to understand: how can it be said that thirty days before the 7th of Adar, he wrote "Today I am 120 years old"? This can be explained only with great difficulty. But this is not the place for it.

## 4878

4878

B”H, 28 Mar-Cheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, learned in Torah, G-d-fearing, and engaged in the study of Torah, Menashe, may he live:

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of the 24th of Mar-Cheshvan, in which you elaborated with erudition and pilpul—following up on our previous correspondence—I must ask your forgiveness, as I did not know how to address it. The points you elaborated upon are not the same subject we discussed in our previous letters. I will clarify my intent, following the order of the three points:

1) Regarding the obligation of child support and whether it is linked to the obligation of spousal support.

The matter we discussed—as you also transcribe in your letter—is that those who hold that the master’s obligation to provide food for his wife is from the Torah, prove that spousal support (for a husband) is from the Torah; this very proof demonstrates that child support (which is also incumbent upon the father) is from the Torah, etc. Surely the *Chochmas Shlomo* also holds this view. From this it is understood (and it would be understood **regardless**) that those who hold that a husband is obligated in his wife’s support based on a **different** derivation—their ruling regarding the father’s obligation for his children’s support **does not** touch upon the discussion we are having. These are the Rambam (*Hilchos Ishus*, Chapter 12), and the Tur and the Smag who copied his language. And it is them whom you discuss in your letter!

**Note:** Although it is not related to the above, the fact that you struggle so much to prove that the Rambam holds that support for children, even very young ones, is only Rabbinic—which is the complete opposite of the simple meaning and content of his language, as you yourself transcribe in your letter—it is not at all understood what compels us to enter into all those forced interpretations and to say, furthermore, that the Rambam and all those who copy his language regarding young children intend to hint at various distinctions in the precision of the phrase “by Rabbinic enactment” (*ketakanas chachamim*) rather than “from Rabbinic enactment” (*mitakanas chachamim*). (Note the *Biurei HaGra* on this, who writes that it requires study, without explaining his intent). And at the beginning of the passage, they elaborate with several words, “as the name, etc.”—the simple meaning of which is **the opposite**, that child support is from the Torah.

2) Regarding what I wrote: that even if we were to say that a practice brought in the *Tanach* is not always a binding legal ruling, as it is possible it is merely a narrative—obviously, there is no difference in this regard between a narrative in the Five Books of the Torah and a narrative in the *Nach*. You object to this by noting that we find many Rabbinic enactments that are supported by a verse (*asmachta*). It is not understood how this refutes my premise, which concerns the method of Torah study—specifically the matter we are discussing: that the Torah law **obligating** a master to provide food for his wife is due to a custom that **lacks** a Torah obligation for a husband to feed his wife. Regarding this, I said that there is no room in logic to differentiate and say otherwise concerning the laws brought in the *Nach* in connection with a narrative or practice; even in those cases, one could say the practice is not an obligation but merely a permission. It is surely simple that one cannot say that the spousal support incumbent upon a husband is Rabbinic, derived from the fact that a master is obligated in the support of his slave’s wife from the Torah, **not** in the manner of an *asmachta*. Obviously, Rabbinic laws are not brought in the Five Books of the Torah, and an *asmachta* does not create a new law; it only serves as a mnemonic for a law that already exists. Incidentally, in this regard as well, there is no difference between *d’oraisa*, *d’rabbanan*, and *Halacha l’Moshe miSinai*—that they were supported by a verse (see *Pesachim* 96b; *Kiddushin* 57b according to Rashi; *Eruvin* 4b, and many other places cited in the *Encyclopedia Talmudit*, entry *Asmachta*).

3) Regarding what I wrote: that it is possible that *tefillah* is a voluntary act, yet when one does pray, several parameters are placed upon it, even from the Torah, due to the matter of the love and fear of Hashem, etc. You object to this, saying that the obligations of love and fear are at all times, not specifically during *tefillah*. Here, too, it is not understood what the difficulty is. At all times and every hour, one is not standing before the Almighty, whereas at the time of *tefillah*, one is. It is similar to the soul—so it is in a place: there is no obligation to ascend and appear (except for the three festivals, obviously), yet when one ascends and appears, there are parameters upon him due to the awe of the Sanctuary, etc. The same applies to *tefillah*. Just as with those great laws regarding the fact that an intoxicated person is forbidden to pray—even though we do not find that this is *d’oraisa*[*], it is understood that this applies when the matter is not done in a way that is the opposite of the honor of the Almighty. However, if one became intoxicated and the time for *tefillah* arrived and he wishes to pray, it is different than if the matter were in a manner of disgrace and blasphemy, which is the opposite of the fear of Hashem, which is *d’oraisa*.

There are countless voluntary matters which, in the **manner** of their performance, involve *d’oraisa* obligations and prohibitions. As I wrote in a letter to someone else on this subject, it is possible that even according to the one who says burial is not from the Torah, the prohibition against burying a wicked person next to a righteous one is from the Torah.

Regarding what I wrote concerning the time of *tefillah* and the formula of *tefillah*, etc.—this is not a matter of the love and fear of Hashem, etc., because the common denominator of all the formulas is that they arrange the praise of the Almighty, etc.

With respect and blessing.

## 4879

B"H, 28 Mar-Cheshvan, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, G-d-fearing, learned, and diligent in communal needs,
R' Avraham Tzvi, may he live, HaKohen

Shalom u'Bracha!

Following the silence—the cause of which I had already been concerned about—your letter was received, in which you write regarding the manner of conducting the shiurim in Chassidus. I have strong confidence that you will not be satisfied with the achievements reached thus far, but will strive to expand the shiur in both quantity and quality, naturally in ways of peace and pleasantness, yet in a manner befitting the magnitude of the matter, as explained in several sichos and letters of our Rebbes, the Nesi'im. And since the tzaddikim are like their Creator and demand this of us, just as the Ribono shel Olam does not come with unreasonable demands and asks only according to one’s strength, surely this is the case here as well—meaning that we have been given the kochos to fulfill this demand in its entirety. And since the days of the month of Kislev, the month of Geulah, are approaching, surely you will take counsel in advance on how to utilize them in the most effective way to disseminate the ma'ayanos in the place where you have merited to be the shaliach for this. May the Ribono shel Olam grant you success.

I await good news regarding all the above, and please convey my regards to all the participants, may they live, in this most exalted shiur.

#### ***4879***

**R' Avraham Tzvi, may he live:** HaKohen, Jerusalem. Additional letters to him—see above 4559, and the references in the notes there.

**The shiurim in Chassidus:** See above, ibid.

*) See Pirkei D'Rabbi Eliezer, ch. 20; Biurei HaGra; Shulchan Aruch Orach Chayim, beginning of siman 296.

## 4880

4880

B”H, Rosh Chodesh Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your *pan* from Erev Shabbos. May it be His will that by the time you receive this letter, you will see—with your physical eyes—an improvement in the health of your eyes. This is especially relevant given that you surely are meticulous regarding the wine for Kiddush and Havdalah*, which affects a person’s "light of the eyes." Since all the words of our Sages are precise, the expression "light" is surely precise as well. Therefore, it is appropriate to increase the "light" of Torah, which is called *Adam*—as it is written, "This is the Torah, [when a] man (*Adam*)..."—which is to say, through the study of the *pnimiyus haTorah*. Although you surely already have *shiurim* in this, what is needed is an increase, in the manner discussed by the Alter Rebbe in *Iggeres HaKodesh*, end of *Siman* 7.

I await good news regarding all of the above.

#### ***4880***

**In the wine for Kiddush and Havdalah*:** See also above, Vol. 7, Letter 1947; Vol. 11, 3889, and the references in the notes there; Vol. 12, 4041; Vol. 13, 4408; below, 5181.

## 4881

4881

B"H, Erev Rosh Chodesh Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter—which does not indicate the time it was written—

You should examine the Responsa of the Tzemach Tzedek, Yoreh Deah, Siman 93. See also his explanation of the verse "And He is merciful" (*V'hu Rachum*), printed in the supplements to *Tehillim Oholei Yosef Yitzchak*, pages 626-627. Additionally, consult the sources cited in *Darchei Teshuvah* to Yoreh Deah, Siman 181, subsections 17-19.

It would be appropriate for you to have your tefillin checked, and to establish fixed times for studying the teachings of Chassidus—at least a few lines every day—and may Hashem grant you success.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

**Secretary**

#### ***4881***

**You should examine this:** Regarding the matter of shaving the beard. See also below, letter 5054.

## 4882

B"H, Rosh Chodesh Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

Your letter was received in due time. It is my hope that since then, you have increased your vigor in your personal study of both the revealed Torah and the teachings of Chassidus, as well as in your avodah of tefillah. This applies likewise to your learning with those you influence, guiding them on the path that ascends to the House of Hashem through instruction imbued with the light and warmth of Chassidus.

From what is explained in the revealed Torah regarding the distinction between "moist enough to moisten" (*tofeach al menas lehatfiach*) and merely "moist," it is understood that one requires a special increase in one’s own personal matters so that the "moistening" [of others] will be as required. However, since the Ribono shel Olam does not deal tyrannically with His creatures, He certainly provides the strength for this, for "according to the camel is the burden."

I trust that you are keeping the three known daily shiurim in Chumash, Tehillim, and Tanya, and may the Almighty grant you success.

With blessings for good tidings.

#### ***4882***

**The distinction between "moist":** See also below, letter 4907.

## 4883

4883

B”H, Rosh Chodesh Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter—which was slightly delayed in transit—in which you write regarding your place of livelihood, that it is not a suitable location, and that this has also affected your livelihood, and you are in doubt as to whether to remove your children from the yeshiva so that they may assist you in earning a living.

Now, surely, one should not even entertain such a thought. On the contrary, through their study of our Torah—Torah Chaim—there will be an increase in the blessings of the Giver of the Torah, He who creates the world and sustains it, with whatever is needed for their parents; that is to say, in his livelihood and in his health as well. One should seek other solutions, but G-d forbid, one should not diminish the very thing through which blessing is added to one’s own affairs.

It is appropriate that every weekday, before the morning davening, you should set aside a few coins for tzedakah. Our Torah—Torah Chaim—has informed us of the promise of Hashem, may He be blessed and exalted, that through this, "I will pour out for you a blessing until there is no room for more."

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

## 4884

4884

B”H, Rosh Chodesh Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

I trust you have received my letter responding to your previous one. It is surprising that I have had no word from you since then. I am certain, however, that this is a sign that you are increasing and elevating your efforts in your personal affairs—by which I mean the study of the revealed Torah and the teachings of Chassidus, as well as the avodah of tefillah. This is what truly establishes a person in spirituality, and through this, in physicality as well. As it is written, “You shall be wholehearted with Hashem your G-d” —wholehearted in the 248 spiritual limbs and 365 spiritual sinews (see *Likutei Torah*, in the *maamar* beginning *Ki Hamitzvah Hazos*), and wholehearted in the 248 physical limbs and 365 physical sinews, for the body of a Yid is also holy.

The three *pidyonos nefesh* were received at the appropriate time.

With blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned matters, and also regarding your influence upon your friends, *shlita*, in all these matters.

## 4885

B"H, Rosh Chodesh Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and refined, engaged in communal needs, R' Nachman Dov, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter and its enclosure. May it be the will of Hashem that you succeed in your efforts to increase the number of those who support this cause in accordance with its true intent—that they may draw closer to all matters of the teachings of Chassidus, its guidance, and its practices [see also Tanya, Chapter 37; and regarding these funds, since one could have purchased the life of his vital soul with them, he is essentially giving the life of his soul]. However, it is obvious that these matters must be clarified and explained, and the main thing is that from the point of vitality, it should spread throughout all 248 spiritual limbs and 365 spiritual sinews, and through this, to the 248 physical limbs and 365 physical sinews. And this is understood [it is understood that one who exerts himself in this takes a leading share], and it is similar to what is written, "You shall be wholehearted with Hashem your G-d" (see Likutei Torah, discourse *Ki HaMitzvah HaZos*, Chapter 2).

With blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned, especially since we have entered the month of Kislev, the month of redemption and miracles.

#### 4885

Published in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 19, p. 477, and completed based on the secretariat's copy.

**R' Nachman Dov:** Vichnin, Montreal. Another letter to him — above, Vol. 4, 1164.

## 4886

B"H, Rosh Chodesh Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter—which was slightly delayed in transit—

At an auspicious time, I will mention you at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, for whatever you may need, and especially regarding the contents of your letter, in which you express your desire to be connected and bound to Chassidus, its guidance, and its customs. May it be His will that in this, too, the promise of our Sages will be fulfilled: "A person who sanctifies himself a little from below is sanctified greatly from above."

Regarding your observation on the words of the Alter Rebbe in Tanya, Chapter 30, that Torah study must be "much more than his desire and will according to his nature and habit," and your question as to how one who does not have much time at his disposal can fulfill this:

The Alter Rebbe did not simply write that one must "study a lot." He specified "much more than his desire and will according to his nature and habit." In this, businessmen and those who sit in the tent [of Torah] are equal, for both must overcome their nature, habit, and personal desire—each according to his own circumstances. When they do so, they will fill **all** the time that is available according to Torah with the study of Torah. This is as explained in the Laws of Torah Study regarding the nature of the obligation of businessmen to study Torah. There is nothing that can stand before the inner will of a Jewish person, who has the capacity to overcome his external desires—see Rambam, Laws of Divorce, end of Chapter 2.

Surely you are aware of the three well-known daily study portions: Chumash, Tehillim, and Tanya. May you keep them, at the very least, from this point forward. However, it is understood that in addition to this, one must have *kavius itim*—fixed times—not only in terms of time, but also in terms of one's soul, by studying the "light of the Torah," which is the teachings of Chassidus.

*Sefer HaZichronos* was received in due time. Thank you.

With blessings for good tidings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe,**

**Secretary**

4887

4887

B"H, 1 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and upright, engaged in communal needs and multi-faceted activities,
R' Moshe Yitzchak, may he live and be well,

Greetings and blessings!

I was pleased to receive your telegram in due time regarding the commencement of the study term at the holy Yeshiva Achei Tmimim Lubavitch under your administration. It was an added pleasure to hear your regards through the esteemed Chossid, multi-faceted activist, R' Chaim Mordechai Avraham, may he live and be well, and the content of your telephone conversation—that the goal you set for yourself of reaching the number of three hundred students has already been realized. Naturally, I was particularly happy with the conclusion of your remarks, where you expressed regret that you initially spoke of three hundred, when you should have spoken of five hundred. This gives me hope that you and your wife, may she live and be well, will recognize the immense possibilities granted to you for the development and expansion of Yeshiva Achei Tmimim in New Haven. The matter depends solely on will, for the capability is ready and waiting to be utilized as much as is necessary.

May it be the will of Hashem that you soon recognize that even five hundred is not the ultimate goal in the city of New Haven, and that one must ascend from level to level, ever higher. This is especially true since we have just entered the month of Kislev, the month of victory for the Alter Rebbe, the founder of Chabad and its concerns. Through the merit of his imprisonment and his mesiras nefesh, which concluded with victory above and victory below, it was decreed in the Heavenly Court that in every matter of Torah, yiras shamayim, and good character traits, the hand of those who are connected to him and follow in his footsteps shall be uppermost.

With blessings to you and your wife for an abundance of true nachas from all your offspring, may they live and be well, and for good tidings in all the above.

***

4887

R' Moshe Yitzchak, may he live and be well: Hecht, New Haven. Additional letters to him—above, Vol. 5, 1464, and in the references noted there.

## 4888

B"H, 1 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

...Regarding what you write about the attitude of [Name] not being as it should be: I am sorry that his approach to the entire matter does not align with my perspective. In my opinion, his work in [Name of institution] is not done to do a favor for [Name] or because it is an institution belonging to [Name], but rather because it is an institution of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. For all those who work in these and similar institutions, it is for their own benefit and the benefit of their families, may they live, both materially and spiritually. Even if their work consists of procuring the necessary means for the institution’s management, they receive far more through this work—by way of the spiritual arousal of the institution’s founder and director, my father-in-law, the Rebbe—than they give to the institution. From this, it is understood and simple that even if he is one hundred percent correct that the attitude and manner of speech of the board member is not as it should be, this does not diminish or detract, Heaven forbid, from the benefit he receives through his work in the institution. It follows that everything you write regarding [Name]’s speech toward him has no bearing at all on the question regarding his work...

Awaiting good news regarding all of the above.

## 4889

4889

B"H, 2 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, the G-d-fearing, learned, and distinguished scholar, Moreinu HaRav Yosef, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of the book *Divrei Yissachar Divrei Yosef*, and I thank you.

I trust that when the opportunity arises, you will also provide the other volumes of your works to the library under my auspices, as they are not currently found there. I thank you in advance for this as well.

As a token of appreciation, and in accordance with the well-known dictum of our Sages that "all idle chatter is bad, but the chatter of Torah is good," I have noted a few comments in the margins of the pages of the book, written in haste as time permitted. As is known, our Sages have commented on the verse, "And Vahev in Suphah" (Bamidbar 21:14).

With respect and blessing.

**Opening of *Divrei Yissachar* at the beginning: That at night, Leah herself was Rachel.** See *Taamei HaMitzvos* by R. Chaim Vital, Parshas Teitzei, regarding the warning against "the hire of a harlot": The signs that Rachel gave to Leah—namely, the portion of her soul that she gave to her—meant that she was Rachel herself. — Incidentally: a) The matter of the signs is explained differently—see *Chessed L'Avraham*, Maayan Sheini, Nahar 61. See also the commentary of the Rivav on the Torah, ad loc. b) What is written regarding *Shivivei Nogah* is from *Sefer HaPardes*, as noted in the annotations. See there, 13, at length.

**Opening at the end: Four stones.** — In *Yalkut Shimoni*, Iyov, Remez 899 (cited in *Yalkut Reuveni* ad loc.), it is stated that this corresponds to the four wives he took. This homiletical interpretation is consistent with the opinion that there were twelve stones—corresponding to the twelve tribes—for according to both opinions, this was a sign that his bed would be complete.

**At the beginning of *Divrei Yissachar*: "He acted improperly, etc." The words of the Maggid, etc., "a Rabbinic prohibition, etc."** It is possible to say that this is not specifically the opinion of the Maggid, for acting "improperly" is even less than a Rabbinic prohibition; it is merely conduct that is not becoming.

**At the end of *Divrei Yissachar*: According to the Rambam regarding priestly garments when not engaged in the [Temple] service.** His words require further study, for the Rambam explicitly writes that it is permitted to derive benefit from the priestly garments even before the service, except for the belt, which is forbidden because of *shatnez* (*Hilchos Klei HaMikdash* 8:11-12. And see *Tosafos Yeshanim*, Yoma 69a, s.v. *V'tipuk*). However, his words are relevant to the opinion of the *Tosafos HaRosh* in Yoma, ibid.

#### 4889

**Moreinu HaRav Yosef:** Hirsch, Brooklyn.

4890

B”H, 2 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter from Motzaei Shabbos, I was pleased to read that you are continuing your efforts to disseminate the "lamp of mitzvah and the light of Torah"—which refers to the matters of Chassidus—in your surroundings, where an addition of holiness is necessary, as it is written, "And your camp shall be holy," etc. Since this is demanded of you, it is certain that you have been granted the strengths and all the necessary means to fulfill this role. Therefore, I am surprised by your writing that you have fallen into a state of slight despair. It is understood that this is also distressing. You must view the situation as it truly is: that you are an emissary of the Ribono shel Olam to awaken your surroundings, to help them recognize that "the light is good," as it is written, "Taste and see that Hashem is good." Through this, they will be protected and also fortunate—both materially and spiritually. If one who sustains a single soul is considered as if he sustained an entire world, how much more so is it within your power to bring many souls of Bnei Yisrael closer to our Father in Heaven, even through actions in daily life.

Especially now that we have entered the month of Kislev, the month of redemption and miracles—the redemption of the founder of the Chabad Chassidic path, our Rebbe HaZaken, who says that "Love your fellow as yourself" is the vessel for "Love Hashem your Elokim"—he is also the one who says that Chassidus is not a matter for a specific sect or a part of the Jewish people, but rather it belongs to everyone, and everyone needs and requires its guidance. This is especially true for the young generation, who are beginning their path in life and facing unique trials, and whose health—both the health of the body and the health of the neshamah—is of concern to many.

May it be the will of Hashem that you, and all those in your near and distant surroundings, recognize and know that you are soldiers in the armies of Hashem, the Giver of the Torah and the Commander of the mitzvos. May you accept upon yourselves the yoke of the Kingdom of Heaven and the yoke of Torah and mitzvos, and may the Torah and the mitzvah protect you wherever you are and bring you to victory in spirituality and to victory in the literal sense.

With blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned,

**M. Schneerson**

P.S. Please be kind enough to convey my greetings to all those in your surroundings and extend to them my heartfelt wishes that their camp may be holy with the holiness of Torah and mitzvos, and that "Hashem your Elokim walks in the midst of your camp to save you and to deliver your enemies before you." May we soon merit, speedily and in our days, the fulfillment of the prophecy that the armies of Hashem—each and every one of Yisrael—will go forth from the galus through Moshiach Tzidkeinu.

## 4891

B"H, 3 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished individual, who engages in communal needs,
R' Schneur Zalman, may he live and be well,

Peace and blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from the beginning of the month of Kislev, the month of redemption and miracles, along with the lists of the students of Beis Rivkah, may Hashem grant them life.

At an auspicious time, I will mention them at the holy resting place of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, leader of Israel, for outstanding success in their studies and conduct, to the great satisfaction of their parents, teachers, and the administration of Beis Rivkah, may they live and be well. Surely, the Rebbe will arouse Divine mercy regarding all the above, as well as for the strengthening and development of Beis Rivkah—both in quantity, i.e., the number of students, and in quality, the level of studies and the standard of conduct. Happy is the portion of all those who strive in this, and of the students who study in this school.

It is self-evident that these matters are also included in the verdict of the Heavenly Court, regarding which the Mitteler Rebbe related that because of this mesiras nefesh (of the Alter Rebbe) for the teachings of Chassidus, they decreed in the Heavenly Court that in every matter of Torah, yiras shamayim, and good character traits, the hand of his followers and those who walk in his footsteps shall be uppermost.

As a sign of my participation in the affairs of Beis Rivkah, I enclose a check for 25, and may it be Hashem’s will that it be "ko lechai" (strength for life), and, of course, the matter is understood according to what is explained in Likutei Torah, in the maamar beginning "Sos Asis," Chapter Two.

With blessings for success.

P.S.
My letters in response to your previous letter surely arrived in due time.

#### 4891

R' Schneur Zalman: Butman, Montreal. Additional letters to him are found above, Vol. 5, 1445, and in the references noted in the footnotes there.

## 4492

B"H, 3 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and learned, engaged in communal needs, R' Nissan, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 26 Cheshvan.

Thank you for the general *pidyon nefesh*, and I confirm that I found everything in order, both in general and in detail. May it be the will of Hashem that the matters discussed during the *farbrengen* regarding the things you write about will achieve their effect upon both the listeners and the speakers.

Regarding the story of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, that he saw a *pidyon nefesh* from his father, my father-in-law, the Rebbe—the Rebbe Rashab—as a preparation for saying a *maamar*, and his holy words were, "May it act upon me and upon the listeners." I have already said this many times at *farbrengens*: although, on the face of it, since this is the *pidyon nefesh* of a *Nasi* of Yisroel, it is a matter of study whether this conduct applies to others as well; however, since these matters have become known in public, it certainly concerns those who heard it as well. And in the present case, it is all the more so and *kal v'chomer*: if the aforementioned wrote such a *pidyon nefesh*, then certainly it applies to people of our stature.

I am sure you are preparing a suitable plan to utilize the upcoming days of 10-19 Kislev and Chanukah in an appropriate manner. May it be the will of Hashem that you bring good news regarding this.

With blessings for success.

***

**R' Nissan:** Nemnov, *mashpia* of the Tomchei Temimim Yeshiva in France. Further letters to him: above, Vol. 13, 4484, and in the references noted in the footnotes there. Below, 5144, 5222, 5266.

**The story:** See also above, Vol. 11, letter 3334.

4893

Brooklyn.

After a hiatus, I was pleased to receive your letter of... regarding the first meetings that have taken place.

And when you work on this with the proper energy and *kiruv halevavos* together, the Creator of the world and its Guide will assist so that it will be successful.

Especially since we have now entered the month of Kislev, the month of *geulah* and miracles.

## 4894

B"H, 3 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and learned, etc., R' Meir, may he live.

Shalom u’Bracha!

Following your long silence, your letter was received—though it lacked the date of writing. It gave me pleasure to read of your establishing a regular shiur for the study of Chassidus, and that some of the yeshiva students have joined. Although it is obvious that their number is not what it should be, it is still my hope that you and the anash in your vicinity will exert yourselves in this matter as well. After all, a young man and a yeshiva student is not yet involved in the affairs of this *alma d’shikra* (world of falsehood), and therefore it is all the more important to instill within him a spirit of *yiras shamayim* and love for Hashem, may He be blessed. The way to achieve this is through the study of the teachings of Chassidus and by following its guidance and customs, and *kal v’chomer* (it goes without saying).

At a time of favor, you should mention all those you wrote about at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

With blessings.

P.S.
I am sure you are preparing a suitable plan to utilize the bright days of 10 and 19 Kislev, and the upcoming Chanukah, in the appropriate manner.

## 4495

4495

B”H, 3 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 21 Cheshvan, in which you write that you have been in Eretz Yisrael (may it be rebuilt and established) for some time and have also become settled in your livelihood; but that for the time being, you have not succeeded in finding your appropriate *zivug*, even though you have been in Eretz Yisrael (may it be rebuilt and established) for four years already, and despite your tremendous efforts.

In the vast majority of cases, the reason for not finding an appropriate *shidduch*, even when one is truly making an effort, is because people are overly particular about the trivialities of the trivialities regarding the person being proposed, forgetting in the process that there is no perfection in creation—and the person proposing and the person being proposed are included in this. It is highly probable that this is the reason in your case as well. Therefore, if you focus only on the essentials—or at most, the secondary aspects of the essentials—and not on matters that are entirely irrelevant, the Creator of the world and its Guide will provide you with your appropriate *zivug*.

Since you are in the land upon which the eyes of Hashem are from the beginning of the year until the end of the year, and we see tangibly that, on the contrary, people go there specifically to find those *zivugim* that they could not find elsewhere, in my opinion, there is no basis for the idea that you should travel to... to search for *zivugim*.

You are surely aware of the three daily study portions established for every soul by my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory: Chumash, Tehillim, and Tanya. You should observe them, at the very least, from this point forward.

It is my hope that since you begin your letter by mentioning that you had a connection with my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, you will continue this *hiskashrus* through fixed *shiurim* in the study of Chassidus and by participating in the *farbrengens* of *anash*. May Hashem grant you success in all of the above.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

**Secretary**

## 4896

B"H, 3 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and meticulous in his communal work,
R' Yaakov Menachem Mendel, may he live, Hakohen

Shalom u’Bracha!

In response to your letter from Friday, it was a pleasure for me to read about the renovation of the mikveh in a manner of *hiddur* (extra care). As is well known, the words of the Tashbetz state that regarding the matter of a mikveh, one must be meticulous and fulfill the requirements according to all opinions. Fortunate is the portion of all those who exert themselves in the *taharah* of Yisrael, for through this they hasten the Geulah, as explained at the end of Maseches Sotah: "Taharah leads to..." and as it is written, "And I will sprinkle upon you pure waters, etc."

You should certainly utilize the days of miracles and redemption of the month of Kislev, including the ninth of Kislev, to the fullest extent possible for the *hafatzas hamaayanos chutzah*. May Hashem grant you success, and may you merit to bring good tidings in this regard as well.

With blessings,

#### ***4896***

**R' Yaakov Menachem Mendel, may he live:** Sperlin, Woodridge. Another letter to him — see Vol. 13, 4442.

## 4897

4897

B"H, 4 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and industrious, engaged in communal needs, R' Michael [Lipsker]
Shlita

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 23 Cheshvan: Thank you for the good news that peace is with you and all that is yours. May the Creator of the world grant this for the days to come, and with an increase, in accordance with the command of our holy Torah to "ascend in matters of holiness." It is known what our Sages of blessed memory said regarding the verse, "He declares His words to Yaakov, His statutes and His judgments to Yisrael" — that everything He commands Yisrael, He also performs Himself. Surely you are also preparing to celebrate the upcoming festivals and days of redemption in peace, and to utilize them for the strengthening and development of the holy *Oholei Yosef Yitzchak Lubavitch* institutions, in accordance with the demands and the necessity of the hour. May it be His will that this, too, be met with extraordinary success. As is known from the story of the Mitteler Rebbe, because of this *mesiras nefesh*—that of the Alter Rebbe—for the teachings of Chassidus, it was decreed in the Heavenly Beis Din that in every matter of Torah, *yiras shamayim*, and good character traits, the hand of his *mekusharim* and those who follow in his footsteps shall be uppermost.

With blessings.

P.S.
- Surely, as per the good custom of previous years, you will distribute Chanukah *gelt* on my behalf to the appropriate students, as in the previous year. It would be appropriate for them to coordinate among themselves so that the amount is the same in all *Oholei Yosef Yitzchak* institutions in your country.

May it be His will that this matter, too, should have its full effect regarding *Torah u'mitzvos* and the light of Torah.

## 4898

4898

B"H, 4 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

It caused me distress to learn that you are not feeling well, and I hope that by the time you receive this letter, there is already an improvement, and may Hashem Yisbarach grant that things continue to improve until you are fully restored to health. Together with your husband, the Rav, may you raise all your children to Torah, to the chuppah, and to maasim tovim, in health and in joy.

Especially now that we have entered the month of Kislev, which is a month of miracles and geulah in general, and specifically in relation to those who are privileged to take part in the institutions of Chassidus Chabad. Our Rebbes, the holy Nesi'im of Chabad—beginning with the Alter Rebbe, Baal HaTanya—arouse great mercy for all those who are in need of it in their private matters, as well as in their work regarding general matters. For when private matters are in order and successful, it adds energy and strengthens one's work in general matters. As is known from the story of the Mitteler Rebbe, because of the mesiras nefesh of the Alter Rebbe for Toras HaChassidus, it was decreed in the Beis Din Shel Maalah that in every matter of Torah, yiras shamayim, and middos tovos, the hand of those who are connected to him and who walk in his footsteps should be on top.

In anticipation of good news from you regarding the above.

## 4899

B"H, 4 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the esteemed Rabbi, G-d-fearing, Torah scholar, descendant of holy lineage, etc.
Rabbi...

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your notification regarding the organizing of a Melaveh Malkah, the *Seudata D’David Malka Meshicha* (the meal of King David, the Mashiach), this coming Motzaei Shabbos.

The days of the month of Kislev—the month of miracles, the days of Chanukah, the completion of the work of the Mishkan, and the founding of the Second Beis Hamikdash—are inherently suited and capable of drawing down and influencing the matters that occurred in those days into our current time.

As is well known, the Arizal explains the verse, "These days are remembered and performed" (Esther 9:28), meaning that each year, when the cycle of these days arrives and they are "remembered," the same matters are also "performed" and drawn down just as they were the first time.

May it be the will of Hashem that through the gathering of many of the Jewish people—for where ten are gathered, the Shechinah rests—and through the *Seudata D’David Melech Yisrael Chai V’kayam*, there will be an awakening to increase in the matters of our Torah, the Torah of life, and its mitzvos, and may this awakening be drawn down upon the days that lie ahead for us, for good.

With respect and blessings.

#### ***4899***

A similar letter appears below on page 170.

## 4900

B"H, 4 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and involved in communal needs, R' Dovid Moshe [Lieberman]
May he live and be well.

Shalom u'Bracha,

In response to your letter of 21 Marcheshvan, in which you write regarding the question of the West Side Parochial School—that the neighborhood where it is currently located is no longer suitable because the number of Jewish residents is steadily decreasing—and that there are two options: to build in a new neighborhood, or to merge with the Parochial School in Albany Park.

It is surprising that you do not mention a third option: to find a suitable building, or at least one that requires some repairs and modifications. It is understood that this should be considered first, as it does not require as much as constructing a building from scratch.

It is also understood that the rationale for merging with the Albany Park Parochial School applies only if neither of the two aforementioned options is possible. Aside from the reasons mentioned in your letter for rejecting the idea of a merger, there is another primary reason: our current era demands an increase in new institutions across the greatest possible number of neighborhoods. It is obvious that one must avoid, if at all possible, reducing the number of existing ones.

Regarding what you write about the lack of resources, activists, etc.—to a significant degree, the solution to any question depends on the approach one takes to resolve it. I mean that if the phrasing of the question is whether to maintain the Parochial School in this neighborhood or another, or not to maintain it through a merger and the like, then there are arguments for and against. Consequently, one remains indecisive, fearing difficulties—even imagined ones, and certainly those that exist in reality.

This is not the case if the approach is aligned with the **necessity** of the hour, which is as mentioned above: that the number of children and youth in Chicago necessitates the development of education on *taharas hakodesh*, not only in quality but also in quantity. It is impossible otherwise, and this is not a matter of doubt. In such a case, one contemplates with intense focus how to overcome the obstacles standing in the way, and eventually, one finds what is needed for this. When one begins the work in practice, one sees that the difficulties are fewer than they appeared beforehand, and *kal v'chomer*...

With blessings,

#### ***4900***

**R' Dovid Moshe [Lieberman]**: Chicago. Another letter to him—see above, Vol. 3, 5710.

## 4901

4901

B"H, 4 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 25 Cheshvan and the telegram that preceded it: May it be His will that you be able to bring good news very soon regarding **actual** activities. For as of now, the details received from the administration... speak only of preparations and clarifying the various differences of opinion and perspectives on the matter. It is already a known *mishnah* that "the deed is the essential thing," and it is also known that one who errs in a *mishnah* must retract, as this does not even fall under the category of a mistake, given the simplicity of the matter.

It is understood that under normal circumstances I would not write this, but since for months on end there has been talk, planning, and the drafting of programs, etc., while the actual work remains on a scale that is entirely disproportionate to the demands of the hour—and not even proportionate to the talk about the work—it is understood that the concern regarding future conduct is greater than in matters where there is no established precedent of past conduct, and the wise will understand.

May it be His will that since we have entered the month of Kislev—the month of miracles and the victory of Chabad affairs and all that is connected to it—there will also be a change and a complete turnaround in the aforementioned. From here on, may you increase your holy work as required of *Tzeirei Agudas Chabad*. We have been promised that when one does so, there will be success. It is a pity, however, about every day, every hour, and every moment that passes by. Aside from the fact that time is a loss that cannot be recovered, in this specific case, it reinforces the established pattern of the lack of activity until now. This is especially true in light of the alarming statement of our Sages, "He who should have acted but did not act," and the wondrous story regarding this, where Rabbi Yochanan ben Zakkai said, "Fortunate are you, O Israel" (see *Kesuvos* 66b, end of side b).

It is understood that my words through the esteemed and G-d-fearing individual, who is involved in communal needs, Shmuel Belininsky, regarding the necessity of activity and a "war of momentum"—even though the term used is "war," it is obvious that it must be conducted in ways of pleasantness and peace. This is as explained in the *sicha*: deep down, every Jew wants to fulfill the *mitzvos* of the *Ribono shel Olam* in their entirety, as per the ruling of the Rambam in *Hilchos Geirushin*, end of chapter 2. The concept of "Know the G-d of your father and serve Him" is a lofty and exalted *mitzvah*, and in fact, it surpasses them all (*Kuntres Acharon* 156b). Furthermore, even if one’s heart desires this in truth only in the slightest measure—as is understood from what is explained in *Tanya*, chapter 41 (58a)—all those who will endeavor to bring them closer, to introduce them to this knowledge of "the G-d of your father," etc., are also desirous and willing for this.

As you requested, I will mention all those you wrote about at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. Since we have entered the month of Kislev—the month of miracles and the victory of our *Alter Rebbe*, and with him, all those who are associated and connected to this—may all the aforementioned, and in general all matters of Chabad and its offshoots, bring good tidings in all their affairs.

I am certain that you have planned an appropriate program to utilize these days of the month of Kislev, which are before us, as is required.

With blessings for good tidings.

## 4812

4812

B”H, 4 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and refined, whose work is the work of Heaven,
R’ Tzvi Abba, may he live.

Shalom u’Bracha!

Following a long interval, I received your letter of 1 Kislev, the month of redemption. In it, you write regarding your arrangements for this period of study. It gave me pleasure to read of your involvement and assistance to the Agudas N'shei U’Bnos Chabad in your camp, and you will surely continue in this. The importance of this matter has already been explained in many places, as well as in sichos at various times, and you are surely aware of this. It is understood that all the above also applies to your public teaching at the Shaarei Tzedek shul, especially since you will surely utilize this for teaching Chassidus to the public in a manner appropriate to the level of understanding of the listeners—whether through accessible maamarim, sayings from the sichos of our Rebbes, the Nesi’im, or the like.

May it be the will of Hashem that you and your wife, may they live, each in their respective areas, succeed in the above with health and peace of mind. This is especially pertinent now that we have entered the month of Kislev. The story of the Mitteler Rebbe is well known: because of that mesiras nefesh (of the Alter Rebbe) for the teachings of Chassidus, it was decreed in the Heavenly Beis Din that in every matter of Torah, yiras shamayim, and good character traits, the hand of those who are connected to him and follow in his footsteps shall be uppermost.

You have surely already prepared a plan for how to utilize the days of this month of Kislev. It is understood that by this I mean you, your wife, and the rest of anash, each and every one of them in your camp, and may Hashem grant you success.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above.

P.S.

Regarding what you were asked concerning the verse, “Angels of Elokim ascending and descending upon it,” specifically why it mentions ascending before descending: your question is surprising, as Rashi explicitly addresses this difficulty, citing the Midrash on the spot; see also the commentary of the Rashbam there. Perhaps your intention is primarily regarding the second point, based on the Midrashic interpretation that “ascending” refers to the angels who were dismissed from His presence, etc., and you were asked how it is possible for them to act against the will of the Creator. Your question here is that angels do not possess bechirah (see the beginning of Chapter 39 in Tanya). Regarding the first point—how it is possible for them to act against the will—it is obvious that they did not intend to act against the will of the Creator; rather, they did not know that it was against the Creator’s will. No created being, not even an angel or those above them, can be absolutely perfect, as per Rashi’s well-known commentary on the verse, “which Elokim created to do”—to perfect. Therefore, it is possible for them to err. An example of this is Adam HaRishon; even though he was the handiwork of the Holy One, Blessed be He, and this was before the sin of the Tree of Knowledge, he nevertheless stumbled due to a perceived permission or the like. See also what is written on this in the introduction to the Shelah, Chelek Beis Yisrael (in most editions, page 18, end of side a).

These days I received a letter from your brother, Shalom Leib, may he live.

#### ***4812***

**R’ Tzvi Abba, may he live:** Lerman, Pittsburgh.

## 4903

B"H, 5 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

I have received the news regarding your state of health and that you are in a hospital. It is my hope that by the time you receive this letter, your condition will have already improved, and you yourself will be able to convey this good news soon. This is especially true as we are now in the month of Kislev, the month of miracles and redemption, the victory of the Torah of Chabad and its Nesi'im, and of those who follow in their footsteps and fulfill their directives.

One of the primary directives is "you shall surely help him" (*azov ta'azov imo*)—with the body. Since the *avodah* must be healthy and complete, it is understood that the body, too, must be healthy and complete. This is especially so because *Yisrael* are "one nation on earth," which means that we extend this unity even into mundane matters. We do not merely elevate the form over the matter; rather, we transform the matter itself into form, so that there may be true unity.

With blessings to hear good news, in a way that is good, visible, and revealed, regarding all that was mentioned above.

P.S.
My letter responding to your previous correspondence surely arrived in due time.

## 4404

B"H, 5 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 23 Mar-Cheshvan and its enclosure, in which you write regarding your ward: It seems that one of the matters that needs to be corrected in general, and specifically as it is a cause for the changes (which, as is evident even from the ward’s own letter, exist, though they are lesser than what others have written), is the fact that he has quite a bit of free time at his disposal. Consequently, he can occupy it as he pleases or leave it idle, and we know that "idleness leads to..." Furthermore, it seems he does not have any specific plan regarding his future.

In connection with the above, and also because it is a primary matter in and of itself, you should take an interest in a *shidduch* for your ward. It is understood that this involvement must be pursued with the necessary energy. As is apparent in the vast majority of cases, it is likely better that he not know that you are dealing with a *shidduch* for him until you have a concrete proposal in hand; *v'd"l*.

It is my hope that even after writing this letter, you will continue to take an interest in the appropriate manner regarding the conduct of your ward. May it be Hashem's will that you bring good tidings in a revealed and manifest good.

With blessings for good news.

## 4505

4505

B"H, 5 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished individual, who engages in the needs of the community, whose work is work for the sake of Heaven,
R' Y.A., may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

I received your letter from Tangier some time ago, in which you write regarding the contract that was signed, and that in a few days you will be moving to Gibraltar. May it be the will of Hashem that it be in a good and auspicious hour, and may the will of Hashem succeed in his hand to disseminate matters of authentic Yiddishkeit there and to raise the horn of the Israel of old. There is nothing that stands before the will, for according to the camel is the load.

Based on what is explained in many places—that in this era of ours, "we do not know how long" [it will last] regarding the secret of the *birurim*—the *avodah* must be in the manner hinted at in the statement of our Sages, that this world is like a wedding feast: "**Seize** and eat, **seize** and drink." Along with the advocacy for the *mitzvos* in their literal sense, there is also a necessity for *avodah* connected to *de'os* (intellectual concepts) and *chovas halevavos* (duties of the heart), as well as the internal conduct and guidance of the Torah, which in our generation has been revealed through the teachings of Chassidus.

Regarding your question—concerning Shabbos and the like, when the Ten Utterances through which the world was created return to their source,

As explained in *Likutei Torah* (judging by his style, he refers to what is written in *Parshas Ha'azinu* 72c), seemingly, according to this, the entire creation should return to absolute nothingness, as is explained in *Shaar HaYichud VeHaEmunah*, chapters 1 and 3, and in many other places—see this question and its resolution in *Likutei Torah*, *d'h* "Shuvah Yisrael," up to chapter 2 (66c), and further explanation on this in what is written in *Likutei Torah*, *Tazria* 21c.

With blessings for good tidings regarding your success in all of the above.

#### ***4505***

**R' Y.A.:** Pacifici, Gibraltar.

## 4496

4496

B"H, 5 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

...I do not know when the bracketed portion of your letter was written, nor if it was during a passing mood or a mood caused by a specific event. In any case, I would like to hope that this mood is in the past. Even though, on the face of it, one might connect the central idea with the verse, "Blessed is the man who trusts in Hashem, etc.," and its opposite, "the man who trusts in man, etc.," yet the second verse explains itself and concludes, "and whose heart turns away from Hashem."

This means that if one’s heart is directed toward Hashem—the **Creator** of man, the world, and its Guide—then even in the person around him, he will see the good and the inner essence. As "water reflects a face to a face," so too is the heart of man toward man; the good that is within the person is brought into actuality and revealed, participating in his affairs, in his sorrow, and in his joy.

## 4907

B"H, 5 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the avreich, the G-d-fearing man whose work is work for the sake of Heaven,
Shmuel Elazar, may he live,

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter, in which you write and inform me that you have been accepted as the spiritual administrator of the Mechina Toras Emes: May it be the will of Hashem that you meet with outstanding success, and may the desire of Hashem succeed in your hand to educate the aforementioned students in such a way that they grow to be a name, a praise, and a glory (see Likutei Torah, Devarim 56b), and may the Mechina grow both in quantity and in quality.

Based on what is explained even in the revealed dimension of Torah regarding the distinction between "moist" (*tofeach*) and "moist enough to moisten others" (*tofeach al menas lehatfiach*), it is understood that you require a special increase in your own personal *avodah* so that the "moistening" will be as required. And since the Holy One, blessed be He, does not deal tyrannically with His creatures, it is certain that the strength for this is granted, and "according to the camel is the load."

With blessings for outstanding success in the above and for good tidings.

P.S.

As you requested, I will mention all those you wrote about at the holy resting place of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

It was pleasant for me to read in your letter about your activities within the framework of Tzeirei Agudas Chabad. Surely in this, too, you will continue and increase, in accordance with the directive of our Sages to "increase in holiness." You should inspire and influence the other workers in this field, and those who need to work in this *avodah*, that they too should increase their efforts in accordance with the **necessity** of the hour. It is known what the verdict of the Heavenly Court is: that because of the *mesiras nefesh* (of the Alter Rebbe) for the teachings of Chassidus, they decreed in the Heavenly Court that in every matter of Torah, *yiras shamayim*, and good character traits, the hand of his *mekusharim* and those who follow in his footsteps shall be uppermost.

***

**4907**

**Shmuel Elazar, may he live:** Halperin, Jerusalem. An additional letter to him appears below, 5084.

**The distinction between "moist":** See also above, letter 4882.

## 4908

4908

B"H, 7 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Blessings and peace!

In response to your letter of 4 Kislev, in which you write regarding your state of health and the doctor’s opinion.

It is understandable that since the doctor says that if you maintain the diet, you can avoid surgery for a year or two or more, there is no place to think about surgery. Especially since, from time to time, new methods emerge in the treatment of such a condition, and therefore it is very, very possible that you will not need an operation at all. This depends on your self-control, not becoming agitated, and more or less adhering to the diet and the general instructions of the specialist doctor.

And when you reflect, even for a short while, on the places where you were a few years ago—and that hundreds of thousands of Yidden are still there today, with their situation there compared to yours here—entire days would not be enough to thank Hashem Yisbarach for His kindness. At that point, one sees how small a place the current difficulties occupy when compared to the aforementioned kindnesses of Hashem Yisbarach. I am certain that it is entirely unnecessary to elaborate further on this.

With the blessing that, since it is now the month of Kislev—which is a month of miracles and goodness—you should report good news regarding all of the above, and also regarding your husband and children, may they live.

With blessings for a complete recovery and for good news,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

**Secretary**

## 4409

4409

B"H, 7 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your inquiry regarding the approach to dividing one’s time between Talmud Torah and involvement in sustaining and disseminating Yiddishkeit:

It is understood that this depends on the details and the fine details, and it is impossible to establish fixed rules for this except in relation to a specific event, a specific place, and a specific person. The general point regarding this, according to Halacha, is understood from what our Sages of blessed memory said in Maseches Moed Katan 9, end of side a and side b; Sotah 21b (see also there in Tosafos in the name of the Yerushalmi); Yerushalmi Berachos, Chapter 5, Halacha 1; Yerushalmi Pesachim, Chapter 3, Halacha 7; Tur Shulchan Aruch Orach Chayim, Section 156 and the commentaries there, and elsewhere.

Certainly, you are keeping the three known daily shiurim in Chumash, Tehillim, and Tanya, and adding to them, especially as we approach the bright day of 19 Kislev, when the light and vitality of our soul was given to us, as the well-known expression goes. May Hashem grant you success.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

## 4510

B"H, 7 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 2 Kislev, in which you write about the point of how a year has passed since you took upon yourself to connect to Chassidus and its matters: You will surely add even more strength as you enter your second year in this (see also Likutei Torah by the Alter Rebbe, Parshas Bamidbar 3a). This is especially true given the well-known statement of our Sages that "all beginnings are difficult," and in the second year, one has already moved beyond the category of a beginning. Therefore, one must demand much more of oneself. This demand will be made easier for you if you contemplate what the Alter Rebbe writes in Tanya, Chapter 30 (p. 38b): "Therefore, **every person** must..." — see there and study it well, applying it to your own soul.

Regarding what you write about worries and preoccupations, etc., the ruling of the Mishnah is well-known: "Whoever accepts upon himself the yoke of Torah is relieved of the yoke of government..." (and surely, anyone who errs in a matter of the Mishnah must retract, as this is an absolutely simple matter). This aligns with the well-known ruling, as told by the Mitteler Rebbe: because of that mesiras nefesh (of the Alter Rebbe) for the teachings of Chassidus, it was decreed in the Heavenly Court that in every matter of Torah, yiras shamayim, and good character traits, the hand of those who are connected to him and follow in his footsteps will be on top. May Hashem grant you success in all that has been said; the matter depends only on your will, for the strength and the ability are in your hands.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above, and regards to all your friends, may they live.

## 4511

4511

By the Grace of Hashem, 7 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the esteemed and distinguished individual, who engages in communal affairs,
R. Shmuel Betzalel, may he live.

Peace and Blessing!

After a long interruption, a letter from you dated 29 Cheshvan has finally arrived. Consistent with your custom until now, you again do not write in this letter how the days of the month of Tishrei passed, particularly regarding the strengthening and expansion of the Chabad institutions in Melbourne—may Hashem grant them success.

Regarding what you write concerning Beis Rivkah and the concern that it might become a burden upon the Yeshiva—it is evident from this that you, and unfortunately a large portion of anash, may they live, have not yet deeply considered the words of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. These words were spoken on many occasions and have been partially transcribed and printed regarding the vital importance of the education of girls—Jewish education in general, and Chassidic education in particular. Just as Chassidim and anash have demonstrated mesiras nefesh for the education of boys—ensuring not only that there be Jewish education in general, but specifically a Chassidic education—the difference between the two is something that you and all those who come from our country do not need to have explained. You have all seen for yourselves how much this touches upon the very foundations of our faith. The same applies to the education of girls, perhaps even more so, taking into account that when a girl grows up, she will be the *akeres habayis* (the mainstay of the home) and, in time, the mother of children whose education will depend in the greatest part upon her.

When looking at this entire question from that perspective, the issue is framed differently. There remains no room for doubt as to whether there should be a Beis Rivkah or not, chas v'shalom—just as there is no doubt as to whether there should be a Chassidic institution for boys or not, chas v'shalom. The only doubt is how to arrange the income so that it becomes self-sustaining (*chai nosei es atzmo*). In this regard, the situation is actually better than that of the Oholei Yosef Yitzchok schools, for when they were founded, there were already many Jewish schools for boys. Regarding Beis Rivkah, according to the information I have heard here from the Feiglin family, the competition is very weak. The temporary deficit you write about is likely based not on any weakness in the idea of Beis Rivkah, but on the difference in the attitude of anash toward this question compared to the question of Oholei Yosef Yitzchok. Consequently, there is a lack of deep contemplation and investigation into this matter, and a lack of effort to strengthen and expand it, etc.

It goes without saying that there is **no basis whatsoever** for any *kpidah* (resentment), chas v'shalom, which you mention at the end of your letter. On the contrary, the more specifically one writes about the situation there, and from the perspectives of more people with varying viewpoints, the clearer the picture of the matter becomes here, leading to a conclusion. However, in the essence of the matter, the existence of a Beis Rivkah is a necessity, and it is only a matter of arranging the affairs in the best possible way.

Incidentally, I would like to draw your attention to another point: it is specifically through Beis Rivkah that anash have a unique opportunity to draw in women from **various** circles within the environment of anash—even those who are currently very, very far from it—which cannot be achieved through Oholei Yosef Yitzchok. This, however, depends on finding the appropriate methods to attract them. I would also like to note that according to the experience here, taking into account the lower expenses for girls' education, Beis Rivkah can become self-sustaining even sooner than Oholei Yosef Yitzchok.

Surely you are making the necessary preparations for how to utilize the days of the month of Kislev, particularly Yud-Tes Kislev and the upcoming Chanukah, regarding Chabad affairs and Chabad institutions. May it be His will that it be with overwhelming success.

With blessings for good tidings regarding general matters and your own personal affairs.

## 4812

B.H., 8 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and involved in communal needs,
R’ Yehoshua Schneur Zalman, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter from the beginning of the month of Kislev—the month of miracles and redemption—I am certain that in the meantime you have received my response to your previous letter, which also contains answers to some of the questions raised in this current letter.

Regarding what you wrote concerning the matter of the Hakafos: surely the response to such a matter has already been received by the esteemed R’ Yaakov Eliezer Herzog, may he live, and you have seen it. It is understood that the very notion that in the future there would, G-d forbid, be no Kiddush or that the Hakafos would be shortened, etc., etc., is absolutely and utterly unfounded.

Regarding what you wrote concerning the difficulties, concealments, and hidden states: you have surely heard of the allusion in the verse (Bamidbar 13:33), "And we were in our own eyes as grasshoppers, and so we were in their eyes." If those who fear Hashem in general, and Chassidim in particular, recognized the powers that have been granted to them, they would approach every matter with a courage of heart incomparable to what they possess now. In a natural way, many concealments, hidden states, and even difficulties would be nullified. This is similar to the concept stated in Tanya, end of Chapter 29: "And therefore, as soon as Hashem was angry with them, etc.—from this every person can learn, etc." And the matter does not depart from its literal meaning, that "every person" refers to each and every one of us, in actual practice.

Regarding what you wrote concerning the *minyan* for the youth, the summer camp, and similar matters, about which there are those who are skeptical regarding their benefit for various reasons:

It is known how to evaluate such things. There is a doubt as to who is the one making the argument; one must observe the results—do they lead to an increase in activity in matters of holiness, or do they hinder activity? As is brought in *Hayom Yom* (23 Sivan), any matter that is beneficial or leads to actual *avodah*—any obstacle that may arise to such a thing, even if the obstacle comes from a "more elevated" source, is merely one of the machinations of the *nefesh habahamit*. There is no doubt that the summer camp and the *minyan* for the youth are among the unique ways to "conquer" the youth and the young, and through them, to also conquer the hearts of their parents and families. Happy is the portion of all those who involve themselves in such things. Furthermore, the arguments you write about—I do not even understand their content to begin with.
