I have already written to the esteemed R’ Shneur Zalman Althuis, may he live, that the tragedy among *anash* who have been geographically separated for several years from the Rebbe, my father-in-law, is that his oral teachings did not reach them. Even his written words regarding the value of *avodah* (the "conquering" of the youth and the young, and the great value of the education of girls and outreach among women) did not reach them. Moreover, even that which did reach them—because they were not accustomed to such things and there was a period of separation in the interim—did not reach them as the *Likutei Torah* (beginning of Parshas Shemini) explains: "it was not touched." Therefore, they relate to these matters without the proper attention. When this [level of commitment] falls, that [the opposition] rises, and there result concealments, hidden states, and even opposition from the opposing side.

It is understood that you may show this letter of mine to all those to whom it is relevant. May it be the will of Hashem that the days of favor of the month of Kislev—and especially the days of the 10th and 19th of Kislev that are approaching—will awaken in them the hidden powers, even the essential, primordial powers, and bring them into revelation until they exert their influence in actual practice. After all, even the essential powers are connected to these days, as in the holy letter of the Rebbe (Rashab) regarding the "essence and the expansion." Surely you have already prepared a plan on how to utilize these days to expand all matters of Chabad in your surroundings as much as possible, and may Hashem grant you success.

With blessings for a joyous *Chag HaGeulah* and regards to all *anash*, may they live.

## 4813

B"H, 8 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and distinguished, who engages in communal needs, R' Moshe, may he live:

Peace and blessing!

I acknowledge receipt of your letter dated 4 Kislev, in which you write regarding the classes, the students—may they live—their study schedule, and their progress. Your mind may be at ease, as you have put my mind at ease with the information provided. It is well known how meticulous our Rebbeim, the founders and directors of Yeshivas Tomchei Temimim—the Rebbe Rashab and his son, my father-in-law, the Rebbe—were regarding every detail of the Yeshivas Tomchei Temimim, down to the most minute particulars. It should be added that they were precise in this because it follows the principle explained at the beginning of Shaar HaYichud, that contemplation must be specifically on the details. After all, man below is called *Adam* because he is *adameh* (similar) to the One Above, and the *tzaddikim* are similar to their Creator, to the point that they are destined to be called by His Name, and this is well understood.

Surely you have already prepared a plan on how to utilize the upcoming days of 19 Kislev and Chanukah for the benefit of developing and expanding all the affairs of the holy institution where you are located, as much as possible, both in quantity and in quality. May Hashem grant you success, in accordance with the known ruling of the Heavenly Beis Din.

With blessings for the Chag HaGeulah and regards to all your colleagues in this holy work.

#### ***4813***

**R' Moshe, may he live:** Lebartov, Pittsburgh. Additional letters to him appear above, 4790, and in the notes referenced there.

## 4414

4414

B”H, 8 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To our brethren, the children of Israel, who love and cherish Torah and mitzvos,
May Hashem grant them life,

Peace and blessing!

In connection with the upcoming annual celebration of the Merkaz HaYeshivos Tomchei Tmimim Lubavitch, this coming Sunday, 12 Teves, I send my greetings and blessing to all the activists of the yeshiva, the helpers, and the participants in the preparatory work for the celebration.

As is known, this annual undertaking represents an important financial support for the Lubavitcher yeshivos. It must generate significant assistance on the spot, win over new friends and activists for the Lubavitcher yeshivos, and generally create the necessary climate for their development and expansion.

It is therefore entirely understandable that no matter how much effort and exertion one invests in the preparations for this annual undertaking, it cannot be too much; the greater the effort, the greater the zechus.

Particularly great is the zechus, as well as the responsibility, of the lead activists—those who must set the tone and show the way for others. In such a case, our Chazal have said, "It is a mitzvah to publicize," so that others, too, may be influenced in this spirit.

I hope that everyone will recognize the great zechus and the holy obligation to help ensure the continuity and expansion of the Merkaz HaYeshivos Tomchei Tmimim Lubavitch, and will fulfill this in the best possible way. In this zechus, everyone, together with their household, will merit the Divine blessings that our holy Rebbes, the Nesi'im, pray for and draw down for the supporters of the yeshivos that they founded and led with literal mesiras nefesh.

With a blessing for success in all of the above and in all that is needed, both materially and spiritually,

**Menachem Schneerson**

## 4915

B"H, 8 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished R' Chaim Abba, may he live:

Greetings and blessings!

In due time, I received your news that you have settled in as a *shochet* for poultry, and that you are also teaching children and serving as a spiritual leader in the *beis medrash*. May Hashem Yisbarach help you to be successful in all of the above, in raising the pride of *Klal Yisrael* through strengthening and disseminating Yiddishkeit. I hope that you utilized the month of Tishrei, and specifically the times of the *Yomim Tovim*, in this direction.

Regarding what you wrote—that they are simple colonists—the saying of the Baal Shem Tov is well-known, which we heard from my father-in-law, the Rebbe: that the *pashitus* (simplicity) of a *ish pashut* (simple person) touches upon the *pashitus* of the *Atzmus* (the Essence). In the language of the *Gemara*, "Everything that is simple is superior."

I hope that you will also prepare yourself in an appropriate manner for the *Rosh Hashanah* of Chassidus, the bright day of the *Chag HaGeulah*, 19 Kislev, which is approaching, in order to utilize it for *hafatza maayanos chutzah*—the matters of Chassidus, its guidance, and its customs. May Hashem Yisbarach grant you great success in this as well. As in the well-known story of the Mitteler Rebbe: because of this *mesiras nefesh* (of the Alter Rebbe) for the teachings of Chassidus, it was decreed in the Heavenly Court that in every matter of Torah, *yiras shamayim*, and good character traits, the hand of those connected to him and those who follow in his footsteps shall be uppermost.

With blessings for success.

#### ***4915***

**R' Chaim Abba:** Weil, Toronto.

## 4816

4816

B"H, 10 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished
R' Zev Dov, may he live and be well,

Shalom and blessings!

It was with pleasure that I received your letter of 25 Cheshvan, informing me of your return and aliyah to Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established, in good order and in peace. You also mentioned that you delivered the regards from here during a Chassidishe farbrengen, which surely was filled with the joy of a mitzvah and with an awakening toward Torah and its mitzvos. According to the well-known teaching that "joy breaks through barriers," may it be Hashem’s will that this also add to the nullification of the barriers and limitations of the *sitra achra*, and subsequently, even those matters that are restrictive from the side of holiness—the *mitzrayim* (limitations) of holiness. May we soon merit the fulfillment of the prophecy, "And you shall spread out to the west and to the east," [resulting in] an inheritance without limitations, the prerequisite for which is the dissemination of the wellsprings without limitation. May it be Hashem’s will that the merit of the *Baal HaSimcha* (the Rebbe of the day) assist each and every one in this matter of dissemination, with health, broadness of mind, and true joy.

With regards to all *Anash*, may they live and be well, and awaiting good news.

#### ***4816***

**R' Zev Dov:** Lodmir, Jerusalem. Additional letters to him appear above in Vol. 10, letter 3261, and in the notes referenced there. See also below, letter 5026.

## 4417

4417

B"H, 10 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

It was with pleasure and joy that I received your letter from Erev Shabbos. Regarding the conclusion of your letter, that you are returning to your work in chinuch—which is the work of Hashem—may it be the will of Hashem that He fulfill the desires of your heart and the heart of your wife, tichyeh, for good, for joy and laughter, very soon. As is known, the Chassidic adage from the early Chassidim states that it is the duty of the believer and the Chossid to fulfill the command of his "Employer"—the Creator of the world—and it is the duty of the "Employer" to provide for all the needs of the one who fulfills His command; and not only that, but also to prepare the vessels for this, as it is written, "And He will sustain you".

With blessings for the Chag HaGeulah and for good tidings in all that was mentioned, and regards to all Anash, shiychu.

#### ***4417***

**And He will sustain you:** See the Maamar beginning "Velo Zachar" 5688, at the end.

## 4818

B"H, 10 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 27 Marcheshvan.

Your reasoning is correct to inquire, when the time comes, regarding organizing in a religious moshav or kibbutz, such as Komemiyut, Chefetz Chaim, and the like.

Surely you are learning with persistence and diligence, both in Nigleh and in Chassidus, and are influencing your friends in this regard as well. This is especially appropriate given the beginning of your letter concerning the current situation, and as is well known from our Sages regarding the verse (Shmuel II 8:15) that the engagement in Torah of those who are "inside" brings success to those who are "in the war." This is especially true of the study of the teachings of Chassidus, the *Toras Chaim*, where there is no place for the *sitra achra* (Iggeres HaKodesh 26).

May it be the will of Hashem that the merit of the *Baal HaSimcha* and the *Geulah* guide you on the good path before you, in both material and spiritual matters alike, to your true happiness.

With blessings.

#### ***4818***

**Those who are in the war:** See above, letter 4790, and the references in the notes there.

## 4419

4419

B"H, 10 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

...Regarding your question about whether to attend the wedding of [Name withheld]. In general, it is not appropriate to hold a grudge. Especially since every Jew believes in Hashgacha Pratis, one must be certain that "G-d intended it for good," particularly according to what is explained in Chassidus in the teachings of the Baal Shem Tov and in the teachings of Chassidus (see Iggeres HaKodesh by our Rebbe the Alter Rebbe, Epistle 25). Therefore, one should participate in the simcha of a Jew, and especially a relative. Regarding how much time to spend there—whether only at the chuppah or also later, and the like—you should consult with Anash who are in that location.

## 4920

4920

B”H, 10 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi and Chossid, G-d-fearing and distinguished, involved in the needs of the community, etc.,
R’ Shlomo Yosef, may he live,

Greetings and blessings!

...With honor and blessing for your birthday—which corresponds to the chapter of Tehillim you begin to recite—may the following be fulfilled in your person: "I will come with the mighty deeds of Hashem... even unto old age and gray hairs," and according to the commentary of the Tzemach Tzedek until the end of the galus—"You will return and bring me up from the depths of the earth," and afterwards, "I will sing to You with the harp, O Holy One of Yisrael" (see the commentary of the Mitteler Rebbe on Tehillim, second edition, section 26).

P.S.

I have received the *Kuntres* supplement to the Encyclopedia, Volume 1, and thank you very much. It is surely unnecessary to arouse you and all *anash* regarding utilizing the upcoming *Chag HaGeulah*, 19 Kislev, as is required, and may it be the will of Hashem that it be successful in every detail.

I happened upon the *Sheilos U’Teshuvos Kol Mevaser*. It appears that in section 48 there—the Rav, R’ Y.A.H., may he live, was stringent even regarding profits from ships, etc.!! It would be worthwhile to obtain the *Kuntres* mentioned there...

It is highly likely that if there were a suitable gathering for your seventieth birthday (to note the language of the Tosafos Yom Tov at the beginning of the first chapter of Berachos regarding the virtue of seventy years; see also *Likutei HaShas* of the Arizal there), words of *hisorerus* for *yiras shamayim*, for the observance of *mitzvos*, and for *Da’as Chassidus* would be heard there, especially given the current time and the general situation, and the days of Chanukah.

*4920*

**R’ Shlomo Yosef:** Zevin, Jerusalem. Additional letters to him—above, 4911, and in the references noted in the footnotes there.

## 4921

4921

B"H, 10 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

...Regarding what you conclude with the praise of your teacher and mentor... I find it somewhat puzzling that you write—stating explicitly—that if he had been among the students of the Baal Shem Tov, he would have been like one of them, and if he had been among the Tannaim and Amoraim, he would have been like one of them. I wonder if there is anyone in our generation capable of testifying to such a thing, or even anything approaching it. For to say of someone that if they had been in those previous generations they would have been on the level of the students of the Baal Shem Tov, or on the level of the Amoraim and Tannaim, one must first possess a precise and thorough understanding of exactly what level and quality the students of the Baal Shem Tov, as well as the Amoraim and Tannaim, actually occupied. If only there were people in our generation who understood what that level truly was—then the generation would not be so orphaned. This is especially true in light of what is explained in the Zohar and Tikkunei Zohar regarding the "mighty ones" (*ha-eitanim*) being synonymous with the Tannaim, and in Zohar Vol. III, 292a, and at the beginning of Shaar Ruach HaKodesh. It is also discussed in many other places, albeit with different explanations, though the point remains the same—based on what is explained in Likutei Torah by our Rebbe, the author of the Tanya and Shulchan Aruch, in the discourse (*maamar*) for Parshas Tazria, titled *Kamפלגי במתיבתא דרקיע* (They debate in the Heavenly Academy), regarding the nature of the Tannaim and Amoraim, as well as in the *Biurei HaZohar* for Parshas Vayishlach.

Due to our many sins, it is clear that not every mind can bear such a thing, to describe and say—even regarding one’s own teacher, whom one is halachically obligated to honor above all else—that if he had been in that generation, etc. It is well known that one should not exaggerate the titles of those who are in the World of Truth, for they have no desire for such things at all.

...May it be the will of Hashem that these days, and especially the 19th of Kislev—the Hillula of the Maggid, the successor of the Baal Shem Tov, and the day of redemption and victory of the Alter Rebbe, the successor of the Maggid and the Baal Shem Tov—will bring an increase in the success of spreading their wellsprings until they reach even the outermost reaches (*chutza*). May this hasten the end of our exile, at which time the reasons for the Torah will be revealed, as per the interpretation of our Sages brought by Rashi on the verse, "Let Him kiss me with the kisses of His mouth," a mere glimpse of which we have tasted in the Torah of the early masters: the Baal Shem Tov, the Maggid, the Alter Rebbe, and their students.

With respect and blessing.

## 4922

B"H, 10 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

Following your longest silence, your letter of 23 Cheshvan was received, bearing the good news that you are feeling much improved. May it be the will of Hashem that—consistent with various matters of Torah and mitzvos where a majority is insufficient and the entirety is required (as explained during the farbrengen and also transcribed in the third *Kovetz Lubavitch*)—you will soon report not only "much improvement," but complete and proper improvement. For in this, too, a majority does not suffice; rather, it must be as the verse states, "You shall be wholehearted (*tamim*) with Hashem your G-d" (see *Likutei Torah*, discourse *Ki HaMitzvah*, Chapter 2). *Tamim*—wholehearted—in the 248 spiritual limbs and 365 spiritual sinews, which in turn draws vitality into the 248 physical limbs and 365 physical sinews.

With blessings for the Chag HaGeulah and for good tidings.

## 4923

4923

B"H, 10 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, G-d-fearing, and distinguished, occupied with communal needs, R' Hillel, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

...And according to what is explained in many places, including in the Iggeres HaKodesh of the Alter Rebbe, end of section 7, that every soul, spirit, and neshamah has a specific mitzvah in which it must exercise preference, extra caution, and greater alacrity—doubled and redoubled, far beyond the caution exercised for other mitzvos—it follows that for every one of anash and those of the lineage of anash, this matter is the "spreading of the wellsprings" (hafatzas hamaayanos), for which our Rebbes, the Nesi'im—who are in the category of the head and the brains of all anash (see Tanya, chapter 2)—sacrificed their lives. Surely, you too will conduct yourself as stated in that Iggeres HaKodesh regarding this spreading, and this will also hasten all your personal matters, as that is one of the virtues of that specific mitzvah.

May it be the will of Hashem that the merit of this day—which is the celebration of the redemption of the Mitteler Rebbe—will also serve to protect each and every one of us in all our needs, for the spreading of the wellsprings in every place, until they reach even the castaways and even the lost, in accordance with the nature of the master of the celebration: expansion and dissemination. As is known from our Rebbes, the Nesi'im, that the Alter Rebbe, the Mitteler Rebbe, and the Tzemach Tzedek are in the category of the Sefirah of Chochma, the Sefirah of Binah, and the Sefirah of Da'as.

With blessings for good tidings.

#### 4923

R' Hillel: Medalia, Leeds. Additional letters to him—above, Vol. 10, 3118, and in the references noted there.

See below 5118, 5250.

## 4924

B"H, 10 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and learned, engaged in communal needs,
R' Alexander Sender, shlita,

Shalom u’Bracha!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from Rosh Chodesh Kislev, in which you write regarding your visit to the Maoz Aviv neighborhood and that you did not find several of them at home at that time. Since they have surely returned by now, it is appropriate that you visit them again to explain to them the concept of "a miracle that happened in those days, at this time," as explained in the *V'al HaNissim* prayer, with added light and vitality based on the principles and foundations of the teachings of Chassidus Chabad. May Hashem be with your mouth, that your words be accepted and exert their influence regarding practical action in daily life. For this is the whole purpose of man in his battle against the *klipos* and *sitra achra*—through his battle with his body and animal soul (in the manner of "you shall surely help him" [*azov ta'azov*], according to the well-known interpretation of the Baal Shem Tov on this). Through this, one conquers his portion of the world, and ultimately the world as a whole, as is explained in *Tanya* in many places, including chapters 36 and 37. This is also hinted at in the well-known statement in *Toras HaNigleh*: "A person is **obligated** to say, 'The world was created for my sake'" (which is obviously true, in accordance with the command, "Distance yourself from a false word"), and "A person should always view himself as balanced, and the entire **world** as balanced," etc.

You will surely utilize the days of 19 Kislev and the upcoming Chanukah to disseminate the wellsprings of the teachings of Chassidus, its guidance, and its customs, wherever your reach extends. This is identical to what was mentioned above, since the coming of Moshiach Tzidkeinu depends on the dissemination of the wellsprings, at which time the fulfillment of the promise mentioned in *Tanya*, chapters 36 and 37, will begin. The reason this is dependent on the dissemination of the wellsprings is not merely a mystical quality, but because of the inherent connection between them, as is also explained in the *sicha* of Simchas Torah 5690 and in many other places. May Hashem grant you success to bring good tidings regarding all the above, with health, peace of mind, and an expanded intellect.

With blessings for good tidings.

4925

B"H, 10 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, G-d-fearing, whose work is the work of Heaven,
R' Yitzchak, may he live,

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter, you ask where to find explanations, supplements, or the like for the maamar written by the Alter Rebbe himself, which appears at the end of Parshas Chayei Sarah in Torah Or.

It is surprising to receive this question, as it has been about two years since Kehot published Torah Or, at the end of which a commentary by the Tzemach Tzedek on a large portion of this maamar was printed. Furthermore, at the end of that same edition, I also provided references to other Chassidic texts where the subjects of most of the discourses in Torah Or are discussed.

Although it is possible that you used the expression at the end of your letter only as a witticism, I nevertheless found it appropriate to comment on it. You write that it is the nature of the *nefesh* that in many of its matters, "delay is equivalent to denial." You surely emphasize the word ***nefesh*** here; in my opinion, this is not the case. On the contrary, as is also proven by the law in the *nigleh* of Torah—that there is no "denial" (postponement) regarding living creatures, unlike that which has no spirit of life, and several other parallels in the laws of *nigleh*—nevertheless, regarding the practical matter, I am responding to your letter without any delay at all, in accordance with your request.

With the blessing that the merit of the *baal hasimcha* (the one whose birthday/yahrzeit we celebrate) protect each and every one of us, in the way of *tichye*, for the spreading of the *maayanos* (wellsprings) to every place, until they reach even those who are temporarily cast aside and even those who are lost—fitting for the matters of the *baal hasimcha*, which involve expansion and dissemination, as is known from our Rebbes, our holy leaders: the Alter Rebbe, the Mitteler Rebbe, and the Tzemach Tzedek, corresponding to the *sefirah* of Chochma, the *sefirah* of Binah, and the *sefirah* of Da'as.

And with the blessing of the holiday.

***

4925

R' Yitzchak: Hutner, Brooklyn. Additional letters to him: above, Vol. 12, 3932, and the references in the notes there. Below, 5027.

It has been about two years: See also above, letter 4923.

## 4926

11 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn

To the G-d-fearing, illustrious, and distinguished individual, engaged in communal needs,
R. Schneur Zalman, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter, in which you write regarding the matter of writing a Sefer Torah and its completion.

Since everything is by Divine Providence, and especially a matter connected to Torah and its mitzvos, and you write to me regarding the writing of a Sefer Torah, it is my duty and privilege to mention here the ruling of our Alter Rebbe—the authority on the hidden aspects of Torah—and the Shulchan Aruch—the authority on the revealed aspects of Torah—to write *petzu'a dacha* with an *alef* at the end. This is as brought in the Responsa *She'eris Yehuda*, siman 16, by the brother of the Alter Rebbe (and the various opinions on this were collected in the sefer *Mishnas Avraham*, siman 32). I heard from my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, that when he was in the city of Worms before the First World War, he saw there the Sefer Torah that they had received by tradition, which was written by the Maharam of Rothenburg, and it was written there as mentioned above. He also saw this in a Sefer Torah in Prague, which, according to the tradition passed down from generation to generation, was proofread by Ezra the Scribe. Furthermore, in the library and manuscripts of Mr. Sassoon in London, there are ancient manuscripts (a Tanach of the grandson of the Rashba, a Tanach in the handwriting of a student of the Rashba, the author of the sefer *Migdal Oz*, a Damascus Chumash written 1,100 years ago, a Tanach Chumash written 500 years ago, as well as a Tanach written approximately 500 years ago, and a Yemenite Chumash manuscript from that same period), and in all of them, *dacha* is written with an *alef*. Moreover, in the Tanach manuscript of the author of *Migdal Oz*, it was originally written *dacha* with a *hei*, but the author of the *Migdal Oz* himself erased it and corrected it in his own hand to an *alef*. I have elaborated on this because, to my astonishment and regret, I have learned that there are few in Eretz Yisrael (may it be rebuilt and established) who are aware of all the above, even though they copied this from my letter in the sefer *Otzar HaPoskim* at the end of Volume 1.

Regarding the importance of writing a Sefer Torah, where the completion of the mitzvah is in its finishing, it does not seem appropriate to me to delay the completion for months, even though it is not the custom to necessarily rush this (and in truth, the reason for this lack of alacrity is a subject requiring study). However, this does not justify a delay of several weeks, and certainly not months. When the scribe reaches the completion, it should be scheduled for auspicious days close to the time of completion, and such days can be found in almost every month of the year—such as the 8th of Shevat, Rosh Chodesh, and according to what is written in *Devarim* 1:3, in the month of Adar, the days of Purim, or the 7th of Adar, and the like.

2) Regarding the two synagogues requesting to receive the Sefer Torah: since you write that in one of them you study the Oral Torah, you should place the Written Torah there as well, so that "they shall be together in perfection."

3) In your letter, you use the expression "to transfer the Sefer Torah to the domain of the synagogue." Although you did not specify your intent with the word "domain," it seems to me that you should not give the Sefer Torah as a gift, but rather place it there while it remains in your own possession, for then the fulfillment of this mitzvah is complete.

It is my hope that you meet from time to time with Anash (may they live) who are in your vicinity, and that you have fixed times for the study of Chassidus. It goes without saying that the instruction of our Sages applies here as well: "We increase in matters of holiness, and whoever adds, they add to him."

As you requested, I will mention you and your family (may they live) at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. May it be Hashem’s will that, with peace of mind and health of body, you will have good news regarding all that was mentioned, and that together with the physical healing of those who turn to you, you also strive for the healing of their souls—and there is nothing that can stand before willpower.

With blessing.

## 4927

B"H, 11 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the distinguished Rabbi and Chossid, G-d-fearing and learned, etc.,
Rabbi Elazar Aryeh, may he live,

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your notification regarding the organizing of a Melaveh Malkah—the *Seudata D’David Malka Meshicha* (the meal of King David, the Mashiach)—this coming Motzaei Shabbos.

The days of the month of Kislev are indeed fitting and capable—being the month of miracles, the days of Chanukah, the completion of the work of the Mishkan, and the founding of the Second Beis Hamikdash—to draw down and influence the matters that occurred in those days into this present time.

As is well known, the Arizal explains the verse, "These days are remembered and performed," [meaning] that every year, when the season of these days arrives and they are remembered, the same matters are also "performed" and drawn down just as they were the first time.

May it be the will [of Hashem] that through the gathering of many of the children of Israel—for "where ten are gathered, the Shechinah rests"—and through the *Seudata D’David Malka Meshicha*, there will be an awakening to increase in the matters of our Torah, the Torah of life, and its mitzvos, and may this awakening be drawn down upon the days coming toward us for good.

With respect and blessings.

#### ***4927***

**Rabbi Elazar Aryeh:** Jungreis.

A similar letter appears above, 4999.

## 4928

B"H, 11 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished individual, occupied with communal needs, R' Daniel, may he live:

Shalom u’Bracha!

I was pleased to receive your letter of the 27th of MarCheshvan, in which I saw that you are occupied with communal needs in general. This gives me the hope that you are occupied with the communal needs of Chassidus—meaning, not only regarding the text of the davening, but also regarding the dissemination of the wellsprings of the teachings of Chassidus, its guidance, and its customs. As is known, the saying of our Alter Rebbe is that Chassidus is a matter for all Bnei Yisrael, not for a specific type or class among them, and there is nothing that can stand before the will.

Regarding your question concerning the logic of purchasing a building for a shul when it is only possible for them to obtain half of the required sum—as is the custom in Eretz Yisrael, may it be built and established, one acts positively in such a case, even when there is less than half the price. Especially since there is room to tell the homeowner that the income of the shul will go mostly, or almost entirely, toward paying off his debt; this promise will facilitate the negotiation with him.

Although you do not mention it, my hope is that you held a farbrengen on the past 10th of Kislev, and that you will do so with even greater intensity on the upcoming 19th of Kislev, the Rosh Hashanah for the study of Chassidus and the ways of Chassidus. May it be Hashem’s will that it be successful.

With a blessing that for many years you will bring good tidings regarding your personal matters and communal matters, with health, peace of mind, and an increase in the fixed times for Torah study.

And with the blessing of the Chag HaGeulah for all Anash, may they live.

***

4928

R' Daniel: Haskind, Tel Aviv. Another letter to him—see above, Vol. 7, 1860.

Saying: See also above, letter 4769, and the references in the notes there.

## 4929

B"H, 11 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the distinguished Rabbi and Chassid, who is G-d-fearing and whose work is the work of Heaven,
R' Yitzchak, may he live,

Peace and blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from the conclusion of Shabbos Parshas Vayeitzei, along with the *pidyon nefesh* enclosed therein, which will be read at an *eis ratzon* at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

Thank you for the trouble of delivering the letter to the distinguished Rabbi Weiss, may he live; surely you showed him, or perhaps even gave him, a copy of the previous letters, even though you do not explicitly mention this.

It was pleasant for me to read in your letter that you are preparing to celebrate the *yom habahir*, the 19th of Kislev, which is approaching, as well as the 10th of Kislev. May it be the will of Hashem that the *hisorerus* be drawn out for the entire year, especially since the day itself causes it—as it is the *Rosh Hashanah* for Chassidus and its matters, and the internal affects the external, for Yisrael is called by the name *tzvi* (Shmuel II 1:19), and according to the words of our Sages at the end of Maseches Kesuvos.

Regarding your observation concerning the change in phrasing in the *Siddur*, where before the *Anenu* prayer it is written "in a public fast" (*ta'anis tzibbur*) and in *Shema Koleinu* it is for a general fast—the intent is difficult to understand, for it could not be written otherwise. Before *Anenu*, it is specifically for a *ta'anis tzibbur*, whereas in *Shema Koleinu*, it applies to all types of fasts; there is no redundancy or change in phrasing or anything of that sort here.

Enclosed is what has been published recently; surely you will merit to enlighten the many with it, and through the many, they will stand.

With blessings for a *Chag HaGeulah* and for good tidings in all the above.

## 4930

B"H, 11 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 10 Kislev—the day of the redemption of the Mitteler Rebbe—in which you write about Chabad Chassidim and anash in general, and their attitude toward others.

Instructions regarding this have already been given to all Chassidim and all Bnei Yisrael by our Alter Rebbe, the founder of Chabad Chassidus, in Tanya, Chapter 32. It is explained even further in his Likutei Torah, in the maamar beginning with the words "Hechaletzu," and in words that carve out flames of fire in the Sefer HaMitzvos of his grandson, the Tzemach Tzedek, regarding the mitzvah of Ahavas Yisrael. In connection with the approaching bright day of 19 Kislev, you should also study the Igeres HaKodesh that our Alter Rebbe wrote after his release from the imprisonment into which he was cast due to an informer, etc. He writes there: "And therefore I have come, etc., a great announcement to all of anash, etc." If he issues such a warning in a "great announcement" regarding that type of person, how much more so does it apply to ordinary Bnei Yisrael, and how much more so to a Jew whom Divine Providence has brought to the same "inn." Indeed, this is the message of our Alter Rebbe in his well-known letter, where he notes that at the moment he recited the verse "He has redeemed my soul in peace," he was set free—and the conclusion of that verse is "for there were many with me." And this is understood.

I have elaborated for the time being on a matter that is so simple, to my surprise at the content of your letter and its conclusion, as the depths of his soul are weighing heavily upon him regarding this matter.

It is my hope that you will participate in the farbrengen of anash on 19 Kislev, the festival of the redemption of our Alter Rebbe. Our Sages have already said that a drink is great, for it brings close—and all the more so does it bring closer those who are already close.

Awaiting good tidings.

## 4931

B”H, 12 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter from the 10th of Kislev, the day of the liberation of the Mitteler Rebbe and the liberation of the Chassidim.

It is astonishing that you ask whether you should participate in the celebration of the installation of a Conservative rabbi. It is surely unnecessary to elaborate on the Conservative movement, which constitutes heresy against the G-d of Israel and His Torah, as explained by the Rambam and the poskim, and as was publicized recently by the well-known prohibition. This is not the place to repeat what I have written in my previous letters on this subject, which you have surely read.

Now, when Jewish people—most of whom possess an appalling lack of knowledge regarding this movement and its tenets—bring in a man and crown him to be their halachic authority, contrary to what is written in the Shulchan Aruch and contrary to the fundamentals of our religion, it is obvious that this is a matter that should arouse deep sorrow in the heart of every Jewish person who is a believer and G-d-fearing. It is not merely that they are acting out of desire—where at the time of the act they know they are committing a transgression, and after satisfying their desire they are awakened with feelings of regret—but they are going further, innovating something new against Hashem and His Torah, Heaven forbid. And you write a letter asking whether you, too, should rejoice in their joy that they have succeeded in crowning the aforementioned individual, or whether you should refrain from participating in this celebration.

It is understood that all of the above does not concern the personal conduct of the rabbi regarding Torah and mitzvos. However, the question here is not regarding the conduct of a private individual or the conduct of a few dozen people, but rather the establishment of a position for the purpose of creating a Conservative congregation and a leader who will guide them in this path.

Furthermore, it is of interest that, as is apparent from your letter, they are planning to hold this celebration on the night of the 19th of Kislev. In the words of our Rebbeim, this day is "the beginning of Your works," the perfection of the true intent in the creation of man on earth: to draw down the revelation of the light of the inner dimension of our holy Torah, etc., the aspect of the depth and inner dimension of the Torah of Hashem and the mitzvos of Hashem from the aspect of the inner dimension and essence of the Ohr Ein Sof, which will shine within the depths of our souls. Every word of this is surely precise. Imagine the two matters: on this night, a Tamim stands and fulfills the aforementioned in practice (even if the revelation of this will only be in the future, as is explained in many places), or, on this night, the opposite picture—where one who was once a Tamim participates in a celebration where they are establishing a community on the foundations of heresy, offering praise and accolades, and strengthening their hands.

Enclosed is a kuntres that was published for this time. You will surely merit to share its contents with the many. May it be the will of Hashem that what is explained in many sichos—that in the teachings of Chassidus there are counsels for all the stratagems and cunning of the Yetzer Hara—will be utilized by you for doubts regarding the subtlest of subtleties of evil, and not for appalling matters such as those mentioned above.

With blessings for good tidings, with the blessing of the Chag HaGeulah, and in connection with your birthday—for a year of success in both spiritual and material matters, in general and private affairs.

***

**4931**

**The well-known prohibition:** See above, Vol. 12, Letter 4162. See also above, Vol. 13, Letter 4306, and the references in the footnotes there.

**In the words of our Rebbeim:** Igros Kodesh of the Rebbe Rashab, Vol. 1, Letter 110.

## 4932

4932

B"H, 14 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed and distinguished, etc., R' Yaakov, may he live, Shochet u'Bodek.

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter: The situation is **not** more serious than it was several months ago, and perhaps even years ago. Regarding relocating due to fear (which has no basis), there is no one who knows which place is more protected. In general, the Guardian of Yisrael neither slumbers nor sleeps, and He watches over each and every person with an open eye. Therefore, you should not alarm yourself, nor others; rather, you should occupy yourself with your internal and spiritual task of being a light to illuminate, as has been discussed orally and in writing many times.

I am certain you are preparing to utilize the upcoming bright day of 19 Kislev, as well as the days of Chanukah, in the best possible way to spread the wellsprings outward and to disseminate Yiddishkeit in general. May Hashem grant you success.

Enclosed is what has been published recently; surely you will merit to bring merit to the many, and the merit of the many is dependent upon you.

With blessings for a Chag HaGeulah and for good tidings.

***

**R' Yaakov, may he live:** Ginzburg, Milan. Additional letters to him: above, Vol. 9, 2889, and the references in the notes there.

**The situation:** The fear of war. See also below, letter 4982.

## 4433

B”H, 14 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn

To the G-d-fearing, distinguished, and esteemed individual, who engages in communal needs with great energy and possesses fine character traits,
Rabbi Zev, may he live,

Greetings and blessings!

In addition to the regards I receive from you and the other members of Shuva Yisrael through their letters to Anash, may they live, here, I was especially pleased to read the good news regarding the strengthening of the shiurei Torah, for both young and old. May Hashem Yisbarach help you to fulfill, without difficulty, the will of the Creator to spread Yiddishkeit, Torah, and mitzvos to ever-larger and more numerous circles. Especially as we approach the days of Chanukah, which are days of miracles and wonders, may it be fulfilled according to the phrasing we recite: "As in those days" — may it be so also "in this time."

Especially for all those connected to the Baal Shem Tov, his student the great Maggid, and his student and successor, Rabbeinu HaZaken, author of the Tanya and Shulchan Aruch—whose liberation from imprisonment we will celebrate at the end of this week, Yud-Tes Kislev—there is special assistance from Above in matters of Torah and mitzvos. As is known, the ruling of the Beis Din Shel Maalah, as recounted by the son of Rabbeinu HaZaken, the Mitteler Rebbe: because of this mesiras nefesh (of Rabbeinu HaZaken) for Toras HaChassidus, they ruled in the Beis Din Shel Maalah that in every matter of Torah, yiras shamayim, and good character traits, the hand of those who are connected to him and follow in his footsteps shall be uppermost.

Please convey my blessing, and the blessing of Chag HaGeulah, to each of the members of Shuva Yisrael. May they also be able to report soon regarding victories in the dissemination of Torah, Yiddishkeit, and Chassidus, until they reach "the outside" (chutza).

Enclosed is a booklet for Yud-Tes Kislev, which I hope you will share with the many, for the merit of the many depends upon it.

With blessings for Chag HaGeulah and regards to all the members, may they live.

***

4433

Published in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 14, p. 525, and completed based on the secretariat’s copy.

Rabbi Zev: Greenberg, Buenos Aires. Another letter to him appears below, 5154.

Shuva Yisrael: See letter 5154 below.

## 4434

4434

B"H, 11 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

I have received the minutes from the board meeting held on 20 Marcheshvan, the birthday of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. It is known from our Sages that on one’s birthday, one’s *mazal* is strengthened. The essence of the "Shepherd of Israel" is that the affairs of his flock should be good in every detail and in every possible way. However, in order to avoid the "bread of shame"—for the *tzaddikim* are like their Creator—free choice is granted to all those who will also cooperate in these matters. This is true even though, very often, an action similar to that described in the beginning of *Midrash Shir HaShirim* is sufficient: "Provided that you extend your hand with us." Even though the weight of the stone was not such that a hand could lift it, nevertheless, **the stone was elevated to Jerusalem**. See there, and in *Koheles Rabbah* at the beginning.

May it be the will [of Hashem] that these days of the month of Kislev serve to bring all of the above into actual practice, for action is the essential thing. This is explained in many places in the *sichos* of our Rebbes, the *Nesi'im*, regarding the concept that "there is no wise man like one who has experience," and that from every Chassidic concept there must be a practical result, and this is well understood.

I await and anticipate hearing good news, detailed in all the above, and with blessings for the *Chag HaGeulah*,

**M. Schneerson**

The letter enclosing the resolutions of the committee for the dissemination of the *ma'ayanos* was received.

I am interested to know about the children of *anash*—may they live—who are of school age: Are they studying in Chabad educational institutions? After all, the blessing and spirit of our holy *Nesi'im* dwell in them especially—(and through the establishment of... the excuse of the distance of the location has also been removed). And for those who, for the time being, have not yet done as mentioned above—what is the true reason for this?

After signing, I received the minutes and a letter—marked "urgent"—from 17 Marcheshvan! And the postmark on the envelope is 12 Kislev!

#### ***4434***

A portion was printed in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 6, p. 206; a portion in *Igros Kodesh*, Vol. 21, p. 417; and it was completed based on a photocopy of the letter.

*) This is discussed in *Responsa Chidushei Yaakov Meisels*, Vol. 2; *Responsa Achiezer*, Vol. 3, 24:20 (and there he references *Aruch LaNer*, *Yevamos* 76a; *Divrei Malkiel*, Vol. 1; *Divrei Eliyahu*, 21:20; *Toras Mordechai*); *Responsa Maharash MiRadomishl*, Vol. 6; *Sdei Chemed*, *Pe'as HaSadeh*, *Asifat Dinim*, *Ma'areches Ishus*, 13:13; *Tzafnat Paneach* (by the Gaon of Rogatchov), 9:2 and 13:19.

## 4935

B"H, 14 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 2 Kislev, in which you write that the doctor wishes to perform a certain examination, and there are those who say that it is forbidden to perform this examination according to the Shulchan Aruch.

When you speak with a specialist in the field, it will become clear to you that this examination is not merely for the sake of gathering information, but that the methods of treatment depend on the results of the examination. Therefore, there are Rabbis*—authorities of Jewish law—who permit it to be done in certain cases. However, since there are several methods for this examination, one should begin with the most lenient method according to the Shulchan Aruch. You should ask the attending physician to explain the methods of the examination to a Rabbi—a teacher of Torah law—in your vicinity, and he will advise you as to which one to begin with.

At a time of favor, you should mention yourself and your wife, may she live, at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. May it be His will that you bring good news regarding the fulfillment of your hearts' desires for the good.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

**Secretary**

#### ***4935***

*Methods of the examination: See also above, Vol. 13, Letter 4557, and the references noted in the footnotes there.

## 4936

B"H, 14 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter, in which you write regarding a marriage proposal for your niece, but the individual in question avoids answering clearly regarding how he will conduct himself in matters of Yiddishkeit, and you ask my opinion on this.

In my opinion—and as I told your niece when she was here—if he does not wish to answer clearly because he does not want it to be interpreted as if he is listening to others and lacks the resolve to decide for himself, that is not of such great concern. However, if he truly does not wish to make any commitment at all, despite having been asked about this several times, there is great doubt as to whether it is advisable to rely on the hope that he will change for the better in the future, since the matter is highly uncertain.

One of the ways to clarify the above is for your niece to speak with him when no strangers are present. In such a setting, he will not be embarrassed that onlookers might say he is listening to others, since they will not be seeing the interaction as it takes place. This is especially true if she also explains to him that it is not her desire that he listen specifically to her voice, but rather that there is no other choice, as it is essential that they live a life according to the *Torah* of life. If, even under such circumstances, he does not wish to promise anything, then there is a great concern that after the wedding he will certainly not want to change anything, since by then they will already be married and the choice will be in his hands.

May the Creator of the world and its Guide lead your niece, *tichye*, on the path that is good for her, in both material and spiritual matters alike.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

## 4437

B"H, 15 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished R' Avraham Moshe, may he live:

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter of 10 Kislev, the day of the redemption of the Mitteler Rebbe, son of the Alter Rebbe.

It gave me pleasure to read of your study of Chassidus in *Likutei Torah* and *Tanya*. Surely you have set times for this, and as our Rebbe the Alter Rebbe explains, this should not only be a fixed time, but also a fixed state of soul. In this way, the law that "a fixed [matter] is not nullified" applies not only to the other studies of the day, but on the contrary, it exerts its influence and leaves an impression upon the entire day. In connection with this, I hope you are aware of the three well-known daily portions—*Chumash*, *Tehillim*, and *Tanya*—which are appropriate for every soul, as instituted by my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. At the very least, you should observe them from this point forward.

This is especially relevant as the time is conducive to making good resolutions regarding the study and practice of Chassidus, for the bright day of 19 Kislev is approaching—the day of victory and redemption for the founder of the teachings of Chassidus, and with it, all that it entails.

Regarding your question on what is written in various places: that the *machshavah* (thought) is only a garment even Above and does not unite with the *Etzem* (Essence), etc., even though in other places it is implied that it does unite with the *Ma'atzil* (Emanator)—there are many levels of such unification, one deeper than the other. After all, even speech is united Above (see *Tanya*, Chapter 21), yet it is not the same as the unification of *machshavah*. There is "high above high." Just as there is a distinction between the unification of the performance of *mitzvos* and *Talmud Torah* (*Tanya*, Chapter 23), so too, even within the *Sefiros* themselves, the unification of the *Keilim* (vessels) is not the same as the unification of the *Oros* (lights).

See the *Hemshech Mayim Rabim 5636*, Chapter 32 and onwards. I will not elaborate further here.

With blessings for good tidings regarding the above, and also regarding your influence on your surroundings in this direction.

## 4938

4938

B"H, 15 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

Following a long interval, I received your letter from Erev Shabbos Parshas Toldos, which took some time in transit. It was a pleasure to read that the matter of the salary has been arranged, as the resolution of the financial matter increases the possibilities for study and the performance of mitzvos without distraction. In truth, the importance here is not so much the size of the salary, but rather the removal of worry, which becomes easier to achieve. This is also understood from the words of the Rambam regarding the days of Moshiach, which is so that they may find rest and increase in wisdom, for they will be free to occupy themselves with Torah and its wisdom, even though in physical matters in that day, "a man will live off a calf of the herd, etc.," as our Sages of blessed memory stated in Sanhedrin 94b.

Enclosed is a Kuntres that was recently published, and surely you will merit to share its content with the Rabbonim, and the merit of the many depends on you.

With blessings for good tidings.

P.S.

Surely you will participate in the farbrengen of the auspicious day of 19 Kislev, the day of our redemption and the liberation of our soul, and you will increase in matters of disseminating the wellsprings (until they reach) the outside. The opinion of the Rambam is well known (Mikvaos 9:9): Only when it goes **outside** the pool does it have the status of a spring to purify through flowing water, and all the more so. The matter of the Mishnah is already known in such a case, and you are not free to exempt yourself from it, and there is nothing that can stand before the will.

#### ***4938***

**The opinion of the Rambam is well known:** See also above, Vol. 13, Letter 4590, and the references in the notes there.

## 4939

4939

B"H, 15 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the Luncheon Committee of the Bensonhurst Ladies Auxiliary, Mrs. Henry Nashorn, Mrs. Ella R. Kramer, Mrs. S. Surchin,
May Hashem grant them life.

Peace and Blessing!

In response to your notification regarding your luncheon on the occasion of twenty years of activity, and in honor of your esteemed president, Rebbetzin Freida, may she live, Kazarnovsky.

I send my greetings and blessing to the esteemed president, to the luncheon committee, and to all the members of the auxiliary, as well as to all the friends and participants in the event.

Since your luncheon is taking place on 23 Kislev, between the auspicious days of 19 Kislev and Chanukah, I hope that the event will be distinguished by great achievements and will exceed the goals it has set for itself and expected.

The statement of our Sages regarding Chanukah is well known: that this Yom Tov is connected to heroic Jewish women and their conduct of mesiras nefesh during that era, disregarding all the difficulties and decrees they had to overcome. Therefore, Chazal say, "They too were part of the miracle" — the women also had a share in the miracle, and even more so, that through them the miracle occurred.

It is also well known that every year that era repeats itself in the sense that it is an *eis ratzon* (time of favor) specifically for achievements in all matters of Torah and mitzvos, according to the phrasing of the blessing: "Who performed miracles for our forefathers in those days, at this time."

And according to the expression of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, the founder of the yeshiva for which you have the merit to be active: one must listen to what the little lights — the *nerois Chanukah* — are saying. May Hashem, blessed be He, grant you success in listening and hearing, and in bringing this into *maaseh b’poel* (actual practice).

With blessings for success in your holy work, as well as in your personal matters.

#### ***4939***

**The expression:** See also above, Vol. 10, letters 3137, 3141; below, 4959.

## 4940

B"H, 15 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and diligent worker in communal affairs, R' Saadia [Librov]
Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 9 Kislev: It is surprising that you do not mention the preparations for the farbrengen on 10 Kislev, the day of the liberation of the Mitteler Rebbe. Even in your current location, it is possible to find texts that explain the concepts of Chassidus, and consequently, texts that illuminate according to the understanding and grasp of the local residents. This is all the more true regarding the bright day of 19 Kislev, which is the day of our redemption and the liberation of our souls, when we were granted both the essence and the expansion, as per the well-known language of the Iggeres HaKodesh. Since you have been privileged to be the conduit in your location and its surroundings, through whom the springs must spread to every place and corner—as the well-known expression goes, "May your springs spread outward"—and since the Holy One, Blessed be He, does not deal unjustly with His creatures, you have certainly been given the strength and the possibility for this. The matter depends only on your will, and I await good news.

The Kuntres of the Maamar "Mayim Rabim" has surely arrived by now.

With blessings for the Geulah and for good news regarding all the above, and for the health of your entire family.

P.S.
If there are any students in Sefrou who are among those receiving Chanukah gelt, you should certainly distribute it to them in my name. Take the opportunity to explain to them concepts of Chanukah according to Chassidus. It would be appropriate that in all the Ohalei Yosef Yitzchok institutions in Morocco, the amount should be uniform.

## 4941

B"H, 15 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the avreich, Raphael Pinchas, may he live,

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter of 5 Kislev, it was a pleasure for me to read of your good activities in spreading Yiddishkeit, Chassidus, and their related matters. Certainly, you will not be satisfied with what has been accomplished thus far; on the contrary, this should inspire you with additional strength and courage to increase these activities, adding and continuing, just as the lesson of the upcoming days of Chanukah, where we add and continue, illuminating even the outside and the darkness of the night, for the mitzvah is performed from the time the sun sets, at the entrance of one’s house, facing the outside.

Regarding your question about the time for lighting the Chanukah candles, our custom is to light between Mincha and Maariv (though if one did not light then, one lights afterwards).

I am certain that you are also increasing your fixed times for Torah study in both Nigleh and Chassidus, and may Hashem grant you success.

With blessing,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,**

**Secretary**

#### ***4941***

Published in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 15, p. 537, and completed according to the secretariat’s copy.

**The avreich, Raphael Pinchas:** Goldstein, Jerusalem.

## 4942

4942

B"H, 16 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 2 Kislev—the month of miracles and redemption—in which you ask which Chassidus you should study to increase your learning.

Regarding this, the general principle is the statement of our Sages: "A person should always study in the place where his heart desires." It is appropriate that this be connected to the time, by which I mean the saying of the Alter Rebbe: "One must live with the times," which means with the weekly parsha (as explained in the sichos of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory). Similarly, it should be connected to the auspicious days and Yomim Tovim of the current time. To this, one should also add the Tanya shiur being studied and the maamarim of Chassidus printed for these times.

May you certainly utilize the auspicious days of the month of Kislev in general, and the bright day of 19 Kislev in particular.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

**Secretary**

## 4943

4943

B"H, 16 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from the 2nd of Kislev, the month of miracles and redemption. I am pained to note that, as is evident from the beginning of your letter, your energy has waned regarding the dissemination of the wellsprings through the distribution of Chassidic sefarim. As for your writing that "the times are responsible," on the contrary: the events of the time should awaken an increase in courage in all matters pertaining to the *ilana d'chaye* (the Tree of Life), where the "other side" has no foothold whatsoever—from which it is understood that there is no *galus* there, etc., and a word to the wise is sufficient.

May it be the will of Hashem that these words, though few in quantity, will be effective in accordance with the matter and its significance. May you not only increase in the aforementioned yourself, but through enthusiasm—with the warmth and light of Chassidus—may you also influence others in this regard, in a manner befitting the subject, through the distribution of sefarim, the dissemination of ideas, and the promotion of deeds. May the Ribono shel Olam grant you success, especially since the time is opportune, with the 10th and 19th of Kislev and the upcoming days of Chanukah.

With blessings for good tidings and success in all the aforementioned.

#### ***4943***

Printed in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 15, p. 518.

**Events of the time:** See above, Letter 4990, and the references in the footnotes there.

## 4944

B"H, 16 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed and distinguished, etc., R' Yisrael Yehuda, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 8 Kislev and the one preceding it, containing good news regarding the activities of anash, may they live and be well. May it be the will of Hashem that you continue to bring good tidings, both in material and spiritual matters—both regarding yourself and your household, and regarding all of anash within the entirety of Klal Yisrael, may they live and be well.

Surely you will utilize the auspicious days of the month of Kislev, and especially the bright day of 19 Kislev, which is approaching—the day of our redemption and the liberation of our souls. As it is written, "He has redeemed my soul in peace, for there were many with me" (Tehillim 55:19), which also hints at the drawing near of the "many"—even those who are currently in the "public domain" (*reshus harabim*), on the outside. And for one who wishes to purify himself, they assist him; and it is explained in *Likutei Torah* (Devarim 88b) that "to purify" means to purify others. And this is well understood.

I await good and detailed news regarding all of the above, and with blessings for a happy Chag HaGeulah.

#### ***4944***

**R' Yisrael Yehuda, may he live:** Levin, Kfar Chabad. Additional letters to him appear above in Vol. 9, 2665, and in the references noted in the footnotes there.

## 4945

B"H, 16 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and upright, involved in communal needs,
R' Michael Yehuda Aryeh Leib, may he live, HaKohen

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby confirm receipt of your *pidyon nefesh* in due time, as well as your letter of 3 Kislev, which brings good news regarding the welfare of all *anash*, may they live.

My hope is strong that despite all the doubts that surely exist among you regarding the matter, you will utilize the auspicious days of the month of Kislev in general, and especially the bright day of 19 Kislev—our redemption and the liberation of our souls—to strengthen all matters of *hafatza* (spreading the wellsprings outward). I intend this for you in particular, just as I do for each and every one of *anash* individually, within the collective of *anash*. The Creator of the world and its Guide—may it be His will that very soon the promise of the Creator of the world and its Guide will be fulfilled, in the language of the Rambam: that "Israel will dwell securely, rest from the kingdoms, find tranquility for themselves, and increase in wisdom, etc., for the earth will be full of the knowledge of Hashem, etc., and I will remove the heart of stone from your flesh, etc." This is as he elaborated in his book *Yad HaChazakah*. And according to the known principle that all details in the Torah are also an instruction, this is also an instruction: that the Rambam explained these matters in *Hilchos Teshuvah* and *Hilchos Melachim* at the conclusion and seal of the book *Mishneh Torah*. And this is well understood.

With blessings for *Chag HaGeulah* and for good tidings, and [I look forward to] details of what is being done by each and every one of *anash*, *shlita*, and especially the residents of Kfar Chabad, where Hashem has commanded the blessing, life forevermore.

With blessings for success in the above.

#### ***4945***

Printed in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 18, p. 257, and completed according to the secretariat's copy.

**To the esteemed Chossid... HaKohen:** Additional letters to him—above, 4705, and in the references in the notes there.

**The doubts that surely exist:** Whether the period of the war in Sinai (see above, letter 4890, and the references in the notes there) is appropriate for engaging in the spreading of the wellsprings.

## 4946

4946

B"H, 16 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, the G-d-fearing man of many accomplishments and fine character,
R' Yosef Eliyahu, may he live,

Shalom u'Bracha!

In response to your letter regarding the recitation of Kaddish—following up on my previous correspondence on this matter:

1) The statement that a Kaddish is effective for an hour and a half is found in Zohar Vol. I, 62b. See also Zohar Chadash Vol. III, 166a. Although R' Chaim Vital explains it differently (as brought also in Shiurei Beracha to Yoreh Deah, 376)—see the commentaries on the Zohar regarding this.

2) The instruction of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe [likely based on what is found in the testament of his father (the Rebbe Rashab), who conducted himself this way during his period of mourning, "except for Shabbos and Yom Tov"], is well known: that a mourner—may Hashem spare us—should endeavor to recite sixteen Kaddishim throughout the twenty-four-hour period. Although he did not specify the reason, it is highly probable that it is based on the aforementioned point: that sixteen Kaddishim, calculated at an hour and a half per Kaddish, provide protection for all twenty-four hours of the day.

3) From this instruction, it is understood that these matters apply to all mourners, may Hashem spare us, and it is not merely a matter for the congregation of those praying alone.

4) One should not raise a difficulty against the above from what is brought in various places regarding a different number of Kaddishim, as mentioned in your letter. I believe that in my own letters as well, I have pointed to what is stated in Eitz Chaim and Pri Etz Chaim, etc., in the introduction of R' Chaim Vital to Shaar HaHakdamos (printed in the introduction to Eitz Chaim), that the word **Tzadik** (צדי"ק) alludes to ninety (צ) Amens, four (ד) Kedushos, ten (י) Kaddishim, and one hundred (ק) blessings. It is also worth noting Tikkunei Zohar, Tikkun 18 (30a), which links it to the word Tzedakah.

For there are many aspects and benefits to the recitation of Kaddish—and not exclusively for mourners (for the deceased) in order to cool Gehinom. In connection with the change in the objective, the number of appropriate Kaddishim changes as well. And this is easy to understand.

I will conclude with a blessing for success in your holy work with Ezras Torah and its supporters—those who study Torah, who even precede the Torah itself, for the Torah merely reveals the virtues that are already within them. This is explained at length in many places in the teachings of Chassidus, and is alluded to in the words of our Sages (Bereishis Rabbah, Parsha 1, 4) that six things preceded the creation of the world: the Torah, etc., and Yisrael, etc. The thought of Yisrael preceded everything, etc. "Command the Children of Yisrael," "Speak to the Children of Yisrael." See also Makkos (22b), where Rava says, etc. (The apparent contradiction from Kiddushin 33b has already been resolved in many places).

With respect and blessing.

Enclosed is what was recently published, which I am sure will interest you.

## 4947

B"H, 16 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 8 Kislev, the month of redemption and miracles. Thank you for the good news that the Tanya shiur has been organized in... Surely, from time to time, you should inquire about the progress and the growth in this, for it is understood that this too is included in the commandment to "increase in holiness," and whoever adds, is added to.

Regarding what you wrote about your lack of satisfaction with your avodah in holiness, specifically that a portion of the students are not receptive to Chassidic influence, etc. — it is surprising to hear such phrasing regarding the souls of Bnei Yisrael. It is explained in Kabbalah and elucidated in Chassidus—including in the Tanya—that every single person has a share in all four parts of Pardes of the Torah. This is especially true regarding the teachings of Chassidus, as the saying of our Rebbe, the Alter Rebbe, is well known: that Chassidus is for all of Yisrael, not just for certain specific circles. Of course, the words must come from the heart and be explained in a manner appropriate to the vessel of the recipient. It is known from our Sages that whoever possesses yiras shamayim, his words are heard. When you contemplate the magnitude of your avodah and your privilege, it will surely add to your courage, and you will immediately see an increase in your success in the aforementioned. It is understood that through this, you will also receive an increase in the blessings of the Giver of the Torah in your own personal matters.

With blessings, awaiting good news.

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

**Secretary**

P.S.
You should certainly utilize the auspicious days of the month of Kislev in general—as mentioned above—and the bright day of 19 Kislev in particular.

Regarding yourself specifically, explain to your students the great value of the day and the necessity of its influence upon the entire year. May it be His will that you bring good news in this as well.

#### ***4947***

**Note:** See also above, letter 4769, and the references in the footnotes there.

## 4948

4948

B"H, 17 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter from the end of Cheshvan and the one preceding it, in which you write regarding your birthday, your entry into the *mechina* of Yeshivas Toras Emes, and your progress in your studies.

May it be the will of Hashem that you continue to succeed more and more, both in the study of *nigleh* and in the study of Chassidus, and in the performance of *mitzvos* with *hiddur*, for this is the whole of man. And if in any place and in any yeshiva our Sages of blessed memory said, "You have labored and you have found," how much more so in a yeshiva founded and directed by our Rebbes, the *Nesi'im* of Chabad—may the merit of the righteous protect us—who stand and arouse great mercy for all who belong to them and are connected to them within the entirety of *Klal Yisrael*, to draw down for them what they require, and especially for the students and *avreichim* of the yeshivos of our Holy Land. It is understood that as you increase in *hasmadah* and *shekidah*, your success will likewise increase. And by influencing your peers in this—which is a matter of spiritual *tzedakah*—you will, with the help of Heaven, see an increase in all that has been mentioned.

With blessings for a year of success, and I trust you observed the customs of a birthday and received good news regarding all that was mentioned, and likewise that you are utilizing the days of the month of Kislev, the month of *geulah* and miracles, to spread the *maayanos* outward.

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

#### 4948

**Regarding the customs of a birthday:** See above, Vol. 13, Letter 4339, and the references in the notes there. Below, 5145.

5155.

## 4849

4849

B"H, 17 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the G-d-fearing, esteemed, and distinguished, who engages in communal needs, R' Chaim, may he live:

Peace and blessing!

After a long interval, I was pleased to receive your letter of 10 Kislev, the Yom HaGeulah of the Mitteler Rebbe, in which you write about the progress in Chabad affairs regarding practical work, with the participation of the members of the Feiglin family, may they live, and other activists. May it be the will [of Hashem] that this becomes ever stronger and more expanded. As I have written several times regarding this: when one acts with more courage and, consequently, with more energy—and, it goes without saying, in pleasant ways—even those who are currently standing at a distance will naturally be drawn in, and certainly those who are already close. The main thing is not to be discouraged if the first time things do not turn out as one wishes; try again and again, and in the end, one will succeed. I hope that you will especially utilize the days of the month of Kislev, the month of miracles and redemption, for at that time the ruling of the Heavenly Beis Din is further strengthened and renewed: that because of this mesiras nefesh (of the Alter Rebbe) for the teachings of Chassidus, in every matter of Torah, yiras shamayim, and good character traits, the hand of those who are connected to him and walk in his footsteps shall be uppermost. I look forward to hearing good and detailed news regarding the above, as well as regarding your personal matters.

With blessings for Chag HaGeulah and good tidings.

#### ***4849***

**R' Chaim, may he live:** Serbranski, Melbourne. Additional letters to him: above, Vol. 10, 3229; below, 5041. 5104.

## 4950

B"H, 17 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished individual, a man of many accomplishments, R' Moshe Zalman, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

From time to time, I receive regards from you and your family members, may they live, as well as news regarding the affairs of Chabad in general. Lately, however, anash have been writing less than before—and this is all the more true regarding the things one wishes to know. As is well known, we have heard the saying multiple times from my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory: that Chassidus has accomplished that every Chossid, wherever he may be or go, is never alone, for he remains constantly connected with Chassidim. If this is true even before one meets them personally, it is certainly true to a much greater degree after a personal friendship has been formed.

Since we are approaching the end of the week, when we will celebrate the auspicious day of 19 Kislev—regarding which Rabbeinu HaZaken said, "He has redeemed my soul **in peace**... for **with many** they were with me" (Tehillim 55:19)—I am certain that among Chassidim and anash everywhere, the feeling and connection of one to another will be strengthened. This will not only be in the mind alone, but will also lead to actual deeds, to the extent of the directive of our Rebbes, to participate in the joy of one's fellow, etc. I hope to receive from you good tidings regarding your personal affairs, as well as regarding the general affairs, and especially regarding the educational institutions, Yeshivas Oholei Yosef Yitzchok and Beis Rivkah, which fall under the category of spreading the wellsprings outward (hafatza). May it be His will that these reports and letters be written in good health and with joy.

With regards to all members of your household, each and every one of them, may they live, and with the blessing of the Festival of Redemption, our redemption and the redemption of our souls, which is the auspicious day of 19 Kislev.

## 4951

4951

B"H, 17 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the G-d-fearing, distinguished, and esteemed R' Chaim, may he live:

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter of Monday, in which you ask—since there are no poor people in your environment, yet on the other hand you wish to fulfill the mitzvah of tzedakah:

a) In general, in such a case, one gives tzedakah and sets it aside in a separate place, and subsequently sends it to a reliable gabbai tzedakah so that he may distribute it there (in the event that, in the meantime, one does not encounter any poor people locally).

b) Since we currently cannot fulfill the mitzvah of *olah l'regel* (pilgrimage), you ask what can replace it.

There are several seforim that discuss the concept of every mitzvah in its spiritual sense, for in spirituality there has been no *galus*. Regarding the mitzvos connected to the Beis HaMikdash, such as the offering of *korbanos*—which we cannot fulfill physically at this time—it is stated that this is the concept of "this service is prayer," which was instituted to correspond to the *korbanos*. Similarly, the mitzvah of *olah l'regel* was for the purpose of "ascending to see and to bow down," to become permeated with *kedushah*, *hishtachavah*, and *bitul* to *Elokus*. The spiritual aspect of this can be fulfilled even now through *hisbonenus* in the greatness of the Creator, may He be blessed, until one arrives at *bitul atzmo*, as is explained in *Likutei Torah*, *Parshas Bracha*, 98b. In general, the *shorshei hamitzvos* and their spiritual explanation can be found in the *Sefer HaMitzvos* of the Tzemach Tzedek according to *Chassidus*, and in *Sefer HaChinuch* in the way of *mussar*...

With blessing.

P.S.
Enclosed is one of the publications connected to 19 Kislev, which we will be celebrating at the end of the week. You will surely read it with the proper focus, derive a *mussar haskel* from it, and acquaint your friends and acquaintances with it as well.

Regarding your question about a visit: when you are in Brooklyn, it is always better to write to the Mazkirus in order to coordinate the day and time.

#### ***4951***

**R' Chaim:** Gastfriend, Toronto.

## 4952

B"H, 17 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and scholarly individual, who engages in communal needs, a man of many accomplishments, etc., R' Mendel, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

I received your letter in due time, as well as the *pidyon nefesh* delivered by my relative, the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and scholarly individual, who engages in communal needs, a man of fine character, etc., R' Azriel Zelig Slonim, may he live. Its content concerns the printing of a manuscript, the contents of which, you suggest, prove that its author is the author of the sefer *Merkoves HaMishnah*, who is the holy R' Adam Baal Shem.

You can understand the surprise this announcement caused me. I delayed my response to your letter until I could find some source or basis for the claim that the author of *Merkoves HaMishnah* is the holy R' Adam Baal Shem. After searching and investigating, I have found for the time being only a conjecture on this matter by Rabbi R' Reuven Margolies, may he live, as printed in the weekly *HaMeishor* on 12 Iyar, 5701. I must say that, in my opinion, his conjecture is a "mountain hanging by a hair," and perhaps even that is an overstatement, and it is simply hanging in the air. I have never heard from my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, that anything of the holy R' Adam Baal Shem has been printed. And while the fact that we have not seen or heard something is not proof [that it does not exist], in a case like this, it is puzzling that throughout all the years he never mentioned or wrote about this, despite the fact that he dedicated much time to the manuscripts of the famous Genizah of Cherson, in which there is much discussion regarding the holy R' Adam Baal Shem.
