It is understood that relying on the introduction of the publisher of *Merkoves HaMishnah*, who I heard claimed that in his generation they called him by the name R' Adam, is not sufficient at all to explain the silence on this matter, given all the stories and traditions transmitted in the house of the Rebbe—Rebbe from Rebbe, back to our Rebbe the Alter Rebbe, who received from the Maggid, who received from our master the Baal Shem Tov—without any mention that the author of *Merkoves HaMishnah* is the very same person whose manuscripts reached the Baal Shem Tov, etc. It is possible that you have another basis and source for this rumor, and I would be grateful if you would inform me of the details regarding this.

It is understood that I am very interested to know if there are, in the collection of manuscripts under your management, any Chassidic writings of the Nesi'im of Chabad, or manuscripts from the Baal Shem Tov and the Maggid that have not yet been published. I would be grateful for the trouble of informing me about this as well.

Enclosed is what has been published recently, which I am sure will be of interest to you.

With blessing.

***

**4952**

**R' Mendel:** Chassida, Jerusalem.

**The author:** The initials of R' Dovid Moshe Avraham are A.D.M. (Adam). And in the publisher’s introduction: "This is the book of the history of the First Adam, the great Adam among giants, etc."

For more on this subject, see the introduction by Y. Mondshine to *Shivchei HaBesht* (Jerusalem, 5742), pp. 58-65.

## 4953

B"H, 15 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed and distinguished individual, occupied with communal needs, R' Chaim Zusia, may he live and be well,

Greetings and blessings!

...Certainly, despite the recent events, you have prepared a suitable plan, as necessary, to utilize the days of 19 Kislev and the upcoming Chanukah—as much as possible—for the development of Chabad affairs. May it be the will [of Hashem] that it be with exceptional success.

With blessings for a Chag HaGeulah and for good tidings,

**M. Schneerson**

I acknowledge receipt of your letters from the days of: 28 Marcheshvan, and 1, 2, and 7 Kislev.

#### ***4953***

**R' Chaim Zusia:** Vilimovsky, Tel Aviv. Additional letters to him appear above in 4785, and in the notes referenced there.

**Recent events:** See letter 4890 above, and the notes referenced there.

## 4954

B"H, 19 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn

To the esteemed Rabbi, G-d-fearing, learned, and pious, etc.,
R' Schneur Chaim, may he live.

Shalom u’Bracha!

In response to your letter of 2 Kislev, which was received, in which you write the details of the offer for a rabbinical position in the city of Adelaide and ask for my opinion on the matter.

I must admit that I see no room for doubt or question in this matter—it is straightforward that you should exert every effort, albeit in an appropriate manner, to accept the position, since they have agreed to the conditions regarding the fixing and improvement of matters related to religion. It is highly probable that their agreement to this is contingent upon your acceptance of the position. It is also evident from the letter I received from them that this is indeed the case. Furthermore, even if matters were already in order—regarding buildings and the like—the primary portion is a position such as this, where there is a near-certain hope that you will be able to bring the members of your community closer to our Father in Heaven, to His Torah and His mitzvos. This is the primary and inner task of every Jew: to bring his own heart and the hearts of other Jews closer to Hashem, as the Mishnah states, "I was created to serve my Maker." As is known from the saying of the early Chassidim, the Chassidim of our Rebbe, the author of the *Tanya*: "The order of the Torah is also Torah," and in the order of the mitzvos, the mitzvah of "be fruitful and multiply" is written first, and its meaning is: a Jew must create another Jew.

Regarding what you write concerning the education of your children, may they live—surely this too can be arranged, for it is impossible that the performance of the mitzvah of the Creator of the world and Giver of the Torah would diminish such a fundamental matter as the education of sons and daughters. In my opinion, it is also not worthwhile to delay until the days of Pesach, as you mention in your letter, for "each and every day must perform its work." Although, of course, haste can weaken one’s prestige, surely a middle path can be found. Generally, if the members of the community are in a state of *hisorerus*, it is not worthwhile to delay the practical implementation; on the contrary, one must take advantage of this, for who knows what a day may bring. It is also appropriate that, since they have agreed to the building of a *mikveh*, even if your actual arrival there is delayed for some time, you should begin this construction as soon as possible, with the argument that such a building requires several weeks, and perhaps even months.

I am happy for this opportunity to answer your letter on this auspicious day, for everything is by *hashgacha pratis*, and especially according to the teachings of Chassidus. May it be the will of Hashem that the merit of this day stand for you and for all that is yours in all that is needed, and through this, also for the fulfillment of your true task with joy and success.

With the blessing of the *Chag HaGeulah* and regards to all *anash*, may they live, who are in your camp, with their father-in-law, the esteemed Rabbi and Chassid, *shlita*, at their head.

P.S.
Enclosed is a *kuntres* that has been published, and surely you will merit to bring merit to the many through its contents, and the merit of the many will depend upon you.

## 4955

B"H, 19 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

...Regarding what you wrote concerning teshuvah, that it is effective even in the manner explained in Tanya, end of Chapter 20, and as recounted in the notes of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, in the introduction to *Pokeiach Ivrim*—this is indeed the case: that teshuvah (of the yechidah) is effective even in this regard, and not by virtue of merit or the like. This is explicitly stated in many places, including *Shaar HaTeshuvah* by the Mitteler Rebbe, in the maamar entitled *Im Yihyeh Nidchacha*, Chapter 5, and in the same work in the maamar *Padah B'Shalom*, Chapter 9.

Regarding what you wrote about the elevation of nitzotzin from the kelipos by a baal teshuvah—the methods for this are explained in the *Biur* to the maamar *Acharei*, printed at the end of *Derech Mitzvotecha* by the Tzemach Tzedek.

Due to a certain reason, the mailing was delayed, and in the meantime, your letter was received with the good news regarding the farbrengens of the auspicious day of 19 Kislev; thank you very much. May it be the will of Hashem that they exert their influence on the heart and mind, and through this, also strengthen all the activities of Tzeirei Agudas Chabad toward outstanding success.

## 4959

B"H, 19 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of Monday, in which you write that although things have improved, from time to time you still experience distracting thoughts, and you ask what the general solution for this is.

According to what is explained in Chassidus, and in Tanya in particular, the general and true answer to this is that there is no time to get caught up in debating this. For a Yid is occupied, and must be occupied, with Torah and mitzvos. By way of course, this weakens the *sitra achra*. This is especially true when taking into account that beyond what one must do for oneself, one must also do for others, as it is written, "And you shall love your fellow as yourself," and in particular for your peers in the yeshiva. May Hashem Yisbarach grant you success so that you may go from strength to strength, and be a *yirei shamayim*, a Chassid, and a *lamdan*.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

**Secretary**

## 4957

B"H, 19 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and upright, engaged in the needs of the community,
R' Menachem Zev, may he live, Halevi

Shalom u'Bracha!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your prompt letter. To fulfill your request, I am responding immediately, and enclosed herewith is the Kuntres that was published (although the maamarim had already been printed, some notes and references were added, especially since it is possible that the Gemara with the maamarim has not yet been received in Montreal).

And regarding the main point for which I am writing: the matter of this day, the Rosh Hashanah of Chassidus. In the phrasing of the Rebbe, may you be inscribed and sealed for a good year in the study of Chassidus and the ways of Chassidus.

***

**4957**

**R' Menachem Zev, may he live:** Gringlas, Montreal. Additional letters to him appear above, 4870, and in the references noted there.

**The Gemara:** Bava Metzia and Bava Basra with the addition of the chiddushim and maamarim of the Tzemach Tzedek (Kehot, 5716).

## 4958

B"H, 19 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter from Motzaei Shabbos.

At an auspicious time, I will mention you at the holy resting place of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, for the fulfillment of your heart’s desires for the good. It is surely unnecessary to remind you that in addition to keeping the three daily study portions—Chumash, Tehillim, and Tanya—it is essential that you have a fixed time for the study of Chassidus every single day, for at least a few moments, and on Shabbos and Yomim Tovim, which are days of special spiritual potential, for a suitable amount of time. Through this, you will draw from the "Tree of Life," where there are no difficulties, into your own personal affairs as well, enabling you to be among those who serve in the armies of Hashem in the war of Torah—specifically the inner dimension of Torah, which is the study and dissemination of Chassidus wherever your reach extends. However, in order to hasten the drawing down of blessings below, there must be the act of Tzedakah, as explained in Iggeres HaKodesh and in many other places. In your case, this also means influencing your friends, may they live, to conduct themselves in the same manner; and there is nothing that can stand before the will.

With blessings for Chag HaGeulah,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

## 4959

4959

B"H, 21 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

...On one hand, I was pleased by your description of the activities that have taken place in recent weeks, but on the other hand, I was pained to read that for some time you did not cooperate due to differences of opinion and did not want to get involved, etc., etc. For what good will all the reasons and explanations do (even if they are entirely justified) to illuminate the darkness that is outside, and which, unfortunately, has also penetrated within? Even the "inside"—and even the inside of the shul and the beis medrash—is in need of healing (see the *hemshech* *V'Kacha*, section 51 and onwards), for rectification and improvement. Today, the focus must be on action, not on talk, debate, and negotiation, which only serve to justify inaction and increase the slumber of the individual, and consequently, the slumber of the galus as well.

May it be the will of Hashem that these days of redemption and miracles awaken each and every one of us, and as the saying of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, goes: one must **hear** what the little lights are telling us. May this lead to actual deeds to illuminate the entrance of one's home (until it also illuminates) the outside, in a manner of *mosif v'holech*—increasing and continuing.

#### ***4959***

Printed in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 15, p. 538.

**And as the saying:** See also above, letter 4939, and the references in the footnotes there.

## 4860

4860

B”H, 21 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of Monday, in which you write about the matters that are troubling you and preventing you from studying Torah as you should, as well as the distractions during davening.

It is appropriate that you have your tefillin checked. Furthermore, every weekday before morning davening, you should set aside a few coins for tzedakah. After davening, including on Shabbos and Yom Tov, you should recite the daily portion of Tehillim, as the Tehillim is divided for the days of the month.

You should contemplate and review until you remember well the content of the matters explained in many places regarding the concept of Hashgacha Pratis. It is exactly as it sounds: the Creator of the world and its Guide oversees each and every person individually in all their affairs. From this, it is understood that you should not be worried about the matters you wrote about. It goes without saying that there is no place for the desire—as you phrased it in your letter—for the opposite of life, chas v’shalom.

You should contemplate the fact that the neshamah of every Jew is hewn from beneath the Throne of Glory and descended below, clothing itself in a physical body (which is a tremendous descent, for there is no comparison between the neshamah as it is Above and the body as it is below). All of this is worthwhile specifically for the actions of a Jewish person upon the earth—namely, the study of Torah and the performance of mitzvos. From this, it is understood that every single action (for example, tefillin) is a matter of immeasurable loftiness, and the same applies to all matters of Torah and mitzvos (for the entire Torah is compared to tefillin).

Contemplating these matters and the like will ground you in the reality of the situation: that you have been given a beautiful portion to fulfill the mission of the Ribono shel Olam in this world through a lifestyle consistent with the instruction of our Torah, Toras Chaim. You should engage in these matters with joy, as explained in the Rambam at the end of Hilchos Lulav; see also Rambam, Hilchos Deios, beginning of Chapter 2, at the end.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

**Secretary**

## 4861

B"H, 21 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and upright, whose work is the work of Heaven,
R' Tzvi Leib Halevi, may he live and be well,

Peace and blessing!

...Since a school for *sofrus* is a correct and even necessary thing, and as the shortage of kosher *tefillin* is well-known in every country, there are certainly ways to establish and develop such a school. However, one must strive to ensure that things are done with the greatest possible *hiddur*, etc. Therefore, it is worthwhile that this be realized through *anash* and Chabad institutions. One must invest the effort and exertion that these matters require, for they are worthwhile in proportion to the magnitude of the matter, and there is no need to elaborate on a simple matter...

Awaiting good news regarding all of the above.

#### ***4861***

**R' Tzvi Leib, may he live:** Levin, Paris. Another letter to him appears above, 3793. See there for the context of the letter before us. See also above, letter 4873. Below, 5144.

## 4862

4862

B"H, 22 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

It was a pleasure to receive your regards through the G-d-fearing, esteemed, and industrious individual, [Name]... However, I was distressed to learn during our conversation that you apparently do not look after your health at all, and are acting contrary to the instructions of the doctors in this regard. It is certainly unnecessary to elaborate on the attitude one must have toward the instructions of doctors; this is brought in many places in Shulchan Aruch, Orach Chaim, in the laws of Shabbos and Yom Kippur and the like, and it is also brought in the laws concerning bodily and spiritual harm and their legal status. The central point is as the Sages said: a person has no authority over his own body at all to cause it pain, etc., for the body is the property of the Ribono shel Olam—a wondrous expression that, with even a slight amount of contemplation, is staggering to the mind. May it be the will of Hashem that this statement of our Sages, which is so rich in quality, will have its effect in ensuring caution regarding the commandment, "And you shall guard yourselves very carefully." Without a doubt, through this, there will also be an increase in your communal activities and in matters of Torah and mitzvos in general. As is known, the saying of the Maggid of Mezeritch—whose yahrtzeit was a few days ago—"A small hole in the body is a large hole in the neshamah."

Enclosed is what has been published recently. Surely you will merit to bring merit to the many, and the merit of the many depends on you.

With blessings for good tidings and for bright and radiant days of Chanukah.

P.S.
Thank you for your attention in delivering the map through Mr. [Name]... Since the aforementioned did not know the details regarding this, and in accordance with the statement of our Sages (Chullin 44b), I have delivered it to the administration of Tomchei Temimim, for a Shabbos meal in public is a mitzvah of the many.

#### ***4862***

**Note:** See also above, Vol. 7, Letter 2255, and the references in the footnotes there.

## 4862*

B"H, 22 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings,

In response to your letter, in which you write that you do not yet know how to learn, and you ask whether, at your age, you can be productive in Torah study.

It is well known how many statements of our Sages emphasize that a person must always study Torah, regardless of their age. If you study with persistence and diligence in the yeshiva, it is certain that you will see progress in your learning.

It is appropriate that on every weekday, before the morning davening, you should give something to tzedakah. Furthermore, after davening every day—even on Shabbos and Yom Tov—you should recite chapters of Tehillim. Through all of this, your success will increase in the aforementioned matters as well, and your journey to Marrakesh will be successful, leading to a good arrangement for you, both materially and spiritually.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,**

**Secretary**

## 4863

4863

B"H, 22 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi and Chossid, G-d-fearing and learned, R' Eliezer, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from the 24th of Mar-Cheshvan... Regarding your concluding remarks concerning the question of ships traveling on Shabbos, the question of electricity, and the like, in Eretz Yisrael, may it be built and established:

My involvement in the question of the ships was because it is a question pertaining to the United States, and New York in particular. Therefore, I did as much as I could—or so it seems to me—to rectify the situation here. It is understood that this was also dependent on the other end, namely, the port of departure for the ships, which is why I was compelled to write to Eretz Yisrael, may it be built and established, regarding this as well. This is unlike the question of electricity, etc., which is a question for Eretz Yisrael, may it be built and established, especially since the differences of opinion among the Rabbis of Eretz Yisrael, may it be built and established, along with their reasons and arguments, are already well-known and publicized.

#### ***4863***

**R' Eliezer:** Krasik, Tel Aviv. Additional letters to him—see above, Vol. 13, 4307, and the references in the notes there.

**Ships on Shabbos:** See above, letter 4796, and the references in the notes there.

**Question of electricity:** See also below, letter 4867.

## 4864

B"H, 22 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 17 Kislev and the one preceding it.

It is puzzling that you ask for my opinion on what you should do and how you should conduct yourself, at a time when I have already written my opinion to you on this matter several times, which remains fully valid even now.

Regarding what you write—that you are "forced," etc.—it is understood that this is **not true at all**. No Jewish person is ever forced, Heaven forbid, to act against the will of the Creator, may He be blessed. Furthermore, it should be noted that there is no **benefit** in this trial, for as long as the soul does not fulfill the task commanded to it by the Giver of the Torah, it returns in *gilgul* after *gilgul* to this world. Moreover, when one has caused further damage, the *gilgul* is even more difficult. There is no need to dwell at length on such a distressing matter. It is also clear that all the reasons and excuses you write are arguments from the *sitra achra* that have **no basis in reality**. May it be the will of Hashem that you recognize the truth, for it is good for you, both in spiritual and material matters.

With blessings for good tidings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

#### 4864

**That you are "forced":** To put an end to your life, Heaven forbid. See also letter 4860 above.

*) See the *drush* of the Maharichu in *Pri Etz Chaim*, before *Shaar Rosh Hashanah*. See also the *Hagahos Shemen Sasson* on *Shaar HaKavanos*, *Drush Chanukah*, section 2.

## 4864

4864*

B"H, 22 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 17 Kislev and the one preceding it.

Regarding what you wrote about the *himshech* (attraction/influence) that you are feeling, etc., the advised counsel in such matters is already well-known: namely, a forceful diversion of thought, avoiding meetings with the person in question as much as possible (it is understood that extremes in this are not desirable at all), and living a family life in the normal manner (without stringencies that have no basis, or a state of withdrawal that leads to the opposite results). From the above, it is understood that traveling away from home for a period of time is not only not a remedy, but on the contrary, and *kal v'chomer*...

I am certain you will report on how you utilized the days of 19 Kislev and the upcoming Chanukah as required, and may the reports be detailed, and may it be G-d’s will that they also be good.

With blessings for good tidings.

## 4965

4965

B"H, 23 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, G-d-fearing, man of many accomplishments, and possessor of fine character traits,
R' Moshe Gershon, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

I received your letter via the esteemed Rabbis and Chassidim, the shluchim who are engaged in the needs of the community, may they live, who visited your camp. As you requested, I will mention you and all those you wrote about at the holy resting place of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

Since the days of Chanukah are fast approaching, and the opinions are well known as to whether they correspond to the Sefirah of Netzach or the Sefirah of Hod—and in either case, they are the "legs of truth" that are external to the body, yet they possess a unique quality, as is known from our Sages, of blessed memory, that in the time to come all the festivals will be nullified except for Chanukah and Purim (cited in Sefer HaChaim, Hilchos Parnassah, beginning of Chapter 7; see also Pirkei D'Rabbi Eliezer, Chapter 46; Yerushalmi Taanis 2:13; the Yotzer for Zachor ; the additions to Torah Or on Megillas Esther, and elsewhere)—all this is in the manner of the quality of Ohr Chozer, which is explained in many places.

May it be the will of Hashem that this quality will illuminate our generation as well—this orphaned generation, the generation of the footsteps of Moshiach at its very end—with a light that increases and continues from the time the sun sets, until it brings about that "the night shall shine as the day," with the coming of the Son of the Fallen [Moshiach] speedily in our days.

With respect and with a blessing for a bright and illuminating Chanukah.

#### 4965

**R' Moshe Gershon:** Goldzweig, Chicago.

 The piyut "Elokim Al Dami" at its conclusion.
 [Translator's note: The source text refers to the liturgical poem recited on Shabbos Zachor.]

## 4866

4866

B"H, 23 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, G-d-fearing, learned, and diligent in communal needs, R' Meir, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter dated the bright day of 19 Kislev, the festival of our redemption and the liberation of our soul through the redemption and liberation of our Alter Rebbe. The publication you sent was also received, and I thank you for your thoughtfulness.

Regarding your observation concerning the Nusach HaAri and the Nusach of our Alter Rebbe, where we say *u'morid ha'gashem* with two *segols*, and similarly the *takanas Shabbos* with a *kuf*—please refer to the *Shaar HaKolel* on the Siddur of my grandfather, the Rav R' Avraham Dovid Lavut.

Enclosed is the latest material I have published. Surely you will merit to bring merit to the many through it, and the merit of the many depends upon you.

The *gashem* with a *gimel* and a *segol* is also mentioned in the seforim *Meorei Ohr*, *Zichron Yehuda* (cited in *Likutei Maharich*), *Darchei Chaim V'Shalom* (by the Gaon and Tzaddik of Munkatch), and others.

With blessings,

#### ***4866***

**R' Meir:** Schwartzman, Winnipeg.

## 4867

B"H, 24 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter from Motzaei Shabbos—after the longest of breaks.

a) Regarding what you wrote about electric light on Shabbos in Eretz Yisrael (may it be rebuilt and established), as well as the question of opening canned goods on Shabbos—this has already been debated in Torah journals in recent years, and I believe also in those published in Eretz Yisrael (may it be rebuilt and established). The various opinions and customs among different circles in Eretz Yisrael (may it be rebuilt and established) are well known.

b) Regarding your note that you read in a letter to someone concerning a mitzvah that cannot be performed by others, which overrides Torah study, as per the ruling of the Gemara in Moed Katan, and you quote what you saw in a book in the name of the Alter Rebbe.

It is surprising that you are quoting this from a book, given that the Alter Rebbe’s Hilchos Talmud Torah has been printed many times. It seems that the words of the book you are quoting are based on what is written there in Chapter 3, Kuntres Acharon, s.v. *Ve’hinei chiluk*. What is even more surprising is that you apparently interpret the book’s intention as explaining the words of the Alter Rebbe to mean that the mitzvah of knowing Torah overrides a positive mitzvah even when it is impossible to have it performed by others. I do not know how to discuss this, for if that were the case, one would be exempt from putting on tefillin, building a sukkah, taking the lulav, etc.—which is the opposite of the explicit Gemara, both Bavli and Yerushalmi, and the established practice throughout the generations, etc., etc. The words of the Alter Rebbe are understood based on what he writes there at the beginning of Chapter 3 in the main text, regarding the distinction between an interruption for a short time versus an interruption entirely, and other details on this matter; this is not the place to elaborate.

c) Regarding what you wrote about the reasons why you stopped studying our Torah, the Torah of Life: how pathetic is this generation of ours, that people interpret matters in a way that is the opposite of their intended meaning. If at all times the study of the inner dimension of Torah is essential, how much more so in a situation involving the concerns about which you write. The argument of distance, etc., is no obstacle to studying in one’s own place, and this is in addition to the assumption that you surely were not locked in your homes for the entire week, unable to go outside for any matter whatsoever. If you went out for optional matters, then all the more so for matters of Torah, the "light of the Torah," and this is obvious.

With blessings for good tidings, and may it be the will that it be in the revealed, true good, in which the "other side" has no hold, for they are connected to the "Tree of Life," etc., as explained in the Iggeres HaKodesh of the Alter Rebbe, Epistle 26.

## 4868

4868

B"H, 24 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, G-d-fearing, and diligent in communal needs,
R' Dober Yehuda, may he live,

Greetings and blessings!

Following a long interval, I received your letter of 22 Kislev. I read with pleasure about the farbrengen held in connection with the bright day of the Chag HaGeulah, 19 Kislev, and that the N'shei Chabad also gathered the following day for a lecture on the themes of the day, etc.

Surely, all of this will lead to practical action, as is well known from the promise of our Torah, the Torah of life: "Great is study, for it leads to action." This certainty is also evident from several halachos in the laws of Talmud Torah. The fact that the letter was received on the eve of Chanukah is a positive sign, for the work of "spreading the wellsprings outward" must be "increasing and adding" from day to day. This is especially true regarding the addition of "pure oil"—specifically the part of the Torah called the *ilana d'chayei* (tree of life), in which there is no impurity, etc. (see *Iggeres HaKodesh* by our Rebbe the Alter Rebbe, Epistle 26). It is already known in many places that the Torah is compared to water, bread, wine, and oil; within the parts of the Torah itself, water and bread represent the *nigleh* (revealed) of Torah, while wine and oil represent the *razin* (secrets) and *razin d'razin* (secrets of secrets).

May it be the will of Hashem that through this dissemination and increase, we will merit, very soon, the fulfillment of the prophecy, "The night shall shine like the day," with the coming of Mashiach Tzidkeinu, who is one of the "eight princes of man," as explained in *Torah Or* by our Rebbe the Alter Rebbe (33d, 34c).

With blessings.

#### ***4868***

**R' Dober Yehuda:** Schochet, Toronto. Additional letters to him—see above, Vol. 13, 4317, and the references in the notes there.

## 4969

B"H, 24 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, G-d-fearing, learned, and distinguished, R' Zev Yechiel, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

A few days ago, your letter regarding the sending of the sefer *Chidushei Sh"Y* was received, and today the sefer itself arrived. Many thanks for this. Although time does not permit, I have nevertheless responded to your suggestion with a few notes in Hebrew, written in haste, which are transcribed in the margins.

As we enter the Festival of Lights, these days of Chanukah, may it be the will of Hashem that you increase and continue in the "candle of a mitzvah and the Torah of light," and the "light of Torah"—which is the inner dimension of Torah that in our generation has been revealed and explained in the teachings of Chassidus. May this illuminate the darkness of the galus, which is compared to night, until the prophecy is fulfilled: "And the night shall shine as the day" in the true and complete Geulah through Moshiach Tzidkeinu, "from eight princes of man" (see *Torah Or* by our Rebbe, the author of the *Tanya* and *Shulchan Aruch*, 33d, 34c).

With blessing.

**To Section 1:** Note the *Rambam*, *Hilchos Nedarim*, Chapter 11, end of Halacha 4; ibid., *Hilchos Shegagos*, Chapter 9, end of Halacha 3; *Shulchan Aruch*, *Orach Chaim*, end of Section 349, and the sources cited there. See *Sdei Chemed*, *Klallim*, letter *Kuf*, subsection 52, and the sources cited there.

**To Section 2:** See *Sdei Chemed*, *Asifat Dinim*, *Ma'areches Yom HaKippurim*, Section 1, subsection 10, and the sources cited there.

**To Section 15:** There is a discussion on this in the sefer *HaMoadim BeHalacha*, *Shavuos*, Section 2 (p. 317).

**To Section 23:** See *Tzafnas Paneach* (by the Rogatchover Gaon) on *Hilchos Sukkah*, Chapter 6, Halacha 2; *Oneg Yom Tov*, Section 49. And further, *HaMoadim BeHalacha*, p. 100.

**End of the sefer:** Six or seven *batei avos* in a *mishmarah*. See also *Tosefta Ta'anis*, Chapter 2, and the commentaries there; the commentaries on the *Shas* to *Ta'anis* 16b and onward; *Responsa Chasam Sofer*, *Orach Chaim*, Section 200.

***

**4969**

**R' Zev Yechiel:** Landau, Montreal.

**Chidushei Sh"Y:** By R' Shmuel Yitzchak Landau, Av Beis Din of Ciechanów.

## 4970

B"H, 24 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished individual, whose work is the work of Heaven,
R' Yosef, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

I have received your letter of 22 Kislev along with the enclosure, and you will find enclosed a temporary receipt in accordance with your instructions.

It gave me pleasure to read in your letter that although the matter is difficult for you at this time, you nevertheless did not wish to deviate from what we discussed. This is especially true in light of the well-known saying of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, that when a person below decides to give an amount to tzedakah that is beyond his current means, at that very moment, new channels are opened from Above through which he will receive additional influence so that he may fulfill his promise. This is particularly true based on the well-known exposition of our Sages on the verse "Aser te'aser" (tithe you shall tithe) — "Aser bishvil shetisasher" (tithe so that you may become wealthy), and it has already been said, "And test Me now with this, says Hashem Tzeva-os."

Regarding what you wrote about the vacant space in the cemetery upon which there is half of a double matzeivah, and that if someone wishes to purchase this vacant space, the matzeivah would need to be removed, etc.:

In matters such as these, one must be extremely cautious, as it touches upon the honor of those who reside in the World of Truth, and the prohibition is stringent. Therefore, it is not advisable to enter into this doubt, even if one were to say that there is no actual prohibition involved—a matter which itself is open to debate. May he and his wife, may they live and be well, enjoy long and good years, and may they increase their efforts in the field of educating the children of Israel in the path of our Torah, the Torah of life, which was given by the Living G-d, the Life-giver of life. May it be His will that this increase be in accordance with the lesson of the days of Chanukah: to increase and continue from day to day.

With the blessing of a bright and illuminating Chanukah.

## 4971

B"H, 24 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, refined, and energetic man of many accomplishments,
R' Dovid Nosson, may he live,

Greetings and blessings!

The wax candles for lighting the Chanukah candles have been received; thank you very much. May it be the will of Hashem that, in accordance with the law of Chanukah, each and every one of us will continue to increase and ascend in holiness until we reach the "outside"—specifically through the dissemination of the wellsprings outward—until "the night shines like the day" with the coming of our righteous Moshiach, speedily in our days, who is from the "eight princes of man" (see Torah Or 33d, 34c).

With blessings for a Chanukah that illuminates and continues to increase.

P.S.
As is appropriate to the passage in the holy Zohar, Vol. II, 128, regarding the concept of *machaa* (protest).

***

4971

R' Dovid Nosson: Lesser, Brooklyn. Additional letters to him: see above, Vol. XIII, 4387, and the references in the notes there.

## 4992

B"H, 24 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter—which does not indicate the time it was written—in which you ask for a *tikkun* regarding the well-known matter.

The advised counsel for this is known: to divert one's thoughts as much as possible, not only from the matter itself but even from the *tikkun* for it. Rather, one should have memorized several chapters of Mishnayos, or at the very least one or two chapters of the *Tanya Kadisha*, in addition to chapters of *Tehillim*. When walking in the street or in similar situations, one should at least repeat them in one's thoughts. Furthermore, one should give a few coins to *tzedakah* every weekday before the morning *tefillah*, and recite the monthly *Tehillim* *shiur* after *tefillah* each day.

[Also,] to influence the youth to bring them closer to our Torah—the Torah of life—and its *mitzvos*.

It goes without saying that all of this is in addition to the main requirement: regret for the past and a firm resolution for the future.

Through the conduct mentioned above, the situation will gradually improve, going from strength to strength. May it be Hashem’s will that you bring good tidings regarding all of the above.

## 4973

4973

B"H, 25 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

The Administration of Tzeirei Agudas Chabad
Branch of the Holy City of Jerusalem, may it be rebuilt and established
May Hashem keep them alive

Peace and Blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter dated 17 Kislev, as well as your second letter of the same date...

There is now an additional demand upon every one of the Bnei Yisrael, especially upon Anash, and even more so upon the young: to engage in activities that are appropriate to the demands of the hour and are made possible by Divine Providence. We see clearly that precisely now, one must act to raise the banner of Yisrael Sabba, for His Torah and His mitzvos, to the extent of permeating wide circles with the teachings of Chassidus and its guidance. Every moment and hour that passes is a staggering loss; who knows if such an opportunity will arise again? Regarding concrete actions and the utilization of the aforementioned possibilities, for the time being, I do not find this in your current letter nor in the previous one (even though they do contain information regarding resolutions and certain activities in the past, which, to our regret, were in insufficient dimensions).

It is obvious that my intention is not to preach, but to awaken you to the beautiful portion that Divine Providence has placed upon you, and that you should utilize these possibilities to their fullest. Great good is hidden within it—spiritual and material good, and good that is visible and revealed. The matter depends on nothing but your will, for the strengths and the possibilities have been given from His holy, open, and wide hand.

May it be the will [of Hashem] that these days of Chanukah, which are [days of] *d'oraisa* and *d'rabbanan*, of "decreasing and increasing"—decreasing in the eyes of the *sitra achra* and increasing in matters of holiness—will add success to the aforementioned avodah. May each and every one of the Tzeirei Agudas Chabad, and all of them together, through their avodah in holiness, cause a decrease from day to day in the matters of the *sitra achra* throughout the entire environment, the entire city, etc., and an increase throughout the entire environment and the entire city in matters of holiness and light: "a mitzvah is a candle and Torah is light," and the "light of Torah"—this is the inner dimension of Torah, the teachings of Chassidus, its guidance, and its customs.

The dispatch was delayed, and meanwhile, your letter of 22 Kislev was received regarding the farbrengens of Yud-Tes Kislev, our redemption and the redemption of his soul. May it be the will [of Hashem] that from this Rosh Hashanah for Chassidus and its affairs, there will be a continued increase of successful activities throughout the entire year and in all areas. For all of them are directed toward the central point—the spreading of the wellsprings outward—and to its peak: the coming of Moshiach Tzidkeinu, speedily in our days, literally.

With blessings for success and for good tidings regarding the above.

## 4974

4974

B”H, 25 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and learned, involved in communal needs,
R’ Schneur Zalman, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter (as well as the general Pidyon Nefesh that preceded it). It is good news that all of anash are whole and healthy and have returned to their homes in peace. I assume you also refer to those who sent me a telegram (it is strange that since the telegram, no word has been received from the senders that they have returned to their homes), and it is already well-known how much our holy Nasi cried out against such strange conduct.

Surely you utilized the days of 10 and 19 Kislev, and will likewise utilize the days of Chanukah, as much as is necessary to strengthen the affairs of Kfar Chabad and the matters of Chassidus in general. May it be the will of Hashem that this be with practical success, in a way that is good, visible, and revealed.

With blessings for a Chanukah that illuminates and shines upon the entire year, and with regards to all of anash, may they live, who are in Kfar Chabad, for there Hashem has commanded the blessing, life forevermore.

#### ***4974***

**R’ Schneur Zalman:** Gerlik, Kfar Chabad. Additional letters to him appear above in 4797 and in the references noted in the footnotes there.

**And have returned to their homes:** From their mobilization for the "Sinai Campaign." See above, letter 4890, and the references noted in the footnotes there.

**Our holy Nasi cried out:** See above, letter 4804, and the references noted in the footnotes there.

## 4975

[25 Kislev, 5717]

Greetings and blessings!

...Regarding your inquiry: At a wedding feast, as is the custom of the land, the guests are seated in separate groups (several of which consist of more than ten people), each at its own table, with waiters serving all the tables (thereby connecting them). As the meal drags on, several of the groups recite Grace After Meals on their own, without waiting for the Grace After Meals at the main table where the chosson and kallah are seated, and without reciting the seven blessings (*sheva brachos*) or the *she-hasimcha bime'ono*, etc.

Is this conduct appropriate, to act in this manner and exempt oneself from the *sheva brachos*, etc.?

It seems the reason for this custom is: a) Not everyone is able to prolong the meal (due to preoccupations, health, valuing their time, etc., and especially since the delay is sometimes caused by undesirable matters). b) Reciting *sheva brachos* before they are recited at the main table by the chosson and kallah would arouse the resentment of the parents of the bride and groom.

Based on this, changing the custom would have the following results: a) If everyone were to wait until the end of the meal, it would cause many to refrain from participating in the wedding feast altogether, thereby diminishing the joy of the chosson and kallah, in addition to the resentment in the hearts of the parents toward those who do not participate, as is evident in practice. b) If they were to recite *sheva brachos* at the aforementioned tables with two cups of wine, as is proper, there is a concern for the aforementioned resentment, and those who would fear that resentment would not attend in the first place.

Since in both scenarios a mitzvah is involved, and since the custom has become widespread and no one protests, it is a mitzvah to find a justification for the custom. It can be said that it is based on three points: a) *Shulchan Aruch*, Orach Chaim 193, end of section 1: To avoid the resentment of the host, it is permitted for ten to divide into groups. b) Ibid., end of section 6: Even if that reason were not sufficient, since it has already become the custom to do so, it is as if they did not establish themselves together as one group (see *Shulchan Aruch*, Yoreh Deah, end of section 88, section 2). c) Based on what is written in *Shulchan Aruch*, Even HaEzer, section 62, in *Taz* subsection 7 and *Aruch HaShulchan* (subsection 138; also cited in your letter), that the obligation of *sheva brachos* depends on whether the eating is also for the sake of the joy of the chosson and kallah—it can be said that the intention of the guests beforehand, that if the meal is prolonged they will recite Grace without *sheva brachos*, constitutes a negation of their establishing themselves for the joy of the chosson and kallah; thus, the matter of *sheva brachos* does not apply from the outset. And this is clear.

## 4996

4996

B.H., 25 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

It is deeply distressing that in the reports I receive regarding the conduct at the Tomchei Temimim Yeshiva where you study—yeshivos which, in all their branches, were founded and are directed by our holy Rebbes—it is evident that some of the students, yourself included, lack adherence to the yeshiva’s schedule, lack punctuality in general, and lack discipline, etc.

Beyond the pain of the matter itself—for time is an irretrievable loss—the phenomenon of casting off the yoke is alarming.

It is well known and publicized that our Rebbes invested immense powers into these yeshivos—spiritual powers and their own personal essence. The foundation of every yeshiva is its students; this means that these powers were invested within the students and for their sake. The mashpi'im, the roshei yeshiva, and the administration are the intermediaries and conduits for drawing and bringing these powers from the source to the recipients, who are the students. Yet, there is a student who disregards all of this with indifference and wastes his time on other matters.

The words of the tzaddikim, the founders of the yeshiva, are eternal, and their actions endure and continue to exert their influence. It is the recipient who turns his back, Heaven forbid, and occupies himself with trivialities.

It is difficult to elaborate on a matter so alarming, especially since the concept of casting off the yoke has already been explained at the beginning of the sefer *Derech Chaim*. Anyone who hears what is stated there (and as is known, the emphasis of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, was on the word *der*—"the" yoke) will have the hair on his head stand on end.

For this reason, I have delayed writing about this until now. However, I am relying on the auspicious and favorable days of Chanukah, that there will be no negative impression, Heaven forbid, from these words, but rather that they will achieve their purpose: that from now on, you will see the situation as it truly is—that observing the yeshiva’s schedule, maintaining punctuality, discipline, and accepting the yoke, etc., is not doing a favor for anyone else. Rather, all of this is your own true benefit, both in spiritual matters and in material matters, in a revealed and manifest way.

Do not delude yourself with excuses—that according to your own intellect, which you consider superior, you can organize your time better than the yeshiva’s schedule, or that due to your high level, you do not need to accept the yoke of another or submit to discipline regarding others. For it is not about you, and you do not need this.

This is nothing but the counsel of the *yetzer*, and one of its ruses and deceptions to lead from casting off the yoke of the mashgiach, the rosh yeshiva, and the administration to casting off an even worse yoke, as the levels of this are explained in *Derech Chaim*; see there.

May it be the will of Hashem that, in accordance with the lesson of the days of Chanukah—which must be "decreasing and increasing"—that just as these and those are the words of the living G-d, according to what is explained in *Torah Or*, one should go from strength to strength in decreasing matters of the *sitra achra* (which include those mentioned in *Tanya*, Chapter 2), and go from strength to strength in matters of holiness. You should begin this conduct immediately: decreasing from day to day, literally, in undesirable matters and specifically in the details mentioned above, and increasing in matters of light—"a mitzvah is a candle, Torah is light," and the light of Torah, which is the inner dimension of Torah, the teachings of Chassidus, its guidance, and its customs.

May it be the will of Hashem that all those who have caused pain until now, as the advantage of light from darkness, through a conduct of *atahpacha* (transformation) from one extreme to the other, will cause much *nachas* and delight through their conduct and studies from now on. May Hashem grant you great success in this.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above, and soon.

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,**

**Secretary**

## 4977

B"H, 25 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and distinguished, involved in communal needs, R' Yehoshua 
Shalom u’Bracha!

After a silence, I was pleased to receive your letter from Monday, bringing good news of the farbrengen where those who fear Hashem spoke to one another. Surely, these words, which come from the heart, will have an impact on the days that follow, especially in light of what we have heard many times from our holy Rebbes: that the day of 19 Kislev is the Rosh Hashanah for the Torah of Chassidus and the ways of Chassidus. It is explained that the precision of the word "Rosh" (head) rather than "Techilah" (beginning) is significant; just as the head contains within it the vitality of all the limbs, and from it, channels of vitality are drawn to the limbs in their respective places, so too is it regarding Rosh Hashanah and the days of the year that follow. May it be the will of Hashem that you bring good news regarding both communal matters and your own personal affairs, with health and peace of mind.

Enclosed is what was sent to you recently. Surely you will merit to bring merit to the many, and the merit of the many is dependent upon you.

With blessings,

P.S.
The note enclosed in your letter will be read at an auspicious time at the holy resting place of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

#### ***4977***

 **R' Yehoshua:** Sheptman, London. Additional letters to him appear above in Vol. 12, 4219, and in the notes referenced there.

## 4978

B"H, 26 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

It was with pleasure that I read in the letter from your husband, shlita, regarding your continued involvement in the activities of N'shei U’Bnos Chabad, even though he adds that various worries regarding livelihood, debts, and the like are somewhat distracting.

It is surely unnecessary to explain at length that the aforementioned involvement is one of the remedies for mitigating these very worries. According to the instruction of our Torah, the Torah of life, "Whoever accepts upon himself the yoke of Torah, the yoke of worldly affairs is removed from him." Our Sages, of blessed memory, are precise in using the word **yoke**. That is to say, even when the matter seems difficult at times and does not seem so logical or understandable, a person must still perform it by way of *kabalas ol* (accepting the yoke). This is the advice for minimizing the yoke of worldly matters—the yoke of *derech eretz*.

It is understood that I do not mean that you should burden yourself with technical matters that can be done by others. However, in every matter there must be someone responsible—someone who takes an interest in ensuring that things are done in the most efficient manner. Not everyone is capable of bearing this responsibility, especially since it requires experience and familiarity with the work.

May it be G-d’s will that you succeed in all the aforementioned, to fulfill the intention of our holy Rebbes to permeate your entire environment with the light and vitality of Chassidus through the framework of N'shei U’Bnos Chabad, and even beyond. May your reward, in the measure of the Holy One, blessed be He—measure for measure—be the addition of light and vitality in the personal matters within the homes of each and every one of the members, and first and foremost, those who are involved and make the effort.

With blessings for good tidings, for an illuminating Chanukah, and regards to each and every one of the members.

## 4979

B"H, 26 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished R' Zev Wolf, may he live and be well,

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter from the night following the *Chag HaGeulah*, it is surprising that you make no mention of the *farbrengen* and the words of *hisorerus* that were surely heard, which would have led to good resolutions regarding practical *avodah* in the coming days.

Regarding what you wrote concerning the third birthday of your son, Yisrael, may he live and be well, which falls on *Rosh Chodesh Shevat*, and your initial thought to perform the *upsherin* on *Lag B'Omer*: I have already written my opinion to you and to several of *anash* that, unless there is a compelling reason—such as a firmly established and deeply rooted custom—it seems to me that one should not delay the *upsherin*, especially for several months or even several weeks. My reasoning is that since, as is evident from the language of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, regarding the *upsherin*, this is a matter of *chinuch* in *yiras Shamayim*—that is, drawing down *kedushah* upon the soul of the child [it is possible that the aforementioned is based on the well-known *Chazal* (*Tanchuma, Parshas Kedoshim*); see also the beginning of *Hilchos Talmud Torah* by the Alter Rebbe, where this is hinted at in the *Torah Shebiksav*: "And in the fourth year, all its fruit shall be holy"]—and when one performs an act in this regard, the matter is strengthened and drawn down into the world of *Asiyah*, as is explained in many places; therefore, why delay this drawing down of *kedushah* for several months or weeks? After all, every addition of *kedushah* in *chinuch* adds to all matters concerning the child, even in physical respects.

May *Hashem* grant you success, so that together with your wife, may she live and be well, you may raise all your children, may they live and be well, healthy and whole, for Torah, for the *chuppah*, and for good deeds.

With blessings for a Chanukah that illuminates and continues to increase.

## 4880

B”H, 27 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

...After asking for his forgiveness, I would like to express my wish that his level of *bitachon* in the Creator of the world and its Guide will increase. Consequently, he will not be overly meticulous or precise in calculating every detail of every proposal (which is, in any case, entirely impossible, for no person knows what is in the heart of his fellow—to such an extent that this lack of knowledge is equated to the lack of knowledge of the *Keitz*, as is evident also in *Iggeres HaKodesh* by our *Alter Rebbe*, Epistle 22). By doing so, the finding of his proper *zivug* will be hastened, both in *gashmius* and in *ruchnius*.

#### ***4880***

Published in *Teshuvos U’Biurim* (5747 edition), p. 430.

## 4981

4981

B"H, 28 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, G-d-fearing, whose work is work of Heaven,
Moreinu HaRav Tzvi Hirsch, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 22 Kislev, in which you write regarding the farbrengen in connection with the bright day of 19 Kislev, the day of our redemption and the salvation of our soul: there is no basis for your distress over having invited a wider circle of people. On the contrary, you did well. The closer circles should be invited on other auspicious days and even on Shabbos, whereas those who, for the time being, have only a very slight connection to the matters of Chassidus—if not on days like 19 Kislev and the like, then when?

Regarding what you wrote, that one person let slip words concerning other Chassidim to those who are not Chassidim: it is surprising that you did not emphasize what is stated in Tanya, Chapter 32. This is the "Written Torah" of the Torah of Chassidus, as explained in the letters of my father-in-law, the Rebbe. Even the opponents of Chassidus (due to a lack of knowledge of the matters) would testify to this; on the contrary, they complain about why we draw even the distant ones close, etc.

With blessings for good tidings in both personal and general matters.

#### 4981

**Moreinu HaRav Tzvi Hirsch, may he live:** Tzaler, Basel. Additional letters to him—above, Vol. 9, 2851.

And in the notes indicated there. Below, 5103.

For its content, see also above, letter 4970.

## 4982

4982

B”H, 28 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of Thursday, Parshas Mikeitz, in which you write that due to the changing times, you might abandon the idea of purchasing a house.

In my opinion, the changing times do not affect you at all, even if we were to assume that there is a change in the times. In my view, this change is that we are drawing closer to the coming of Moshiach Tzidkeinu; consequently, we must involve ourselves more in matters of Torah and mitzvos in general, and in the dissemination of the teachings of Chassidus, its guidance, and its customs in particular. All other matters should be conducted as they were in their established order, and the Guardian of Yisrael, who neither slumbers nor sleeps, watches over them with Divine Providence, and will protect them wherever they may be. May He soon redeem us from exile with the true and complete redemption through Moshiach Tzidkeinu.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe Shlita**

**Secretary**

#### 4982

For the content, see letter 4972 above.

## 4983

4983

B"H, 28 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and industrious, R' Yehoshua, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

I was pleased to receive your letter of 21 Kislev, in which you write—albeit very briefly—about the farbrengen and its success. Surely your intention is also in accordance with the teaching of the Mishnah: "The study is not the primary thing, but the action is." That is to say, the words of inspiration and the resolutions should lead to actions regarding the actual work in the days that follow. As we see tangibly, this requires reminding and encouragement from time to time, especially during auspicious days and times of favor.

I have already said to many of anash that we have merited reaching a point where there is no longer opposition to the dissemination of the wellsprings. Therefore, it is a great pity to miss any opportunity to utilize this area. Furthermore, even those who state that they are opposed, and even those who take some actions in that direction, do so only out of an alarming lack of knowledge, which a simple explanation in a pleasant manner would dispel. This is clearly evident to all those who contemplate it for themselves. Surely you are also utilizing the days of Chanukah for this, as the core point of these days is to increase and continue to illuminate the darkness, even at the entrance of one’s house from the outside.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above, and with a blessing of success to all those participating in these most exalted activities.

#### ***4983***

**R' Yehoshua, may he live:** Olbaum, Jerusalem. Additional letters to him appear below: 5021, 5162.

## 4884

B"H, 29 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn

To the G-d-fearing, honorable, and distinguished, who engages in communal needs, a man of many accomplishments,
R' Moshe Zev, may he live,

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from 18 Kislev, along with the *pidyon nefesh* enclosed therein, which will be read at an auspicious time at the holy *tziyun* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. I trust that you have already returned home in peace, and that you utilized your visit to the places you were in to strengthen and disseminate Yiddishkeit there as well. This includes the matters of *pnimiyus haTorah*—which is the Torah of Chassidus, its guidance, and its conduct. There is a unique merit in places such as these, where one encounters people from various locations; when one influences them to make a fundamental change for the good, this change reaches the very places to which those people return.

The days of the month of Kislev—specifically 10 and 19 Kislev, and the days of Chanukah—are especially conducive to such influence. May it be the will of Hashem that you engage in this in accordance with the instruction of these days of Chanukah: that one must be "increasing and adding" in the light that comes from pure oil, until it illuminates the entrance of one’s house from the outside after the sun has set. As the Sages state, "until the foot of the Tarmodites has ceased" (*Shabbos* 21b). This is based on the allusion found in the *Shas* regarding the people of Tarmod (*Yevamos* 17a; *Bereishis Rabbah*, end of ch. 56): "Tarmod" (*תרמוד*) consists of the same letters as "rebellious" (*מורדת*). It is in the hands of the Jewish person to extinguish and completely remove this spirit through the dissemination of the light of Torah, and the "Light" within the Torah—which is *pnimiyus haTorah*.

With blessings for success in the above.

***

## 4985

4985

B”H, 29 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and learned, engaged in communal needs, R’ Shlomo Zalman, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letters and their enclosures. Although you do not mention it, you will surely find an opportunity to convey at least the key points from the farbrengen in which we participated together. For the purpose of every farbrengen is that it should make an impression regarding practical matters—and not only for the participants themselves, but that through them, it should reach those who did not attend. This is especially true when the subject concerns someone who has influence within a certain circle that is steadily expanding, and the rest is understood. And there is nothing that can stand before the will.

The *pidyon nefesh* enclosed in your letter was read today at the holy resting place of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. May it be the will of Hashem that he brings good tidings regarding its contents. I also mentioned the esteemed... may he live.

Regarding his question—the aforementioned individual’s—about a remedy for his temper, which presumably refers to the trait of anger: it is appropriate that he study the twenty-fifth chapter of *Iggeres HaKodesh* by our Alter Rebbe until he understands the concepts well. When he feels himself beginning to become agitated, he should review the content of those matters in his thoughts.

With blessings for good tidings in your personal matters and in general matters.

#### ***4985***

**R’ Shlomo Zalman:** Hecht, Chicago. Additional letters to him—above 4765, and in the references noted there.

## 4886

B"H, 29 Kislev, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and distinguished man of many accomplishments, R' Yehoshua Shneur Zalman, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter dated 18 Kislev. Regarding the study curriculum and so forth, the matter has been referred to the Merkos L'Inyonei Chinuch, and they will correspond with you regarding this in accordance with the procedures here.

Regarding what you wrote concerning a constitution for the Yeshiva: It is understood that, in general, it is better not to bind oneself. On the other hand, there are constitutions where the manner of binding is comprehensive. A copy of the bylaws used here will be sent to you; if there is no alternative, you may use them, and if it is possible to shorten them even further, that would be better. It is understood that the main point requiring caution is the selection of directors and the method of electing new directors from time to time. It is also understood that it is essential to establish that there are matters that are unchangeable—in the essence of the bylaws—regarding the spiritual aspects of the matters and the like.

Surely you utilized the days of 10 and 19 Kislev and Chanukah for the dissemination of the wellsprings in general, and for the development of the institutions in particular. Please share good and detailed news regarding this. May Hashem grant you success in all the above, with proper health, true joy, and abundant success.

With blessing and regards to all Anash, may they live.

#### ***4886***

Printed in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 21, p. 426, and completed based on the secretariat's copy.

**R' Yehoshua Shneur Zalman, may he live:** Serbranski, Melbourne. Additional letters to him—see above, 4876, and the references in the notes there.

## 4887

4887

B"H, Chanukah, 5717
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To those gathered at the farbrengen of the auspicious day of our redemption and the liberation of our souls, Yud-Tes Kislev, and foremost among them, the esteemed and G-d-fearing, engaged in communal needs, etc., Moreinu Harav Shneur Zalman Shei Bezpalov and Moreinu Harav Shmuel Shei Shifrin, may Hashem grant them life.

Peace and Blessing!

I received with pleasure the telegram of blessing from the participants and those gathered at the farbrengen of the auspicious day of Yud-Tes Kislev in your camp. May it be the will of Hashem that this farbrengen serve as a good beginning for the dissemination of the wellsprings of Torah and the inner dimension of Torah, and for the fulfillment of mitzvos with *dchilu* and *rechimu* (awe and love) as they are illuminated by the light of the teachings of Chassidus, to the widest circles of our brethren, the Children of Israel, in your camp.

There are several fundamental concepts regarding Chanukah, among them: that the miracle of Chanukah occurred specifically through pure oil; that the lighting of the candles must follow the order of "increasing and continuing" (*mosif v'holech*); and that their light must be placed at the entrance of one’s house, on the outside, to illuminate the darkness of the night.

All these matters apply to the dissemination of the inner dimension of Torah—the teachings of Chassidus, the "light of Torah"—which infuses light and vitality into the revealed dimension of Torah and the mitzvos. As is explained in several books, Torah is compared to water, wine, and oil: water represents the revealed Torah, while wine and oil represent the inner dimension of Torah.

In these days in particular, when "darkness covers the earth," a double and redoubled darkness, it is only through the "pure oil"—the inner dimension and the light of Torah—that one can illuminate the darkness of the night. Each person must begin in his own home and from there spread it among those who are still "outside," to the widest possible circles, in a manner of "love your fellow as yourself," as explained in Tanya, Chapter 32, and in the spirit of "increasing and continuing," as is the case with all matters of holiness, where we must always ascend in holiness.

According to the well-known statement of our Sages that "all beginnings are difficult," since the beginning has already been made in this direction—especially on the auspicious day, the day of redemption of Yud-Tes Kislev, which is called the Rosh Hashanah of Chassidus—it is my hope and blessing that Hashem will assist you, and that you will continue to increase and grow in the dissemination of the wellsprings outward. Through this, you will expand the channels and vessels for the blessings of Hashem, not only in your general affairs but in your personal ones as well, in both material and spiritual matters alike.

With respect and blessing.

## 4888

[Kislev, 5717]

... May his strength be fortified, and great is his merit for having succeeded in doing the aforementioned, thereby bringing merit to the many, for the merit of the many depends upon him. And certainly, **over the course of time**, through pleasant words and in a respectful manner, they will add to the aforementioned until it is higher than a person’s stature. This is one of those measures that is determined by the person (Keilim 17:11), for one must follow the rationale of the matter.

#### 4888

Printed in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 16, p. 615.

**Doing the aforementioned:** A partition (mechitza) in the shul.

**Higher than a person’s stature:** Regarding the question of whether this refers to 6 feet, the response was: At least.

## 4889

[Kislev, 5717]

...To my greatest regret, no news reaches here regarding the activities of anash in the vineyard of Chabad in general, and in the dissemination of the wellsprings outward in particular. It seems that even the clear, pointed instruction from Above regarding the recent events has not brought about any movement whatsoever. When we see with our own eyes that when a flesh-and-blood person calls for mobilization, all those who are on a level even above the one giving the order—even though they are roughly equal, and sometimes the recipient of the order is even on a higher level than the one giving it, save for the fact that in that specific field the one giving the order has some priority—obey without any hesitation or doubt and carry out the commands.

It is understood what lesson and moral instruction must be drawn from this regarding the fulfillment of the commands of our Rebbes, the Nesi’im, who stand between us and Hashem our G-d, and who convey the commands of the King of Kings, the Holy One, Blessed be He. The primary principle in this is "Love your fellow as yourself," which implies that one must instill authentic Yiddishkeit, our Torah and its mitzvos, with the light and warmth of Chassidus. Instead, people occupy themselves with all sorts of other matters (and I fear—first and foremost—with empty fears of what the morrow may bring, or what will be in an hour or two, which has no practical relevance to the action one must perform in the present; this fear indicates a deficiency in bitachon and that one’s emunah is only in a peripheral way). No one takes to heart the fulfillment of the spiritual and internal task of disseminating the wellsprings, which is the demand of the hour in particular, since the heart of Israel has been awakened and shaken to its core, and many breaches have been made in the partition separating Israel from their Father in Heaven.

It is simple that Heaven forbid I should judge anyone unfavorably; rather, I am trying again and again, perhaps in spite of everything, they will begin to recognize the reality of the situation and what the hour demands of each and every one, and they will not listen to empty words... May it be the will [of Hashem] that you find the appropriate words to explain the above in every place your influence reaches. The main thing is that the explanation should be in a manner that leads to action, not to empty resolutions and mere talk. May they utilize the beautiful portion that Divine Providence has placed them in, for the good of each and every one, within the entirety of Klal Yisrael, and may you be blessed with goodness, you and your household, may they live in goodness that is revealed and manifest.

## 4990

*[Rosh Chodesh Teves, 5717]*

I was pleased to receive your letter dated the auspicious day of 19 Kislev, the day of our redemption and the liberation of our souls, in which you write about the farbrengens held over the past weeks. You will surely continue this good custom of writing about how "those who fear Hashem spoke to one another," etc. Certainly, words of inspiration will eventually have their effect, as our Sages of blessed memory said: "Great is study, for it leads to action." You will surely endeavor to increase the number of participants from circles that are expanding from time to time, as the needs of the hour demand. This is the instruction of the days of Chanukah—to increase and continue from day to day in matters of "the mitzvah is a candle and the Torah is light," and the "light of the Torah," which is the inner dimension of the Torah, referred to as "oil," as is explained in many places. May Hashem grant you the merit to be among those who strive in this as much as is required.

#### ***4990***

Published in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 10, p. 277.

## 4991

4991

B"H, 1 Teves, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

Your letter from the 3rd of Kislev, which was somewhat delayed in transit, has been received. Enclosed is a maamar that was studied here in public on Shabbos Mevarchim Kislev—the beginning of the events of the past few weeks—in which you will find an answer, as well as my perspective on several of the points you touched upon in your letter.

I wish to add that the approach that during times of extraordinary events the time is not suitable for pursuing the matters discussed in our previous correspondence—your letters to me and my replies—is, in my opinion, incorrect. On the contrary: if in ordinary times, when the world follows its natural course, the matter of Torah and mitzvos is vital for the individual and for the collective of our people, Bnei Yisrael, then all the more so during times of disruption to the order of life, and certainly when those disruptions are fundamental and affect the very issue of existence.

The instructions of our Torah are characteristic in this regard: regarding an army in times of war: a) The soldiers are not to fast, not even as a prayer for victory in the war, since this weakens the physical strength through which the war is conducted (instead, during wartime, one accepts upon oneself a fast to be observed after the victory and peace). b) One must increase holiness in the army camp, **even when not at war**, in addition to what is practiced in normal life. In other words, a state of emergency requires an increase and extra caution in observing the Torah of life, which was given to us by Hashem, "Hashem is a man of war, Hashem is His name." Upon this depends the victory of the war, even in a war such as this, where according to Torah as well, one requires an army, strength, and chariots.

It is obvious that you are correct in writing that in such days one does not mention sins, chas v'shalom. However, this is not the issue regarding what you write about *tochachas mussar* (reproof); on the contrary, during wartime and in an army camp in general, the necessity for this is even greater.

The distinction is simple: mentioning sins is a matter of the past; *tochachas mussar* and teaching a way of life concern the present and the future.

Regarding the army: the fundamental point of conducting a war and the conduct of an army in general is the point of *kabbalas ol* (accepting the yoke) and absolute discipline of the subordinate toward the superior, and of the one who is below toward the one who is above him—discipline that **does not** stem from prior deliberation until one understands the command and the matter with his own intellect, but rather discipline whose foundation is "we will do" preceding "we will hear." Certainly, for someone like you, there is no need for further explanation regarding the analogy.

Based on the promise that "many waters and rivers... will not be able to extinguish" the love of the Ribono shel Olam for Yisrael, and likewise the internal and essential love of every single one of Bnei Yisrael for the Ribono shel Olam, may it be His will that this love be revealed in actuality. And regarding the practical outcome, then we will surely emerge, together with all of Klal Yisrael, from the straits and the narrow place to the true expanse. Certainly, anyone whom Divine Providence has gifted with the power of influence has a merit in this, as well as a responsibility, and the strengths granted to him for this are very great.

With blessings for days of Chanukah that illuminate the darkness of the night and the *galus*, until we merit the fulfillment of the prophecy to give thanks and praise to His great Name in the Third Beis Hamikdash, in our true redemption through Moshiach Tzidkeinu, and with esteem.

## 4990

B"H, 1 Teves, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

Regarding what you wrote about a *tikkun* for the known sin: it is already brought in the *sefarim* that one must increase the letters of Torah and *tefillah*, and that many of them should be engraved in one’s memory. Then, even when one is thinking about other matters, these remain in the memory, and through knowing them by heart, there will also be the fulfillment of the statement "you shall honor the face of the elder" (*vehadarta pnei zaken*) in *Likutei Torah*, end of *Parshas Kedoshim*. Especially [important is] influencing the youth to bring them closer to Torah and *mitzvos*, as is known from our Sages, of blessed memory, that whoever teaches his friend’s son Torah is considered as if he had given birth to him. Also, giving *tzedakah* every weekday before the morning *tefillah*, and sometimes before *Mincha* as well.

Regarding what you wrote about your diminished desire and exertion in *Gemara*: this is surely one of the stratagems of the *yetzer* to link that diminution with the fact that you have taken upon yourself holy matters, etc. Yet it is known that "one *mitzvah* draws another *mitzvah*," and not, *chas v'shalom*, the opposite. When the *sitra achra* sees that this does not cause weakness, *chas v'shalom*, in your dedication to *Toras HaChassidus*, its guidance, and its customs, it will let go of you.

Regarding your question as to whether it is worthwhile to set a daily quota of *Gemara* pages, how many times to review what is learned, which commentaries to use, etc.: naturally, all of the above depends on the student’s talents, the learning environment, and so on. Therefore, you should consult with friends who know you and your environment. The point common to every soul is that there must be both *iyun* (in-depth) study and *girsa* (fluency) study, both in *Toras HaNigleh* and in *Toras HaChassidus*, in addition to reviewing the *halachos* necessary for daily life, Shabbos, and *Yom Tov*. Regarding *iyun* study, it is appropriate that it be in accordance with the words of our Sages, of blessed memory: "in the place where his heart desires."

I was pleased with what you wrote about your activities. You should certainly increase such efforts from time to time, in accordance with the directive to "increase in holiness." Since you have been appointed as a supervisor and leader in certain areas of *hafatzah*, may it be His will that you be "moist enough to moisten others" (*tofeach al menat lehatpiach*) with added energy and activities as much as is needed. Your reward is explained in the beginning of *Torah Or*: that one’s mind and heart become refined a thousand times over.

With blessings for good news in all the above.

4993

B”H, 1 Teves, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the Avreich, the G-d-fearing, learned, and distinguished, etc.,
R’ Avraham Avish Mordechai, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter from the first day of Chanukah.

**1)** The instruction of our Rebbe, the Baal HaTanya—the arbiter of the *nistar* (hidden) dimension of Torah—and the Shulchan Aruch—the arbiter of the *nigleh* (revealed) dimension of Torah—is that one who gives charity should not give more than a fifth, as is the standard practice. This is different, however, when one does so for the healing of the soul, as is found in many places, including the end of Chapter 3 of his Iggeres HaTeshuvah.

**2)** Regarding the question of our Sages (Kesuvos 67b) concerning Mar Ukva: why did he give more than a fifth, even though he was a *baal teshuvah*, as brought in Rashi (Sanhedrin 31b)?

There is no contradiction to the above, because this was after he had already completed his *teshuvah*—meaning his healing was complete. And this is well-understood, as the Alter Rebbe writes in Iggeres HaKodesh, Epistle 10.
