## 1660

B.H., 22 Teves, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed and distinguished individual, who engages in communal needs,
R' Yisrael David, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

It was a pleasure to learn from my conversation with [Name], may he live, that he meets with you from time to time and that you assist him in business matters, particularly regarding precious stones.

It is surely unnecessary to elaborate on the great merit of this matter, as it is even brought down as a halachic ruling: "one who provides him with work, etc." It is my hope that in the future you will not only continue in this manner, but also increase your efforts as much as possible, more frequently and in greater matters. However, relying on our Sages' teaching that "one only urges those who are already diligent," I am writing regarding this only to add further encouragement. See also Shulchan Aruch, Yoreh Deah, in accordance with our Sages' teachings in Shabbos 63a, Pesachim 53b, and the Mishnah at the end of Horayos, that a Kohen takes precedence, etc., and in many other places.

With blessings for the expansion of your livelihood, and that you may fulfill the well-known saying of our Rabbeinu HaZaken, author of the Tanya and Shulchan Aruch: "Hashem Yisbarach gives Jews gashmius (materiality), and they make ruchnius (spirituality) out of that gashmius."

I look forward to hearing good news regarding all of the above, as well as regarding your good work in expanding the yeshiva you founded—an expansion in both quantity and quality.

#### ***1660***

**R' Yisrael David:** Gluck, Far Rockaway.

## 75

B"H, 23 Teves, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 17 Teves.

At an auspicious time, I will mention all those you wrote about at the holy resting place of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

Regarding your question about a remedy for memory: it is known from the statement of our Sages on the verse "ordered in all things and kept" (Eruvin 54a), which means that one’s learning must be done with vitality. It goes without saying that one must not be affected by the enticements of the *yetzer*, which seeks schemes and cunning ways to prevent a person from his service to his Creator. One of its methods is to cast sadness upon a person and entice him into thinking that he is not succeeding in Torah and *mitzvos*. However, when one thunders and makes a commotion against it, and does not listen to its words at all, the enticements, concealments, and hiddenness are nullified. This is explained throughout the holy *Tanya*, and specifically in Chapter 29.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

**Secretary**

## 71

B.H., 23 Teves, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

After a very long hiatus, your letter of 15 Teves was received. It gave me pleasure to read that you are continuing your studies in the yeshiva, and it is my hope that you are increasing in this from time to time, in accordance with the instruction of our Torah that we increase in matters of holiness.

Beyond the inherent importance of this matter, it is understood that it also adds to the blessings of Hashem, may He be blessed, in all that a person and his family require, for the attribute of the Holy One, Blessed be He, is "measure for measure," only many times over.

Regarding what you write—that there are differences between one person and another in the matter of influencing others—it is understood that this is so. However, every person can influence others and their surroundings. Just as with physical tzedakah, where everyone is obligated—even a poor person in Yisrael, as per the ruling of our Torah—so too is it with spiritual tzedakah.

Regarding your concluding question as to whether you should cease your activities with the *Chaver HaPe'ilim* [Torah activists] during the school term, and engage in this only during the *bein hazmanim* [inter-semester breaks]:

It is understood and simple that this notion is absolutely refuted, for the activities for the dissemination and strengthening of Yiddishkeit must continue throughout the entire year. However, it is also understood and simple that there is a distinction between the types of workers within that framework. There are those from whom one should demand more prolonged and frequent work; there are those who, for various reasons, work only at fixed times; and there are those who work only during the *bein hazmanim* from their yeshiva studies. The aforementioned depends on the details and the fine details, and one cannot learn from one person about another. According to the description of the current situation in Eretz Yisrael, may it be built and established, those who can fulfill their obligation in activities for the dissemination of Yiddishkeit only during *bein hazmanim* are extremely few, for it is a time of emergency regarding the love of Hashem and His Torah, the beauty of performing mitzvos, and the study of our holy Torah as it should be.

In any event, you should surely from now on observe the three daily *shiurim* that are equal for every soul, as established by my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory—the well-known [daily portions of] Chumash, Tehillim, and Tanya.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,**

**Secretary**

## 72

B"H, 23 Teves, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter from Erev Shabbos Kodesh Parshas Shemos: It appears that you are focusing excessively on the matters you write about. This is similar to what is explained in Chassidus regarding the verse "and she shall trim her nails" (*ve'as'sa es roshah*). Focusing excessively on the measure of one's livelihood is like wearing a garment that is longer than the person wearing it; not only is the extra length of no benefit, it actually hinders one from walking properly.

Therefore, since you mention your lawyer, who is surely an expert, you should conduct yourself according to his instructions and serve Hashem with joy and complete trust that He is watching over you and your entire family—may they live and be well—with *hashgacha pratis*. After all, the Ribono shel Olam is the essence of good, and it is the nature of the good to bestow good; may this be drawn down into your affairs in a way that is good, visible, and revealed.

Surely you have also been occupied in recent weeks with the dissemination of Yiddishkeit in general, and Chassidus in particular, among your acquaintances in the places you visited and in your surroundings there. It is my hope that you remain in contact with them via letter even now, to strengthen them in the observance of mitzvos and in setting fixed times for Torah study, each according to his status and situation. May the Guardian of Yisrael, who neither slumbers nor sleeps, and who sustains and provides for all with grace, kindness, and mercy, guide you and your entire family—may they live—in the best possible way, both materially and spiritually.

With blessings for good tidings.

## 173

173

B"H, 23 Teves, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 17 Teves.

It is appropriate that you, and your wife—may she live and be well—each give to tzedakah forty times eighteen [times] three. The calculation in our current currency is explained in my letter printed in *Kovetz Lubavitch*, issue eight. This means that you should set aside for tzedakah, from time to time, until the aforementioned sum is collected for each of you. See also *Iggeres HaKodesh*, Epistle 21, and in the *maamar* of the Tzemach Tzedek regarding our Sages' teaching that "every single *prutah* adds up." May Hashem grant you merit and success in raising all your children—may they live—to Torah, to the *chuppah*, and to good deeds, with expanded consciousness, proper health, and peace of mind.

With blessings.

#### ***173***

**In response to your letter:** A request for a *tikkun* regarding having been a *niddah*, etc. See also below, Epistle 292.

**In my letter:** Above, Vol. 2, 199.

## 74

B"H, 26 Teves, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the avreich Shmuel, may he live:

Peace and blessing!

After a long and significant interruption, your letter of 19 Teves has arrived. I was pleased to read in it that everything in camp is in order. I hope that in due time, arrangements will be made to maintain contact with them even after they leave camp, in an appropriate manner. Through this, one will be able to instill in them vitality in Yiddishkeit, Torah, and mitzvos. I hope that at least some of them will remain to study in the Chabad institutions in their camp [of life] through the influence of their time in camp.

I await good news regarding all of the above.

#### ***74***

**Shmuel, may he live:** Gurary, Melbourne.

**In camp:** See above - 68.

## 575

575

B"H, 23 Teves, 5717
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the leaders of the community, the eyes of the congregation
of the holy and G-d-fearing community of Agadir, may Hashem protect them,

Shalom and blessings!

It was with great pleasure that I received your letter of December 17th, with the good and joyous news regarding the completion of the construction of the pure and beautiful mikveh, and that the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing man, and man of action, one of our finest students, Rabbi Azriel Haikin, may he live, was the driving force behind this lofty endeavor. You also merited to celebrate the completion with the inauguration of the mikveh building on the eighth of the month of Teves.

May it be the will of Hashem that with the inauguration of the building, there will come a good beginning for an internal and essential inauguration: the education of the women and daughters of Israel in the observance of the laws of family purity with beauty, and their dissemination in every Jewish home in your camp, without exception.

It is well known what our Sages, of blessed memory, said: "Purity leads to... holiness... to Chassidus... to Ruach HaKodesh." May the verse be fulfilled in them and in all the house of Israel: "And I will sprinkle pure water upon you, and you shall be purified... and I will place My spirit within you... and you shall be My people, and I will be your G-d," with the coming of the righteous Redeemer, speedily in our days.

With respect and blessings for success.

## 76

B"H, 26 Teves, 5717
Brooklyn

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and learned individual, who engages in communal needs, R' Binyamin, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter dated 18 Teves. It gave me pleasure to read that, despite the difficulties, you participated again this year in preparing the farbrengen of 19 Kislev, the day of our redemption and the liberation of our souls. It is already well-known that although this—on the face of it—is the physicality of the farbrengen, we see in practice and tangibly that it affects and touches upon the spiritual as well. Our Sages of blessed memory said, "Great is the drink that brings [people] close," and this should not be taken out of its literal meaning. While our Rebbes, the Nesi'im, strongly discouraged excessive drinking—what the world calls drunkenness and revelry—drinking in the manner of Torah and the pleasant ways of Chassidus is quite the opposite.

However, the trouble is—based on information I have received previously, and which is also evident from your letter—that there was not sufficient effort to ensure that the number of guests, in both quantity and quality, was as high as possible. After all, this day is a day of *segulah*, and the heart of a Yid, especially one from the lineage of Anash, is then most sensitive to the teachings of Chassidus and the matters of Chassidus. May it be the will of Hashem that for the upcoming days of *segulah*, you will exert the necessary effort in this regard, and may Hashem grant extraordinary success in this. This is especially true since we are speaking of a farbrengen in Kfar Chabad, for there Hashem commanded the blessing, life forevermore.

With blessings for good tidings.

***

76

R' Binyamin: Levin, Kfar Chabad.

Despite the difficulties: Of the war period. See above, Letter 4890, and the references in the footnotes there.

## 157

B"H, 27 Teves, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and diligent worker in communal affairs,
R' Yehoshua Shneur Zalman, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letters from the 12th and 17th of Teves, in which you write regarding the various difficulties in your avodah, particularly concerning Beis Rivkah. Surely, when you recall the difficulties that existed in your country in the past—which you nevertheless overcame successfully, for the verse in the weekly parsha was fulfilled in you: "With our youth and with our elders, with our sons and with our daughters..." —you will realize how insignificant the current difficulties are. Even though at times the test of prosperity and expansion is greater than the test of the opposite, such contemplation adds courage to overcome difficulties with joy and a firm confidence in victory in all details.

I trust that in the meantime you have received my letters in response to your previous ones.

Regarding what you wrote about... one must take into account that by working in the vineyard of Chabad, there is not only the aspect of "doing good," but also the negation of working in a manner of competition. There are many forms and modes of competition, and while it is understood that one cannot concede on essential matters, every issue should be handled in a diplomatic fashion—that is, with the necessary firmness, yet with pleasant words. Nevertheless, it is not worthwhile to "stretch the string" too tight; on the contrary, one should add a form that is pleasant, without conceding on the inner content and matters of importance. May Hashem grant you success and bring good tidings regarding communal matters and your own personal affairs.

With blessings and regards to all Anash, may they live.

P.S.
Surely you will utilize the upcoming yahrtzeit, Yud Shevat, for the development of the holy institutions of Lubavitch in your camp.

#### ***157***

**R' Yehoshua Shneur Zalman, may he live:** Serebanski, Melbourne. Additional letters to him appear above, 476, and in the notes indicated there.

## 578

578

B"H, 27 Teves, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings,

In response to your letter from Erev Shabbos Kodesh, in which you write regarding the proposal to serve as a mashpia in... and describe your experience in this role.

As it appears, this position is not suited for you, as the primary requirement there is the strength of simple discipline. Surely, with proper contemplation, you will be able to utilize your true talents in Chabad chinuch, and may it be Hashem’s will that you be successful.

You are surely preparing in the appropriate manner for the upcoming yom hilula, especially since this year it falls on Shabbos Kodesh, the Shabbos of Krias HaTorah. Both interpretations of this—that of the Zohar and that of the Arizal—are true, as is explained in Chassidus in many places. It is also a well-known saying of the Alter Rebbe: "One must live with the times," which means living with the content of the weekly parsha.

With blessings for good tidings regarding the above.

## 157

B.H., 27 Teves, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the Organization of N'shei U’Bnos Chabad in Kfar Chabad,
May Hashem grant them life,

Blessings and peace!

I have received your collective letter with your signatures, in which you write about the strengthening and consolidation of your organization, and primarily about the improvements in matters of mitzvos, especially those related to women. Regarding every one of them, it is said that they are daughters of Sarah, Rivkah, Rachel, and Leah; the meaning of this is that Hashem grants them special strengths from the souls of the holy Matriarchs.

This knowledge strengthens one’s courage so as not to be affected by obstacles and hindrances. One sees tangibly that approaching a matter with breadth and strength in itself diminishes the obstacles, and furthermore, it ensures that the subsequent attainment of the desired goal is met with greater success.

May Hashem grant that you report good tidings regarding all that was mentioned above, which will certainly bring an increase in the blessings of Hashem in all that you and your entire families, may they live, are in need of.

Enclosed is a letter in connection with the Yom Hillula of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, who is the founder of Kfar Chabad and continues to lead it even now. He is certainly standing on high, arousing great mercy for each and every one of the residents of the Kfar and for all of them together, so that the Kfar may rightfully bear his holy name, **Chabad**, and that there may be within it an abundance of blessing and success, both materially and spiritually.

With the hope of receiving good tidings from you soon regarding all of the above.

#### ***157***

**A letter:** See above, 154.

## 80

B”H, 27 Teves, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter from Motzaei Shabbos Parshas Shemos.

It is surprising that you wish to interpret what I wrote to you previously as meaning that your current activities in kosher chinuch are not the type of activities about which I wrote and urged you. It is obvious that chinuch on taharas hakodesh, which draws the hearts of the students to our Father in Heaven, to His Torah, and to His mitzvos, is among the most sublime activities, and as our Sages explain, it is "the work of Hashem." However, one does not fulfill his obligation properly through this alone, similar to the teaching of our Sages regarding Nakdimon, that "he did not act as he should have," even though he contributed generously to tzedakah, and the rest is understood. If this applies to everyone, it is all the more so for those who have merited to taste the Torah of Chassidus, its guidance, and its customs, for a special ability has been granted to them in activities whose totality is the "great principle of the Torah": "Love your fellow as yourself." Among the essentials of this are the *hafatzas hamaayanos*, chinuch on taharas hakodesh in general, and so on.

From the above, it is also understood that regarding what you write—that your work now is difficult—all difficulties are worthwhile for such a lofty avodah. May Hashem grant you success in increasing your strength in all the aforementioned areas, and also in increasing your *kvius itim* in the study of the revealed Torah and the Torah of Chassidus, and may you bring good tidings regarding all of this very soon.

With blessings,

**M. Schneerson**

## 881

B"H, 27 Teves, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

Regarding your question about your son’s shlit”a haircut: since it falls during the winter months, there is a concern that the sudden change from a full head of hair to a precise haircut might cause a cold, chas v’shalom, and therefore you thought to postpone it for some time.

In my opinion, this is not correct. The well-known language of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, regarding the three-year-old haircut implies that it draws down holiness—an addition of holiness—to the child’s neshamah through the leaving of the peyos, etc. Why, then, should one delay this drawing down? On the other hand, there is no necessity for a precise haircut; you may leave the hair longer for the time being, provided that the matter of leaving the peyos is recognizable.

May Hashem grant success to you and your wife, shichyu, in raising him to Torah, chuppah, and maasim tovim, with proper health and peace of mind.

With blessings.

#### 881

**Why, then, should one delay this:** See also above, Letter 4788, and the references noted in the footnotes there.

## 882

B”H, 27 Teves, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings,

In response to your letter, sent promptly on the day of the Hillula of our Alter Rebbe, in which you write regarding the woman, her daughter, and the aforementioned individual who visited you, and the doubt that arose concerning the conversion, etc., and that you subsequently received a telephone message informing you that they are no longer interested in the matter.

Since you have already been presented with this situation, it is worth trying once more to influence them in an appropriate manner. You should explain that the very name "Reform" indicates that it is a reform, not the true essence of the matter. This is a concern that impacts generations and generations to come; therefore, it is imperative that they correct the distortion now, specifically before she becomes engaged. Even though, as I have stated previously, my opinion is that you yourself should not be on the Beis Din performing the conversion, you should endeavor to arrange all the details—that is, to speak with three rabbis who deal with such matters, etc. Once everything is prepared, you should connect the aforementioned individuals with those rabbis. Although the general attitude toward conversion is known, this case is different, as it appears they have already been absorbed and are intermingling among the Bnei Yisrael, and the surrounding community is unaware and does not even suspect that there is anything here that is not permitted and appropriate.

With blessings,

## 83

B"H, 28 Teves, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, learned, and distinguished, who engages in communal needs, R' A. Shklovsky, may he be well.

Peace and blessing!

After a long silence, I was pleased to receive your letter of 19 Teves along with the enclosure. Thank you for writing about the farbrengen of 19 Kislev, the day of our redemption and the liberation of our souls. Surely, you will act appropriately to implement the resolutions, and in general, ensure that there is a visible **effect** from the hisorerus of the Rosh Hashanah of Chassidus throughout the entire year, as is known the expression of our Rebbe, the Nasi, regarding 19 Kislev: "May you be inscribed and sealed for a good year in the study of Chassidus and the ways of Chassidus."

And if this is true in general, it is all the more so regarding the resolutions and hisorerus that are accepted at a farbrengen of a minyan—which is called an "edah kedoshah"—in a holy place located in the Holy Land. It is understood and simple that all those who exert themselves in this, and subsequently work toward the implementation of these resolutions, take the lead in whatever is required for them. This is also part of their communal work for the benefit of the many, from which comes the "increasing and continuing to increase," and "they shall go from strength to strength."

The above strengthens my confidence that you are certainly making the necessary preparations for the yom hahilula of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, on 10 Shevat, may it come upon us for good. This is especially so since this year it falls on Shabbos Kodesh, the Shabbos of Krias Yam Suf, which led to the song to Hashem, "For He is highly exalted." This also contains the concept of "Awake and sing, you who dwell in the dust," and He is among them, according to the teaching of our Sages on the verse "Then Moshe sang," which is cited in Rashi’s commentary there. It is well known that a true leader of Bnei Yisrael and their "faithful shepherd" is called by the name Moshe, for "the extension of Moshe exists in every generation."

With blessings for good tidings in all the above, and with regards to all those who gathered at the aforementioned farbrengen, and all those who will gather on the yom hahilula of 10 Shevat, may it come upon us for good, and for all of Israel for good.

## 84

B.H., 28 Teves, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the avreich, the G-d-fearing, whose work is the work of Heaven,
Shmuel Elazar, may he live,

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter from Erev Shabbos Parshas Shemos, in which you write about the study schedules at the Mechina of Yeshivas Toras Emes, and the content of the words of hisorerus for the students—which I trust the students are listening to, etc.—may it be the will of Hashem that this be a good beginning for development in all of the above, as much as is necessary, both in quantity and in quality. That is to say, in the number of students, the volume of study, and the inwardness of their conduct. After all, the merit of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, and his father, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, assists all those who occupy themselves and strive in the matters of Toras Emes, which is their foundation and into which they invested their inner and essential powers.

It is surely appropriate, as you write, that when dealing with *aggada* in the Gemara, you expand somewhat on matters of hisorerus for Chassidus. You should also explain to the students, in terms suitable for their understanding, the concept of *nedarim* (vows) in general, as it is explained in *Likutei Torah* in Parshas Matos and in *Derech Mitzvosecha* in the corresponding *maamarim*. If this has not yet been done, there will be many suitable opportunities, such as at the conclusion of a chapter and the like. However, it is understood that these words of hisorerus should not replace the study of the text as it is, as is also explained in *Kuntres Eitz Chaim*—after the lengthiness regarding the necessity of studying Chassidus, it explains how one must toil in the study of *nigleh*. And this is simple.

I was pleased by the conclusion of your letter, that you are participating in the activities of Tzeirei Agudas Chabad. I trust you are encouraging all the other members of Tzeirei Agudas Chabad to "add soldiers." This is not only the demand of the hour, but the necessity of the hour, and there is no need to elaborate on what is simple.

With blessings for good news in all that has been mentioned.

## 85

B"H, 28 Teves, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 23 Teves, in which you write regarding the doubts you are experiencing concerning the potential engagement with [Name], and that because of this, these days are extremely difficult for you, to the point that your tears do not cease, etc.

It is surprising that these doubts have affected you in such a way. It is a common occurrence to have doubts in matters such as these; not only is it common, it is also understandable, as this is a major step in life. Since there is no perfection in creation, it is understood that there is always room for doubt. Even though one must contemplate all of the above with all the necessary seriousness, it should not lead to the aforementioned state—especially since, in order to reach a proper decision, one must do so from a state of tranquility of soul, and not from a state that is the opposite of this.

Furthermore, it appears from your letter that your own attitude is not entirely clear to you in your decision-making. In such cases, the correct way to clarify everything necessary is to cease the meetings, and even communication via letters, for a few weeks. This break almost always clarifies the relationship toward one of the two extremes. It is understood that the number of weeks depends on the temperament of each individual and their speed in reaching decisions; generally, three or four weeks are sufficient. May the Creator of the world and its Guide lead you on the good path and toward a good decision in both material and spiritual matters. The merit of your involvement in *chinuch* on the purity of holiness in the institution of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, will surely assist you in all of the above.

With blessings for good tidings.

## 186

186

[29 Teves, 5717]

I received with pleasure the news regarding the strengthening of the shiur you are teaching in public, and that in addition to what was studied until now, you have added to the holiness by also studying the halachos required for daily life from the Shulchan Aruch.

Being today at the holy resting place of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, I mentioned the participants in the shiur—and, of course, the teacher of the shiur among them—for whatever they may need.

May it be Hashem’s will that you continue to increase in the aforementioned study, in a manner consistent with the instruction of our Sages, of blessed memory: "Great is study, for it leads to action." Surely, the Giver of the Torah and the mitzvos will add His blessing to the participants of the aforementioned shiur for whatever they may need. And you should certainly endeavor to also increase the number of participants in the shiur. May Hashem grant you success.

#### ***186***

Printed in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 23, p. 493.

## 87

B"H, 29 Teves, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

Your letter from the day of the Hillula of Rabbeinu HaZaken was received, in which you write regarding the increase in studies, the hours of study, etc., and concluding with your efforts to arrange Seudah Shlishis on Shabbos for the high school students.

It is surely unnecessary to elaborate on the magnitude of this matter, for we see tangibly the success of drawing students closer through such activities, and how things are imprinted through this more easily and more deeply than through other methods. This is especially true given that the holiness of Shabbos assists in this, for this holiness affects even a complete am ha'aretz (see Demai, beginning of chapter 4), and all the more so with students, particularly at the time of Mincha on Shabbos, *Reva d'Chol Revin* (the most favorable of all favorable times).

May it be the will of Hashem that you succeed in all the points you wrote about, and you have already been promised regarding this in the well-known statement of our Sages: "You have labored and you have found."

And may the merit of the founder and director of the Yeshiva, my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, whose day of Hillula is approaching, stand by you to diminish the concealments and hiddenness and to increase success.

With blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned.

#### 87

Printed in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 16, p. 524.

## 88

B"H, 29 Teves, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and diligent in communal affairs, R' Moshe, may he live:

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter of 26 Teves, I hope that even though you do not mention it, you have explained to the students the significance of the yom hilula of 24 Teves, as this is also preparation for explaining to them the yom hilula of 10 Shevat, which is approaching.

Regarding what you wrote—that there is a claim that you have influenced some of the students to become Chassidim "before their time," etc., and you ask for an explanation of the matter:

In a case like this, since you have already influenced them, it does not make much of a difference how the order should have been beforehand. You must utilize their hisorerus, at the very least in matters that pertain to them, so that it results in an increase in vitality and the like. In this, you also fulfill their desire to lengthen their davening slightly on Shabbos. However, you must instruct them, and also supervise them, to ensure they carry out the instruction that their thoughts be focused on appropriate matters, rather than thoughts wandering here and there. Even though a person does not know the thoughts of his fellow, supervision is possible in this regard as well, by asking them beforehand (on Erev Shabbos or on Shabbos before davening) what they will think about, etc., or by giving them instruction beforehand (that it should be relevant to the parsha of the week or to the explanation of the words) that it is appropriate for them to think about such-and-such a topic...

With blessings for success.

#### ***88***

Published in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 24, p. 403, and completed according to the secretariat's copy.

**R' Moshe, may he live:** Bartov, Pittsburgh. Additional letters to him—above 4,790, and in the references noted there.

## 89

B"H, 29 Teves, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and learned, engaged in communal needs,
R' Ephraim Eliezer HaKohen, may he live:

Shalom u’Bracha!

As has been the custom in recent months, your letter and its enclosure were received this morning, before my departure to the holy resting place of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. I mentioned you and your family, may they live, there, in accordance with the contents of your letter...

Regarding your notification of your birthday on the upcoming 5th of Shevat (and one may note the Tosafos in Bava Metzia 106b), may it be the will of Hashem that your *mazal* be strengthened (and the interpretation in Chassidic teaching is well-known: "Israel has no *mazal*"), and may the coming year be a year of success. Primarily, regarding your role as *mara d'asra*, may the impression of your authority be evident in your locale through strengthening Yiddishkeit and disseminating it in general, and specifically through the dissemination of the *maayanos*. For this is the *mitzvah* of the day—"today, we have come" (Sanhedrin 98a)—the dissemination of the *maayanos* of the Baal Shem Tov and his successors, as is known from the *Iggeres HaKodesh* regarding the soul-ascent of the Baal Shem Tov.

With blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned.

#### ***89***

**R' Ephraim Eliezer:** Yalles, Philadelphia. Additional letters to him—see Vol. 13, p. 460, and the references in the footnotes there.

## 90

B"H, 29 Teves, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished individual, who engages in communal needs,
R' Shalom Mendel, may he live, a shochet.

Greetings and blessings!

Following your longest silence, your letter of 13 Teves was received, although even from it, the reason for the silence is not understood.

It gave me pleasure to read how you witnessed Hashgacha Pratis and the immense benefit that came from your being in Montmorency during Sukkos, where you succeeded, together with some of Anash, in "extracting the precious from the vile," etc. There is nothing greater than this, as is explained in many places, and it is also understood from the Gemara (Bava Metzia 85a). There is no need to elaborate on such a simple matter. Fortunate is his portion, and the portion of all those who participated in the aforementioned. May it be the will of Hashem that the promise of the Mishnah, "a mitzvah draws another mitzvah," be fulfilled in them, that they may go from strength to strength in activities that bring the hearts of the Bnei Yisrael closer to our Father in Heaven. The Giver of the Torah and the Mitzvos is surely certain to reward them measure for measure, and may it be with an abundance of Chassidishe nachas from all your descendants, may they live, and through their activities in the refining of the sparks in general—which is the concept of "extracting the precious from the vile," as explained in the teachings of Chassidus. My hope is strong that you will not be satisfied with what has been accomplished until now, and then there will be an increase in success, in addition to the success achieved thus far.

It is surprising that you do not write how you utilized the auspicious days of the month of Kislev—the 10th of Kislev, the 19th of Kislev, and the days of Chanukah—for you surely engaged in the aforementioned during these auspicious days.

With blessings for good tidings, in the manner of "may it be so," regarding communal matters and your own personal affairs.

## 190

B"H, 29 Teves, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

While visiting the holy resting place of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, today, I mentioned his wife, may she live and be well, as written in his *pan*. May it be the will of the Healer of all flesh, who performs wonders, that He grant him merit and success in bringing good news regarding this, as well as in all his affairs. We are currently on the eve of the new month, which is the *Rosh Hashanah* for trees—and since "man is the tree of the field," and especially according to the opinion of *Beis Shammai*, this is the eve of this *Rosh Hashanah*, as stated in the *Mishnah*. Furthermore, it is well known, as explained in *Likutei Torah* (Bamidbar 54c), that in the time to come, the *halacha* will follow *Beis Shammai*.

Regarding *anash* and the *temimim* in particular, this is the month of the *hillula* of the *Nasi* of our generation, my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, who stands and arouses *rachamim rabim* for all those who are connected and bound to him. As his words are well known, that the shepherds of Israel never depart from their flock.

With blessings for good tidings.

## 192

B"H, Rosh Chodesh Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

Mr. Yitzchak Damiel,

Shalom u’Bracha!

In response to your letter, in which you write that a small group of those you guide has approached you with a request to set aside special hours for discussions, in addition to the lessons you give them within the general framework. You mention that this has caused you doubt, as they believe you have already found your path and can lead them through this gate, while in your own opinion, you are still standing at the threshold.

I was very pleased to hear this news. It is well known—and we see this tangibly—that very often, others recognize a person’s virtues more clearly than the individual sees them in himself.

As with all matters, this is also reflected in the laws of our Torah, the Torah of life, which states that a person cannot testify about himself—not only to his credit, but also to his detriment.

Furthermore, regarding your current state, I have a strong hope that, as the saying goes, "spirit brings spirit and draws forth spirit." The *hisorerus* of those you guide and teach will also add to you an increase of spirit and a drawing forth of spirit, revealing your hidden strengths—and certainly your revealed ones—to further increase your own inner *emunah* and to draw that *emunah* from the level of *makif* (encompassing) to the level of *penimi* (internal). When this happens, it is expressed through an increase of light and vitality, and it impacts the Torah and *mitzvos* of the believer. I have already written to you that fortunate is his portion, for he merits to see the fruits of his labor, and he merits to see the reward alongside it, in their added preparation to absorb Torah and *mitzvos* in general, and the matters of Chassidus in particular.

Certainly, for someone like you, it is unnecessary to emphasize that every good action performed for the youth is multiplied many times over in its fruits and consequences—both literally, when they build a home in Israel and are blessed with sons and daughters, and also in the deeper sense, through the impact they have on others.

With esteem and with the blessing of success in the above.

## 93

B"H, Rosh Chodesh Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To Mr. Chaim, may he live, known as Dr. Shoskes,

Shalom u’vracha!

I have gratefully received your two books, *Unknown Lands* and *Your World and Mine*. I hope that in time you will also send your other books, and I thank you for your thoughtfulness.

On this occasion, I would like to express my satisfaction at having had the opportunity to meet you personally and to have a conversation with you.

According to the well-known saying that when two Jews meet, it should result in a benefit not only for the two of them, but also for a third Jew, I hope that this will be the case in our instance as well. And since you are a world traveler, it is to be hoped that this benefit will extend to many of our brethren, the Children of Israel, in various parts of the world.

In this regard, I would like to refer to the well-known statement of our Sages, "The Holy One, Blessed be He, performed an act of charity with Israel by scattering them among the nations." This expresses the idea that the purpose of a Jew—both as an individual and as a collective—is not to be limited to acting for oneself alone. Rather, one should have an "eye of benevolence" toward others, until the ultimate goal and purpose is fulfilled: "to perfect the world under the sovereignty of the Almighty," when "I will turn to the peoples a pure language, that they may all call upon the name of Hashem, to serve Him with one consent."

This is, in truth, the inner intent of the galus. It is not merely a matter of atonement for sins ("because of our sins"), but, as with all Divine matters which contain an inner good, the dispersion brings a benefit to oneself and to one's surroundings. By being in a particular place, a Jew can accomplish more than from afar. It is impossible to estimate what even one Jew can accomplish for their entire surroundings, an entire city, and even an entire country. It is not necessary to be in a place for a long time to carry out one's Divine mission; very often, one can do this "in a single moment," provided that one acts with Jewish warmth and light—which is one of the fundamental teachings of Chassidus.

I therefore wish you that your travels, whatever their external purpose may be, should serve the aforementioned idea: that a Jew, wherever they are and wherever they go, must illuminate their surroundings and assist in the victory of the spiritual over the material (*hagdaras hatzurah al hachomer*), as our holy Torah defines it. And when one truly desires this, Divine Providence already provides the possibilities and opportunities to carry it out in practice, wherever one stands and goes.

With respect and blessing,

## Letter 494

B"H, 1 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the Avreich, Nachum, may he live,

Greetings and blessings!

...Regarding your question about the customs of a Yahrtzeit/Yom HaHilula when it falls out on Shabbos, as it does this year: it is understood that mundane activities and the like should not be performed on Shabbos, but rather on Erev Shabbos, in accordance with the ruling of the Acharonim on such matters. See Kaf HaChaim on Orach Chaim, Siman 568, Ikrei HaDaT, Yoreh Deah, Siman 36, subsection 35, and elsewhere (and not as is written in the sefer Gesher HaChaim regarding this).

I was pleased to read at the end of your letter that you participate in the farbrengens of Tzeirei Agudas Chabad and Anash in general. It is certainly unnecessary to remind you that the essential point is the "therefore" (*uvchein*)—that this inspiration should result in practical action in thought, speech, and deed.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

***

**Letter 494**

**The Avreich, Nachum:** Margalit, Jerusalem.

**Customs of the Yahrtzeit:** See above, letter 885.

## 105

B.H., 2 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 24 Teves, in which you request a blessing to ascend the ladder that leads to Beis-El: at an opportune time, I will mention you at the holy resting place of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

It is surprising that you do not write in your letter regarding your daily study schedule or your efforts in the *hiddur* (beautification) of fulfilling *mitzvos*. After all, the Torah and the *mitzvos* are the ladder of which it is said, "a ladder set on the ground and its top reaches the heavens" (*Bereishis 28:12*). As our Sages of blessed memory famously state, this ladder is Sinai (the numerical value of the former is equal to the numerical value of the latter), and "this ladder is *tefillah*."

It is understood and simple that you must increase as much as you are able, both in the study of Torah—in both *nigleh* and *Chassidus*—and in the *hiddur* of fulfilling its *mitzvos*. There is always room for ascent in this, as it is written, "Torah scholars have no rest... they go from strength to strength" (*Tehillim 84:8*). And there is nothing that can stand before the will.

Surely you are aware of the three daily *shiurim* established for every person by my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory—the well-known *Chumash*, *Tehillim*, and *Tanya*. At the very least, you should observe them from this point forward.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

## 107

B.H., 2 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 24 Teves, which is the yahrtzeit of our Rebbe, the author of the Tanya and Shulchan Aruch:

1) How to conduct oneself in order to influence a yeshiva student to continue his studies in the yeshiva.

It is understood through explanation that our Torah is a Torah of life, even in its literal sense—life in this world. It is understood that since it was given by the Creator of the world in general, and the Creator of man in particular—the only One who can know what is truly good for a person situated in this world—the Creator has provided us with detailed instructions in our Torah. One of the primary commandments therein is the study of Torah. There is additional importance to studying it during the days that serve as a foundation and preparation for a person’s path when he goes out beyond the four cubits of his home, the shul, and the beis medrash. Many words of inspiration and explanation regarding this are found in books in general, and in the books of Chassidus in particular.

2) How to banish the strange thoughts that distract during davening and study.

One of the pieces of advice for this is to study from a book. Even when one is going over a sugya or an analytical point in his mind, the book should be open before him regarding the very subject he is contemplating. When he feels his thoughts beginning to wander, he should look inside the book, and the letters will grasp, clarify, and illuminate.

3) The matter of distracting dreams.

It is known, and also hinted at in the statements of our Sages, that a person only sees what his heart contemplates; dreams are the offspring of idle thoughts, etc., that occurred during the day. When one diminishes the cause, the effect naturally diminishes as well. Since there are other causes for dreams (see Tanya, Chapter 29 and the sources cited there), one should be careful with the Krias Shema al Hamitah, ensure that his body is pure by being careful with tevilas Ezra, and ensure that the mezuzah at the entrance to his room is kosher.

4) Regarding strengthening one’s emunah and bitachon, etc.: one should study Chassidus in abundance, and all of the above will be increased.

5) How to conduct oneself with those who oppose Chassidus.

It is understandable that it is alarming how one can oppose a part of Hashem’s Torah; this surely stems from a lack of knowledge regarding what the Torah of Chassidus is. Therefore, it is proper not to enter into arguments, but rather to spread the light of Torah and the "light" within the Torah—which is the inner dimension of the Torah—as much as possible, by increasing one's own study and by influencing one's surroundings so that this study spreads there as well. As mentioned, this should be done without entering into arguments. In this way, the opposition will naturally diminish, and knowledge and wisdom in the Torah of Chassidus will increase.

You surely know that on the tenth of this month, Shabbos Parshas Beshalach, is the yahrtzeit of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. All those who wish to be connected to his instructions and his Torah awaken and resolve on this day to increase their study of Torah and their meticulousness in the performance of mitzvos.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above.

P.S.
Regarding your request, and as an exception, I am signing this myself. However, even when the secretary signs, it is obvious that I read all the letters received in my name beforehand, and I read the response to all those letters signed in my name, and before they are sent, the response is read by me. It is strange to harbor the bizarre suspicion that this is not the procedure, for the Jewish people are not suspected of such things as the suspicion mentioned.

## 97

B.H., 4 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

I am pleased to receive news from time to time that you are well. May Hashem Yisbarach help that the news from you should be good, not only in spiritual matters but also in material ones. For among Jews, both go together, as is known from the words of the Rambam: that maintaining a healthy and sound body is among the ways of Hashem. This is also the teaching of the Baal Shem Tov on the verse, "If you see the donkey... you shall surely unload it **with him**"—that one must serve Hashem Yisbarach not through fasts and self-affliction, but together with the body, "In all your ways know Him." If this is true at all times, it is even more so in our time, when there are, relatively speaking, very few workers for the matters of Torah and mitzvos. Therefore, every worker in this field is many times more precious, and every good deed is more valuable. We see tangibly that when the body is weakened, it also results in a lack of the soul’s activities, which come about through the body, as is explained in Tanya, Chapter 37; see there.

Actually, I had assumed that for someone like you, there is no need to elaborate on this. However, rumors have reached me that you are not taking care of your physical health as required, as stated. And the verse explicitly says, "Take heed to yourselves, and guard your souls diligently." I am certain that this neglect is surely with good intentions, but as mentioned above, each of us in this time is a soldier who must follow orders and instructions. Since we have been told that in the footsteps of Mashiach, the primary avodah is through tzedakah and action (see Iggeres HaKodesh by our Rebbe the Alter Rebbe, end of Epistle 9), one must do everything in one's power to be capable of doing as much as possible. Therefore, the instruction we heard from my father-in-law, the Rebbe, our leader, to have a taste of food in the morning before davening is well known—and for many, not just a simple taste, but actual mezonos—in order to feel stronger. Furthermore, one can then daven with more concentration. It goes without saying that you are aware of the famous letter from the Baal Shem Tov to his student, the author of Toldos Yaakov Yosef, which rejects the practice of adding fasts; see there (printed in HaTamim, Issue 1, Sefer Ginzei Nistaros, Part 1, Section 68, and elsewhere).

I thank you in advance for writing to me that, from now on, your conduct will follow the instruction of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, to guard your physical health. Consequently, there will be an increase in the health of the soul and its avodah. You should replace the practice of fasting with tzedakah and "fasting of speech" and the like (see Toldos Moharash, page 72), and also continue working for the holy institutions, and for Kollel Chabad in particular, in a manner that does not overexert the body. This is also a benefit to the avodah itself, for then one has more strength to accomplish. My hope is strong that with such conduct, you will see an increase in success in a short time, and will be able to report for many more years of success in holy matters.

Enclosed is the most recently published [material], and you will surely share the merit of this with the public as well.

With blessings.

## 998

B"H, 4 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, learned, and distinguished R' Dovid, may he live:

Shalom and Bracha!

In response to your letter of Monday;

It is obvious that any hiddur (beautification) possible in any mitzvah is most appropriate, and this is certainly true regarding tzitzis and tallis. As our Sages of blessed memory stated: "This is my G-d and I will beautify Him"—beautify yourself before Him in the performance of mitzvos, [such as] a beautiful set of tzitzis (Shabbos 133b). It is well known that the kavanah when wrapping oneself in tzitzis is to draw upon oneself His sovereignty, may He be blessed, which is the sovereignty over all worlds, and to unify it upon us through this mitzvah (Tanya, beginning of Chapter 41). Therefore, every possible hiddur in this matter influences all of a person’s other affairs—whether it be that the wool must be from sheep without any doubt, or that the work be done lishmah (for the sake of the mitzvah) in all aspects, and so on, as explained in the Shulchan Aruch, in the Shulchan Aruch of our Alter Rebbe, in the Responsa Divrei Nechemiah, and elsewhere.

May it be G-d’s will that one mitzvah leads to another, to increase the "mitzvah candle, Torah light, and the light of Torah"—which is the pnimiyus (inner dimension) of Torah—within yourself and your portion in the world, until the prophecy is fulfilled: "And the night (exile, which is compared to night) shall shine like the day," with our true and complete redemption through Moshiach Tzidkeinu.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,**
**Secretary**

***998***

**R' Dovid:** Huisman, Brooklyn.

## 99

B"H, 4 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the administrators of the Gemilas Chasadim fund
of the Karlin and Kobrin Synagogue
May Hashem protect them and grant them life.

Peace and blessing!

I was pleased to learn, through the esteemed Rabbi, the G-d-fearing, learned, and distinguished man, who is involved in communal needs and possesses fine character traits, R' Shlomo Zalman Hecht, that you have graciously accepted his proposal to assist the Gemilas Chasadim fund of the Kolel Chabad in the Holy City of Yerushalayim—may it be rebuilt and established speedily by Mashiach Tzidkeinu. You have also sent me your check for the aforementioned Gemilas Chasadim fund, and the receipt is enclosed herewith.

At a time of favor, I will mention all those involved in this matter, as Rabbi Hecht shlita writes, at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. May Hashem Yisbarach grant that for many years to come you will be able to dedicate yourselves to matters of Torah and mitzvos, and especially tzedakah, which is equivalent to all the mitzvos, as explained at length in the holy Tanya, Chapter 37. Furthermore, within the mitzvah of tzedakah itself, gemilas chasadim is one of the highest levels of tzedakah (as stated in Shulchan Aruch, Yoreh Deah, sec. 249, clause 6).

And according to the promise of Hashem Yisbarach, "He who lends to Hashem favors the poor, and He will repay him for his deed" (Mishlei 19:17), it is certain that each of the aforementioned individuals will be repaid in whatever they and their families—may they live—are in need of, both in material and spiritual matters.

With blessings for good tidings.

## 100

100

By the Grace of Hashem, 5 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the G-d-fearing, esteemed, and distinguished, engaged in communal needs,
R' Shmuel Betzalel, may he live.

Greetings and Blessings!

I acknowledge receipt of your letter dated 14 Teves. Simply put, there is no place to ask forgiveness for writing about the matters of Beis Rivkah. On the contrary, one must know the situation as it truly is. It is always better to receive information as it is from friends, especially since, in the end, reports about the shortcomings arrive anyway from other sides—from those who are not currently friends or close associates—which is also reflected in their writing about it with exaggeration.

Regarding the core question: Now is a time when one must seize what one can, especially in matters of chinuch. Even if it is sometimes connected with expenses for which one does not see an immediate source of coverage, one will eventually find a way to manage the money—even if that solution comes months later. It is merely "uncomfortable" to be a debtor for a few months. However, if one lets go of the chinuch of the students even for a moment, they do not wait; they head in a direction that one is not always happy with. I hope that anash will increase their attention to all these matters that Hashgacha Elyona has given them to accomplish in Melbourne in particular, and in Australia in general, and primarily, as mentioned, in matters of chinuch. If one acts in this with simcha, this naturally diminishes the *marah shechorah* (melancholy), and from Above, too, one is given matters to be in simcha, as is known from the language of the holy Zohar on this, and as is known from the saying of my father-in-law, the Rebbe: that when a soldier marches in a parade with a victory march, that itself increases and hastens the victory.

Based on the above, it is also understood that, in general, regarding children, as long as they are in school, one must utilize their time in this to the maximum extent possible. For if, for any reason, they detach themselves from it, it is very difficult to influence them to return to learning—and certainly to have them do so with *hasmadah* (diligence)—and the rest is obvious.

Regarding what you write concerning a *shadran* (emissary) from Toras Emes in Lod to Australia: It seems to me that I have already written my opinion on this same matter before—that all three parties must coordinate: anash of Australia, Toras Emes of Lod, and the Merkaz Toras Emes in the United States, in order not to confuse one another, but rather to assist one another. May the merit of the *Baal HaHillula*, the director of Toras Emes and the Chabad institutions throughout the world, stand to bring good tidings in all that has been mentioned.

With blessings for an expansion of livelihood and to hear good tidings, and with simcha, in the custom of Nikolayev and its people from back then.

## 101

101

B"H, 5 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 4 Shevat, in which you write regarding the differences of opinion that have arisen in the shul where you serve in holiness regarding the recitation of Tachanun, and which have led to arguments and shouting—to the point that you write it has even reached the level of public shaming of a fellow—and you ask for my opinion on this.

It is surprising that you ask regarding the public shaming of a fellow, given that the words of our Sages are well-known and explicit on this matter, as are many maamarim regarding the condemnation of machlokes, and their words on this are clear.

At the same time, since you write that the shul is known as Nusach Ari, the halachic ruling in the responsa of the Acharonim is well-known, which states that while it is permitted to change from Nusach Ashkenaz to Nusach Sefard, or from Nusach Sefard to Nusach Ari, one may not change from Nusach Ari to Ashkenaz, etc. Since you serve in holiness there, you will surely endeavor to handle this through the ways of our holy Torah, whose ways are ways of pleasantness and ways of peace, and may Hashem grant you success.

In connection with the beginning of your letter, where you mention that you are a shamash in the aforementioned shul, you should certainly strive to fulfill the saying of the Tzemach Tzedek, who said to a certain shamash: "Call the sun (shemesh) and let the light shine (v'yizrach or), call the sun and let the light shine," to illuminate the shul through the recitation of Tehillim, public learning, etc., and may Hashem grant you success in this as well.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

**Secretary**

#### ***101***

**As the saying goes:** See also Vol. 13, Letter 4,692, and the references in the footnotes there.

## 102

B"H, 5 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, G-d-fearing, man of action, possessor of fine character traits, etc.,
R' Aryeh Leib, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

It has been a long time since I received any letter from you, although in response to my inquiries, I receive news from time to time regarding how you are doing and your well-being.

It was a pleasure to receive your regards through Mr. Galin, may he live. Surely you are gathering strength in your holy work to strengthen and awaken the hearts of the downtrodden. For as long as we are in a time of which it is said, "The needy (even the needy in spirituality) will not cease from the midst of the land" (Devarim 15:11), the merit and responsibility of all those who fulfill "You shall surely give" (Devarim 15:10)—even a hundred times—is great. This itself hastens the fulfillment of the promise, "There shall be no needy among you" (Devarim 15:4), which refers to the days of Moshiach, regarding which there is a dispute with Shmuel (see Shabbos 151b; Zohar Vol. III, 125a: that from their perspective, there is indeed no [needy], unlike those who have tasted from the Tree of Life).

The day of the Hillula of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, is approaching. He sacrificed his life to increase the number of those who taste from the Tree of Life, and "a tzaddik, after his passing, is found in all worlds even more than during his lifetime."

With respect and blessings for success in your holy work, and for much nachas from all your offspring, may they live, for long and good years.

#### ***102***

**R' Aryeh Leib:** Levin, Jerusalem. Additional letters to him – see above, Vol. 12, 3918, and the references in the notes there.

## 103

B"H, 6 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

Mrs. Esther, may she live.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter: it is a privilege to receive your letter a few days before the yahrtzeit of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. Divine Providence has granted you the merit to utilize your talents in the institutions that bear his name and are conducted in his spirit. If, regarding every Jew, the shepherds of Israel arouse great compassion for all their needs, then how much more so is this true for all those who take part and participate in the institutions of these shepherds of Israel. This is the greatest satisfaction one can possibly cause a soul. Just as with a shepherd in the literal sense, whose essence and primary characteristic is to match the pastures to the sheep and the flock—as the Sages of blessed memory state (Shemos Rabbah 2:2) that this is how they tested David and Moshe Rabbeinu, the faithful shepherd—so too is it exactly the same regarding the shepherds of Israel. Their primary focus and characteristic is to match the goodness bestowed through their prayers to the needs and matters of the one in need of that goodness; that is to say, in good that is visible and revealed, both in material matters and in spiritual matters. Especially now, as the time of your wedding approaches, in a good and auspicious hour, for the building of a home in Israel upon the foundations of Torah and mitzvos, it is fitting that your heart be confident in the Creator of the world and its Guide, the Essence of Good, that you will be blessed with all that you require in the aforementioned, and that you will be happy in spirit and in material matters.

With blessings for success in your holy work at the Chabad school, and for the success of your preparations for your wedding in a good hour, with joy.

I await good news.

## 104

104

B"H, 6 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed and distinguished individual, occupied with communal needs, R' Chaim, may he live:

Peace and blessing!

I have received your letter of Rosh Chodesh Shevat, conveying the success of the camp. May it be Hashem’s will that the impression from these weeks will influence the children, the parents, and the camp directors regarding their practical conduct throughout the entire year, and may you succeed in raising students who are a source of praise, renown, and glory (see Likutei Torah, Devarim 42b). May the merit of the Baal HaHillula, whose yahrtzeit is approaching, stand by each and every one of those working and exerting themselves in this, to ensure success in their efforts, and for an increase in blessing and success in their personal matters as well.

I am certain that you are making the necessary preparations for the yahrtzeit, 10 Shevat, which this year falls on Shabbos, the Shabbos of the Torah reading of Beshalach, Shabbos Shirah. May you share good news regarding this as well.

With blessing.

***

**R' Chaim, may he live:** Serbranski, Melbourne. Additional letters to him appear above, 4,849, and in the references noted there.

**The success of the camp:** See letters 568 and 574 above.

## 105

B.H., 6 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 24 Teves, in which you write the reasoning that so-and-so from the bochurim should travel here to study, and that specifically here he will succeed, whereas in his current location he will not.

My opinion on this is already well-known, as I have written it clearly to many who have asked about such matters, and it is also based on the results and the situation of those who have already done as mentioned above, which is:

To our regret, the Yetzer Hara also travels with the bochur who changes his location. Furthermore, there is the confusion of thought and the distraction of the mind that comes with living in large cities, which is the greatest waste of time, as well as the preparations, the travel, the settling into a new place, and various strange new excuses that correspond to the new location. One who truly wants to learn as he should can succeed in the Tomchei Temimim Yeshiva in Lod with outstanding success, and it is free of all those distracting matters found in a large city, etc. May it be the will of Hashem that you find the appropriate words for all of the above, first for yourself, until these things are absorbed within you as a sound premise—and this is not far-fetched, since it corresponds to reality—and then you will surely find the words to explain this to the bochur and his mother, may they live.

I trust you are making the appropriate preparations for the day of the Hilula, 10 Shevat, may it come upon us for good, and may the merit of the Baal Hilula stand for each and every one of Anash, may they live, in all that they need, both physically and spiritually.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,**

**Secretary**

## The Line

B"H, 7 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn, N.Y.

Greetings and Blessings!

...Regarding what you wrote concerning traveling here—my opinion on this is well-known: it is a holy obligation for the students of Tomchei Temimim in Lod to study in such a way that this Yeshiva becomes renowned throughout all corners of Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established, for praise, for name, and for glory. This depends solely on the students themselves, and they should not distract themselves with thoughts of traveling to any place whatsoever. May you merit and succeed in influencing yourself and the other students, may they live, in this regard, for this is what is best for them, in both a revealed and good manner.

#### ***The Line***

For its content, see the previous letter.

## 107

B"H, 7 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

Your letter of 5 Shevat, along with the enclosure, has just been received.

It is well known what the Gemara says regarding dreams—that a person sees [in a dream] the thoughts of his heart, what he thinks about during the day. When it concerns a *ro'eh Yisrael*—in this case, my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, *ztzukulleh"h, nbg"m, zy"a*—whose entire essence was doing good for Yidden, both materially and spiritually, this is certainly *chelma tava chazi* (a good dream is seen). This dream is a sign for even better things in the areas where you are in need, and above all else, for raising your son and daughter, *shichyu*, together with your wife, in the Jewish way and the Chassidic way. May you derive from them much true *nachas*—which is Jewish *nachas*, Chassidic *nachas*—for long, good, healthy, and happy years. The more one invests in children regarding *Yiddishkeit*, warmth, and the vitality that Chassidus provides, the greater the *nachas* one has from them, and most importantly, this brings true happiness into their lives.

May the *zechus* of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, whose *hilula* is this coming Shabbos, Shabbos Shirah, stand by you—and it will surely stand by you—especially for all those who have merited to receive his holy blessings in everything they require.

Enclosed is one of the recently published (reprinted) items specifically for Shabbos Shirah, which will surely be of interest to your wife, *tichye*.

With blessings for good tidings.

***

107

Specifically for Shabbos Shirah: See above, letter 1058.

## 108

B"H, 7 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the Avreich, Asher Masoud Nagar, may he live.

Peace and Blessing!

I was pleased to receive your letter in which you write about the founding of the Association of Yeshiva Students. Surely the goal of this association is as expressed in the prayer, "and may they all form a single association to do Your will with a whole heart." When one truly desires this, one succeeds not only regarding oneself, but also in influencing one’s peers and the entire environment so that they, too, will do the will of the Omnipresent with a whole heart.

As you write that you have adopted Chabad customs, your intention is surely to conduct yourself in the spirit of Chabad as well, two of the foundations of which are: "And you shall love Hashem your G-d..." and "And you shall love your neighbor as yourself." It is my hope that this will also add vitality and energy to the aforementioned matter. May it be His will that you succeed, that you increase your courage in the study of the holy Torah and the performance of its mitzvos with hiddur, and that you influence your peers in this as well. May Hashem grant you success and enable you to share good news regarding all of the above.

With blessing,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,**

**Secretary**

#### 108

**Asher Masoud Nagar:** Tel Aviv.

## 109

B"H, 7 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the avreich Shlomo Zalman, may he live,
To the avreich Shlomo Zalman HaKohen, may he live,

Peace and blessing!

I was pleased to receive your letter from 20 Teves, in which you write about your study schedule in the teachings of Chassidus, and that you are continuing to increase your efforts in this.

Surely the promise of our Sages, of blessed memory, will be fulfilled in you: "Whoever adds [to their efforts], they add to them [from Above]." And the addition from the Ribono shel Olam is far greater than the original portion. Since in the language of the holy Zohar (Vol. III, 152a) the study of Chassidus is called the "soul of the Torah," and all the expressions of our Torah are precise, just as we see below that an addition to the soul adds vitality to all the limbs of the body, from this it is understood that an addition to the "soul of the Torah" adds to the "body of the Torah"—the *gufi Torah*, the study of the revealed aspects of Torah—such that there will be increased success, and the manner of study will be what our Sages, of blessed memory, termed "meritorious, and they are right-leaning in it" (Yoma 72b; see Rashi’s commentary there; Shabbos 63a). And as is understood from the aforementioned statements of our Sages, there is also an increase of light and vitality in the fulfillment of mitzvos and their embellishment—*hiddur*—"beautifying oneself before Him in mitzvos," which also includes the *hiddur* in spirituality (see Shabbos 133b, "beautify yourself before Him..." and it is written regarding this, "for His sake"). This is in accordance with the words of our Sages, "And you shall love [Hashem your G-d]," [which implies] that the Name of Heaven should become beloved through you (and in Sifri there: "Make Him beloved to all creatures," and see Tanya, Chapter 32). Surely you also exert influence upon your surroundings in all of the above by being a living example of this. May it be the will of Hashem that success continues in this as well.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above.

The *pidyon nefesh* and the *pidyon nefesh* enclosed were received.

***

**109**

**The avreich Shlomo Zalman:** Auerbach, Bnei Brak.

**The avreich Shlomo Zalman HaKohen:** Reichman, Bnei Brak.

## 110

B"H, 7 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter—which lacks a date—in which you write about the misfortune that has occurred to you, *l'o aleinu*, and that you have not yet been blessed with sons.

It is necessary to clarify whether, at the time of your marriage, there was any infringement upon the honor of another Jewish man or woman—that is to say, whether there was a prior commitment to another Jewish man or woman. Furthermore, you must clarify whether you are observing the laws and statutes of *taharas hamishpachah* in accordance with the instructions of our holy Torah, the Torah of life. If there was an infringement upon the honor of a Jewish man or woman, you must seek their forgiveness, or at the very least do so in the presence of ten Jews. If there was any laxity in the observance of the laws and statutes of *taharas hamishpachah*, it is understood that, first and foremost, there must be complete remorse for the past and a firm, absolute resolution for the future to be as careful in this regard as is required. Hashem, who sees into the heart, will see that you have truly resolved this, and your iniquity will be removed and your sin atoned for, and He will gladden your hearts by fulfilling your heart’s desires for the good. In any case, you should have your *tefillin* checked, as well as the *mezuzos* in your home, to ensure they are all *kosher* according to the law. May it be His will that you bring good news regarding all that has been mentioned above.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

## 111

[7 Shevat, 5717]

...The holy work of spreading Chassidus has the unique quality of bringing about the birth of children, as is well known from the Iggeres HaKodesh of the Alter Rebbe, printed in Shaar HaYichud VeHaEmunah (Part II, 32a), Ginzei Nistaros (Part III, 61), and elsewhere.

## 112

112

B"H, 8 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the esteemed Rabbi, G-d-fearing, Torah scholar, and energetic communal worker,
Rabbi Menachem Ben-Zion, may he live,
General Manager of Keren Hatzala, Chicago, may it be protected by G-d.

Peace and blessing!

I hereby acknowledge with many thanks the receipt of your letter dated Erev Shabbos Parshas Bo, 2 Shevat, along with the allocation from Keren Hatzala for our holy institutions, Ohalei Yosef Yitzchok Lubavitch, in North Africa.

I also read with pleasure in your letter that this year you have increased the Keren Hatzala allocation for our yeshivos in Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established. May it be His will that you be able to continue "increasing in holiness" from year to year in the work of Keren Hatzala in general, and in your assistance to all our institutions in particular—not only in accordance with the measure of their development and expansion, but in even greater dimensions, to enable an extra measure of growth and flourishing, as this depends solely on financial means.

Since every matter, great and small, is under the providence of the Creator, may He be blessed—and especially in matters concerning Torah and mitzvos as they are illuminated by the inner dimension and the "light" of Torah, than which there is nothing greater—it is appropriate to note that the Keren Hatzala allocation arrived a few days before the yahrtzeit of 10 Shevat of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, the *tzaddik yesod olam*, in whose name the holy institutions in North Africa are named. He is the founder of these institutions, and he carries the prayer and plea on behalf of all who participate in his work and support his institutions, especially on this day of *segulah*, the day of the yahrtzeit, when the memory of all those who act and their deeds comes before him.

Fortunate are you and your colleagues in the administration of Keren Hatzala, and all who participate in your lofty endeavor, for having merited to be among the supporters of the institutions of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe. You, in particular, as the general manager of Keren Hatzala, upon whom and upon them the merit of the many depends. These institutions are the vessels and channels to draw down blessing and success in all the work of your hands and in all that you and your families, may they live, require in all the best, in revealed and manifest goodness.

With respect and blessing for good tidings, 

#### ***112***

**Rabbi Menachem Ben-Zion:** Zaks. Another letter to him—see Vol. 12, p. 24.

## 113

113

B"H, 19 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed and distinguished individual, whose work is the work of Heaven,
R' Avraham, may he live,

Greetings and blessings!

After a long interval, I received your letter of 23 Teves and the *pidyon nefesh* that accompanied it. It gave me pleasure to read of the good fruit of your labor in *chinuch al taharat hakodesh* at the institution of my father-in-law, the Rebbe. May it be Hashem’s will that this merit stand for you and your entire family, may they live, to provide them with all their needs, both materially and spiritually.

It is surely unnecessary to urge you regarding the strengthening of connections in matters of Torah and *mitzvos* with the soldiers. Beyond the general rule that applies to every individual—that there is a strength in *ta'amulah* (outreach) which does not return empty—in this case, the matter is even more important, as they are cautioned specifically and to a greater degree than ordinary people. You are a holy educator, and beyond the point that the aforementioned individuals are in an atmosphere permeated with the concept of *kabalas ol* (accepting the yoke) and fulfilling orders without questioning the reason for them, as has been explained at length many times...

At a time of favor, I will mention all those you wrote of in your letter at the holy *tziyun* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, regarding all the above, as well as regarding your holy work in *chinuch al taharat hakodesh*, which is also the channel for drawing down the blessings of Hashem for all your needs and for your entire family, may they live.

With blessings for good tidings.

#### ***113***

**R' Avraham:** Hershkowitz, Rishon LeZion.

## 114

114

B”H, 9 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter in which you ask whether you should continue your studies in the yeshiva, given that you sometimes fall asleep during learning, etc.

It is understood and simple that this, too, is one of the counsels and enticements of the *yetzer* [hara], which seeks to distract a person from the *avodah* of the Creator in every possible way—even through strange and unusual methods—to the point of tempting a person into believing that he lacks the ability to serve Hashem as is required, such as in the aforementioned case of falling asleep. You should request that the yeshiva administration provide you with a *chavrusa*, and you should learn with *chiyus*, in accordance with the teaching of our Sages on the verse, “arranged in all things and secured” (*Eruvin* 54a). When you truly desire it, the promise of our Sages will be fulfilled in you: “You have labored and you have found.”

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,**

**Secretary**

Therefore, once the reliability of the vaccine’s manufacture has been verified, there is no concern at all about having the injections; on the contrary...
