## 116

116

B"H, 9 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter in which you write that there is a set custom to hold a Kiddush in the shul in a specific place, and that three weeks ago it was agreed to establish a Shas study shiur immediately after Musaf davening. You note that they wish to study specifically at the table where the Kiddush is held, as those who organize the Kiddush are among the many participants in the shiur, and you ask:

a) Whether it is permissible to hold a Kiddush in the shul.

This question has already been discussed in many volumes of responsa. The majority of authorities permit it, and it is a simple matter of permission, especially in our times, when it is already known that the custom has spread to hold Kiddush in shul when the shul is being built.

b) Since there is a room adjacent to the shul, why not hold the Kiddush there and study specifically in the shul?

Regarding such matters, one must be careful to avoid conflict. In the vast majority of cases, conflict and division of opinion lead to a decrease in the number of those who study, as well as those involved in the communal needs of the shul. Since those who organize the Kiddush were there first and are greater in number, the concern for conflict increases as a result. Therefore, in my opinion, if they can speak among themselves about the place of study and the place of the Kiddush in a pleasant and peaceful manner, that is best. If there is a concern for conflict, then the new study group should establish a new, separate place for itself, without entering into a conflict.

I am certain that you do not satisfy yourself with the aforementioned shiur, and that you have a set time for Torah study specifically. As a yeshiva student, I hope that you have this set time every day, both morning and evening, as brought down in the early and later halachic authorities. May Hashem grant you success.

With blessings.

## 116*

B"H, 9 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

On the eve of the holy Shabbos, the day of the Hilula of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, who merits to act in his holy matters.

May it be His will that this merit stand for him and for all his family members, may they live, in all that they require, both materially and spiritually. May he increase his courage in his holy work mentioned above, and may it be His will that it be with outstanding success. The concealments and hiddenness are steadily diminishing, and the good, the holiness, and the light are steadily increasing, until the wellsprings spread outward in their fullness, and the night (for the exile is compared to the night) shall shine like the day.

With blessings for success and for good tidings,

**M. Schneerson**

## 1117

B"H, 11 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

...I am distressed by the conclusion of your letter, from which it appears that there is still nothing new. I fear that if, regarding a matter that is clearly inappropriate, it is said that "once he has repeated it, it becomes permitted to him," then how much more so is this the case when one is merely going through the motions to fulfill an obligation out of habit, etc. Surely there is no need to transcribe and detail the statements of our Sages regarding this matter and its gravity, for they are well-known and famous. May it be the will of Hashem that, since the entire matter of a *shidduch* and *zivug* is above the natural order—as our Sages of blessed memory famously compared it to the splitting of the Red Sea, and as it is written, "a prudent wife is from Hashem"—may it be His will that even if he does not create the vessels through natural means, the matter should be brought about through a miracle.

#### ***1117***

Printed in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 21, p. 456.

## 118

B"H, 11 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, G-d-fearing, and diligent in the needs of the community, Moreinu HaRav Hillel, may he live and be well,

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 6 Shevat: at an auspicious time, I will mention [the name] shlita at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, for the improvement of his health. The saying of our Rebbes, the Nesi'im, is well known: "Think good and it will be good." This is especially true regarding the aforementioned's ailment as you describe it in your letter, for even the doctors are in complete agreement on this. You will surely find the appropriate words to explain this concept of bitachon based on what is explained in the holy seforim, as well as the need for an increase in matters of Torah and mitzvos appropriate to his standing and situation. Even if his standing is already good, since "its measure is longer than the earth and broader than the sea" (Iyov 11:9), there is always room for increase.

This is even more necessary and understood based on what is explained in many places in the maamarim of Chassidus: that regarding the soul of Moshe Rabbeinu, which ascended on high and is in Gan Eden, it is also said that "Torah scholars have no rest, etc., not even in the World to Come," as he has been studying Torah for several thousand years, even though he received the Torah at Sinai...

With blessings for good tidings.

***

118

Moreinu HaRav Hillel: Medalia, Leeds. Additional letters to him appear above in 5903, and in the references noted in the footnotes there.

Saying: See also above, Vol. 10, letter 3116, and the references noted in the footnotes there.

## 119

119

B.H., 12 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn

To the G-d-fearing, esteemed, and distinguished individual, engaged in communal needs with vigor,
R. Yosef Boruch, may he live,

Greetings and blessings!

In addition to the general satisfaction of meeting together and discussing matters of strengthening Yiddishkeit in general, and specifically regarding *chinuch* on the basis of *taharas hakodesh* and the matters of *mikveh* and *taharas Yisrael*, I wish to express here once again my great *bitachon* that Hashem will grant success in the aforementioned work in your community. I also have the certainty that you, for your part, and *anash*, may they live, there for their part, will not only continue doing what you have taken upon yourselves until now, but that each one—and all together—will add to this *koh v’koh* (many times over), in accordance with the instructions of our holy Torah: "One ascends in matters of holiness." And "Talmidei Chachamim have no rest, etc., they go from strength to strength."

It is well known, the saying of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, *Nasi Yisrael*, that when a Yid down here takes upon himself a commitment in matters of *tzedakah* that at the time seems to exceed his capabilities, simultaneously, new sources and channels are opened Above to provide that Yid with the ability to fulfill his promise in full. We have seen this tangibly realized in cases we know of, and it will certainly be so in your case as well—especially since both of the aforementioned matters are pillars upon which the entire House of Israel rests.

It is a known teaching of the *Mishnah*: "One *mitzvah* leads to another." One *mitzvah* not only does not hinder the second, but on the contrary, it assists it. Therefore, I hope that you will also take upon yourself the observance of the three *shiurim* that are appropriate for every soul, which my father-in-law, the Rebbe, publicized as being appropriate for every soul and a *segulah* for many matters: 

a) Every morning after *davening*, saying the monthly *shiur* of *Tehillim*—as the *Tehillim* is divided according to the days of the month. 
b) Studying every day a *parsha* of *Chumash* with the commentary of Rashi from the *sedra* of the week—Sunday from the beginning of the *sedra* until the second *aliyah*, Monday from the second *aliyah* until the third, and so on. 
c) Studying every day a *shiur* of *Tanya*—as the *Tanya* is divided according to the days of the year.

At a time of *ratzon*, I will read your *pidyon nefesh* at the holy resting place of my father-in-law, the Rebbe. May Hashem help that you should be able to report good tidings regarding the contents of the *pidyon nefesh* and also regarding everything mentioned above.

With blessings,

## 120

120

B.H., 12 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

It was with pleasure that I received your letter from Rosh Chodesh Shevat and the one preceding it, in which you write about the fixed study session in Chassidus that you have with two avreichim. Surely you do not give room for it to be dragged into pilpulim and arguments, which in the vast majority of cases serve only to arouse the trait of contentiousness. Rather, you should explain the matters to them as they are in their truth, calmly and in pleasant ways, without showing any movement or desire for victory. For then, "as water reflects a face to a face," even if initially a desire is aroused in the listener to say the opposite, this desire weakens over time until it passes entirely, and eventually, he recognizes the truth. As has been discussed many times at farbrengens: one who wishes to influence his fellow Jew in matters of Torah and Yiddishkeit finds great assistance from the pnimiyus of the one he wishes to influence, which has always been faithful to Hashem, as explained at length in Tanya. It is only the "leaven in the dough" that hinders, concealing and hiding it so that it is not revealed outwardly in action and speech, yet it continues to exert its influence from within; and this is well understood.

Regarding what you wrote—that your feeling has weakened, even though you want to taste the flavor of avodas Hashem, yet it feels distant from you:

It has already been said, "It is not hidden from you... nor is it far away," and the matter depends only on one's ratzon. Provided, however, that you do not expect a change from one extreme to the other in a single hour or a single moment. For the order of kedushah is, "Little by little I will drive them out from before you." Rather, one must perform his work in this regard every single day. It is certain and simple that one should not be impressed at all by the enticements of the yetzer hara that one does not see success in his avodah, etc., for how is it possible that the word of Hashem—our Torah in general, and the teachings of Chassidus in particular—would not succeed, chas v'shalom?

Certainly, you should not be satisfied with the two aforementioned avreichim; you must strive to spread the wellsprings more and more, for this is the demand of the hour and also a necessity. One of the proofs of this is that suddenly, opponents to this have appeared—specifically from those about whom our Sages said that even in the "shadow of a shadow" they have [a connection to the truth], yet they wish to delay, chas v'shalom, the Geulah and the coming of Mashiach Tzidkeinu, and to strengthen the galus of the Shechinah, etc., by confusing minds so that they will not occupy themselves as much as is required with the spreading of the wellsprings. They seek to diminish the focus even of those who recognize this necessity, by drawing them into pilpul, or into back-and-forth debate, or into some engagement of any kind with the opponent. And is it truly worthwhile to waste the final moments in the footsteps of Mashiach, before the coming of Mashiach, on pilpulim to refute empty claims, at a time when they should be utilized for the spreading of the wellsprings—the teachings of Chassidus? And this is well understood.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

**Secretary**

## 121

B"H, 12 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings,

In response to your letter from Monday.

As you requested, I will mention you and your wife, may she live, at the holy resting place of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, for whatever you need, in accordance with what you wrote.

Regarding what you wrote—that you are lonely and have no one to turn to, and so on—the saying of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, is already well known: that Chassidus introduced the concept that Bnei Yisrael are connected to one another, and wherever a person goes or finds himself, he is not alone. As is well known from the story told in the name of the Baal Shem Tov, the mitzvah of "love your fellow as yourself" applies even to a fellow Jew who is at the ends of the earth and whom one has never seen. Moreover, just as "water reflects a face to a face," this love acts upon the one who is at the ends of the earth, such that this feeling is awakened within him as well.

I am certain that you participate in the farbrengens of Anash in your area, and that from time to time you increase not only your shiurim in the study of Nigleh, but also in the study of Chassidus. May Hashem grant you success.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

**Secretary**

#### ***121***

**Saying:** See also Letter 4550, and the references in the notes there.

## 122

122

B"H, 12 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the G-d-fearing, esteemed, and distinguished, etc., R' Shmuel, may he live, Shochet u'Bodek.

Peace and blessing!

I was pleased to hear of your participation in the farbrengen for the yom hilula of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, Nasi Yisrael. May it be His will that in this farbrengen as well, the well-known interpretation of our Sages regarding the hilula—"seize and eat, seize and drink"—be fulfilled. As explained in Chassidus, this refers to "seizing and eating" and "seizing and drinking" in matters of Torah and mitzvos, which are likened to bread and water, etc. This must be in a manner of "those who stand among these," where a person becomes a "walker" [one who progresses] among those who stand. Happy is his portion, for through him, many maamarim and sichos that awaken the Jewish soul to this avodah have been and continue to be printed; the merit of the many depends upon him.

With blessings for good tidings and that we may meet at future Chassidic farbrengens.

#### ***122***

**R' Shmuel:** Krakowsky, Montreal. Another letter to him appears above, Vol. 2, p. 404.

## 5123

B"H, 12 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the avreich, the G-d-fearing, learned, and distinguished, etc., R' Yaakov, may he live.

Shalom u'Bracha!

Regarding your inquiry about the Jewish custom—which is Torah—not to study Torah on the night of Nittel until midnight, and what the reason for this is: How is it possible that the study of Torah could cause matters that are inappropriate or undesirable, and for that reason, we abstain from studying?

Regarding the reason for this, you should consult the books of *Taamei HaMinhagim*, where there are many such reasons cited in the names of the great Torah scholars of Israel. Our custom is also as you mentioned, as recorded in the *Hayom Yom* for 17 Teves 5703, in the name of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory: it is so as not to add vitality [to the forces of impurity]. He said, "For the *masmidim* who are affected in their soul by the eight hours [of study], I do not hold [of this custom]," and it is already known that this applies only until midnight.

2) Regarding how it is possible for the study of Torah to cause undesirable results, or worse:

There are other examples of this, even in the *nigleh* (revealed) aspects of Torah. For instance, the prohibition against studying most parts of Torah on Tisha B'Av, or the prohibition against an *avel* (mourner), G-d forbid, studying most parts of Torah. Or, for that matter, the study of Torah by one of whom it is said, "But to the wicked, G-d says: 'What right have you to recite My statutes?'" See also the *Hilchos Talmud Torah* of our Alter Rebbe, Chapter 4, Section 3, and the *Kuntres Acharon* there.

## 124

124

B"H, 13 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and upright, engaged in communal needs,
R' Pinchas Leibish, may he live.

Shalom u'Bracha!

In response to your letter of 5 Shevat, in which you ask my opinion regarding the matter of Nittel, and the custom of Yisroel not to study Torah on this night until chatzos, and that this is indeed our custom, as is brought in the Hayom Yom for 17 Teves 5703.

Since the matter of refraining from Torah study is a significant innovation, one must not extend it beyond its specific parameters; therefore, one should not refrain from study for more than one night.

Based on what I wrote to [the recipient], it appears to me that in every country one should conduct oneself according to the calendar of the non-Jews in that country. If there are different customs among them regarding this, one should follow the custom on the night that the majority of non-Jews celebrate—that is, on the night accepted by the majority of non-Jews. For the principle of "following the majority" applies also to non-Jews, as is proven in the sugya regarding following the majority, and there are further proofs, but this is not the place to elaborate.

Thank you for continuing your good reports regarding the farbrengens, and surely you will continue this good custom in the future as well.

With blessings.

***

124

Published in Likutei Sichos, Vol. 10, p. 319, and completed according to the secretariat's copy.

R' Pinchas Leibish, may he live: Hertzel, Jerusalem. Another letter to him—above, Vol. 12, 3717.

## 125

B"H, 13 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of Monday, in which you write that in a year’s time you will finish at the institution where you are studying, and after that you have nowhere to turn, as you are alone, etc., since your family members do not wish to acknowledge an education based on the path of Torah, etc.

It is understood that since you yourself conclude in your letter that you have nowhere to turn but to the Ribono shel Olam, this is a sufficient and complete answer to your question, and an **absolute** negation of any feeling of despair, chas v'shalom, or even of emotional depression. The stronger you are in your trust in the Creator of the world and its Guide, the more clearly you will see your path in life even after these years of study, and you will succeed in it.

With blessings for good tidings in all that was mentioned,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

## 126

B"H, 14 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and distinguished, R' Shalom DovBer, may he live:

Greetings and blessings!

...You are surely occupied with the imperative of the hour—spreading the wellsprings outward—in addition to your holy position, which is also its essence. It is surprising that you do not report at all on activities such as these. We see clearly that the policy of "walking humbly" in such matters leads to a decrease in activity. And even if one were to argue that this is good for a person's own avodah—since it reduces the concern for *yeshus* and self-recognition—how can the benefit of the individual in this regard be weighed against the benefit that can be brought to the many through spreading the wellsprings? And this is understood.

It could be said that regarding this, too, it is stated: "Do not be overly righteous."

With blessings for good tidings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, may he live,**

**A. Quint**
**Secretary**

***

**126**

**R' Shalom DovBer, may he live:** Notik, Paris. Another letter to him—above, Vol. 3, 354.

## 127

B"H, 15 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn

To the avreich Menachem Ben-Tzion, may he live,

Shalom u’Bracha!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from the Motzaei Shabbos of the yom hilula of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, along with the pidyon nefesh enclosed therein, as well as your previous letters. Thank you for writing regarding the farbrengens of anash; you will surely continue this good custom in the future. May it be His will that you be able to add to these good tidings—meaning, that the number of locations where farbrengens are held will increase, and the number of participants will likewise increase, which will naturally lead to an increase in quality as well. As is known, in matters of holiness, an increase in quantity eventually leads to an increase in quality. Our current era is especially suited for this—even if one might ask, "Has the generation truly improved?"—on the contrary, that is precisely the point, and the wise will understand.

This also serves as a response to what you write regarding someone who has spoken out against the matter of spreading Chassidus. The only response should be solely through an increase in energy and strength, **as much as is necessary** for the spreading of the wellsprings until they reach even the outermost reaches. Eventually, this will penetrate even those who are like the aforementioned individuals, for in the course of time, they will inevitably do teshuvah for their aforementioned actions. All those who listen to me, and to whom the matter of spreading the wellsprings is dear, can prove that they pay no attention at all to baseless words by strengthening themselves and increasing their efforts in spreading the wellsprings—and not through any other form of response, which, on the contrary, only distracts from the work of spreading.

Especially in light of the explanation that "for each one, days are fashioned, and for Him, one of them"—everything is measured and limited. When one uses this time for other matters, it naturally results in a deficiency in fulfilling their own role, which, regarding Tzeirei Agudas Chabad and anash in general, is the matter of spreading [the wellsprings]. Their hearts should be firmly set on the living G-d, knowing that the Torah of Chassidus is His wisdom and His will, may He be blessed, so that the ruling of the Heavenly Beis Din will be fulfilled: that in all matters of yiras shamayim, Torah, and mitzvos, their hand will be on top. It is a pity to take time that should be dedicated to spreading the wellsprings and occupy it with other matters, and the wise will understand. You will surely explain the above to your friends, may they live.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

**Secretary**

## 128

128

B"H, 15 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter in which you ask what to do with the tzedakah funds that you and your wife, may she live and be well, set aside: It would be most appropriate to contribute them to the fund of the network that engages in the education of the sons and daughters of Israel in accordance with the purity of holiness. May Hashem grant you and your wife, may she live and be well, success, and may the blessing of the Creator and Guide of the world be fulfilled in you: "Tite'aser" (tithe so that you may become wealthy). May you raise all your children, may they live, to Torah, to the chuppah, and to good deeds, in comfort and abundance.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

**Secretary**

## 129

129

B"H, 15 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

Regarding what you wrote about... that it did not touch or affect [the person], the saying of our Rebbeim is well known: there is a strength to *ta'amulah* (outreach) that it does not return empty. Certainly, even if it is not yet visible on the outside, it has surely touched and affected him. This itself increases the effort of the aforementioned to cover and conceal so that it should not be seen on the outside, in order that they should not draw him further and further into the place where he truly belongs. From this, there is a directive that although diplomatically (because of the aforementioned's fear, as mentioned), one must arouse him from time to time—and it is best to do so indirectly and from the side, *v'ka'l*—regarding matters of Torah and *mitzvos*, and also matters of *Chassidus*.

Certainly, even if he does not mention it, he is striving as much as possible for the benefit of the *Achai Temimim* in Rishon LeZion. May it be the will of Hashem that in this, too, there will be outstanding success, even more than until now.

With blessings for good tidings, *tichyeh*.

## 130

B"H, 16 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and learned, engaged in communal needs,
R' Ephraim Eliezer HaKohen, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

It gave me pleasure to read in your letter of the 12th of Shevat the content of your words at the farbrengen during *Rava D'Ravin*—a time when there is no anger, for the King is revealed—and especially on the day of the *hillula* of the *Tzaddik Yesod Olam* who labored with *mesiras nefesh* that Hashem **should add** to me another son. As has been discussed previously, since there is a delay in having a son, and in "another," this also includes the lowest category, regarding which it is proclaimed, "outside of another." Yet, even for him, the advice offered in our Torah—Torah of life—is that he should push himself, and then he will enter. And for one for whom miracles upon miracles were performed (as our Sages say, *Berachos* 57a, "Yochanan, miracles upon miracles were performed for him"), he even puts an end to the smoke.

One could suggest an additional hint in this, based on what is seen tangibly: that the more the physical matter and wood increase, the more the smoke increases as well. Conversely, when the matter is refined, and the physical becomes spiritual, there is no place for smoke, for it is already entirely spiritual and light.

**Yitzchak**, upon whom the Holy One, Blessed be He, unified His Name (Tanchuma, *Toldos* 7; *Iggeres HaKodesh* 15), is also [like] the dust piled upon the Altar (the most coarse element, the dust).

Regarding what you noted concerning an explanation for the point of *taanug* and *ratzon* that follows the *Tzimtzum* and withdrawal, some places are noted in the margin from which the matter is understood*.

With wishes in accordance with the well-known *maamar* beginning "To one who is performing a mitzvah, one says, 'Complete it,'" that you continue in the dissemination of the wellsprings within those circles about which you write, and may it be His will that it be with success.

***

**130**

**R' Ephraim Eliezer:** Yalles, Philadelphia. Additional letters to him—above, 89, and in the notes indicated there.

*) *D'H Margla B'Pumei* 5709 (in *Sefer HaMaamarim 5709*).

**Should add:** Hints at the name of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory—see also above, Vol. 6, letter 1702.

## 151

151
B"H, 18 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from 11 Shevat (and the one preceding it). I am hastening to reply, not in the order of frequent correspondence, because of the matter of the vaccination injections. It is surprising that you asked about this, as many from Eretz Yisrael have already asked me, and I answered them in the affirmative, since the vast majority of people here do this, and successfully. It is understood that if there are injections produced by different pharmaceutical companies, one should use the one that is tested and proven.

Many thanks for the good news regarding the improvement in the health of the esteemed [name], may he live and be well. It is surprising that he himself did not inform me of this, as this is one of the ways to fulfill the mitzvah of "Love your fellow as yourself" — by gladdening the heart of a fellow Jew with good news, especially since he had previously informed me of the situation prior to the treatment. It is understood, and it seems that the saying of our Alter Rebbe, that "Love your fellow as yourself" is a vessel for, and also one with, "Love Hashem your G-d," has not yet penetrated all circles.

Moving from one subject to the same subject: regarding what you wrote about the relations between [names], this too is a branching out of the negation of the commandment, G-d forbid, of "Love your fellow as yourself." Even this sharp expression is not sufficient for the importance of the matter and its consequences. Based on what I have written many times, the promise of the verse, "As water reflects the face to the face, so is the heart of man to man," does not distinguish who is the first to change his heart. And whoever is first has merited the great principle of the Torah, as per the well-known statement of Rabbi Akiva regarding this. By becoming a *merkavah* for the two "loves" that are one, according to the aforementioned saying of the Alter Rebbe—and as is known, by performing a mitzvah, a person becomes a *merkavah* for that mitzvah, as is explained in many places, including in the Tanya—there is no need to elaborate on such a simple matter.

With blessings, and looking forward to hearing good news.

#### ***151***

**And I answered them:** See above, Letter 4862, and the references in the notes there.

**Saying:** See also above, Letter 4890, and the references in the notes there.

## 132

B"H, 18 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn

To the Administration of the Gemilas Chasadim Society Shomrei Shabbos,
May Hashem protect them and grant them life,

Peace and blessing!

I received with pleasure your letter informing me of the scheduling of your annual meeting for this day, Sunday, Parshas Mishpatim, 5715.

I also received the outline of the Gemach’s activities over the past year. As I have said in the past, there is room to increase this activity many times over. Specifically, regarding the number of loans: throughout the entire year, they were granted to only one hundred and twenty people. This is at a time when, unfortunately, we are still in the era of which it is said, "the needy will never cease," especially those who are truly destitute. As is well known, the statement of our Sages (Sukkah 49b) is that Gemilas Chasadim is greater than Tzedakah, for Gemilas Chasadim applies to both the poor and the wealthy.

May it be Hashem’s will that this, too, will be rectified as necessary in the coming year.

This itself brings closer the fulfillment of the promise, "there shall be no needy among you, for Hashem will surely bless you," for when Bnei Yisrael do the will of Hashem (Sifrei, Devarim 15:4).

And may the promise be fulfilled speedily in our days, that "all of Yisrael will taste from the Tree of Life, through which they will emerge from galus with mercy." This is unlike those who do not taste from this tree, for whom there is no difference between galus and the days of Moshiach other than the subjugation to the kingdoms. This is not the case for the wise of Yisrael, of whom it is said, "and Bnei Yisrael went out with a high hand," for this is how He will bring them out with all this glory in the final redemption.

This is according to the words of the Ra'aya Mehemna, Moshe Rabbeinu, the first redeemer and the final redeemer (Zohar Vol. III, 125a), which were cited and explained by our Alter Rebbe in Iggeres HaKodesh (Section 26).

With blessings for good tidings in all the above,

**M. Schneerson**

P.S.
It is possible that the reason for the aforementioned number of loans is hinted at in your writing that there is a shortage of cash in the Gemach fund. I have instructed the Gemach fund of Machne Israel to provide you with a loan until the end of the year 5718.

It is my hope, in the near future, that even before this, the true and complete Geulah will arrive, and the aforementioned promise will be fulfilled, and the matter of Gemilas Chasadim will be for the wealthy, similar to the story regarding this from my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

## 133

133

B"H, 18 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished individual, occupied with communal needs,
R' Avraham Chanoch, may he live.

Shalom u'Bracha!

In response to your prompt letter of 12 Shevat and its enclosure: Generally, regarding technical correspondence, one should consult on the spot, and specifically with a multitude of counselors. The drafting of letter templates is a technical matter. However, since it has already been sent here and is awaiting a response, I am replying to this—not in the order of the frequent correspondence—that in one of the letters, the language should be changed from a command to a suggestion. That is to say:

That you are suggesting to him the place where he is being called, and likewise, one should also add that if, in any case, it is impossible for him to be available for this Shabbos, he should certainly notify [us] for which Shabbos it is possible.

Furthermore, it will surely be difficult for the invitees to give their consent blindly, without knowing in advance the location of the visit, etc. Moreover, there is reason to fear that giving consent will be dependent on who the partner is, as it is well known that it is human nature that one does not wish to join with just anyone; conversely, sometimes knowing the partner will lead to consent with a whole heart. Likewise, regarding transportation, it seems one should add that if he does not require it and will travel independently, we request that he notify us in good time.

Certainly, the newspaper clippings that you write you are sending include everything that has appeared in the press, including regarding the article. It is astonishing that one needs to be prompted about this. I have already remarked many times on the strange conduct of Anash, where information is received from outside sources several days before there is any hint from Anash regarding it. At a time when, regarding joyous news, this is nothing less than a deficiency in the mitzvah of "Love your fellow as yourself" by bringing joy to a fellow Jew, regarding matters of distress, Heaven forbid, there is often also damage caused by the lack of complete and accurate information regarding such-and-such a matter, etc. From this, it is understood and simple that if, for some strange reason, newspaper clippings of a certain type have not yet been sent, they will surely make up for this in the follow-up.

With blessing,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,**

**Secretary**

## 134

[18 Shevat, 5717]

I received your letter with some delay, along with the proofs of your maamar, "When the World Was Water within Water and the Secret of the Shemitin and Yovelos."

In response, I am writing briefly, as time permits, especially since a proper explanation requires great length, given that you touched upon several fundamental points in the aforementioned maamar.

1) Regarding the matters of the creation of the world and the statements of our Sages related to this—such as those mentioned above, "when the world was water within water"—the majority of which are explained primarily in the books of Kabbalah, there are some who mistakenly claim that everyone is free to interpret these matters as they see fit, since it does not pertain to halacha l'maaseh according to the Shulchan Aruch.

While it is true that regarding matters consisting only of the interpretation of a verse, a statement of our Sages, or a derash, if the interpretation or derash is imbued with yiras shamayim and certainly does not contradict our faith, then the derash may be expounded, and one does not argue with a derash.

This is not the case, however, regarding matters of reality—even a reality of the past—which seemingly have no practical halachic consequence. For it certainly occurred in a specific manner and not another. In a place of doubt or dispute, there are established rules that clarify the doubt and determine the halacha, which are brought in the books that discuss this part of Torah. The general and central point is that regarding all matters of Kabbalah, the halacha follows the words of the Arizal over all who preceded him. This is known even to beginners in the study of Kabbalah, and R. Chaim Vital elaborated on this in his introduction to Shaar HaHakdamos. This rule has been accepted in all circles of the Jewish people—Sefardim and Ashkenazim, Chassidim and the "olam'she" (the non-Chassidic world), and so on.

2) Regarding the stories of the Torah—which was the tool of the Ribono shel Olam’s craft in the creation of the world, as our Sages state in Bereishis Rabbah at the beginning, "He looked into the Torah and created the world," and in the language of the Zohar (Vol. II, 161a), "He looked into the Torah and created the world"—in which the entire Seder Hishtalshelus is included, all the worlds from the highest world down to our own physical and material world, they recount not only about this world but also about the worlds above it. And these things apply to both the Torah Shebichtav and the Torah Shebe'al Peh.

For example, at the beginning of the Torah, "In the beginning, Elokim created the heavens and the earth," this recounts the beginning of the hishtalshelus of the Sefiros in the highest world, the world of Atzilus, as interpreted by the Zohar (Vol. I, 30b, 31b, and elsewhere). It is obvious that it also recounts the creation of the physical heavens and earth of this world, and it is understood that there is no contradiction or difference of opinion here, for one master says one thing and another master says another, and they complement one another.

It is also understood that despite the above, there are several verses and statements in the Torah that speak only of the spirituality of the worlds—for example, the attributes of Hashem, "the hand of Hashem," "the eye of Hashem." And there are several verses and statements in the Torah that speak only of the physicality of the worlds, or at the very least, only of the lower worlds.

Such as the matter of sin and prohibition, for only on earth is there an evil inclination (Shabbos 89a), and the halacha is established that "nothing impure descends from the heavens" (Sanhedrin 59b). Although it is understood that even these matters have some root in the worlds above them, for there is no matter below that does not have a *mazal* above. This is explained in the Pardes of the Ramak, Shaar 25, Chapter 1; in Torah Or by our Rebbe the Alter Rebbe (the author of the Tanya), Bereishis, in the discourse *Lehavin Inyan HaBrachos*; and in the glosses of the Tzemach Tzedek there, and elsewhere. And this is not the place to elaborate.

3) Regarding the matter of the Shemitin (the seven thousand years) and the Yovelos, which is brought in several places in the books of Kabbalah: it is true that previously there were divided opinions. Some say that our world is the second Shemitah, and before this there was also a full world that also lasted for seven thousand years, and after this world there will be another five Shemitos of seven thousand years (Sefer HaTemunah, Introduction to Temunah 3, and elsewhere). And some say that our world is the fifth Shemitah (the Ramak in his book Shiur Komah). But the Arizal came and established the halacha that we call these seven thousand years—in which we are currently situated—the second Shemitah in relation to the world of **spiritual Sefiros** that preceded them, but **there was no physical world before this, no physical heavens and earth, etc.** As his words are explained in many places, among them Likutei Torah of the Arizal, Parshas Kedoshim, and Shaar Maamarei Rashbi (37a). His words were brought and explained by our Rebbe the Alter Rebbe in Torah Or, Shemos, in the discourse *Vayomer G-d, "Mi Sam Peh,"* and by his son, our Rebbe the Mitteler Rebbe, in Sefer Toras Chayim, in the discourse *Vayomer G-d, "Lo Ish Devarim."* As mentioned above, this is an established halacha accepted by all the Mekubalim, just as in other matters of Kabbalah.

## 135

B"H, 19 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished individual, whose work is the work of Heaven,
R' Yitzchak, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

It was with pleasure that I received your letter of Tuesday, the day on which "it was good" was doubled, in which you write about the farbrengen held in honor of the joy of our redemption and the liberation of our soul—the liberation of our Rabbeinu HaZaken, the Baal HaTanya, the arbiter of the hidden dimensions of Torah and the Shulchan Aruch, the arbiter of the revealed dimensions of Torah—on 19 Kislev, which, in the language of the Nesi'im, is the Rosh Hashanah for the teachings of Chassidus and the ways of Chassidus.

May it be the will of Hashem that, all the more so—based on the statement of our Sages that when ten people sit together, the Shechinah rests among them, and even if they are not speaking words of Torah (see Tanya, Iggeres HaKodesh, ch. 23), the Shechinah rests—it is certainly the case that the inspiration is infinitely greater when those who fear Hashem speak to one another about words of Torah and words of awakening for yiras shamayim and ahavas Hashem. From this, all those who participate in the farbrengen, they and their households, may they live, are blessed with all that they require.

This is especially true when those gathered at the farbrengen do so with joy. It is well known from the Zohar that when a person below maintains a joyful countenance, then he is illuminated from above, etc.; the joy of a person draws forth another, higher joy (Zohar, Tetzaveh 184b). May it be the will of Hashem that you go from strength to strength until the time of the fulfillment of the promise, "You shall go out with joy," in the true and complete Geulah through Moshiach Tzidkeinu.

With blessings and regards to each and every one of the participants in the holy community of Gressif mentioned in your letter...

## 136

136

B"H, 19 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

...Regarding your question about the meaning of the words "appropriate way of life" (*orach chaim mis'taim*) that I used in my letter: it is understood that this refers to the conduct of "in all your ways know Him" (*b'chol derachecha da'ehu*). For the matters that are explicitly commanded and required are not merely an "appropriate way of life," but rather an explicit directive. This is in contrast to the aforementioned conduct of "in all your ways know Him," where there is always room to add more, since there is no perfection in creation. On the other hand, the One of whom it is said "know Him" is infinite and boundless; and this is well understood.

With blessings for increased confidence and for growth in matters of Torah, mitzvos, and the aforementioned way of life, for you and your wife, may she live, and for good tidings.

## 137

B"H, 19 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter from Erev Shabbos Kodesh, in which you write that when you put on your Tefillin shel Rosh, you are unable to determine if they are placed in their proper position.

The established practice regarding this is to ask someone nearby to check if they are positioned correctly. It is a simple ruling of our holy Torah that one may rely on this, just as in many other matters of Torah.

I trust that you are keeping the three daily study portions—Chumash, Tehillim, and Tanya—that are well-known, and may Hashem grant you success.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,**

**Secretary**

## 138

138

B"H, 19 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn, N.Y.

Brownsville-East New York Ladies Auxiliary
for the
Yeshivas Tomchei Temimim Lubavitch,
May Hashem sustain them.

Blessings and peace!

In response to your notification that your annual banquet has been scheduled for Wednesday, Parshas Mishpatim, 21 Shevat, 5717.

May Hashem Yisbarach grant that your endeavor be successful, both in material and spiritual matters—which, for Jews, are both closely intertwined. This is especially true when it concerns generating material support for the Yeshiva, which is then transformed into spirituality—into Torah, yiras shamayim, and the performance of mitzvos with hidur.

One of the fundamental lessons that Parshas Mishpatim (where the first detailed laws are given following Matan Torah) teaches us is to fulfill the Torah and mitzvos with **kabbalas-ol**, just as all Jews proclaimed at Mount Sinai: **Naaseh V’Nishma**—first **Naaseh**—we will do, and only afterwards **V’Nishma**—we will understand. Therefore, Parshas Mishpatim begins with the laws of an **eved ivri** (Hebrew servant) and **amah** (maidservant), as an introduction to show that every Jew, man or woman, must be an **oved Hashem**—to serve Hashem, blessed be He, with the greatest devotion and mesiras nefesh.

It is also well known what our Sages, of blessed memory, said: that before the Torah was given, the Ribono shel Olam instructed Moshe Rabbeinu to speak first with the women and only afterwards with the men. For it is only with the fullest assistance of the Jewish woman that one can ensure the eternity of Torah study and the existence of institutions of Torah and proper chinuch.

May Hashem Yisbarach help each and every one of you to increase your work on behalf of the Yeshiva with greater devotion and mesiras nefesh, and also to strengthen the foundations of Torah and mitzvos in each of your own homes and through your influence on the surrounding environment. By doing so, you bring down Divine blessings to succeed in your personal affairs, both materially and spiritually.

With blessings for good tidings regarding all that has been mentioned above.

#### ***138***

**R' Yitzchak:** Murciano, Meknes.

## 139

139

B"H, 19 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the Chabad Girls, Crown Heights branch,
May Hashem grant them life.

Blessings and peace!

I acknowledge receipt of your letter regarding the previous meeting and the *seder halimud* at that time. I was especially pleased to read therein about the thought given to the *farbrengen* several days in advance, to ensure that it would be conducted in an orderly fashion. When this is done, the benefit is manifold—not only for those who adhere to and maintain the *seder*, but for all those present, *uzchus harabim talui bahen*.

This is also a general principle in the totality of a person’s *avodah*: when it is performed with a *seder*, one sees greater *hatzlachah*, and the progress is much faster and more significant.

When one invests oneself in this, and possesses the perseverance to organize one’s communal work according to a pre-determined program, one succeeds in accomplishing and exerting influence much more easily than if one were to approach it without prior preparation. Furthermore, one sees the fruits much sooner than one had anticipated.

May Hashem Yisbarach help that the *zechus* of our holy *Nasi* in general, and of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, *Nasi* of our generation, in particular, stand by you in your work of *chinuch* for the Chabad girls, with *hatzlachah*. May you succeed in drawing in a greater number of girls, and in expanding and deepening the learning and the matters to which you dedicate yourselves.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,**

**Secretary**

#### ***139***

**Regarding my letter:** Above, 4461.

## 140

140

B"H, 19 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished individual, whose work is the work of Heaven,
R' Yaakov, may he live,

Greetings and blessings!

I was pleased to receive your letter of 12 Shevat, in which you write about the farbrengen for the day of the Hilula, as well as the visit and the conversation regarding the gathering of the former students of the Kishinev Yeshiva. May it be the will of Hashem that the words of hisorerus and the resolutions that will be adopted should be brought into action in all the matters you write about. Certainly, one should also have a "good eye" regarding others, to draw in many more to the circle of their influence and activities. As is known, the early Chassidim explained that the first mitzvah written in the Torah is "be fruitful and multiply"—a Yid must ensure that there is another Yid. They concluded that the same applies to Chassidus: one must ensure that there is another Chossid.

With blessings for success in the aforementioned and for good tidings in this regard.

#### ***140***

**R' Yaakov, may he live:** Peles. Another letter to him—see above, Vol. 11, 3,390.

## 151

B"H, 19 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

...It is understood that there is no room for apology regarding the content of your previous letters. As I have written many times to various members of anash, reporting the situation exactly as it is—without any embellishment or artifice—even if the situation is not as it should be, and consequently the report reflects this, is not only something for which one need not apologize; it is obvious that one must not conceal matters as they truly are. This is well known as the conduct of Chassidim and anash in previous generations.

This is especially true in our generation, a "generation of orphans," where, in addition to the reasons that existed until now, there is the added factor that, ultimately, reports regarding unsatisfactory matters are being received from those who, for the time being, are not well-wishers. Consequently, they are not deterred from exaggeration and addition. It is understood that when a report from lovers and friends precedes this, it prevents additional heartache. There is no need to elaborate on such a simple matter. It is also understood that the above applies as well to the rest of anash in our camp.

With blessings, and awaiting good news.

## 142

142

B"H, 20 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To Mr. Shimon, may he live, known as Dr. Bernstein,

Peace and blessing!

It was with pleasure that I learned that you have taken upon yourself the editing and preparation for publication of a second edition of the *Sefer HaRikmah*, originally published by the late Dr. Michael Wilensky, with the addition of the late scholar’s notes compiled after the first edition.

I knew the deceased as a man of excellent talents and extraordinary precision. When I was in Berlin at the time he was engaged in research for this book, I saw him completely immersed in it, turning nights into days in this work of his.

I wish to offer you my best wishes for success in this second edition, which will surely surpass the first in the refinement and scholarship that were so dear to the deceased.

With respect and blessing.

#### ***142***

**To the publication... Wilensky:** See above, Vol. 13, letters 4344, 4397; below, 151.

## 143

143

B"H, 20 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished R' Nachum Yaakov, may he live:

Peace and blessing!

...It was a pleasure for me to read in your letter about the organizing of a farbrengen in connection with the auspicious day of 19 Kislev. It is my hope that in connection with the yahrtzeit of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, which took place this past Shabbos Shira, you also exerted yourselves in such matters. May it be the will of Hashem that the impression of the farbrengen and the words of hisorerus that were spoken will be evident throughout the entire year.

It is surely unnecessary to elaborate on the great importance of Kolel Chabad in Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established, and the effort to support it from every place. The letters of our holy Rebbes on this matter are well-known, and their words require no confirmation. Therefore, my hope is strong that you will not only find a way to ensure that the donations remain at their current level, but that you will also exert yourselves—and thus succeed—in increasing the donations as much as is necessary, in accordance with the increased expenses and the needs of the hour. May Hashem, blessed be He, grant you success in this as well.

With blessings.

***

**R' Nachum Yaakov:** Parmet, Buenos Aires. Another letter to him appears above in Vol. 12, p. 261.

## 144

B"H, 20 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and learned, engaged in communal affairs, R’ N.N. [Namenov] — may he live.

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter of 17 Shevat: It is surprising that you make no mention of the construction matters. According to the esteemed G-d-fearing and learned R’ B.G. [Gorodetzky] — may he live — there is already a full and absolute agreement from the Joint to build four apartments for the *Rosh Yeshiva* and the *Rosh Yeshiva* of Tomchei Temimim. Consequently, their apartments in the building will be vacated, allowing for the expansion of the Tomchei Temimim premises, and it will also be possible to carry out certain repairs in the Tomchei Temimim building. It is understood that one must remain on guard to ensure that the repairs are carried out as required and that they fulfill their promises in this regard. As the aforementioned person told me, it is expected that by the end of this week or the beginning of next, a detailed list regarding the apartments and repairs will be received by our office in Paris as well. It is understood that this vigilance must be maintained by the administration of Tomchei Temimim in Brunoy, with you at the head, and you are surely making the necessary preparations for this. For several reasons, it is not advisable to delay, naturally, in matters that depend on them, since the agreement for all of the above was not achieved through their own initiative, but rather through negotiation, discussion, etc. From the above, it is also understood that now is not the time to come forward with any further financial demands, and a word to the wise is sufficient.

I was pleased to hear that there are students suitable for learning the craft of making *batim* and writing *parshiyos*. Beyond the simple matter of *parnassah* in this, there is the additional advantage that the students will not seek out matters of commerce and the like, where who knows what environment they might end up in. On the other hand, it is also understood that there is no place at all for all the students, or even the majority or half of them, to learn the aforementioned craft. For the students of Tomchei Temimim, this is not their primary focus, especially since it changes the character of the *yeshiva* and transforms it into a school for *stam*, etc., etc. The path of pleasantness in this is to estimate in advance how many students there are for whom this is the greatest possible elevation one can hope for, and to organize the affairs of the *stam* school, etc. — naturally in consultation with the esteemed R’ Z. Levin regarding this — so that there will be no room for accepting additional students. A more internal approach to this, consistent with what we discussed when you were here, is that in any case, the most essential thing is the infusion of *ahavah* and *koch* in the study of both *nigleh* and Chassidus within the students, even if they must be compelled for a short time by the remedy of our Sages: "A person should always study Torah... even if not for its own sake, for from 'not for its own sake' one comes to 'for its own sake.'" For it is understood that in the present case, "not for its own sake" is acceptable, and surely you will find the appropriate approach for all that has been said.

I await good news.

After writing the above, information was received that changes have occurred regarding the construction schedule, etc., and there may be further changes. Surely you remain in constant contact regarding this with the office in Paris.

Regarding the students for *stam*, it is understood that one cannot be satisfied with three or four, since the Joint representative stipulated that this must be a school, and their support is dependent upon this.

## 145

145

B"H, 20 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn, N.Y.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your notification regarding your birthday.

Surely you have followed the custom of Anash in recent times: giving tzedakah on your birthday before Shacharis and Mincha—or on the preceding day if the birthday falls on Shabbos or Yom Tov—studying an additional shiur in both Nigleh and Chassidus, receiving an aliyah to the Torah on your birthday or the Shabbos preceding it, and studying the three established daily portions—Chumash, Tehillim, and Tanya—on your birthday.

May it be the will of Hashem that this be a year of success for you in the study of Torah and the performance of mitzvos with hiddur.

Regarding your studies in the yeshiva and their order—you must follow the instructions of the administration.

Among the suggestions for focusing one's thoughts: studying from the inside [of the text], and at the very least, having the sefer open before you at the place where you are studying. Be careful in matters of *ch"n* (and be careful regarding *tevilas Ezra*)—[as is understood from the *maamar* "Va'era" by our Rebbe the Alter Rebbe, printed in the sefer *Torah Or*, Lemberg 5611].

With blessings for Torah study with yiras shamayim.

**On behalf of the Rebbe Shlita,**

**Secretary**

#### ***145***

**Regarding the custom of Anash:** See also above, letter 4848, and the references in the footnotes there.

## 146

146

B"H, 20 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the esteemed and G-d-fearing, etc., R' Yaakov Meir, may he live:

Peace and blessing!

...Regarding what you wrote concerning the order of study for Tanya: It is appropriate to study Part III, then Part II, Part I, and Part IV. Just as with the study of all parts of our holy Torah, during the first few times one should not dwell on the details of the concepts, questions from other places, and the like. As our Sages of blessed memory said: "First learn, and then understand."

#### ***146***

**R' Yaakov Meir:** Zohar, Tel Aviv.

## 147

147

B"H, 20 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter, written the day after the Yahrtzeit of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, the Nasi of Israel. During a time of favor, I will mention you and all your family members, may they live, once again at the holy gravesite of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, for whatever they may need, as per your letter.

Regarding what you write concerning the distribution of funds after one hundred and twenty years:

It is already well-known from several of the sichos of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, that the intellect is not satisfied with such arrangements. For why should any good deed be postponed for several decades—and may Hashem lengthen the days and years of each and every one of us, in good health, for much longer—at a time when it is possible to perform the good deed itself while one is alive in this world? Through this, one will actually add to the length of one’s days and years, as is well-known regarding the virtue of tzedakah and doing good in this regard.

And even though the instruction is known that one who distributes should not distribute more than a fifth, whereas this does not apply after one hundred and twenty years—aside from the fact that it is possible to distribute a fifth according to all opinions, there are, in addition to this, several circumstances where it is permitted, and even a great mitzvah and obligation, to give tzedakah beyond the fifth, as the details are brought in the Acharonim. Some of these are also found in Iggeres HaKodesh by our Alter Rebbe in several places; see especially Iggeres HaTeshuvah, end of Chapter 3, and Iggeres HaKodesh, end of Siman 10. Elaborating on a matter that is understood by one such as yourself is certainly unnecessary.

Enclosed is what I recently sent out; surely you will merit to share it with the many, and the merit of the many depends upon you.

With blessings.

#### ***147***

**It is already well-known:** See Igros Kodesh of the Rebbe, Vol. 5, letters 1,456 and 1,535.

## 1445

B"H, 21 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn

To the G-d-fearing, distinguished, and upright, who engages in the needs of the community, his work being the work of Heaven,
Rabbi Yisroel HaKohen,

Greetings and blessings!

The list of names has been received, along with the check. As indicated by the amount, this is the annual contribution, and it has been delivered to the Kupas Shaphah. May it be the will of Hashem that, following the example of this general tzedakah, every day of the coming year will fulfill the command of our holy Torah, that every single day one must perform one's work in matters of Torah and mitzvos. This is especially true for a man who has stood for the community in the fundamentals, in matters that affect the many. Divine Providence has granted you the merit to be active in matters of proper chinuch, and there is nothing greater than this, especially in our generation—a generation that is orphaned. When one truly searches, one finds the ways to instill in the students, and in those being educated in general, yiras shamayim, diligence in the study of Torah, and hiddur in the performance of mitzvos.

However, it is understood that in order to influence others, one must possess a measure of "moistening to moisten" in all these matters, and this is well understood.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe Shlita,**

**Secretary**

***

**1445**

**Rabbi Yisroel:** Stock, Bridgeport.

**To the Kupas Shaphah:** Keren HaShanah (Annual Fund) – a coin for each day. See above, Letter 1424, and the references in the notes there.

## 149

B"H, 21 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

I acknowledge receipt of your *pidyon nefesh*, in which you write about your remarks in the *beis medrash* regarding the content of the *yom hahilula* and the *baal hahilula*, and that you also made a *siyum*. You ask whether it is advisable to make a *siyum* in public, given that this makes it known that you studied the *masechta*.

I do not see any room for a question here. What is the *ravta* (great achievement) in having studied one *masechta* over the course of the year, following the education you have received and after Divine Providence has granted you the merit of being placed in a position of light, teaching students *Torah u’mitzvos*, etc.? Furthermore, even if we were speaking of a person for whom studying a *masechta* is indeed a *ravta* at his level, one should still make a *siyum masechta* in public and share this joy with the many. The *zechus harabim* (merit of the many) assists the one making the *siyum*, so that not only will he not suffer any spiritual loss, but on the contrary, he will even increase [in his spiritual standing]. *V’kal l’havin*.

Regarding what you write about reciting *Kaddish D’Rabbanan*: in general, there are no restrictions on this, and anyone can recite it. However, for the person raising the question, since it is better to be safe, one should clarify that his father and mother—may they live and be well—do not object to it.

You do not mention any *hachlatos tovos* (good resolutions) in connection with the *yom hahilula*. Yet, we are commanded to increase in matters of Torah and *mitzvos*, especially in a place where one can benefit the many. Regarding yourself in particular—to benefit your wife, sons, and daughters, may they live—this should, of course, be done in terms appropriate to their status and situation, and specifically through *darchei noam* (pleasant ways), especially since one is more successful that way.

## 150

21 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn

To my dear friend, the G-d-fearing, Torah-learned, and distinguished individual, occupied with the needs of the community, Rabbi Shmuel Menachem Mendel, may he live:

Peace and blessing!

...As you requested, I will mention all those you wrote about at the holy resting place of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

Regarding what you wrote about the improvement in the health of [name omitted], may she live, etc. — thank you for the good news. Regarding the words of the great Rabbi, the great Chassid, the great Tzaddik, R' Hillel of Paritch, that every action he takes is for the inner purpose of the deed — so that a word of Chassidus might be impressed upon it — this is certainly true of your own activities. And regarding the information that reaches you, as mentioned above, you utilize it for your primary task in this generation, the footsteps of Moshiach, in which we are the laborers of the day: the dissemination of the wellsprings of the teachings of Chassidus until they reach even the outermost reaches. 

Do not leave room for any matters that distract from this, even if it is possible to cloak them in the guise of zeal for Chassidus. For our avodah can be accomplished through the activities of the line of "do good," and through this, the evil and the *sitra achra* will diminish and fade away. For a little light pushes away much darkness — and how much more so a great deal of light, especially the light of the Torah, which is the inner dimension of the Torah. 

As I have written to some of *anash*, do not allow yourselves to be drawn into debates and arguments with those who oppose us, for it is a waste of time. It is certainly one of the ruses of the *sitra achra* to distract from the avodah of disseminating the wellsprings by attempting to draw those engaged in it into claims, counter-claims, disputes, and quarrels regarding the holiness of the teachings of Chassidus. Consequently, the time available for the ultimate avodah — the essence of which is the dissemination of the study of Chassidus everywhere — will be diminished.

With blessings for good news,

**M. Schneerson**

## 151

151

B.H., 21 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn

To the Avreich, Moshe Betzalel, may he live,

Greetings and blessings!

It was a pleasure to receive your letter in which you write about the fixed times for the study of Chassidus established in the shul, and that you and several of your friends participate in this shiur consistently. May it be the will of Hashem that the desire of Hashem shall succeed in your hands to increase and glorify all parts of Torah through the spreading of the *maayanos* (fountains) to every place.

This is, of course, based on the language of the holy Zohar (Vol. III, 152a) that the *pnimiyus* (inner dimension) of Torah is the *neshamah* (soul) of the Torah, while the *nigleh* (revealed dimension) of Torah is the *guf* (body) of the Torah. An increase in the vitality of the soul is immediately felt in **all** the limbs of the body. Since the descriptions and analogies in our holy Torah are precise and absolute, it is understood that everything mentioned above applies also to the study of the *pnimiyus* of Torah and its effect on the study of the *nigleh* of Torah, and this is well understood.

See also the statement of our Sages regarding "a *kav* of mustard seeds and a *kor* of wheat" (*Shabbos* 31a, end), from which we understand the matter regarding the **consistency** of study. Furthermore, this analogy in the Zohar also adds to the **vitality** within the study of the *nigleh* of Torah through the study of the *pnimiyus* of Torah (and according to what is explained in *Likutei Torah* in the *biur* to the *maamar* beginning *Lo Tashbit*).

With blessings for immense success in the holy *avodah* mentioned, of spreading the *maayanos* of Chassidus to every place.

It is surely unnecessary to awaken you and your friends, may they live, not to allow yourselves to be drawn into *shakla v'tarya* (debate and argument) with those who oppose it. It is a waste of time, and it is certainly one of the tactics of the *sitra achra* to disrupt the *avodah* of spreading the *maayanos* by attempting to draw those engaged in it into arguments, counter-arguments, and disputes regarding the holiness of the teachings of Chassidus. Consequently, the time available for the purpose of the *avodah*—the essence of which is the spreading of the study of Chassidus everywhere—will be diminished.

Regarding what you noted concerning what is written in *Tanya*, beginning of Chapter 4, that the greatness of the three *levushim* (garments) exceeds that of the *nefesh, ruach, neshamah*—as it is written in the Zohar that "Torah, the Holy One, blessed be He, and Yisrael are all one," for this is also said regarding Yisrael. The language regarding Yisrael is: "Three bonds are bound together" (see *Likutei Torah*, *Nitzavim* 46a; *Zohar* Vol. III, 73a), meaning that even separate things become bound together. "Yisrael are attached to Him" (*Zohar* Vol. III, 7a), the explanation of which is as written in *Zohar* Vol. III, 260, bottom of page 2, and further in a similar vein. See also *Tanya*, Chapter 37, regarding the souls of *Atzilus*: "to be nullified... and to be included... through the fulfillment of Torah and *mitzvos*." See also there, Chapter 23, regarding the superiority of Torah in this regard even over *mitzvos*, and the *Hagahos* of the Tzemach Tzedek there (printed in *Sefer Kitzurim L'Tanya*).

The common saying, "Yisrael, Torah, and the Holy One, blessed be He, are all one"—for the time being, I have not found a source for it. Perhaps it was derived from the aforementioned statement, "Three bonds are bound together." Even if you were to say that it is found, the difference in the statement quoted in *Tanya*, where only two are mentioned, compels us to explain that the unity of the three is not the same as that of the two, but rather in the manner of "attached to Him," as mentioned above.

With blessings for good news,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,**

**Secretary**

## 152

B"H, 21 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 18 Shevat, in which you write about what happened several years ago, when certain undesirable words were spoken by a woman, etc., and you ask what you should do.

It is appropriate that if you remember the name of the woman in question, you should ask for her forgiveness in the presence of ten Jewish men. It is not necessary to detail all the specifics to them; you need only say that since you do not know where this woman is—especially since she was previously overseas—you are asking for her forgiveness in their presence (the presence of these ten men) (and you should specify the woman's name) for any wrong you may have done her or any way in which you may have harmed her honor, and each of these ten men should say that she is completely forgiven.

Furthermore, if there were any consequences from the argument that took place at that time, and it is in your power to rectify them, it is understood that you should do so.

Most importantly, you should resolve with the greatest possible firmness that when the Ribono shel Olam, the Guide of the world, brings about your *zivug*, you will conduct your home as is fitting for a daughter of Israel, based on the foundations of Torah and *mitzvos* in the fullest sense. And every weekday morning, you should set aside a few *prutos* for *tzedakah*.

May Hashem Yisbarach fulfill your heart’s desires for the good, with a good *shidduch* in both material and spiritual matters.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

***

**152**

**Words spoken by a woman:** That I would never get married.

*) See *Torah Or*, beginning of *Parshas Vayishlach*, the *hemshech* "Mayim Rabim."

## 153

B"H, 22 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and distinguished, active in communal needs,
Reuven Tzvi Yehuda, may he live and be well,

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter, you inform me regarding the purchase of the house and its completion.

May it be the will of Hashem that it be in a good and auspicious hour, for the acquisition of "houses" in a spiritual sense, as explained at length in the maamarim of Chassidus—among them in *Likutei Torah* and the *Siddur* in the *maamar* "Mizmor Shir Chanukas HaBayis," the *Hemshech Mayim Rabim* 5636 at its conclusion, and elsewhere. And also in the literal sense, that it be a beautiful dwelling that expands the mind of the person who lives in it, for then their needs are also provided for them in all that is required, and with an open hand.

And naturally, this follows the well-known expression that "the needs of Your people are many"—specifically if—"their minds are limited,"* and from the negative you infer the positive. May it be the will of Hashem that all of this occur without difficulties or concealments, and may you bring good tidings regarding the above, and regarding your activities in the field of proper Chabad education, which are the gateway for drawing down goodness in personal matters and for elevating Torah and mitzvos.

Awaiting good tidings.

Your letters from the 13th and 18th of Shevat were received. — You should inaugurate the house with a Chassidishe farbrengen for success in body and soul.

#### ***153***

**Reuven Tzvi Yehuda, may he live and be well:** Feigelstock, Montreal. Additional letters to him—above, Vol. 7, 1800, and in the references in the notes there.

**Houses in a spiritual sense:** See also above, Vol. 13, Letter 4323, and in the references in the notes there.

## 554

554
B"H, 22 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn

To the members of Shuva Yisrael, headed by the G-d-fearing, esteemed, and distinguished Rabbi Ze’ev Greenberg shlita, and the activist Shimon Avraham HaLevi, may they live:

Peace and blessing!

It was with pleasure that I received the news that you have purchased a plot of land where you will teach our holy Torah—the Torah of life—to the children of the house of the Rebbe, and that it will also serve as a place where the children can spend time during the summer and the heat.

Certainly, even then, you will teach them Torah, mitzvos, and the customs of Yisrael, for this is the whole of man. This is especially true according to what is known from our Sages, of blessed memory, that the world exists solely on the breath of the mouths of school children. Enclosed is a check for 18 dollars as my participation in the purchase of this field, in the spirit of the verse: "I am a companion to all who fear You and to those who keep Your precepts."

May the Giver of the Torah, Who commands the mitzvos, grant you merit and success in increasing your activities for the strengthening and dissemination of Yiddishkeit, illuminated by the light and warmth of Chassidus, with health and peace of mind for the members of Shuva Yisrael, their wives, and their sons and daughters, may they live.

With blessings for success, and awaiting good news.

## 155

B"H, 22 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and faithful, whose work is the work of Heaven,
R' Yisroel, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter from Monday, in which you inquire regarding the tefillin and the parshiyos of Anash and Chabad Chassidim.

It is well known that in such matters, it is extremely difficult to convey these details through letters; it requires a tradition from sage to sage, from scribe to scribe. Surely, one can find in Eretz Yisroel, may it be rebuilt and established, scribes who are familiar with the form of the letters according to the tradition from the Alter Rebbe. For there are several nuances even in the form of the letters, and not only in the form of the letters on the batim, the yud, etc.

Regarding what you wrote about the tefillin of Shimusha Rabba and Ra'avad:

The order of wearing them is explained in many places, and some of it is noted in the margins. In general, the distinction between Shimusha Rabba and Ra'avad is like the distinction between Rashi and Rabbeinu Tam (except that both Shimusha Rabba and Ra'avad follow the right side of the one wearing them). Therefore, one combines the Shel Rosh of Shimusha Rabba with the Shel Yad of Rashi, and the Shel Rosh of Ra'avad with the Shel Yad of Rabbeinu Tam. See Hayom Yom, 19 Menachem Av, 5703.

As you requested, I will mention all those you wrote about at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

Regarding your notification of your son, Yitzchok Leib, may he live, entering the yoke of mitzvos on the upcoming 26th of Shevat: May it be His will that he accepts upon himself the yoke of Torah and the yoke of mitzvos, and that he grows to be a Chassid, a G-d-fearing person, and a scholar.

With blessing,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**
**Secretary**

P.S.
You are surely aware of the three daily portions that are incumbent upon every person—Chumash, Tehillim, and Tanya—as is well known. At the very least, you should observe them from now on.

***

 R' Yisroel: Rosenzweig, Jerusalem.
 According to the tradition: See also above, letter 4,789, and the references in the notes there.

## 156

156

B"H, 23 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

Your first letter, dated 5 Shevat, has just been received. It is a pity that you did not utilize your travel days more effectively for activities related to Yiddishkeit in general, and Chassidus in particular. However, there is no point in crying over the past, even though it is quite surprising that there was a lack of proper activity in the aforementioned areas. My hope is that, at the very least, you will utilize every possible opportunity for this in Eretz Yisrael (may it be rebuilt and established). After all, your trip here involved many difficulties, concealments, and hidden obstacles; it is obvious that the benefits of this trip should not be limited to just two people, whoever they may be, but should extend to a much larger number—and the more one increases in this, the more praiseworthy it is. Especially since not everyone has the opportunity to travel here themselves. When you contemplate this matter sincerely, you will surely find several ways and means to achieve this, not only regarding your influence within the institution where you serve in holiness, but also within a broader framework, such as Tzeirei Agudas Chabad. May Hashem grant you success and enable you to share good news regarding all of the above.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,**

**Secretary**

***

**156**

**Your travel:** Returning from a visit to the Rebbe.
