## 157

157

B”H, 23 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter in which you write about your heartfelt wishes that Hashem Yisbarach bless you with more living and healthy children in addition to your daughter, she should live and be well, and you add that your husband did not want to consult a doctor regarding why you have not been blessed with children for some years.

It is well known that the Torah’s approach is that even though Hashem Yisbarach is the Creator of the world and its Guide, and He heals all flesh and performs wonders, a person must still act within the natural order. In the words of our Sages, of blessed memory, the Torah gave permission to the physician to heal. Therefore, you should speak once again with your husband, he should be well, that both he and you should be examined by a specialist doctor in this field and follow his instructions.

It is understood that along with this, both of you must increase your involvement in matters of Torah and mitzvos, especially in the observance of the laws of Taharas HaMishpacha, modesty, and the like, as well as in giving tzedakah, and pray to the Giver of the Torah and the mitzvos that He fulfill your aforementioned heartfelt wishes for the good.

It is appropriate to have your husband’s tefillin and the mezuzos in your home checked to ensure they are all kosher according to the law. You should maintain the good custom of kosher daughters of Israel to set aside tzedakah before lighting candles every Erev Shabbos and Erev Yom Tov for the fulfillment of your aforementioned heartfelt wishes.

As it appears from your letter that you are a bas kohen, and if your husband is not a kohen, then in addition to all that is stated above, it is appropriate that your husband be proficient in the content of a tractate of the Shas; this can also be one of the smaller tractates, such as Maseches Kallah or the like.

With blessings for good tidings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,**

**Secretary**

## 158

B"H, 23 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter, in which you write once again regarding the question of which yeshiva you should attend to study, and that you wish to utilize the time to see "the great Judaism," etc.

I have already written to you that, in my opinion, you should go to study at Yeshivas Tomchei Temimim in Brunoy. Beyond the fact that this is within your means regarding expenses, there is a further point—and this is the main thing: in order to ensure, as you write, that you do not, Heaven forbid, leave the straight path, the advice for this is not to "see the great Judaism," but rather to internalize a great measure of *yiras shamayim*. As the Mishnah states, "One whose fear of Heaven precedes his wisdom, his wisdom endures." It is my firm hope that if you go to Tomchei Temimim in Brunoy, study there with *hasmadah* and *shekidah*, and conduct yourself in accordance with the yeshiva’s standards, you will receive the aforementioned effect.

With the blessing of the One who gives the Torah, the Creator of the world and its Guide, may He lead you on the path that is good before Him, as stated.

Especially in connection with your birthday—and surely you followed the customs of this day—may it be a year of success for you, in both spiritual and material matters.

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,**

**Secretary**

#### ***158***

**Customs of this day:** See also above, Letter 4948, and the references noted in the footnotes there.

## 159

B”H, 23 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

I am in receipt of your letter from the end of Teves, in which you write regarding your relationship with those in your home and how it causes you depression, etc. In your opinion, the cause of this matter is the difference in age, and the like.

Now, in many homes there exists an age difference similar to the one in your home. While it is understood that this sometimes leads to friction, differences of opinion, and the like, it does not generally reach the state described in your letter. Therefore, I hope that in your eyes, the severity of the situation is greater than it is in reality; it is merely the tendency of youth to often react to matters with excessive intensity. In any case, even if the situation were as you describe in your letter, it is understood and simple that the path you suggest—taking drastic measures—is not the way, nor is it necessary.

Rather, the solution is for you to contemplate your path in life. During these years of your youth, you must prepare yourself by acquiring knowledge, absorbing a spirit of emunah and bitachon, and adopting an appropriate way of life, etc., so that when the time comes to build a home of your own, in a good and auspicious hour, that home will be happy—both materially and spiritually. This will be achieved because the mistress of the house will be properly fortified and equipped with everything necessary for this. All of this depends on your way of life and your actions now. Compared to such a lofty role, how insignificant are all the phenomena you write about, which are nothing more than passing matters that leave no impression at all, provided one does not magnify them.

I hope that these few lines, small in quantity, will suffice to clarify the central point of what has been said and will calm your spirit, so that from now on you will see the situation as it truly is. May the Creator of the world and its Guide, who is the ultimate good and the essence of good, lead you on the good path in all details.

It is certainly unnecessary to remind you that, in addition to the above—which is a matter relevant to all daughters of Yisrael—there is another point regarding you that is also fundamental: you are a scion of a Chassidic family. Your affairs must be imbued with the light and warmth of Chassidus, and your responsibility is greater, since people will attribute any undesirable matter to the account of a Chassidic upbringing. On the other hand, there is no need to worry or fear this responsibility, for the Ribono shel Olam does not demand anything beyond our strength, and every person is given sufficient powers to fulfill their own unique role.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above.

## 160

160

B.H., 25 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, learned, etc.,
Dr. Y. Fosberg, may he live,

Greetings and blessings!

I acknowledge receipt of your letter in which you write regarding the state of health of Mr. ... may he live. I was pleased to read your letter and to discern from your manner of writing that you are approaching this with warmth and heart. While this is something required of every doctor, unfortunately there are those who possess nothing more than a routine approach. Even among those who do have a warm and heartfelt approach, there are many different levels. When one sees an added measure of this, it strengthens the hope that more and more will follow, and through this, the effect upon the patient will also increase.

Regarding the matter itself, you surely know that in the interim there have been changes in the patient’s condition, about which I have been informed. You have surely also been apprised of the content of my response, which is in agreement with your suggestion that he should follow the doctors’ instructions. I await further news, and may Hashem Yisbarach, the Healer of all flesh who performs wonders, help that there should be good tidings.

It is surely superfluous to point out that ruchniyus and gashmiyus are interconnected. Even the science of medicine recognizes that the health of the body is connected to the health of the neshamah—and not only the neshamah in the simple sense, but also the nefesh ha-elokis. Therefore, I hope that while healing your patients, you pay attention to their state in this latter regard, and endeavor to heal their neshamah and nefesh ha-elokis as well, not only their body and nefesh ha-bahamis. Even if some patients do not feel the need for this and even resist it, that in itself is the truest proof that they are in even greater need of healing in this area. May it be His will that you be successful in this as well.

With respect and blessing,

P.S. You surely know that I lived for several years in France and read French; therefore, you may write to me in whichever language is more comfortable for you.

## 161

161

B”H, 25 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and involved in communal needs,
R’ Tzvi, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 22 Shevat.

Enclosed is my response to Mr. Sherman, may he live, and you will surely find the appropriate words to explain the matters to him, should such be necessary.

Regarding what you wrote about your participation in communal affairs (it seems you are referring to matters involving both Jews and, lehavdil, non-Jews together in the neighborhood):

It is difficult to set hard-and-fast rules in such matters. The point is that one must reject any activity that could be interpreted as an agreement with the "Brotherhood" movement in a broad sense, or with the Reform and Conservative movements, chas v’shalom. However, this is understood to apply if the intent is religious in nature—in other words, involving representatives of the **movement** of Reform, etc. This is different from dealing with an individual as a private person regarding general matters of charity and the like. In such cases, the instructions of our Sages of blessed memory are well-known in many places, as is the actual practice of many great Torah-observant Jews who participate and meet [with such individuals], although, naturally, one should not seek out such opportunities.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,**
**Secretary**

***

161

**R’ Tzvi:** Shusterman, Chicago. Additional letters to him appear in Vol. 13, p. 326, and in the references noted in the footnotes there.

## 162

B"H, 26 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter from Erev Shabbos Mevarchim Adar Rishon.

As you requested, I will mention you at the holy resting place of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, regarding what you need. May the Creator of the world and its Guide lead you on the path that is good for you, in matters of your personal well-being, both materially and spiritually.

It is surely unnecessary to remind you that the commandment to "increase in holiness," and the teaching of our Sages, "whoever adds, is added to," applies many times over to people who have influence in their environment—especially among Anash and the Temimim, who are called "candles to illuminate." This is specifically true in proportion to one's capacity. For some time now, the impression of this should have been evident in both external and internal engraving within your surroundings, in matters of disseminating the teachings of Chassidus and the conduct of Chassidus as much as is required. There is nothing that can stand in the way of will, since Hashem does not deal tyrannically with His creations and asks only according to their strength. And this is understood.

With blessings for good tidings regarding the above.

## 163

163

B"H, 27 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, G-d-fearing, pious, and learned, engaged in communal needs,
R' Yitzchak, may he live, HaKohen

Shalom u'Bracha!

After a very long interval, I received your letter from Erev Rosh Hashanah La'Ilanos, in which you share the good news regarding the farbrengen held on the day of the hillula of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, and the hisorerus it generated. May it be the will of Hashem that the seeds planted on the day of the hillula result in growth and fruits throughout the coming year, specifically in practical, daily life—for as the Mishnah states, "the deed is the essential thing." Since the merit of the many depends on you, and your activities in Torah and mitzvos—and especially the needs of the many—require physical health, as explained at length by the Rambam in Hilchos De'os, beginning of chapter 4, may it be His will that your physical health, and that of your wife, be increased, so that you may add to your good works as much as is required, in accordance with the demands and necessities of the hour.

Regarding your question as to the source of the Chazal that "He is within them, within each and every one," it is found in the Shelah and in various other places, as noted in the margin.*

With blessings for good tidings regarding the spreading of the wellsprings outward, with health and peace of mind.

P.S. Your request regarding the sending of a Tanya will be fulfilled in these coming days.

## 164

B"H, 27 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings,

In response to your letter of 10 Shevat, in which you write regarding the difficulties you are having with your neighbor, etc.

In such matters, the custom is to request individuals of influence who are familiar with the details of the situation to exert their influence on the aforementioned party, and to find a way to reach a compromise—a solution that is equitable to both sides. After all, every person is ultimately susceptible to influence when the right approach is found for a specific matter and a specific situation.

Since you write in your letter that you explain to yourself that this matter is intended to refine your soul, etc., it is understood from this—though it is simple and obvious even without this—that the more you increase your involvement in Torah and mitzvos, the more you will be granted the blessings of Hashem in the areas you require. This matter is also included in that, and there is nothing that can stand in the way of willpower.

You are surely aware of the three daily study cycles for every person, established by my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory: the daily portion of Chumash, Tehillim, and Tanya. Please ensure that you keep them, at the very least from this point forward.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

## 165

165

*[27 Shevat 5717]*

In response to your letter regarding the incident where someone who davens Nusach Ari served as shaliach tzibbur in a shul where the congregation davens Nusach Ashkenaz, and you write that they protested against him, adding your own view that the question here is one of "lo tisgodedu" (do not form separate factions), etc.

Regarding whether "lo tisgodedu" applies here, this matter has been debated for many years. See the Rosh in Yevamos 14a, which presents two resolutions, and the rulings of the Rosh there, as well as the commentaries on Shulchan Aruch Orach Chayim, siman 493 (Magen Avraham, sub-paragraph 6, and others), and in many other places.

See also the halachic ruling of the Tzemach Tzedek in his Responsa, Orach Chayim, siman 236, regarding a **maaseh rav** (a definitive precedent) by the great Torah authorities (and the notes there), that this does not constitute a violation of a negative commandment. See there.

In matters such as these, the essential thing is to avoid machlokes, which is prohibited by the Torah according to all opinions. Peace is great, for the entire Torah was given to bring peace to the world (as cited in Rambam, end of Hilchos Chanukah).

And since we have touched upon this—regarding the differences between Nusach Ashkenaz, Sefard, and Ari when switching from one to the other, this too has been discussed at length and is brought in the responsa noted in the margin*.

There is a **maaseh rav** where Rav N. Adler (and the author of the Hafla'ah) served as shaliach tzibbur using Nusach Sefard while the members of his minyan davened Nusach Ashkenaz (Responsa Chasam Sofer, Orach Chayim, 15). In Responsa Meishiv Davar by the Netziv, and in the sefer Pardes Mordechai in the name of the author of Eitz Chayim, it is stated that the shaliach tzibbur should recite his prayer **aloud** according to the nusach of the shul. I do not have them before me to see if they cite the aforementioned maaseh rav and their resolution of this issue.

#### ***165***

Printed in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 14, p. 226.

**The one who davens Nusach Ari:** See also above, 444, and the references in the notes there.

## 166

166

B"H, 28 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the Committee for Organizing Melaveh Malkah Gatherings,
And at their head,
The esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and upright, involved in communal needs, R' Moshe HaKohen,
May they live.

Peace and blessing!

It was with pleasure that I received the news of the organization of this committee, and subsequently, the regular reports of the *se'udata d'David Malka Meshicha* (the meal of King David, the Moshiach) every Motzaei Shabbos.

May it be the will [of Hashem] that the impression of this meal be evident throughout all the days of the week that follow, and in accordance with what was discussed at the *farbrengen* on the day of the *hilula* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory—based on the words of the Beis Yosef in *Orach Chaim*, siman 300, and what is brought in the *Siddurim*—from which one can understand the magnitude of the effect of this matter on all the affairs of the weekdays that follow it.

May it be the will [of Hashem] that, *a fortiori* from the statement of our Sages that "when ten sit together, the Shechinah rests among them"—and even if they are not speaking words of Torah (see *Tanya, Iggeret HaKodesh*, siman 23), the Shechinah rests—it is certainly the case that the inspiration is far greater when those who fear Hashem speak to one another about words of Torah and words of *hisorerus* for *yiras shamayim* and the love of Hashem. From this, all the participants in the *farbrengen* are blessed—they and their households, may they live—with whatever they are in need of.

Especially since those who gather in a *farbrengen* do so with joy, and the statement of the *Zohar* is well known: "When a person below is in a state of a shining countenance, then [the forces] above shine upon him... the joy of a person draws toward him another, higher joy" (*Zohar, Tetzaveh* 184b). May it be the will [of Hashem] that they go from strength to strength until the time of the fulfillment of the promise, "You shall go out with joy," in the true and complete Redemption through Moshiach Tzidkeinu.

With blessings for success,

**M. Schneerson**

Enclosed is a check—my participation in the committee's expenses.

## 167

167

B"H, 28 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings,

I have received your *pan* in addition to the information provided over the telephone. It is astonishing, despite the fact that we have spoken many times about there being no basis for *marah shechorah* (*chas v'shalom*), and given that for every one of the Jewish people—and for *Chassidim* in particular—the commandment to "serve Hashem with joy," as emphasized in the teachings of the Baal Shem Tov and his disciples and their disciples, is binding—how much more so is this true for one of the tribe of Levi, whose inheritance is Hashem and who resides in the "tent of Torah and *tefillah*." Therefore, the aforementioned astonishment is all the greater. See also the words of the holy *Zohar* (*Tetzaveh* 184a), "Come and see... when he stands with a radiant countenance below, then he is illuminated from above"—study it well.

May it be the will of the *Ribono shel Olam* that these words be engraved in your memory (see *Likutei Torah*, end of *Parshas Kedoshim*, *d'h V'hadarta Pnei Zakein*) and that they exert their influence in your daily life, fulfilling the commandment to "know Him in all your ways," from a place of true and inward joy.

I await good news regarding all that has been mentioned, and I sign with a blessing for a speedy recovery and a complete recovery.

## 168

168

B"H, 28 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter from the conclusion of Shabbos, the day of the Hilula of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, in which you write that you are unable to fulfill what was written in my previous letters due to an abundance of preoccupations, etc.

It is understood and simple that for a person to occupy himself with Torah and mitzvos is for the person’s own benefit, as well as for the benefit of all those connected to him through family ties and the like. From this, it is also understood that if, in addition to the usual necessary matters, one requires additional blessings from Hashem, which entails additional effort and further preoccupation, the advised counsel for this is to increase in matters of Torah and mitzvos, and not the opposite. From this, the negation of the decision mentioned at the beginning of your letter is understood.

It is also understood that this is not a demand from anyone for the benefit of others, but rather an instruction, in my opinion, as to how the current situation can be rectified, and this is easy to understand.

Regarding what you write about having seen in a certain book a matter of a *segulah* and the like: stay away from this path of *segulos*, for not all people are the same in this regard, nor are all circumstances the same, and it is not in the hands of everyone to aim correctly and know clearly how and what. Meanwhile, the *segulah* that is tested and proven is the practice of Torah and mitzvos, along the lines of what I wrote in my previous letters. May the Healer of all flesh, who performs wonders, illuminate your eyes regarding all that has been said, and may He send His word for the complete healing of your mother, *tichyeh l'yamim tovim*.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

**Secretary**

#### ***168***

**Matter of a *segulah*:** To write "Kera Satan" on parchment.

## 169

169

B.H. 28 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of the lists of *anshei maamad* along with the enclosed check. Since you do not write anything regarding how you are doing, I assume this is a sign that all is well.

You have surely heard the story of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, which was mentioned several times during the *farbrengen*, that one who engages in *tzorchei tzibbur* (communal needs) is entitled—and it is appropriate—for the fund to bear the burden of the expenses associated with this work.

Since it has been some time that you have been engaged in the work of the *maamad* faithfully, according to the aforementioned story, it is proper and good—as stated above—that the fund should allocate a stipend for your time, etc., amounting to approximately one-fourth, starting from this list that you sent; that is to say, from the beginning of Teves, 5712. A check is enclosed.

May it be His will that you should utilize this in good health and happiness for healthy and happy matters.

With blessings for outstanding success and for a good arrangement very soon.

P.S.
You surely utilized the *yom hahilula* of 10 Shevat that just passed, both regarding yourself and those you influence. May it be His will that its impression be evident throughout the entire coming year.

## 170

170

B.H. 29 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn

Greetings and blessings,

In response to your letter in which you write about the change you experienced several months ago, when suddenly, out of the blue, idle questions and doubts beset you, and the reinforcements of various proofs and the like were of no avail.

One can see clearly in such matters that the best advice is to divert your attention from the entire subject for a period of time and not to engage in a battle with these idle thoughts. In the vast majority of cases, this causes the skepticism, doubts, and questions to weaken.

The explanation for this is that such doubts and questions come from the outside. For it is said of every Jew that they are "believers, the children of believers," and that which is inherited over dozens of generations is, obviously, strong and firm. However, due to various peripheral causes, this does not always exert its effect openly, and it is possible for opposing and contrary things to cover and conceal it. By battling them, the battle itself reinforces their reality and even adds to their importance (similar to the concept of "prohibition grants importance").

Conversely, diverting one's attention and paying them no mind at all—without entering into any sort of debate—but rather diverting one's attention through engagement in a matter of understanding and intellect of an **entirely different** nature, weakens the hold of these foreign and opposing things on a person’s soul and train of thought.

It is understood that one cannot get rid of foreign thoughts except by replacing them with a different, positive thought, since the mind is always wandering and cannot be entirely vacant. When one focuses intensely on other thoughts regarding matters of Torah—which is light—this pushes away much darkness.

It is also understood that one must contemplate how it happened that a foreign and opposing matter took hold in the soul. According to what is explained in *musar* seforim, and even more so in *Chassidus*, this comes from matters of *chayn* (idle chatter) and a lack of caution regarding trivialities. Therefore, together with the aforementioned diversion of attention, you should, *bli neder*, be careful from here on in to be cautious regarding *tevilas Ezra*, to divert your attention for a time from matters of *chayn*—**even from their rectification** (see *Shu"t Tzemach Tzedek*, *Shaar HaMiluim*, section 62)—and to increase in the letters of Torah and *tefillah*, giving *tzedakah* (at least small amounts), and influencing the youth to bring them closer to Torah and *mitzvos*. As our Sages said, "Whoever teaches his friend’s son Torah, it is as if he birthed him." Above all, arrange yourself in a manner of "serve Hashem with joy," as is explained in *Tanya* in many places. When the *yetzer hara* incites you, asking how it is possible for you to be joyful when you know your standing and situation, draw him to the *Tanya Kadisha*, where it is explained at length that one should not mix the joy of the soul with the sadness of the body (and see *Rambam*, end of *Hilchos Lulav*), and how every matter has its time. When one stands with strength of will and persists in *avodah* with joy, one succeeds in this as well, with the additional special assistance from Above, as explained in the holy *Zohar* (Vol. II, 184b, *dih* *Chadva d'bar nash* etc.); study it well there.

I await good news in all the aforementioned, and with true and inner joy.

## 171

171

B"H, 29 Shevat, 5717
Brooklyn

To the esteemed Rabbi, G-d-fearing and diligent in the needs of the community,
R' Shmuel, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter from Erev Shabbos Parshas Yisro.

It was a pleasure for me to read about your travels through many settlements in Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established, and the potential that exists there to strengthen and disseminate Yiddishkeit.

Regarding what you write about urging anash to engage in such activities: I have long been in correspondence with those who listen to my voice, urging them to act in the aforementioned manner in every possible way. It goes without saying that one should not be discouraged if, even at the first attempt, one does not see one hundred percent success. As is well known, the Baal Shem Tov emphasized the verse, "Though your beginning was small, yet your latter end will greatly increase" (and through this, it will increase). This is especially true since we do not know the full picture; man sees with his eyes, yet at the same time, the success may be far greater than what the one exerting himself anticipated, but he does not see it for various reasons.

The most essential point in all of this is that each person must perform the task they were commanded by the Creator of the world and its Guide, the Giver of the Torah. In this, one fulfills the mitzvah of "Love your fellow as yourself" and "You shall surely rebuke your neighbor," and Hashem will do what is good in His eyes. This is also proven by the words of our Sages, that "You shall surely rebuke" implies up to the point of (a reprimand, and some say up to the point of) striking, etc. That is to say, as long as one has not actually been struck, one must continue with the matter of "You shall surely rebuke." Especially since Bnei Yisrael are not suspected, Heaven forbid, of striking in such matters, provided that one speaks to them in the ways of our Torah, of which it is said, "All its ways are ways of pleasantness, and all its paths are peace." We also see tangibly that with such an approach, one succeeds incomparably more than through scolding, reprimanding, shaming someone in public, and the like.

With blessings that we may merit, immediately and imminently, the time when it will be fulfilled, "And they shall make Me a Sanctuary, and I will dwell among them"—within each and every Jew.

***

171

R' Shmuel: Huminer, Jerusalem.

The Baal Shem Tov's emphasis: See also above, 4874, and the references in the footnotes there. Igros Kodesh 18, 366.

## 172

172

*[Shevat 5717]*

...Regarding what was written—some time ago—in Zohar Chadash (107b) explaining "just as the rest of the Bnei Noach"—that this refers to the necessity of doing teshuvah, and not to the assurance that one will be forgiven—there is no issue here of "providing an opening for the wicked," a claim that this provides them with support. And this is clear. See also Gittin 5a, top of the page.

#### ***172***

This letter is to R' Yehoshua Elbaum, and was written on the margin of his letter dated 29 Teves 5717. Additional letters to him appear above in 1583, and in the references noted in the footnotes there.

## 173

173

B"H, Rosh Chodesh Adar Rishon, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter from Tu B’Shvat—the New Year for Trees, which also pertains to man, as it is written, "For man is the tree of the field"—the content of this [connection] is that many of man’s actions are similar to the tree of the field, to that which grows. This means that in those matters where he grows and is educated, he does not undergo a fundamental change, but rather develops from a seed to a sapling, and from a sapling to a tree.

You write regarding your perception, which is not as quick as that of your peers in the yeshiva, etc., and how this causes you a sense of low spirits and discouragement, and [you wonder] what the ultimate outcome will be.

It is well known, the story of our Sages in Avos D’Rabbi Nosson (Chapter 6) regarding Rabbi Akiva, who began to study Torah at the age of forty. The turning point was when he saw drops of water falling upon a stone, drop after drop, until they eventually wore through the stone, as it is written, "Water wears away stones." His end was that he became the teacher of all Israel, raising thousands and myriads of students, and the designation of our Sages—"an anonymous Mishnah," "the Tosefta," etc.—is all according to the opinion of Rabbi Akiva (Sanhedrin 86a).

Now, who is he who would dare to compare himself to Rabbi Akiva? But on the other hand, our Oral and Written Torah are not, Heaven forbid, a collection of stories, but rather "Torah," which implies instruction (*hora'ah*) for the life of every single Jew, since everyone is obligated in the study of Torah. From the aforementioned story, it is understood that even if one’s Torah study is like a drop of water falling upon a stone—where one sees tangibly that its impression is not visible to the physical eye at all—nevertheless, through the strength of will, persistence, diligence, and by not being impressed by the temptations of the *yetzer*, one can reach the greatest heights. The analogy regarding the content of your letter is understood.

You should cease to dwell on measuring the capacity of your intellect. Instead, invest your power of contemplation into the study of our Torah—the Torah of life—and study specifically in the company of friends. It is then almost certain that the headache you write about, which you feel during study, will pass. You should ask your Rosh Yeshiva to provide you with a study partner suitable for you, and when you study with joy, you will eventually be able to share happy news regarding your success in your studies.

It is appropriate to have your tefillin checked, and every weekday, after the morning davening, you should recite some chapters of Tehillim. It is best to follow the monthly schedule, as Tehillim is divided for the days of the month.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,**

**Secretary**

## 174

174

B”H, Erev Rosh Chodesh Adar Rishon, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of the 27th of Shevat, in which you write that you were cautioned regarding what is stated in the Will of Rabbi Yehudah HeChossid concerning one’s dwelling, etc.

Regarding matters such as these, the general approach is that for someone who is not particular, we are not particular with him. However, since you have already been cautioned about this and are asking here as well, and surely the matter does not involve excessive difficulty, you should make an effort to change your dwelling.

Regarding what you wrote about the fracture, you should consult with a specialist; perhaps wearing a suitable brace will suffice. You should also determine the reason why it has flared up again. Based on these two points, you should make your decision, and may Hashem grant you success.

I trust you are keeping the three daily study portions—Chumash, Tehillim, and Tanya—as is well known.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,**

**Secretary**

#### ***174***

**Regarding the dwelling:** That two married brothers should not live under one roof.

## 175

175

B"H, Rosh Chodesh Adar Rishon, 5717
Brooklyn

Peace and Blessing!

In response to your letter, in which you write about your lack of peace of mind, etc.:

It is explained in many places that these matters stem from the *le'umas zeh* (the side of impurity). This implies that they are not within the person’s soul itself, but rather come from outside it. From this, it is understood that it is within a person’s power to rid themselves of them and nullify them completely. Granted, not all individuals and not all situations are the same, and therefore the time required for this is not the same for everyone.

The appropriate path, and the common denominator in all these approaches, is the contemplation that each and every one of us is a part of our society and environment. One must be not only a recipient but also a *mashpia*—or, more accurately, one should primarily be a *mashpia*, with being a recipient only being secondary. Since the essence of a person is their soul and their *tzurah* (form), rather than their body and their *chomer* (matter), it is also understood that the primary influence concerns the spirituality of one’s environment and its *tzurah*. This is achieved by imbuing it with the spirit of life of our Torah—the *Toras Chayim*—which is simultaneously the ultimate in spirituality and that which gives life to the physical and the material, even in the lowest and most lowly levels. This is as per the well-known teaching of our Sages, that the Torah was given specifically to those who have a *yetzer hara*, to Egypt, the "nakedness of the land," and not to the ministering angels (*Shabbos 88b*).

Since this is the primary purpose of man’s creation, he is guaranteed to succeed in it through appropriate *hishtadlus* (effort), provided that he does not let his hands grow slack if he encounters difficulties, and if success is not one hundred percent the first time he tries, or even the second or third time. Success will eventually come.

When one fixes their mind firmly—in the language of the Alter Rebbe—on fulfilling this task, broad horizons of lofty activities are revealed before them. Their *sipuk hanefeshi* (soul satisfaction) grows many times over when they see such a sublime purpose in their life on earth. Furthermore, additional inner content is added even to their private matters, those of their household, and even their own personal affairs. For it is precisely through combining all of this together that success will increase in fulfilling one’s primary task and seeing the reward for one’s labor—by strengthening the *tzurah* over the *chomer*, the spirit over the physical, the soul over the body, and the *nitzotz Eloki* (Divine spark) within them over everything that surrounds them.

One could elaborate on all that has been said at great length, especially in accordance with each person’s environment, their talents, the manner of their upbringing, etc. However, as mentioned above, what has been said is the common denominator for everyone. It is my hope that for you, this will serve as a point that you will develop in accordance with your own environment and personal matters. I look forward to hearing good news.

With blessing,

## 176

176

B"H, Rosh Chodesh Adar Rishon, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of the 23rd of Shevat and the one preceding it, my response was delayed due to a multitude of preoccupations.

It gave me pleasure to read of your efforts to organize a group of bochurim in Tel Aviv for the study of Tanya.

And although, as you write, they are currently searching, we are assured by the power of our holy Torah—Torah Chaim—that "great is study, for it leads to action." While it is true that the time between study and action is not the same for every person, the result is bound to come. It is understood that if you also speak with them about action in addition to study, you will hasten that intended goal. Fortunate is the portion of all those who exert themselves in this and similar matters.

Regarding what you wrote about your material situation, it is understood that one should not be impressed by this at all; it is merely a passing matter. The more you and your wife, may she live and be well, are strong in your trust in the Creator of the world and its Guide, the more the blessings of the Creator will draw near in actuality and in a manner visible even to physical eyes. This is especially true since you are engaged in preparing the vessels for blessing—which are the matters of Torah and mitzvos and exerting influence in your surroundings in this direction. My hope is strong that before long you will bring me good news regarding the improvement of your material situation as well.

Since you do not mention anything regarding health, I interpret this to mean that everything is in order and peaceful, both with your wife, may she live and be well, and with you.

With blessings for a good month and for good tidings.

## 157

157

B"H, Rosh Chodesh Adar Rishon, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed and distinguished, etc., R' Moshe Zev, may he live,

Greetings and blessings!

Following a silence, I was pleased to receive your letter of Wednesday, Parshas Matan Torah, in which you write details of your visit to the Chabad institutions and your perspective on their affairs. I was especially pleased to read about the improvements you have already managed to implement; surely this will increase your energy many times over to continue your efforts in this regard, for "the merit of the many assists."

It would be appropriate for you to also include in your efforts the matter of arousing Anash regarding the absolute necessity of becoming active in Chabad affairs specifically, and in matters of Yiddishkeit in general. One should not be satisfied with merely being in a state of receiving influence in this area, and certainly not, G-d forbid, to be on the level regarding which the Rambam writes, "Awaken, you sleepers... from your slumber" (Hilchos Teshuvah, Chapter 3, Halacha 4). Since you have succeeded in your efforts in other areas, my hope is that success will shine upon you in this as well. As your assistants increase, you will surely find helpers in this point too—that is, in mobilizing Anash for vigorous and expanded activities.

Although you do not mention your wife, may she live, I hope that, as has been your custom since before, she continues to take an active part in the aforementioned activities.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above, with peace of mind.

#### ***157***

**R' Moshe Zev, may he live:** Yaroslavsky, Tel Aviv. Additional letters to him appear above, 4,884, and in the notes indicated there.

## 178

178

B"H, 2 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

To my greatest regret, complaints continue to arrive regarding the manner in which he is conducting himself in the work of Heaven—that is, the matter of chinuch... Even though the statements of our Sages regarding the chinuch of young children, and the responsibility and merit involved therein, are well-known and explicit, and there is an additional matter concerning anash and the temimim based on what our Alter Rebbe wrote in Hilchos Talmud Torah, at the end of the first chapter—words that are truly staggering—such that in truth, only the promise of our Sages that He does not demand except according to their ability makes it possible to engage in this holy work; from this it is understood that if, after all the aforementioned, reports are received that a tamim is not relating to this work of Heaven with a full sense of responsibility, and its impact is immediately noticeable in his classroom, it is beyond belief...

Awaiting good tidings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,**

**Secretary**

## 179

179

B.H., 2 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the Administration of Yeshivas...
May Hashem grant them life,

Peace and Blessing!

In response to your letters (two) dated 21 Shevat and those preceding them:

1) I have already written many times that the benefit of the many takes precedence over the benefit of the individual. From this, it is self-evident that not everyone who wishes to be accepted into the yeshivah must be accepted. For unruly conduct—especially the kind you mention in your letter, where students have attended several yeshivos and were sent home from all of them because of it—is the opposite of the benefit of the many. It is surprising that there is even any doubt regarding this.

2) Regarding what you wrote concerning a student who is already within the walls of the yeshivah and has been studying there for some time, it is understood that this is not comparable to the aforementioned case, especially when there is reason to fear that he will perceive this as a total rejection. However, even here, ultimately—when it comes to the individual versus the many, the many take precedence. Yet, there is no place to begin with such a step; one must first utilize other means and not start with total expulsion, etc. There are many ways to handle such matters if there is constant attention paid to the students' conduct, which naturally prevents the trouble in many areas before it starts, and the intelligent will understand.

3) Regarding individual students, it is understood that the members of the administration must deliberate on the spot, for in every single case there are specific matters that can influence the decision regarding how to conduct oneself with him.

4) Regarding what you wrote about the esteemed Chossid... I found even the initial thought—your suggestion that he teach a class of young Sephardic students—to be surprising. Even though the difficulty with such students is known, it is a matter of discipline in its most simple sense, which requires strong physical health on the part of the teacher and a lack of excessive emotional agitation, etc. From this, it is also understood that even though he promised he would not demand severance pay—and it seems you have seized upon this and want to dismiss him, even without severance—the matter does not seem right to me at all. Severance pay is not because the teacher demands it, but because it is due to him. Furthermore, beyond what has been said, one must contemplate and create possibilities to utilize him in the field of Chabad education, since our resources are extremely limited, and he has already been working in this field for several years.

...And once again, I emphasize most strongly:

9) That if you were to take an interest in the students' situation and their conduct as it ought to be—not only when they are sitting in yeshivah classes, but also during the hours they are at their own disposal, to know what they are occupied with, whom they are meeting, their conduct, their speech, etc.—it is **obvious** that many phenomena would never have occurred. Instead of reporting in every letter you send that the spiritual state of the students is deteriorating, you could have brought news that it is not only holding steady, but improving. I fear that even now, many matters fall into the category of "a pot belonging to partners," where each person finds an explanation that it is not his concern, and that he has already fulfilled his duty during his designated hours, while in the meantime, the state of affairs is heading in the aforementioned direction.

Awaiting good tidings in all the above,

**M. Schneerson**

## 180

180

B"H, 2 Adar, 5717

Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

...Regarding what you wrote concerning health: I believe I have already pointed out in one of my letters the well-known saying of our Sages, of blessed memory, that *Kiddush* and *Havdalah* are deeply connected to the "light of a person’s eyes." From the language of our Sages in this context—"the light of his eyes"—it is also understood that the "light of Torah"—meaning the *pnimiyus* of Torah and its concepts—is also connected to, and serves as a special *segulah* for, the light within a person. And this is easy to understand. May it be His will that you bring good news regarding the above, as well as regarding general matters, in a way that is good, visible, and revealed.

With blessings, and awaiting good news.

#### ***180***

**Kiddush and Havdalah:** See also above, Letter 4880, and the references noted in the footnotes there.

## 181

181

B"H, 5 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

After a very long hiatus, your letter of 24 Shevat was received, in which you write the main points of the farbrengen with the students. Surely, even though you do not mention it specifically, you are endeavoring to inquire in an appropriate manner—with pleasant ways and the necessary discretion—regarding the conduct of the students outside the hours they are under your supervision and oversight. This is especially true regarding the days of Shabbos Kodesh, which are holy to Hashem, yet on the other hand, it is precisely then that the student is left to his own devices, and not all parents know how to guide them in the desired direction.

It is understood that my words here apply not only to you and your class, but to all those engaged in kosher chinuch, pure and holy. As has been explained at length on other occasions, these days are not like the early days, when the role of the cheder and yeshiva was only to add knowledge. In our times, by contrast, their primary role is to educate [the student] to be a Jew who observes Torah and mitzvos. Even regarding children whose homes observe Torah and mitzvos as they should, they too spend time in the street, where undesirable winds blow, etc. From this, it is understood that the responsibility for the conduct of the children on Shabbos and Yom Tov, and in general during those hours when they are not in the appropriate institution, also rests upon the shoulders of their educators. Although it is understood that the form of supervision over them during these days and hours is possible only in a completely different manner than during study hours—and often one requires the assistance of the parents, etc.—the ones who initiate this, and the responsibility, lies, as mentioned, upon the shoulders of the educators.

May it be the will of Hashem that all the above be accepted by you and settled well, and then you will surely find the right words to explain these matters to your colleagues in the field of kosher chinuch, and Hashem will grant you success.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,**

**Secretary**

## 182

[5 Adar, 5717]

In response to your letter, in which you write that you have a *shiur* in *Chassidus* every night, but you do not provide the details—i.e., what you are studying, the length of the study session, and how long you have been maintaining this *shiur*—it is obvious that the extent of one's understanding of the subject matter depends on these factors. Consequently, the specific nature of the answer to your question also depends on them. Furthermore, it is not the proper way to study Torah—any part of it—to begin with questions before one has acquired sufficient knowledge of the subject being questioned.

Since our Sages have instructed us to judge every person favorably, I hope that your intentions are purely for the good. Regarding your question:

What is the purpose of the effort to study *Chassidus Chabad*, and why is it not sufficient to study the *Chassidus* of Poland, given that they all drank from the same fountain—the Baal Shem Tov and the Maggid of Mezeritch?

The crux of the answer is similar to the answer to the question: What is the necessity of studying the *Gemara* in addition to the *Mishnah*, given that since the *Gemara* is mostly an explanation of the *Mishnah*, one should be satisfied with studying the *Mishnah*, as they all drank from the same fountain, etc.? The distinction in the aforementioned case is the same: in the teachings of the students of the Baal Shem Tov, etc., the concepts were presented as individual points. For one who studies them on his own—even if he wishes to explain them with lengthy commentaries—it is not within the capacity of every mind to accurately pinpoint the true interpretation. *Chassidus Chabad* came along, as its name indicates, with the goal of understanding and explaining the concepts that were presented in the "Mishnah" of the teachings of *Chassidus*. Just as in the *Gemara* many matters were added through explanation and *shakla v'tarya*—matters that were only hinted at in the *Mishnah*, and furthermore, new concepts were even innovated in the *Gemara*, and according to our Sages, "everything that a veteran student will eventually innovate"—the same applies here. It is obvious that the *Chabad* teachings also include the "Mishnah" of the Baal Shem Tov's teachings; i.e., it contains both the *Mishnah* and the explanation, and "he who has two hundred has a hundred." It is understood and simple that in the aforementioned, there is, Heaven forbid, no slight—not even the slightest bit—but rather guidance. As is also evident in practice, the addition of *Gemara* study to *Mishnah* study is necessary, and similarly, in our days, the addition of *Chabad* study to the study of the "Mishnah" of *Chassidus* is necessary. And this is well understood. See also the parable cited by the Rashbetz, and the explanation of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory (in *HaTamim*, Vol. 2, p. 56 and onwards).

2) Regarding what you wrote about there being *Chassidim* (and you surely refer to those who study and conduct themselves according to *Chassidus*, not merely those who call themselves *Chassidim*) who delay the time of *Krias Shema* and *tefillah*, etc., because of the *hisbonenus* (contemplation) before *tefillah*, etc.

Their reason and justification are explained in many places: it is because of the preparation for *tefillah*. Not all people are the same regarding the length of time required for this preparation, as it must be suited to their soul’s nature, even though all people are obligated in this preparation. As the *Mishnah* states, "One should only stand to pray with a heavy head (solemnity)," which is brought as a practical ruling in *Orach Chaim*, section 92, in the *Hagah*. This preparation also includes the purification of the body; although the obligation of the *tevilah* of Ezra was abolished, it was only the obligation that was abolished, but the purification of the body is specifically beneficial for *tefillah*.

This is brought by Rav Hai Gaon, and it is further explained according to *Chassidus* in *Likutei Torah* by our Rebbe the Alter Rebbe, *Parshas Tavo*, 59a.

You should refrain from the path of questions, difficulties, and refutations, at a time when one should be filling and utilizing the time with study. In particular, refrain from questioning the conduct of others; it is in your hands to rise early in the morning and prepare in all the aforementioned matters, in a way that you will be able to fulfill *Krias Shema* and *tefillah* at their proper time. And if there is not enough time, you can rise even earlier than the usual wake-up time—who is stopping you?

You surely know and observe the three *shiurim* in *Chumash*, *Tehillim*, and *Tanya*, and in any case, you should observe them from now on.

With blessings.

## 183

183

B"H, 5 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and learned, engaged in communal needs,
R' Alexander Sender, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter from the end of Shevat, which included your notebooks of chiddushim on Shas, and mentioning that you were told it would be worthwhile to publish them for the benefit of those studying Daf Yomi, etc.

Regarding this, one should consult with those who are familiar with this method of study, so that based on that, you may decide on the aforementioned. And although there are surely differences within this as well, there is still a common denominator in this shiur, since it is limited—studying one daf of Gemara per day, one daf of Gemara per day—with limitations above and limitations below. From this, there are also instructions regarding the manner of chiddushim and explanations that are appropriate for such a study.

May it be the will of Hashem that, from a state of expanded mind and proper health, you will increase your efforts to magnify Torah and make it glorious—the study of Nigleh and the study of Chassidus—and in a manner of "learning in order to do," which is a condition both in the study of Nigleh and in the study of Chassidus, for our holy Nasi demands that from every matter there should be action.

With blessings.

***183***

Published in Likutei Sichos, Vol. 21, p. 365, and completed according to the secretariat's copy.

**R' Alexander Sender:** Yudassin, Tel Aviv. Additional letters to him—above 4802, and in the references noted there.

**His chiddushim on Shas:** Published subsequently under the title "Tomchei Temimim" (Holy Land, 5724).

## 184

184

B”H, 5 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

It has been a very long time since any news has been received from you, dating back to your letter from the end of Teves, which itself arrived after a long hiatus.

Regarding what you wrote about the shortcomings in the work of certain teachers, and that this does not have a positive influence on the way they teach the students, etc.:

One sees tangibly in such matters that when one speaks with the teacher in pleasant ways time and again, one eventually succeeds. Especially since all the teachers are surely G-d-fearing and tremble at His word; when one explains the concept of "doing the work of Hashem" as elucidated in the Shas and Poskim—among them the Laws of Torah Study by our Alter Rebbe at the end of Chapter 1—this will surely bring about a change for the better, a change that impacts actual practice.

I was pleased to hear that you succeeded in opening the restaurant, and that you have also managed to increase the number of meals. I look forward to continuing to hear good news in this regard. After all, "great is the drink that brings [people] close," especially since in such instances, the time of eating should be utilized for education in the customs and laws of Yisrael, in the laws of proper conduct, etc., etc.

You should certainly utilize the upcoming days of Purim to strengthen the bonds between the students and the teachers, and furthermore—and this is the main point—with the parents of the students. Beyond the fact that this relates to the education of the students, the matter itself, as an influence upon the parents, is of the highest order. This, too, is educational work, and it is called "the work of Hashem," with all the merit and responsibility attached to it, and the magnitude of the reward, which cannot be sufficiently explained.

The Pidyon Nefesh was received.

With blessings for success in your holy work and for good tidings in all the aforementioned.

## 185

B"H, 6 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings,

In response to your letter of Sunday, in which you outline the main points of your life’s history and conclude by asking for my opinion regarding your future arrangements.

Regarding the two options you mention: since you have studied for some time in yeshiva, and have also spent time studying toward receiving *heter hora'ah* (rabbinic ordination), it is appropriate that you continue this study at least until you receive *semicha*. As for your doubt regarding whether you have the ability to serve as a rabbi, etc.—given that the number of communities in need of G-d-fearing rabbis is constantly growing, "even the weak shall say, 'I am a hero'" (Yoel 4:10). Furthermore, it is highly likely that your concerns regarding your own talents in this regard are unfounded. There is nothing that can stand in the way of *ratzon* (will); if you require any further development in order to influence others or the like, in order to succeed in a rabbinic position, this too will come...

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**
**Secretary**

P.S. You surely know and observe the three daily *shiurim*—Chumash, Tehillim, and Tanya—and at the very least, you should observe them from this point forward.

## 186

186

B"H, 6 Adar Rishon, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

I received your letter with great astonishment, in which you write about the despondency of your husband, *shichyeh*. Is it believable that after having witnessed the wonders of the Creator, *yisbarach*, and His kindnesses in a tangible way—which should awaken great joy, for in the light of the King’s countenance is life, the King of Kings, the Holy One, Blessed be He—there should instead be despondency? It is clear that this is merely from the enticements of the *yetzer*. My hope is strong that this state was only a passing matter, and that it has already passed and gone completely. We have been promised by our holy Torah, the Torah of life, that whoever is shown kindness, it is for long, good days and years; surely this promise will be fulfilled regarding you as well. I look forward to hearing good news regarding the above, and very soon.

With blessings.

## 187

187

B"H, 6 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter from Sunday.

As you requested, I will mention you and your wife—may they live—at the holy resting place of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, for the fulfillment of your hearts' desires for good and for revealed, tangible good.

Based on what is explained in many places, it is understood that to the extent that you increase your influence in your surroundings by spreading and strengthening Yiddishkeit—regarding which our Sages said, "What is the fruit of mitzvos?" and "Whoever teaches his fellow’s son Torah... it is as if he birthed him"—the measure of the Holy One, blessed be He, is "measure for measure," only many times over. The reward is in revealed, tangible good, and for the fulfillment of "And you shall teach them to your children" in its literal sense, in addition to the interpretation that this refers to students. There is nothing that can stand before the will, especially since there are surely a number of Torah-observant Jews in Los Angeles who can participate and assist in your work in the aforementioned.

You have surely met with the esteemed Chossid, etc., Raitshik, may he live, and it would be appropriate for you to set a regular time to study Chassidus together. May Hashem grant you success.

With blessings for good tidings.

## 188

B"H, 7 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished Rabbi Yaakov, may he live:

Peace and blessing!

In response to your two letters from the 26th and 28th of Shevat, in which you write regarding the various proposals you received from the people of Porto Alegre and its activists.

Regarding the matter of the school where several hundred children study, and the director—as you write—is not an observer of Torah and mitzvos, etc.:

In my opinion, for the time being, it is not advisable for you to enter that position as a subordinate of the director. If you do, it is highly unlikely that you will be able to change matters for the time being, and there is room for concern that they will use you as a "hechsher" (a stamp of approval) for all the school’s conduct.

It would be different if your involvement were in a different capacity, where you would influence them to improve as much as possible in school matters, without being dependent on them for your livelihood. In such a case, they would certainly be more inclined to listen to your wishes and instructions, and they would not be able to justify all their conduct by claiming it has your agreement, since you would not be one of the people responsible for the school's affairs.

This does not rule out the possibility of you teaching a religious subject at the school at set times, provided that it is done in a way that leaves no room for any concern of a "hechsher." If one must be cautious about this, then on the contrary, it is a good thing, because through this you will know what is happening there and they will be more compelled to listen to your guidance. Furthermore, once they see that you come and go in the school, yet you do not undermine their livelihood or their honor, etc., this too will serve to increase your influence there...

## 189

189

B"H, 7 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the Administration of Talmud Torah Beis Yosef
Lubavitch Yeshiva in Toronto
May Hashem preserve them and grant them life.

Peace and Blessing!

I was pleased to receive your letter from the day that was "doubled in goodness" (Tuesday), in which you write about the commencement of your holy work in various areas, as well as the main points of your work plan for the near future. Surely—even if it was not explicitly mentioned in your letter—among the lines of your work is the effort to bring in more students. Although there may be difficulties in this, as we are now in the middle of the school term, we have seen the experience from the United States—and surely the situation in Toronto is the same—that there are always those who, for various reasons, want to and are able to change their place of study, even in the middle of the term. Even if they are not many, he who sustains one soul of Yisrael is considered as if he sustained an entire world. In our days, education in a school on the purity of holiness is not merely a matter of adding knowledge in Torah and its subjects; rather, the entire connection of the student to Yiddishkeit in general—whether for the good and as is fitting, or, heaven forbid, the opposite—depends upon it.

I have instructed the administration of Merkos L'Inyonei Chinuch to send, as a gift to the library attached to the institution, a number of books from their publications. Surely you have the Kehot Publication Society catalog at hand, and it would be appropriate for you to write to Merkos to specify which books are particularly suitable for the age and knowledge level of the students.

With a blessing for success in your holy work—an extraordinary success, commensurate with the demand and the necessity of the hour for increased courage and immense strength in the education of the children of Yisrael on the purity of holiness, in the spirit of the Yisrael Sabba.

Awaiting good tidings in all the aforementioned.

## 190

B"H, 9 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter from the end of Shevat, in which you write about your resolution regarding how to utilize your school vacation days, and it seems to you that you did not succeed in actualizing your resolution as you had wished, particularly regarding your studies.

It is surprising that you do not mention the manner of your study, which certainly should not have been done in isolation. As is well known, Chazal have taught the necessity of studying specifically with a *chavrusa*. Surely, even in your current location, it is possible to find a *chavrusa* for this. It is possible that this condition was not met as required—at the very least, this implies that there was no *chavrusa* at all, or an unsuitable *chavrusa*, which also diminishes the success of one’s studies. In any case, even if there is no *chavrusa*, it is understood that Torah study is mandatory. It is likewise simple that despair and a feeling of defeat do not stem from the *sitra d’kedusha*, for we see tangibly that this is not the way to arouse diligence and perseverance, as is explained in many places, including in *Tanya*.

My hope is strong that through the effort to ensure that your study is at least with one other person, and through the endeavor to study with joy and a strong trust in the Giver of the Torah, you will increase the success of your studies. It would be very good to make sure to recite the monthly *Tehillim* *shiur*—according to the division of *Tehillim* for the days of the month—each day after morning *davening*, to give *tzedakah* each weekday before morning *davening*, and to be careful regarding *tevillas Ezra*. All of this adds *siyata d’shmaya* in what a person and his household need in general, and in matters of Torah and *mitzvos* in particular.

You do not mention in your letter the activities of your wife, *tichyeh*. My hope is that she, too, is endeavoring to utilize her talents in the field of proper education in an appropriate manner. May Hashem grant you success in all that has been mentioned.

With blessings for good tidings.

## 191

B"H, 9 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

I have received your letters from 26 Shevat and 3 Adar, including the enclosure. I am responding to them without waiting for the regular correspondence queue, as it is evident from your letter that you are in a state of great agitation regarding the letters you have received and are forwarding to me. Furthermore, your concerns that perhaps letters sent here were inappropriate or undesirable are also surprising.

Your agitation is both surprising and puzzling. Anyone familiar with communal affairs and the needs of the community knows that such undesirable phenomena are almost always present. Who is greater than Moshe Rabbeinu? Yet our Torah informs us, "and they gazed after Moshe," as per the well-known Chazal on this matter. It is obvious that the Torah does not recount this merely as a story, especially not to publicize the improper conduct of the *Dor Deah* (the Generation of Knowledge). Rather, there is a lesson here: one who is engaged in communal work should not lose heart if they encounter a similar situation, for this has already occurred throughout history.

It is also understood that this lesson is not intended solely to prevent the communal worker from becoming discouraged, but—more importantly—to strengthen them in their efforts, so that not only will there be no weakening, but they will actually increase their activity. Further elaboration on this point is unnecessary.

Although I trust that there was no weakening in your work due to the aforementioned letters, our Sages have said, "One only urges those who are already diligent." The main point is that the times demand not merely the absence of weakening, but rather an increase from time to time, as necessitated by the circumstances—especially since it is a command to "increase in holiness."

...Your concerns that perhaps letters regarding your activities and work have reached me and made an impression, etc., etc.—are unfounded...

With blessings for your work with joy and great success, and for good tidings in your personal matters as well,

**M. Schneerson**

## 192

192

[10 Adar, 5717]

...I would appreciate it if you would write, at the very least, an outline of your involvement in the matters of Chassidus and the inner point of spreading the wellsprings outward. Surely you will not be satisfied with what has been accomplished until now, for the commandment of the Torah is that "we increase in holiness," and the commandment of the Torah itself provides the strength (in addition to the strengths that come through nature and the *neshamah* of the *nefesh elokis*), provided that a person utilizes them to the full extent required. The reward for one’s involvement in this is beyond expression. And the essence of the reward is—as the *Mishnah* states—that the reward of a *mitzvah* is the *mitzvah* itself, which is to say, an increase in success and new activities...

#### ***192***

Published in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 18, p. 254.

## 193

193

B”H, 10 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter dated 29 Shevat, along with the enclosure, in which you write regarding the health condition of your mother, may she live and be well.

Since the regimen of the vegetarian doctor has been successful regarding your mother, it is appropriate that you inform him of the recent treatments, the doctor’s examinations, etc. (which are returned herewith), and follow his instructions regarding the future. Especially since the physician currently treating your mother says that the vegetarian’s diet is appropriate. If it is possible to arrange a consultation between the two doctors, that would be best; however, if that is not possible, you should rely on the opinion of the vegetarian doctor.

With blessings for good news regarding the above, as well as in general matters, and especially regarding the activities of Tzeirei Agudas Chabad and the activities with your former colleague. I hope that you are also maintaining contact with those you met during the days of 19 Kislev of this past year, as well as those before and after.

#### ***193***

Printed in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 21, p. 458, and completed based on a copy.

## 194

194

B.H., 10 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from Erev Shabbos Terumah and the one preceding it. As you requested, I will mention you at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, for the improvement of your health and for good tidings in all the matters about which you write.

Regarding what you wrote—that for the time being you have not been offered a job—it is understood that, as with all matters of livelihood, one must create a *keli* (vessel), as explained in *Kuntres U'Maayan*. It is not the proper way to wait until others offer; rather, one must come forward with proposals of his own, and so forth. This is especially true since the matter concerns you most directly. According to the story told by my father-in-law, the Rebbe, in the name of his grandfather, the Rebbe Maharash, of blessed memory: when it comes to a matter that touches one’s true essence, one reaches the depths of intellect, broad reasoning, and wondrous inventions. May Hashem grant you success, in good health and with peace of mind, to engage in *hafatzas hamaayanos chutzah*.

With blessings for good tidings in communal matters and in your personal affairs,

**M. Schneerson**

#### ***194***

**And according to the story:** See *Hayom Yom*, 9 Elul.

## 195

B”H, 11 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished individual, who engages in communal needs,
R’ Yaakov Yosef, may he live, Shochet and Bodek

Shalom u’Bracha!

In response to your letter of the 4th of Adar.

It is obvious that my words regarding the separation of maaser were intended only to add, and G-d forbid, not to detract from the holy. From this it is understood that the donations you have made until now to various institutions should not be reduced in the future. On the contrary, this also includes Chabad institutions, for funds that enter the Rebbe’s treasury are used for many matters, and it will not always correspond to the donations that those institutions previously received from a specific individual, and so on. Whoever has the ability may separate maaser for the Rebbe’s treasury in addition to the aforementioned; whoever does not have the ability may include his aforementioned donations in the calculation of his maaser, and contribute the remainder to the Rebbe’s treasury.

Certainly, for someone like yourself, it is superfluous to point out the well-known statement of our Sages that one should not be overly precise with tzedakah, and that one should add a certain amount to the maaser designated for tzedakah. There is room to say that by doing so, one gains an additional advantage: the restriction of a vow (neder) does not apply to it, since the percentage is not always uniform, unlike when one is precise with the maaser.

Regarding what you wrote about the study of Chassidus: not all people are the same in this regard, although generally, the common denominator is that the more one increases, the more praiseworthy it is. Furthermore, through this, one also sees success over time, and understanding comes to him more easily than at first. Likewise, the common denominator for everyone is that there must be study for the sake of fluency (ligras) and study for the sake of depth (le’iyuna), and the study for depth should be in the area that his heart desires, according to the well-known statement of our Sages...

With blessings for mazal tov and good tidings.

***

195

R’ Yaakov Yosef: Raskin, Rishon LeZion. Additional letters to him: above, Vol. 3, 784; Vol. 5, 1765.

Regarding the separation of maaser: To the Rebbe’s treasury. See also below, letter 529.

## 196

196

B.H., 11 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To Mr. Yitzchak, may he live,

Peace and blessing!

I received your letter of 7 Shevat, which was delayed in transit. In the meantime, you have surely received my letter responding to your previous one, and I look forward to hearing good news regarding the matters contained therein.

Regarding what you write concerning your seminar and the discussion that followed your lecture, etc.:

I have already stated that at all times, and especially in this era, the virtue of the youth is that they are not satisfied with compromises and do not subscribe to such an approach. Even those among them whose own way of life is a compromise still wish to be presented with an absolute and consistent system. Surely the youth in Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established, possess this trait—and, in fact, with even greater intensity. Therefore, this is the place to inform them of matters as they are in their truth regarding Yiddishkeit, the lifestyle of our ancestors, and the like. At most, one can add afterwards that there is no perfection in creation, and therefore there is no place for discouragement, and certainly not for despair, even if one does not currently find sufficient spiritual courage to accept the system in its entirety, and all the more so to act in practice in accordance with this system. However, one must recognize the truth as it is, and in any case, begin to act in practice in those areas and matters for which the student and listener is already prepared, and add from time to time as much as possible. The statement of the Mishnah, "a mitzvah leads to another mitzvah," is also understood by the intellect and is tangibly visible, and it explains this approach even further.

I was pleased to read at the end of your letter that you have reviewed the material of the sichos for the youth, and surely you will continue to assist them in the future as much as possible—and certainly not only in editing the style.

Regarding what you noted concerning the matter explained in the maamar "Mayim Rabim," and regarding the nations of the world who were not commanded against *shituf* (associating other powers with Hashem), and whether it is possible to say regarding the nations of the world, in relation to their seventy ministers, that it is analogous to the partnership of a father and mother:

In my opinion, it is impossible to say this at all. For although the nations of the world are not commanded against the prohibition of *shituf*, they were certainly not commanded to associate others, Heaven forbid. Indeed, regarding a father and mother, we are commanded to honor and fear them.

The reason for this is also understood: the seventy ministers are not beings with free choice like other angels (see Tanya, beginning of Chapter 39, and Likutei Torah, Emor 38b). Rather, since the influence for the nations of the world ultimately descends through the seventy ministers, this provides more room for error, and they were not commanded to be wary of this error (see Rashi, Devarim 4:19).

As you requested, I will mention you at the tziyun of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, for physical health. It is already known what our Torah, the Torah of life, has ruled—and in the words of the great teacher (Rambam, Hilchos Deos, beginning of Chapter 4)—that having a healthy and sound body is one of the ways of Hashem.

## 197

197

B"H, 11 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

I acknowledge receipt of your letter from Wednesday. It is surprising to read what you write regarding your downcast spirit, especially since Divine Providence has placed you in a position to encourage the spirits of the downcast. Since the attribute of the Ribono shel Olam is *middah k'neged middah* (measure for measure)—and indeed, many times over—you are certainly being encouraged from Above in various matters and ways, so that, to begin with, you should not even come to such a state, *chas v'shalom*. This is in line with the verse, "I will not place upon you [the diseases], for I am Hashem your Healer," which is the manner of healing of the Creator and Guide of the world: that, to begin with, an undesirable situation should not occur.

This is in contrast to healing that takes place after a situation has already occurred, where at least some impression remains, as our Sages of blessed memory state in *Yoma 86a*.

May it be the will of Hashem that from now on, the statement "Serve Hashem with joy" be even more emphasized by you. This is a form of *avodah* that the Baal Shem Tov, his disciples, and his disciples' disciples—the Nesi'ei HaChassidus—strove so much to disseminate. This is especially so according to the ruling of the Rambam regarding the necessity of this specific manner of *avodah*; see his words in *Hilchos Lulav*. They are wonderful words that provide direction for the perplexed in this matter, especially in our generation, which demands additional strength from each and every one of us to fight against the undesirable forces. It is a well-known saying of the Rebbe, my father-in-law, that a soldier going out to war sings a joyful song of victory while in the midst of the battle; this in itself enables and brings closer the victory. And this is understood.

These days are auspicious for adding in all of the above, as our Sages of blessed memory ruled: "When Adar enters, we increase in joy." In this year, an additional thirty days have been added to the fulfillment of this, and it is simple that the intention is to draw this joy into the entire year.
