With respect and blessings.

#### ***197***

Printed in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 19, p. 521.

**Saying:** See also Letter 100 above, and the references in the notes there.

## 198

198

B"H, 11 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn.

Mrs. Miriam, may she live,

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of the 27th [of Shevat], in which you write that, unfortunately, only a portion of the members are attending the study sessions in the group that you lead.

One of the pieces of advice to rectify this is to involve the members in the work itself—meaning, not only that they should come to listen, but that they should participate in the activity, even through discussions, questions and answers. From time to time, each of the members should provide an explanation and assessment of the subjects being studied, having prepared for it beforehand. Involving them more in the work can also be achieved by issuing a bulletin where everyone takes on a suitable part.

Certainly, you also have studies for yourself in addition to what you teach the group, and it would also be appropriate for this to be done in a *chavrusa* with a few women.

With blessings for success and for good news regarding all the above.

***

**198**

**Mrs. Miriam, may she live:** Gordon, Worcester.

## 199

B"H, 12 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 4 Adar.

At an *eis ratzon*, I will mention all those you wrote about at the holy *tziyon* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, *ztzukulleh"h, nevag"m, zya*, for whatever they may need, as you wrote. Certainly, when you have good news regarding this, do not withhold the good from informing me. This is one of the details of the fundamental *mitzvah*, "Love your fellow as yourself"—to cause joy to a fellow Jew. If in all generations people yearned for this, how much more so in our current era, which is full of hardships and sorrow, where every matter of goodness and joy is most precious.

Regarding what you write about your son, *sheyichyeh*—it would be proper for him to be careful to put on *tefillin* every weekday, and that the *tefillin* should be checked and *kosher* in all their details: the *parshiyos*, the *batim*, the straps, and the manner of tying them. Since the matter of *tefillin* is to subjugate the heart and the brain—so that both the intellect and the emotions will be aligned with the will of the *Mitzvos* and the Creator of the world (see *Tanya*, beginning of Chapter 41)—I am confident that this will bring a change for the better in your son’s thoughts and feelings, and the treatment through natural means by a specialist in this field will then succeed.

It is surely unnecessary to elaborate to someone like you that since Divine Providence has placed you in the field of education as a school principal, the more you increase your efforts to bring the hearts of the students closer to our Father in Heaven, so that they may be "wholehearted with *Havayah* our G-d" and healthy in their souls, then in the measure of the *Ribono shel Olam*—which is *middah k'neged middah*, but many times over—there will be an increase in the health of your son and your household.

With blessings for success in raising students who fulfill their role in life as the *Mishnah* states (end of *Kiddushin*), "I was created to serve my Maker," and for good news regarding all the above.

Awaiting good news regarding the above.

## 5100

B"H, 12 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, G-d-fearing and learned, etc., Moreinu HaRav Mordechai Aharon, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter of 3 Adar, in which you write regarding secular newspapers—and those even worse, in which not only secular matters are printed, but also matters that are forbidden—and how one must be careful to avoid them.

Indeed, matters that are well-known require no proof. It is an explicit ruling in the Shulchan Aruch that it is forbidden to read forbidden materials, and it seems that recently, letters and proclamations regarding this have been published in Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established.

It is my hope that since you write to me regarding the above, you are surely interested in the study of Chassidus—and interested in the literal sense, meaning that you have fixed times for studying the teachings of the Baal Shem Tov, his students, and his students' students. This also leads to conduct that is consistent with the teachings of Chassidus, as our Sages of blessed memory said, "Great is study, for it leads to action." However, since everything requires Siyata Dishmaya—and all the more so to merit seeing the pleasantness of Hashem in the inner dimensions of Torah—you will surely exert influence from here on in every place you can reach to disseminate this study. For in the measure of the Holy One, Blessed be He—which is measure for measure, only many times over—may you be privileged to see good fruit from your labor in Torah and in the inner dimensions of Torah. May it be His will that you soon bring good tidings regarding all that has been mentioned.

With blessing,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**
**Secretary**

P.S.
You are surely aware of the takanah of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, regarding the three daily study portions accessible to every soul—in Chumash, Tehillim, and Tanya—which are well-known. In any event, please observe them from here on.

## 101

B"H, 12 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished R' Yitzchak Nach, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter of Monday, in which you write regarding the signing of the contract and regarding a *heter iska*.

The text of a *heter iska* can be found in several *sefarim*, among them the *Kitzur Shulchan Aruch* published by Rabbi Feldman. In your case, a contract is simpler than a standard loan. If you cannot find the text, you can add the words "according to *heter iska*," which will suffice until a Rav can draw up a precise version. If the words "according to *heter iska*" can be written on the contract itself, that is certainly best. If, for any reason, it cannot be written on the contract, you can write on a separate paper: "That the contract between so-and-so and so-and-so, which was made on such-and-such a date, is according to *heter iska*. And to this I have come to sign on the day of [date], in the year [year], here in the city of [city]. Signed [signature]." And then it should be written: "In our presence, the undersigned witnesses, the aforementioned matter was performed, and in our presence, so-and-so and so-and-so signed with their own hands. Signed [witness], signed [witness]."

Most importantly, I was pleased to read in your letter that your father, may he live, is feeling better. May Hashem help that it should go from good to even better, and may you report good news regarding all the others you mentioned in your letter.

With blessing,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

**Secretary**

## 500

B"H, 12 Adar Rishon, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 7 Adar Rishon.

1) Regarding your question on the interpretation of the matter of "studying as a recipient" — it is surprising that one always searches for hidden intentions and allusions, when the simple, literal meaning is also sufficient. The intention of the aforementioned is that if you had not received the position of *hashgacha* (supervision), you would certainly have studied as a recipient; therefore, even after accepting the aforementioned position, you must establish **study** as a recipient, even if the consistency is in a smaller quantity due to your involvement in the matter of *hashgacha*.

2) Regarding what you wrote about how to conduct yourself now concerning the length of *davening*, since the matter of *hashgacha* weighs upon you — here too, your question is surprising. Is it not a definitive *halacha* in the *Hilchos Talmud Torah* of our *Alter Rebbe*, end of Chapter 1? However, it is understood that you can fulfill the length during the times available to you in a way that will not affect your health or the *hashgacha*, or at a time when you will have a completely suitable replacement...

With blessings for success in your holy work, with proper health — including physical health in the literal sense — and expansion of the mind.

## 103

103

B"H, 13 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed and distinguished individual, who engages in the work of Heaven,
Rabbi Tzvi Hirsch, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter from Erev Shabbos and its enclosure, I trust that in the meantime you have received my letter responding to your previous one, and it is my hope that by the time you receive this letter, the state of health of... [your child], may he live, has already improved.

Regarding what you wrote about the vaccination: in this country, it is customary to do this for all children. However, there is room to clarify the origin of the vaccine—that is, from which manufacturer it comes—and whether it is tested and proven. Since you wrote that many in your city are doing this, there is no reason to be an exception in this matter. May Hashem grant you and your wife, may she live, the merit to raise him to Torah, to the chuppah, and to good deeds, with peace of mind and proper health.

With blessings.

***

**Rabbi Tzvi Hirsch, may he live:** Tzaler, Basel. Additional letters to him appear above in 4,881, and in the notes referenced there.

**Regarding the vaccination:** See also the letter above, 4,862, and the notes referenced there.

## 104

104

B"H, 14 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn.

My dear uncle, the esteemed and G-d-fearing, our teacher Rabbi Ben Zion, may he live and be well,

Peace and blessing!

It was with joy that I received your letter of the 7th of Adar, in which you write regarding the improvement of your health and that you have already settled into your apartment. May it be the will of Hashem that your general settlement regarding matters of livelihood also be realized very soon, in accordance with your desires.

Regarding what you write—that to achieve various matters in Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established, one must resort to acquaintances and *protektzia*—I am certain that *anash* there will surely assist you in any way you propose to them. It is understood and simple that if I am able to do anything in this regard, I will do so with a willing heart, provided that you inform me of the details: to whom, and regarding what.

And regarding what you wrote concerning the language: surely for most immigrants, their knowledge of the language is very slight, yet in the end, they manage. As we hear here, even in the various offices, in recent years they take into account that it is impossible for a recent immigrant to have an extensive knowledge of the language, as that requires time. If in our country, in the past, there were daunting difficulties, and yet He who sustains and provides for all provided the livelihood for him and his household, surely there is no room at all for worry in this matter. As we have seen tangibly, as the measure of *bitachon* grows, so too does one's energy, and consequently, one's settlement also progresses.

Thank you for the regards from several *anash* whom you mention at the end of your letter. If, in your opinion, it is their desire and permitted according to the situation there to send them packages, perhaps you would be so kind as to inform me of the address to which the packages should be sent, and I will endeavor to do so.

Awaiting good news in all that was mentioned, and may it be the will of Hashem that just as you and your wife, may she live and be well, have seen the kindnesses of Hashem until now, so too may you find only goodness and kindness in your current location for a successful settlement, with health and peace of mind.

I sign with honor and blessing.

#### ***104***

**Rabbi Ben Zion:** Schneerson, Tel Aviv.

**From several *anash*:** From behind the Iron Curtain.

## 505

B"H, 14 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed and distinguished individual, who engages in communal needs, Moreinu HaRav Shlomo HaKohen, and his wife, our relative, Mrs. Dvorah Leah, may she live and be well.

Greetings and blessings!

Since we did not have the opportunity to meet on the day of the Hilula of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, I am writing to you, as I frequently inquire about and receive regards regarding how you are doing.

I am taking this occasion and the opportunity of Purim Katan—which, along with its month, Adar Rishon, is included in the directive of our Sages, "When Adar enters, we increase in joy"—to wish that this joy be extended from this month and these days to the entire year. May it be a joy consistent with the teachings of the Baal Shem Tov, the teachings of Chassidus: a joy of the soul and a joy of the body, a joy from spiritual matters and a joy from material matters. May it come about through true health and peace of mind. In the witty phrasing of the verse, "This small one shall be great," may it be that from this Purim Katan—about which it is said that even a small joy "breaks through barriers"—all of Bnei Yisrael, and each one of us among them, merit the fulfillment of the promise, "You shall take the head of Bnei Yisrael," to lead them out of their straits and limitations into the expanse in all their affairs.

With respect and blessing.

#### ***505***

**Moreinu HaRav Shlomo:** Rabbi Shlomo Hornstein. Another letter to him appears above, Vol. 8, p. 236.

## 156

B"H, 14 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

Through the intermediary of the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and refined man, who is involved in communal needs with great energy and possesses fine character traits, R' Yehuda Zvi Fogelman, I received your letter informing me of what has transpired with you and the state of your health.

At an auspicious time, I will mention you at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, for an improvement in your health.

I am confident that when you see the kindness of Hashem in this, you will certainly increase your strength in matters of Torah and mitzvos. This applies not only to yourself, but also in influencing your surroundings as much as is necessary. It is understood and simple that first and foremost are the members of your household, and in particular, your children. In the language of the Mishnah, among the mitzvos a father has toward his son is to teach him Torah. The purpose of Torah study is to "observe and to perform," and the central point is as it is written: "And Hashem commanded us to perform all these statutes, to fear Hashem our G-d." Although there is a difference in the manner of study when a son is young versus when he is older—as the Sages state (Kiddushin 30a), "until your hand is upon your neck"—this only concerns the manner of study. But even afterwards, one is obligated in this, in addition to the mitzvah of "You shall surely rebuke your neighbor" and "Love your neighbor as yourself," and the matter is understood.

We have seen tangibly that when one makes the effort, once, twice, and three times, using appropriate methods, one eventually succeeds, for there is nothing that can stand before the will. This is especially true when a son has influence in a certain environment, where the aforementioned study affects many of the children of Israel; therefore, even additional exertion and effort are worthwhile.

May Hashem, the Giver of the Torah, who sees into the heart, see your resolve in this matter and increase your merit to hasten your recovery.

With respect and blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned.

## 1007

B"H, 14 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

...Especially since this day is *Purim Katan*, and it, along with its month, *Adar Rishon*, are included in the directive of our Sages, "When *Adar* enters, we increase in joy." May it be the will [of Hashem] that this joy be drawn from this month and these days into the entire year, and that it be a joy consistent with the teachings of the Baal Shem Tov—the teachings of Chassidus: a joy of the *neshamah* and also a joy of the body, a joy from material matters and a joy from spiritual matters, which should come through health and true *harchavas hada'as*.

And to use a play on words, "this small one shall be great"—from this *Purim Katan*, of which it is said that even a small joy "breaks through barriers," may all *Bnei Yisrael*, and each one of us among them, merit the fulfillment of the promise, "You shall lift the head of *Bnei Yisrael*," to bring them out from narrowness and limitation into the expanse in all their affairs.

With blessings for good tidings.

#### ***1007***

A similar version was written to several others.

## 208

B"H, 14 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and learned, engaged in communal needs,
R' Alexander Sender, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 7 Adar, it gave me pleasure to read of your visit to Maoz Aviv. It is my hope that even when you were forced to cut your visit short, you maintained contact with them through correspondence, and especially now, you should strengthen your influence there, since your health has improved...

Regarding what you wrote about the story told by the Mashpia, R' Shneur Zalman Gourary, of blessed memory, concerning the Tzemach Tzedek’s response not to "expand" [mitpashet] etc., there is a place where this story is cited in *Drushei Chassunah*, Kuntres 2; see there for the exact wording of the Tzemach Tzedek.

Regarding what you wrote about the conduct of a Yahrzeit when it falls during the period of mourning*, to my knowledge, the custom is in accordance with those who hold that one conducts oneself as one does on any other Yahrzeit.

May Hashem bless each and every one of you, and may you soon bring tidings of only good news, in a way that is good, visible, and revealed. And regarding matters that are not desired, may the principle of these months be fulfilled in them: "And it was turned around" [v'nahapoch hu] into joy and a festive day.

With blessings.

***

208

R' Alexander Sender: Yudassin, Tel Aviv. Additional letters to him: see above 4,802, and the references in the notes there.

*) See Responsa *Duda'ei HaSadeh*, 54; *Yalkut Yosef*, 14; *Toras Mordechai*, 166; *Yalkut Das V'Din*, p. 56.

Story: And the Tzemach Tzedek answered him: "One must not 'expand' [mitpashet]; then there will not be a [negative] impression."

In *Drushei Chassunah*: *Sefer HaMaamarim Kuntresim*, Vol. 1, p. 19a. See also above, Vol. 13, Letter 4,291.

Regarding the conduct of a Yahrzeit: See *Rosh Chodesh*.

## 159

14 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn

Greetings and Blessings!

I was pleased to receive the news through the esteemed Chossid, a man of many accomplishments, [Name], that with the help of Hashem, you have set the date for your daughter’s wedding. May Hashem Yisbarach help that it should be in a good and auspicious hour, and that she may build a home that is truly happy.

It is surely unnecessary to remind you that the happiness of every person depends upon the blessings of Hashem Yisbarach, the Creator and Guide of the world, who provides individual providence for each of us within the entirety of Klal Yisrael. From this, it is also understood how necessary it is to conduct oneself according to the instructions of the Creator of the world, as He has prescribed in His holy Torah. When one does so, one has a strong and justified hope to receive the blessings of Hashem Yisbarach. If this is true at all times, it is even more important in the preparation for a wedding, for that is when a new period in a person’s life begins.

From this, it is understood that you and your wife, may they live, will surely utilize your fullest influence upon your daughter, may she live, so that she should take upon herself a firm resolution—and most importantly, translate it into action—to observe all those matters that depend upon her, so that her home will be conducted in the Jewish way, which brings happiness into the home. Specifically, this includes observing the laws of Taharas HaMishpacha (which should be begun even before the chuppah as a preparation for it), observing the kashrus of food, keeping Shabbos, etc. May Hashem Yisbarach help you to report good tidings regarding all that has been mentioned above.

With blessings of Mazal Tov, Mazal Tov,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,**

**Secretary**

## 118

B"H, 16 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

...A remarkable piece of information has reached me (which perhaps partially explains the remarkable difficulties you are encountering regarding shidduchim). It is that for a period of time while you were in..., despite the fact that you traveled from here with a specific intention, you chose to study in a yeshiva that wages war against the study of Chassidus and more... In any event, may it be the will of Hashem that you have fulfilled your obligation for all of the above through the anguish you have experienced until now, and from here on, may Hashem guide you speedily, in a manner that is good, both visibly and revealed.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe Shlita,**

**Secretary**

## 5211

5211

By the Grace of Hashem, 16 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn.

Mrs. Meri, may she live:

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letters of 27/1 and 13/2, I am rushing to send my reply, as I see from your letter that you are upset regarding the explanation you were given concerning the photographing of the Sefer HaRikmah.

As far as I know about the offset printing process—in which we have had much experience—there is always the possibility of first making an offset of the sefer as it is. The pasting and smudging of the margins is done not on the original, but on the offset copy. It is nothing more than the fact that in such a case, one must photograph and pay twice. This can also be done through various libraries, as the larger libraries have photostat services. Especially when it concerns a scholarly work, they allow one to photostat the sefarim they have in their possession. When things get closer to the actual work, one will be able to find out the price and where the sefer is located.

As for a library giving a sefer to be taken apart and later glued back together, as far as I know, this is against the law. It is self-understood that there is a possibility that a private individual who possesses the sefer might provide it for this purpose. Perhaps you know of such people to whom your husband gave the sefer as a gift when it was first published, and in that case, one could approach them with the aforementioned request.

It is also understood that when photostating library copies, one only needs the pages that contain the marginal notes. For the pages that do not have any marginalia, one can photograph them from your own copy—and only once—which will reduce the printing expenses.

In any event, in my opinion, the whole matter still has time. First, the preparation of the marginal notes should be completed, and then a way will be found not to smudge the original.

With wishes for good news regarding the above.

## 1112

1112

B”H, 17 Adar Rishon, 5717
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the Administration of the Reshet,
May Hashem preserve them and grant them life,

Shalom u’Bracha!

I was visited by... and we had a pleasant and friendly conversation regarding the Reshet schools, specifically concerning the following points:

a) Given the unique nature of the Reshet schools, he recognizes, of course, that the appointment of teachers must be in the hands of Chabad Anash. The differences of opinion that existed until now, and the demands he made, were intended only to ensure that the teachers appointed possess pedagogical aptitude and, naturally, at least the minimum qualifications in their teaching subjects; otherwise, they would be unable to fulfill their roles as teachers and educators.

b) I emphasized specifically that the most decisive quality among a teacher’s merits and talents must be yiras shamayim, and he agreed to this as well.

c) He complained that there were instances where teachers were hired without notifying his office, and they only learned of these changes after the fact, when requests for their salaries were submitted. He sees no justification for such a thing, noting that it is detrimental not only to the central office but to the school itself. Naturally, I agreed with him completely on this matter, and I expressed my assumption that these were surely only exceptional cases resulting from some oversight.

d) Regarding the separation of boys and girls, I told him that it is impossible to compromise on this, and that a concession or compromise in one place would lead to concessions and compromises in Chabad educational institutions in many other places. This was also accepted, and he promised to exert all his efforts to ensure that the principle of separation in Chabad schools is maintained in practice. If he is provided with examples of other state-religious educational institutions in Eretz Yisrael where this is the case, it will facilitate his efforts. It will also be easier for him to advocate for this if he can state that this matter is a requirement from me; naturally, I told him he has permission to say so, for that is indeed the case. **I emphasized** that my demand for maintaining the principle of separation in Chabad institutions is not simply because they are Chabad institutions; rather, it is my clear opinion that this is how it should be in all schools and educational institutions. In our generation—an orphaned generation—this is unfortunately not merely a religious question, but also a matter of morality and tzniyus in the most basic sense. We see the results in institutions where this principle is not practiced, where incidents have multiplied to such an extent that, despite all efforts to conceal them and prevent unpleasant publicity, they nevertheless break out from time to time and reach the public ear...

#### ***1112***

Partially printed in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 21, p. 405; partially ibid., p. 426; completed according to the secretariat’s copy.

## 213

B”H, 23 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

...Regarding what you wrote about the state of your *parnassah* not being as it should be: surely for someone like you, it is unnecessary to explain that you should not be impressed by this at all. As is well known, the precision of the verse, "And Hashem your G-d will bless you in all that you do," implies that there must be actual, physical action—so that *parnassah* may have a foothold within the ways of nature, and people will not be astonished by seeing miracles—but certainly, G-d forbid, there should be no worry. Furthermore, one should not invest the power of thought and contemplation [into this worry]; as the verse states, "When you eat the labor of **your hands**, you are fortunate and it is good for you"—referring to the labor of the hands, not the labor of the head and the mind. See also *Tanya*, beginning of Chapter 41, regarding the *mitzvah* of *tefillin*, which is that one should not utilize the wisdom and understanding of one’s soul except for Hashem alone; and the entire Torah is compared to *tefillin*, for this is the concept and goal of the entire Torah.

Regarding your request for a time of favor, I will alert *anash*—may they live—that it is appropriate to write to you. Although they surely do not need such a reminder, as they know and recognize you, nevertheless, I wish to fulfill your desire in this as well. May it be Hashem’s will that soon you will bring good news regarding the expansion of your *parnassah*, and may you merit to fulfill the instruction of our *Alter Rebbe*, that Hashem gives Jews physicality, and they transform that physicality into spirituality.

With blessings.

## 1114

B”H, 24 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter from Erev Shabbos Kodesh, in which you share the good news of your positive resolution to keep the three daily shiurim established by my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory—Chumash, Tehillim, and Tanya—and you conclude by noting that this takes up a significant portion of your time and interferes with your other daily schedule.

It is already well-known and explained in many places that matters of Torah and mitzvos possess a unique quality for a person’s benefit, both materially and spiritually. From this, it is understood that it is completely illogical for them to interfere with his true well-being. Any such thoughts are merely enticements of the yetzer hara, which was created solely so that a person, through his own free choice, might battle it and overcome it. It is obvious that one should not listen to its words at all.

Furthermore, regarding the matter of time: we see quite simply that sometimes one labors over a single subject for a long time and yet does not understand it as well as one should, while at other times, in a brief moment, one grasps the matter thoroughly and clearly. This depends on Siyata Dishmaya. And how does one merit Siyata Dishmaya? Especially when a person contemplates what he is and who he is to demand assistance from Above—this, too, is explained in the sefarim: it is through prioritizing "we will do" before "we will hear." That is to say, action performed through kabbalas ol. For then the statement of the Mishnah is fulfilled: "Nullify your will before His will, so that He may nullify the will of others (see the commentaries on the Mishnah, which explain that this also refers to the Will from Above) before your will."

Surely these few lines will suffice to remove from your heart any thought that the shiurim will interfere, etc. May the Giver of the Torah, who commanded the mitzvos, grant you success in increasing your Torah study and mitzvos with peace of mind.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, Shlita**

**A. Quint**
**Secretary**

***

1114
Printed in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 18, p. 434.

## 5115

B"H, 24 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of the 15th of Adar, in which you write that you cannot find peace of mind because you feel that you have not fulfilled your obligations, etc.

It is well known that among the primary duties of a woman and mother—which is also her privilege—is the conduct of the home in accordance with the instructions of our Torah and mitzvos, the education of the sons and daughters, and so on. From this, it is understood that any matter that interferes with this, even if it only causes weakness, must be absolutely and completely rejected, since it prevents the fulfillment of the role placed upon her by the Giver of the Torah and Creator of the world.

From this, it is also understood regarding what was mentioned above, that on the contrary, if it is her desire to fulfill her obligation regarding the matters about which she writes, then not only should the memory of this not interfere with the fulfillment of her aforementioned role, but on the contrary, she must increase her efforts in this area. I refer to the conduct of the home and the education of the sons and daughters in a traditional, Torah-true, Chassidic way of life. For this, naturally, **peace of mind and a settled state of mind** are required. It is precisely in such a manner that one brings true *nachas ruach* to the soul of the departed, since his memory causes and leads to an increase in Torah and mitzvos, imbued with warmth and Chassidic vitality.

May Hashem grant you success, together with your husband, the Rav, shlita, that in good health you may raise all your children to Torah, to the chuppah, and to good deeds.

Awaiting good tidings.

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,**

**Secretary**

## 516

B"H, 25 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, G-d-fearing, learned, and diligent in communal affairs, R' Yitzchak Leib, may he live:

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter of Sunday, I was pleased to read of your connection to Anash, and that your teacher was also a Chassid of Chabad, as were several of your relatives. Certainly, since you recall this and mention it, its impression should be evident in practical matters—that is, in setting fixed times for the study of Chabad Chassidus and following its guidance and customs, at least to a certain extent.

It is also understood from the Mishnah that one must learn from every person. It goes without saying that this learning should be in the desired manner—that is, learning that leads to action. The action of a Yid is, in essence, the fulfillment of his purpose: "I was created to serve my Creator" through the matters of Torah and mitzvos. Since Hashgacha has given you the opportunity to influence your surroundings, every increase in the one who influences is multiplied many times over, according to the number and nature of those he influences. It is simple to understand, and "there is nothing that can stand before the will," especially when the merit of the many assists him.

While on the subject, and since you mention in your letter that you are a Rav, a Shochet, and also a teacher, it is surely unnecessary to awaken you to the greatest necessity in these days: that every shiur taught in public should have at least a portion dedicated to the study of the halachos required for daily life. The great benefit of this is tangibly apparent, and on the other hand, the great lack of knowledge in this area—more so than in other parts of Torah—is evident. It is precisely in the halachos required for daily life that the ignorance of the masses is greatest.

As you requested, I will mention you at an auspicious time at the holy resting place of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, for success in all the aforementioned, with proper health and peace of mind.

With blessings for good tidings.

P.S. Regarding what you wrote about establishing a yeshiva in your area, you should speak about this with the Chabad activists who are on-site and in the vicinity.

## 1117

B"H, 25 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, learned, and distinguished, etc., R' Dober, may he live, known as Dr. Segal.

Peace and blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter along with the *pidyon nefesh*, which will be read at an auspicious time at the holy resting place of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

It gave me pleasure to read in your letter about your lineage and the rock from which you were hewn, namely the great Gaon, the great Chassid, the great Tzaddik, etc., Rabbi Levi Yitzchak of Berditchev.

If in physical matters we have reached the conclusion that nothing in the world is ever lost from *yesh* to *ayin* (existence to nothingness), then how much more so is this true in spiritual matters—and certainly regarding the heritage of one’s ancestors, which is eternal and stands forever. This means that it continues to exert its influence upon children and grandchildren, etc. This is especially so given that the defining trait of the great Gaon, the great Chassid, the great Tzaddik, etc., Rabbi Levi Yitzchak of Berditchev, was *ahavas Yisrael*. Love brings people closer; it draws even those who are distant near, and certainly those who are close in body and soul.

It is understood that all the above is not intended as a sermon, but rather as an added impetus to exert effort in spreading and strengthening Yiddishkeit in your entire environment through *ahavas Yisrael*. The merit of your ancestors will assist you in succeeding in this. The reward for this is as the Mishnah states: "The reward of a mitzvah is the mitzvah itself"—the most genuine and profound soul-satisfaction from the good and kindness you accomplish through this. This is in addition to the blessing for the one who exerts himself in this, the blessing of the Lover of Israel, the Ribono shel Olam, the Creator of the world, whose measure is "measure for measure," only many times over.

I noted in your letter that you have an only son who is studying in a yeshiva. Surely, his lineage is evident in him as well. You are exerting effort in this regard, and in any event, you should exert yourself from here on to bring this into action: to set aside times, at frequent intervals, for the study of *Chassidus*, without being deterred by any obstacles. As is well known, the great Gaon, the great Chassid, the great Tzaddik, etc., Rabbi Levi Yitzchak of Berditchev, endured persecutions and disparagement for his dedication to the teachings of the Maggid of Mezritch and the Baal Shem Tov, and [it is known] the great friendship he had for our Alter Rebbe, the author of the *Tanya*. All these persecutions not only failed to weaken—G-d forbid—his *hiskashrus* to *Chassidus* and its matters, but rather, he became a pillar of fire and a pillar of light wherever he went, [spreading] the warmth and light of *Chassidus* in his immediate and distant surroundings. It is both the duty and the privilege of your son to take this holy obligation upon himself. Many generations are demanding of him that he be the channel to bring their teachings into his environment, and they also grant him the strength to fulfill this holy task as much as is required, provided only that he desires it. May it be the will [of Hashem] that you find the appropriate words to instill this matter in your son even further, should he require it.

With respect and blessing for good tidings in all the above, in good health.

## 518

518

B"H, 26 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby confirm receipt of your letter from the conclusion of Shabbos Parshas Terumah and the one preceding it. I trust that in the future, too, you will continue to inform me of developments regarding general matters, as well as your own personal affairs.

It is surprising, and indeed a great wonder, that you write that you are unable to make a decision regarding the *heter*, etc. For the decision does not depend **on you**, but rather on our Torah, and there is no room to distinguish between one *psak* and another. It is even more surprising that you write of your indecision with such composure, as if it were a *middas chassidus* not to heed the instruction of the *mara d'asra*. However, since the matter has already occurred, surely this too will be for the good. May the merit of your involvement in *chinuch*—based on the purity of holiness and *Chassidus*—stand for you and your wife, *tichyeh*, so that in good health and with peace of mind, you may raise your children to Torah, *chuppah*, and good deeds.

With blessings for good news in all the aforementioned.

#### ***518***

**Regarding the *heter*:** Concerning the use of a *moch*, as mentioned above in letter 513—to him.

*) Vol. 2, 163a. See there carefully.

## 119

B"H, 26 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 17 Adar, in which you write about your state of mind, which at times also hinders you in your work—the work of Heaven, the work of chinuch.

This matter has already been explained in several places, among them in the holy Tanya, that the matter of sadness, in whatever form it may take—whether regarding spiritual matters or worldly matters—is nothing but a ruse of the Yetzer. As is explained there at length, and especially regarding you, having seen open miracles and the kindnesses of Hashem even with physical eyes, it is clear that there is no basis for sadness. On the contrary, you must increase in simcha, in the manner of the conclusion of the laws of Purim in the Shulchan Aruch and the conclusion of the entire section of Orach Chayim: "And a good heart is a constant feast." This is especially true given that Divine Providence has placed you in a beautiful portion—the chinuch of the children of Yisrael—than which there is no holier or more pleasant avodah, which causes immense nachas ruach Above, from which one can also understand the reward of the one who causes this nachas ruach.

May it be the will that the days of this month—when these lines will reach you—whose unique quality is that "it was turned for them into joy and into a holiday," will bring this about.

And the concept of "v'nahafochu" (it was turned) means that even matters that do not appear to be good in a revealed and manifest way, the days of this month have the capacity to turn them into joy and into a holiday visible to physical eyes; may they effect this result in your aforementioned matters.

With blessings for good tidings.

## 5220

5220

B”H, 26 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings,

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter. Surely, for one such as yourself, it is unnecessary to elaborate that there is no room for a person to lose heart due to any difficulties encountered along the ways of the world while fulfilling one’s true purpose—which is, as the Mishnah states, “I was created to serve my Maker.” For this is one of the stratagems of the *yetzer* to bring a person to a state of despondency, whereas the entire purpose of the *yetzer* is to be overcome [and, in truth, this is its own desire, as is understood from the parable in the holy *Zohar* cited in *Tanya*, end of Chapter 29]. Ultimately, even matters of concealment and *hester* are themselves transformed into good—and not merely in the sense of “everything the Merciful One does, He does for the good,” i.e., that good results from it, but rather in the sense of “this, too, is for the good,” where the matter itself becomes good [and this distinction is understood from the story of Nachum Ish Gam Zu, where the transformation into dust became a revealed good, unlike the statement “everything the Merciful One does, etc.”]. And this is well understood.

We are now entering the month of Purim, regarding which our holy Torah says, “The month”—meaning that the entire month is such—“which was transformed for them from sorrow to joy, and from mourning to a festival.”

The concept of “transformed” implies that this month has the capacity to turn even matters that are not good in a revealed and manifest sense into joy and a festival that are visible to the physical eye.

With blessings for good tidings,

#### ***5220***

**And this distinction is understood:** See also above, Vol. 9, Letter 2883, and the references in the notes there.

## 5221

B"H, 27 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, learned, and diligent individual, engaged in communal needs, R' Dovid, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from 12 Adar. It was a pleasure to read that you continue to connect with members of Anash and arouse them regarding their source and foundation, including in practical terms. After all, this is the main thing, as the Mishnah states: "The study is not the main thing, but the action." It is a well-known adage of our Rebbes that there is a strength in *ta'amulah* (outreach) that it does not return empty-handed, even though it is understood that the time for growth is not the same for every individual. Happy is his portion that he has already merited to see fruits from this *ta'amulah* in several instances, which surely adds to him not only personal satisfaction but also increased courage in the aforementioned activity. May Hashem grant him success.

As we approach the upcoming days of Purim, may it be the will of Hashem that the joy be complete and overflowing in every respect. This is the essence of the days of Purim, which must reach the level of *ad d'lo yada* (until one does not know), in addition to the joy of the festivals. There is a special *segulah* for this, in that things are transformed from one extreme to the other—meaning, that undesirable matters not only do not hinder, but on the contrary, as the *Megillah* expresses, "many of the people of the land became Jews," adding strength to holiness and its matters. This is understood, at least to some extent, from the words of the Alter Rebbe in his explanation of the concept that through *teshuvah* out of love, deliberate sins become like merits; see *Tanya*, end of Chapter 7.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above.

P.S.
I am sending you what has been published recently, and surely you will merit to share it with the many, and the merit of the many depends on you.

Regarding what you wrote about the reason for giving *tzedakah* before *tefillah*—it is according to our Sages (Bava Basra 10a) and is explained according to *Chassidus* in *Iggeres HaKodesh* in *Tanya*, Section 8.

***

5221
Printed in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 6, p. 370, and completed according to the secretariat's copy.

R' Dovid: Hellman, Tel Aviv. Additional letters to him—see above, 50, and the references in the notes there.

## 1272

1272

B”H, 27 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and upright, involved in communal needs, R’ Nissan, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letters from the 7th and 12th of Adar. I was pleased to hear of the invitation (from Anash, may they live) and of your trip to Antwerp. May it be Hashem’s will that it be successful in a manner directed toward the ultimate goal — the dissemination of the wellsprings.

Surely you are preparing in the required manner to utilize the upcoming days of Purim to also strengthen and develop the holy institution under your leadership, and for the dissemination of the wellsprings outward, in addition to the specific matters of this day. This is especially so based on what is explained in many places in Chassidus, founded on the statement in Tikkunei Zohar that "Purim is like Yom Kippur." May it be Hashem’s will that this, too, be met with extraordinary success, and may all matters that hinder and obstruct be nullified.

Especially as we are entering the month of Purim, regarding which our holy Torah says, "The month" — meaning all the days of the month — "was transformed, etc., into joy and into a holiday."

The concept of "transformed" means that even those matters which do not appear good in a revealed and manifest way, this month has the capacity to transform them into joy and a holiday that is visible to the physical eye.

With blessings for good tidings.

#### 1272

**R’ Nissan:** Nemnov, Mashpia of the Tomchei Temimim Yeshiva in France. Further letters to him — above 4892, and as noted in the footnotes there.

**Surely you are preparing:** What follows below was written to several heads of Chabad institutions.

## 5223

B"H, 27 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, G-d-fearing and learned, occupied with communal needs, R' Michael, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

... Regarding your question concerning the writing of Rabbeinu Tam tefillin: In *Keset HaSofer*, section 26, and in the sefer *Yismach Moshe* on the Aggados of the Shas, it is written that one should be careful that a person who does not wear Rabbeinu Tam tefillin should not write them. In *Os Chaim*, section 34, subsection 11, the author is stringent even regarding the *bedieved* (post-facto validity) of such tefillin (!). However, one could analyze the language of the Alter Rebbe in *Orach Chaim* 34:9, "because he is not concerned with them at all"—and if so, one who refrains from wearing them because it would appear as *yohara* (arrogance/ostentation) is not included in this category, at least regarding *bedieved*. This is also stated in the sefer *Me'asef LeChol HaMachanos*, section 34, subsection 26, citing the responsa *Pnei Meivin*.

#### ***5223***

**R' Michael:** Lipsker, Meknes. Additional letters to him appear above in 5897, and in the notes referenced there.

## 5224

5224

B"H, 27 Adar Rishon, 5717
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the avreich, the G-d-fearing, learned, and distinguished, etc., R' Yosef, may he live,
and his bride, Mrs. Sheindel Shoshana, may she live,

Shalom u’Bracha!

In response to the notification regarding the scheduling of your wedding for Sunday, Rosh Chodesh Adar Sheni, may it come upon us for good.

I hereby wish to express my blessing of Mazal Tov, Mazal Tov, and may it be at a propitious and successful time. May you build a home in Israel, an everlasting edifice founded upon the foundations of Torah and mitzvos, as they are illuminated by the "Light of the Torah"—this being the teachings of Chassidus.

With the blessing of Mazal Tov, Mazal Tov.

Enclosed is a gift. This is in accordance with the custom of Israel and the words of our Sages, of blessed memory. See Zohar Vol. III (96b, regarding the bride, "from all of them"). Tosafot, s.v. *HaAchin* (Bava Basra 144a, end of side b, regarding the groom, from the groomsmen). Zohar Vol. I (149a, regarding the bride and groom, that they benefit from the feast).

#### ***5224***

**R' Yosef, may he live:** Geldzahler.

**Enclosed is a gift:** A *drasha geshenk*. See also above, Vol. VII, Letter 1937, and the references noted in the footnotes there.

## 525

B"H, 28 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter from Monday, in which you ask my opinion on whether to participate in classes where boys and girls study together and where there are both male and female teachers, and where there is also the additional concern that this will be interpreted as giving your stamp of approval to such conduct.

It is surprising that there is any doubt regarding this matter, as it is clear and simple. Especially when it is even called "religious," which only increases the stumbling block and the misleading of the many. It is understood that the aforementioned is not at all comparable to a situation where a teacher sits on the side and observes how a class for girls is conducted by a teacher or two; there is no concern in that, which is not the case in the manner described.

With blessings for success in your holy work.

It is understood that regarding the above, and in general in such matters, one must be extremely careful and guarded not to provoke the opposing side into feelings of contentiousness (by means of provocation and publicizing with great fanfare that they will not listen to their voices, that they are rebelling against them, etc.). For contentiousness causes a person to lose even their own sense of reason, and this is liable to cause them to take harsh measures, and not everyone will be able to withstand the test then. The intention must be that they do not participate in practice, and this rules out any matter that puts this at risk. Surely you will consult among yourselves how to manage the above, and "in the multitude of counselors there is salvation."

## 5226

B"H, 28 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 17 Adar, in which you write that several incidents have occurred to you and your family (may they live and be well), and you do not know to what to attribute them.

I note in your letter that your wife (may she live and be well) is named Chaya Sarah Mirel, and your mother’s name is Chaya Sarah. I am interested to know how your wife is actually called—whether all three names are used or not. In any event, you should endeavor that from now on, your wife should be called in a manner that creates the greatest possible difference from the way her mother-in-law (may she live and be well) is called. Furthermore, it is appropriate to have the mezuzos in your home checked, as well as your own tefillin, to ensure they are all kosher according to the law. Additionally, every weekday before morning davening, you should set aside a few coins for tzedakah, and your wife should do the same before lighting candles every Erev Shabbos and Erev Yom Tov. May Hashem grant you success and may you share good news regarding all the above.

You are surely aware of the three daily study portions established by my father-in-law, the Rebbe (of blessed memory), in the Chumash, Tehillim, and Tanya. In any case, you should be diligent in keeping them from now on.

With blessings.

## 5127

B"H, 28 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 17 Adar and those preceding it, in which you write regarding the progress of your paperwork and that you have received the release authorization.

May it be the will of the Creator of the world and its Guide, who oversees each and every one with Divine Providence, to lead you toward success in a revealed and manifest good, and to assist you in arranging all matters favorably.

Certainly, for someone like yourself, there is no need to elaborate that the more you and your wife, *tichyeh*, increase your involvement in the vineyard of Chabad, the more the *hisorerus* of the *rachamei rabim* of our holy Rebbes will be drawn down upon you and all your needs, adding to the blessings of Hashem in this.

Regarding what you wrote concerning studying Tanya for yourself and with others, and that you were told it is necessary to first know the accepted commentaries, etc.:

While such knowledge is of the greatest benefit, it is also understood and simple that one must not, *chas v'shalom*, refrain from this study—the study of Tanya in public—if this knowledge is not complete, or even if one does not possess it at all. This is especially true in our times, when the dissemination of the *ma'ayanos* outward is among our primary tasks, as is explained in many places. You surely know the instruction of *k'vod kedushas* Admur (Rashab), *n'ei*, regarding the study of Tanya, as found in his well-known letter, which is also printed in the supplement to *Kuntres Eitz HaChayim*.

With blessings for success in your *avodah* and in the *avodah* of your wife, with peace of mind and with much *nachas* from your son, *sh'y*.

P.S.
The delay in responding to your letter is due to the pressures that have accumulated; there is no room for any concern on your part, *chas v'shalom*. May Hashem bless each of you, *tichyeh*, with peace of mind and with good tidings regarding both specific matters and general matters.

## 5228

5228

B"H, 28 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

...It is my hope that from time to time you are increasing the regularity of your study of Chassidus, in addition to keeping the three daily shiurim established for every soul by my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. May it be His will that you bring good tidings regarding all of this. Especially since the merit of your lineage assists you, you should invest your zeal—the legacy of your ancestors—into spreading the teachings of Chassidus, its guidance, and its customs wherever your reach extends. It is well known how much our holy Rebbes sacrificed themselves for the dissemination of this Torah; from this, one can also understand the *nachas ruach* caused to them by this dissemination, and the *rachamei shamayim* that they arouse for all those who engage in these efforts, providing them with what they need, both materially and spiritually.

Awaiting good tidings.

## 5229

B"H, 28 Adar, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

...Regarding what you wrote, that you do not know my opinion concerning your activities: it is understood and simple that any action that brings the hearts of Bnei Yisrael closer to our Father in Heaven is of the highest possible value, in every place and at every time—and all the more so in Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established, and in these final generations before Moshiach. Furthermore, when one thereby rescues others from darkness to a great light, it is also understood that within the activities of holiness themselves there are many levels. As I have written several times regarding this, relying on the verse, "They made me a keeper of the vineyards" (i.e., the Torah itself testifies that they are vineyards, and yet one laments, "My own vineyard I have not kept"). However, as I have written previously, it is not the custom to neglect a source of livelihood before a suitable new source has been arranged, and the same applies to the matter at hand.

Regarding what you wrote about maaser: I have already written to some of Anash that this depends on the ability of each individual—whether the aforementioned maaser should be in addition to the maaser for tzedakah that one has given until now, or whether it is included within it. But it is understood and simple that the intention is not at all to diminish the tzedakah that one has been accustomed to giving until now, but rather to add to it. That is to say, when someone comes from a certain institution, one should not say that they are already giving to the fund that I have in mind, for the matter is to add and not to diminish. And this is clear...

#### ***5229***

**As I have written several times:** See above, Vol. 13, Letter 4359, and the references in the footnotes there.

**I have already written:** See also above, Letter 5195.

## 230

230

B"H, Erev Rosh Chodesh Adar Rishon, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the Administration of Yeshivas Tomchei Temimim in Lod,
May Hashem protect them and may they live.

Peace and blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letters from the 23rd and 25th of Adar Rishon, along with their enclosures. It is understood that the testing of the bochurim is very important, and the main thing is speaking with them privately. However, as is tangibly evident, this brings incomparably greater benefit if, in addition to all the formal discussions at the conference, there is also an informal conversation—with someone who is closer to the bochur and to whom he can reveal his entire heart, and the wise will understand...

With blessings for good tidings,

**M. Schneerson**

It is certainly unnecessary to emphasize that the primary benefit of the aforementioned tests is if, subsequently, appropriate measures are taken to rectify the deficiencies that were discovered.

## 5131

B"H, Erev Rosh Chodesh Adar Rishon, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

Following the interval, your letter from the 13th of Adar Rishon was received, in which you write regarding your current situation. May Hashem help you become settled, both materially and spiritually—and all the more so, and with greater reason. As for what you accomplished there regarding Torah and mitzvos and the point of "spreading the wellsprings outward"—even though it involved actual *mesiras nefesh*—it is certainly true here, where only *mesiras nefesh* in potential is required. One must not stop even for actual *mesiras nefesh* according to the interpretation of Chassidus, [which is] *ratzon* (will), for then one can achieve results in an extraordinary manner. May it be His will that as long as that *mesiras nefesh* of yours is still warm, it should influence *anash* in the Holy Land (may it be built and established) in particular, and especially those among them in whom, over time, the matter has cooled to an alarming degree—to the point where it is no longer possible to distinguish between them and those in whom our holy Rebbes did not invest their own efforts. May it be His will that you succeed in this as well. And although *anash* possess free choice—and furthermore, possess it with the strength of *da'as*—when this is used in the wrong place, it is not what the Rebbes intended. However, the saying of our holy Rebbes regarding the study of *Pesach Sheni* is well known, as transcribed in *Hayom Yom*.

Surely you are maintaining the connection with the *Temimim* and *anash* there—may they live and be well—in an appropriate manner, and, of course, in a cautious way.

With blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned, with stability, health, and expansion of mind.

#### ***5131***

**Location:** In Russia.

## 5232

B"H, Rosh Chodesh Adar Rishon, 5717
Brooklyn.

Mr. Yosef Pazin, may he live and be well,

Greetings and blessings!

Since I have received information that you are a scion of a Chabad Chassidic family, and my confidence is strong—as experience has shown that the rock from which a person is hewn exerts its influence for generations and generations—I take the liberty of writing these lines to you.

It has been written to me that recently, several new immigrants from our Sephardic brethren from Morocco have arrived and settled in an area and location where your influence reaches. You are in a position to accomplish much good, not only regarding their material settlement, but also concerning their spiritual matters—first and foremost, and as the foundation for the existence of any group or community, in the area of education.

Now, the Creator of the world and its Guide, the Giver of the Torah, has willed that each of us should possess free choice, specifically so that one may choose the good of his own free will. As it is written: "See, I have set before you today life and good, and death and evil... therefore choose life, that you and your offspring may live" (Devarim 30:15-19). Thus, the ability lies in man’s hands to utilize his possibilities and strengths for the opposite side as well, Heaven forbid. And since the *yetzer* is cunning in its craft, it sometimes comes with arguments that are not so easily identified as to who is the one making the claim, for they are cloaked in a mask of integrity and justice, and sometimes even in a mask of *yiras shamayim* (a counterfeit one, of course).

It is understood that even had I not written these lines, I am firm in my confidence that you will utilize every possibility and ability at your disposal to guide the immigrants and their children in traditional education according to our Torah, the Torah of life. However, since our Sages have said, "One only urges those who are already diligent," and out of my concern regarding the aforementioned counterfeit reasons and arguments—which, to our greatest sorrow, have succeeded in some places in severing the hearts of the children of Yisrael from our Father in Heaven—I have found it a duty to write these lines. It would be pleasing to me to learn that the hope is indeed well-founded: that as a member of the council, you are influencing educational matters so that they align with the education of our fathers and forefathers—a traditional education permeated with the light and warmth of Chassidus.

I am confident that it is unnecessary to elaborate on the great value of this matter and the magnitude of the responsibility. For in education during the days of childhood and youth, even a small change—for the better or, Heaven forbid, the opposite—can cause the most significant and consequential results throughout the life of the one being educated. From this, it is understood that every effort and labor to improve the situation is worthwhile.

With respect and blessing, and for good tidings in all that was mentioned.

P.S.
I would be interested to know if you are from the Leib Pazin family of Vitebsk, who is mentioned in the records of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

## 233

B"H, 1 Adar Rishon, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 17 Adar Rishon, in which you write regarding your conduct and the matters that are, for the time being, not in order.

In such a situation, the advice is as explained in several holy seforim: do not dwell or spend time contemplating the situation as it is—that it is undesirable, etc.—but rather conduct yourself in accordance with the instruction of our holy Nasi, that one action is better than a thousand sighs. Furthermore, an action in the realm of holiness should not be attempted by jumping from one extreme to the other, for that is merely a temporary measure; rather, it should be done step by step, in the manner of "little by little I will drive them out" (*Shemos 23:30*), and as it is said regarding the study of Torah, "he who gathers little by little will increase" (*Mishlei 13:11*). It goes without saying that this conduct must be carried out with the greatest possible strength—that is to say, with complete and total regret over the stubbornness of the past, and by strengthening oneself in those matters that have already been improved upon since yesterday. This in itself adds vigor to the fulfillment of these matters and serves as good preparation for further progress tomorrow. And although one might argue that this path is too long, it is also said of this path that it is "long and yet short." The observance of the three daily shiurim—Chumash, Tehillim, and Tanya—established for every person by my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, and giving a few coins to tzedakah every weekday before morning davening, will add to the Siyata Dishmaya in all of the above. In many places in the holy Tanya, Part I, you will find answers to some of the doubts mentioned in your letter, which will also help you dispel any feelings of sadness.

It is understood and also simple that, first and foremost, you must observe all the schedules of the yeshiva, to the point that you become a living example to your peers in this regard.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

**Secretary**

## 5234

B"H, 1 Adar II, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed and distinguished individual, who occupies himself with communal needs,
R' Shimon Aharon, may he live, Shochet and Bodek

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 28 Adar, regarding the good news that you are preparing to hold a gathering for the children of the Shabbos clubs this coming Sunday.

May it be Hashem’s will that their efforts be successful in drawing the hearts of all the participants—boys and girls—in this gathering closer to our Father in Heaven, which will, as a result, lead to an increase in the observance of mitzvos in the days that follow as well.

And just as "Shabbos is the source from which all days are blessed," may it be Hashem’s will that the holiness of Shabbos—which you explained is the ultimate purpose of the Shabbos club—will draw down an added measure of holiness upon the children and their leaders, and upon their heads as well, throughout the weekdays, to make them a holy nation in the land. May their teachers and parents, may they live, derive from them much true nachas, in both spiritual and material matters.

The reward for all those who occupy themselves with this is beyond expression, and it will be measured by the Holy One, Blessed be He, in the manner of "measure for measure," by fulfilling their heart’s desires for the good.

With blessings to all those who occupy themselves, exert effort, and participate in the aforementioned gathering.

#### ***5234***

Printed in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 17, p. 525, and completed according to the secretariat's copy.

**R' Shimon Aharon, may he live:** Simpson, Montreal.

## 535

B"H, 1 Adar II, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 23 Adar I, in which you write the main points of what you and your family have been going through.

As you requested, I will mention you at a time of favor at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, for whatever you may need.

If you have kept a copy of your letter, and you read through it once more, you will see—**in the inner dimension** of your letter—that thank G-d, you are a member of the people called "believers, the children of believers," and you yourself are a believer, the child of a believer. The very recognition of this fact hastens the complete dismissal of doubts that come from the outside, from a foreign environment and an opposing environment. The speed of this is especially important, because as you write, you have the ability to influence circles of people to bring them closer to our Father in Heaven. Therefore, every moment is precious—all the more so because it affects many others. Furthermore, your service in the army should have accustomed you to the concept of *kabbalas ol*, fulfilling an order even before understanding the purpose of the order, provided it comes from someone who stands above you, at least in some capacity—even if only by human appointment. How much more so when the matters are directed toward fulfilling the command of the King of Kings, the Holy One, Blessed be He. And this is understood.

Regarding such doubts, as mentioned, the best and most correct approach is to avoid engaging in *pilpulim*—even *pilpulim* with yourself—in the manner of what is said: "G-d made man upright, but they sought many accounts." By doing this, your liberation from these doubts will also be hastened, and you will walk securely on your path, the path of the King of the World, as He has instructed us in His Torah, the Torah of life. I look forward to receiving good news from you that you are progressing on this path, as well as regarding how you are settling into your matters of livelihood.

With blessings.

## 5236

B"H, 1 Adar II, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter from the 23rd of Adar, after the longest of breaks.

As you requested, I will mention you and your family—may they live—at the holy resting place of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, for whatever you may need, as is appropriate to write, and especially regarding the expansion of your livelihood, which you discuss at length in your letter.

It is surely unnecessary to remind you that for a Jewish person, material livelihood is bound up with spiritual livelihood. For we are called "one nation on earth," and according to the interpretation of the Alter Rebbe, this applies even to the material earth and material matters. From this, it is understood that to the extent that one increases their efforts in matters of spiritual livelihood—which is dependent upon the person, for "everything is in the hands of Heaven except for the fear of Heaven," and free choice has been granted to the Jewish person—so too will their material livelihood increase (which is dependent upon the Giver of the Torah and mitzvos, who is the One who specifically sustains and provides for all). And whoever adds and adds, is given more in return.

It is appropriate, specifically, that on every weekday before the morning davening, you set aside a few coins for tzedakah, and likewise before the afternoon davening, though it is understood that this should be done without a vow.

With blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned.

P.S.
I trust you are keeping the three daily study portions established for every soul by the directive of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory—the well-known portions of Chumash, Tehillim, and Tanya.

## 5237

5237

B”H, 2 Adar II, 5717
Brooklyn, N.Y.

Mr. Yitzchak Koren
Greetings and blessings,

I acknowledge with thanks receipt of your letter dated 23 Shevat and the one preceding it. Please forgive the delay in my response, as I was awaiting a reply from the residents of Kfar Chabad regarding the state of affairs there.

In response to the contents of your letter, I first wish to express my appreciation for your interest in the affairs of Kfar Chabad, which I am certain you will continue to show in the future. It is my firm hope that this will prove beneficial for the development of the village in the desired direction. Regarding the specific questions you raised:

1) Regarding loans for the farmers: As you write that it is possible to ensure that the liability rests solely and exclusively upon the farmers, provided that the Village Committee allows for various activities necessary for the advancement of the farmers—since you do not specify which activities you have in mind, it is difficult for me to express an opinion on this. However, since it is possible to arrange that the liability for the loans rests only upon the farmers, I am confident that the Village Committee will certainly cooperate in facilitating these loans, especially since there are farmers on the Village Committee as well. As for the non-farmers, since their portion will not be diminished, this is a case of "this one benefits and that one does not lose," and ultimately, they too will benefit.

2) Regarding the question of the instructor: To my regret, I have received reports that the instructor is intervening not only in agricultural matters but also in the educational institutions there and the like. Another point that is **entirely** undesirable regarding the instructor’s role in the village is that various forms of aid and assistance are not only received through him but also distributed by him. This gives him the power to impose his opinion on a certain segment of the village residents. As is well known, great care must be taken to separate authorities so that all power is not concentrated in one place. It is my hope that it is certainly possible to find a way for the distribution of aid to be handled in a manner that does not grant him a say in other matters.

3) It is understood that it is difficult to find a person of such integrity that he will express his opinion only on the questions he was sent to resolve, and will not say anything regarding all the other affairs of the place where he is located, even though he is interested in those as well—especially since there will surely always be people who wish to exploit his influence to realize their own views. However, in my opinion, given the current state of the village, it is imperative that the instructor concern himself only with agricultural matters, without intervening at all in affairs outside his purview. If it is absolutely impossible to find such a person, I am very doubtful whether the gain of the instruction will not be outweighed by the loss. In particular, the loss includes the alienation of the hearts of the village residents when a person coming from the outside intervenes in their affairs and, through the aid distributed by him, forces favoritism toward one segment of the residents over the other.

4) Regarding the question of establishing a central institution for purchasing and selling, and for centralizing agricultural produce, etc.: It is understood that there are various ways and degrees to this, whether by limiting its scope or expanding it. In any case, in my opinion, there are several drawbacks to this idea, among them that it will surely diminish the private initiative of the residents. Furthermore, one must take into account the psychological factor involved for the village residents, who came from a country where they suffered greatly from central institutions that took control of their lives and forced them to fight in the underground, etc. Even though I am confident that the institution you propose does not have these characteristics, there is still room for concern regarding the undesirable impression it may make on some of the village residents.

Perhaps it is possible to combine this with the cooperative store that already exists in the village, to which they are already accustomed; the adjustment would be easier, and it would not arouse fear of coercive methods and compulsion.

5) It seems that in our conversation when you visited here, I expressed my hope that the village would develop through the addition of new residents, and not only those from our former country. This is yet another reason why there should not be any element of compulsion or excessive centralization within the village framework, in order to allow a wider circle of candidates to settle there. After all, many of the candidates will fear the types of centralization to which they are not accustomed.

6) It is a simple matter, as you write, that it is necessary not only to stabilize the situation of the current farmers but also to develop it and increase the number of farmers. Therefore, I am confident that the non-agricultural segment will not only refrain from hindering this but will also assist. After all, they share the same worldview and have endured various hardships and tribulations together, which brings them even closer to one another. But it is also simple that one cannot force the non-agricultural segment to forgo all their interests for the sake of the development of the agricultural side. I must say that the plan for the village includes not only its development as an agricultural settlement, but also a handicraft sector and its role as a Chabad center for our entire Holy Land. This is connected to appropriate educational institutions, vocational schools, schools for boys and girls, etc., which you surely visited when you were in Kfar Chabad.

I reiterate my hope that you, for your part, will assist in every way within your power for the development of Kfar Chabad in the direction of the goal of its founding—not only by facilitating the obtaining of loans now, but also in the future should such assistance be required, as we discussed here, without this changing the character and form of Kfar Chabad even in the slightest.

With respect and blessings for good tidings.

## 5238

5238

By the Grace of Hashem, 2 Adar Sheni, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, learned, and distinguished individual, occupied with communal affairs, R’ Boruch, may he live.

Shalom u’Bracha!

I acknowledge receipt of your letter from Erev Shabbos. To my great surprise, you mention nothing regarding the well-known matter of the shul, and I found no reason in your letter for this omission. Your statement that "there is much to write" is untenable; for if that were the case, at least half of the letter you did write should not have been written, and perhaps even more than half.

Enclosed is a copy of a *michtav klali* in connection with the *Sefer Shemos* from which we have just emerged, which will surely interest you. In the margins, I have also included a brief explanation regarding the question you raised. Although there is much to write—and as with every matter of Torah, which is "longer than the earth and broader than the sea"—that does not exempt one from writing at least as much as is possible.

I hope that on Chanukah you lit the candles **in your entire environment**, without considering that the time for this is when it is dark and gloomy in the street, and specifically lighting more and more, and specifically "at the entrance of his house, on the outside"—the *nimshal* is understood. And prepare yourself also for Purim, to accomplish, in the language of the *Megillah*, "the people of the land—becoming Jews."

With blessings for good tidings.

***

Regarding your question in *Tanya* (76a): "And between one level and another, before he reaches the higher level, he is in a state of 'falling' from the first level"—why must there be this "falling" before he reaches the higher level?

The explanation for this is as follows: One is currently situated and found at a certain level—through the fact that he acts, thinks, and labors in the matters that bring him to that level. In order to attain (other) higher matters, and after a certain amount of labor in their understanding and feeling, he arrives at a higher level. However, one cannot be immersed and absorbed in two types of matters simultaneously; one must cease to be occupied and involved in the previous matters, to make a *hefsek* (break) from them. Consequently, the result of that involvement—his first level—is also missing. However, a *reshimu* (impression) remains.

For example: A good speaker will, through an impassioned speech, bring his listeners to actual tears. If, after him, a second speaker were to speak—one even better than he, and about a subject that is more profound and touches the listeners more deeply—he will bring them to weep aloud, or to be "out of their vessels" with joy.
