## 5,189

B"H, 19 Adar Sheni, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

After a long interval, I was pleased to receive your letter of 12 Adar Sheni.

Regarding what you write concerning the words of the doctors:

It is a well-known principle and the conduct of every Jew that one must act in accordance with the instructions of our holy Torah. Since our Sages of blessed memory tell us that "the Torah granted permission to the physician to heal," this is a clear indication that the physician is a *shaliach*. Even if the *shaliach* does not always know the inner intent, what the *shaliach* says regarding the practical action comes from the *Meshaleach* (the Sender). In this sense, it is a matter of Torah. Therefore, it is understood that even if one does not fully understand, or if there are questions regarding the *shaliach*—as you write in your letter—this should not have any influence on following what the doctor says. Therefore, I am confident that upon receiving this letter, you will begin to follow what the doctor says. May Hashem Yisbarach grant that following and acting according to their instructions should be with *hatzlacha muflaga* in all matters.

Furthermore, it is self-understood that the conduct of not eating at proper times, eating only once a day, and the like, is entirely not the *derech* of the Baal Shem Tov. Regarding this, we have heard many times from our *Raboseinu Nesi’einu* that there must be *azov ta’azov imo*—helping with the body—but not through *ta’aniyos* and *sigufim*.

I hope to receive a quick reply from you that you are beginning to act as stated above. *Yehi ratzon* that it should be with *hatzlacha*.

With blessings.

#### ***5,189***

**Azov ta’azov imo:** See also above, letter 4,924, and the references noted in the footnotes there.

## 190

B"H, 19 Adar Sheni, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and upright, whose work is the work of Heaven,
R' Moshe, may he live,

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from the 8th of Adar Sheni, in which you write regarding the situation of the students, may they live, and their affairs. Surely you will continue this good practice in the future, and most importantly, with vigilant supervision over each and every one of them. Beyond the necessity of this matter in its own right, this very act inspires the students to fulfill the will of the one who supervises them. This is in accordance with the nature of human beings—even of the nefesh habehamis—and all the more so as it is said, "As water reflects the face to the face, so is the heart of man to man", and according to the saying of the Mitteler Rebbe regarding the matter of two nefesh habehamis [working] upon one nefesh habehamis, and so on...

With blessings.

***

190

Printed in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 21, p. 350, and completed according to the secretariat's copy.

R' Moshe, may he live: Bartov, Pittsburgh. Additional letters to him appear above, 4790, and in the references noted there.

Saying: See also above, Vol. 11, letter 3794, and in the references noted there. Below, 512.

## 191

B.H., 19 Adar Sheni, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

I acknowledge receipt of your letter—which was somewhat delayed in transit—in which you write that you were born to observant, G-d-fearing parents and received an appropriate education. You add a summary of your experiences since then, and complain that you do not feel a vitality of holiness, etc., and that contradictory thoughts pass through your mind, etc.

Now, surely—and especially since you have been in the army for some time—it is superfluous to elaborate on the order that prevails and is visible in creation (notwithstanding that which sometimes appears otherwise to the physical eye). This is particularly true regarding the aspect of creation where Bnei Yisrael are found, to whom free choice has been granted. As it is written, "See, I have set before you today life and the good... **and you shall choose** life." This, too, is an act of the Creator’s kindness; He desired that a person should reveal his nature and essence, which is good in its essence and in its interiority. Even if the vicissitudes of time cover this interiority with a layer of the opposite, this is merely an external matter that comes to a person so that he may remove it through his own strength and toil. Through this, the blessing and influence from Above are increased, and not only that, but they are received in their fullness. As the well-known expression goes, there is no complete pleasure from "the bread of shame" (*nahama d'kisufa*), as opposed to that which one has labored and toiled over.

From what has been stated above, it is understood that there is no finer portion in life than when Divine Providence provides the opportunity not only to choose life in one's own private world and private life, but also to influence a certain circle and tip the scales for them toward the good, so that they, too, will choose the true life and the true good.

From this, it is also understood that the *yetzer hara* and the *sitra achra* fight and confuse one who influences others in a positive direction more than they confuse a private individual who only lights the lamp of his own *neshamah* to illuminate his own portion alone. Through this, it is understood and absolutely clear that one should not let his heart fall because he is being confused as mentioned above. Since this is a sign that he is meant to accomplish more than an ordinary person, the *yetzer* fights and prevails more—as the Sages of blessed memory famously said, "Whoever is greater than his fellow (which includes greatness in influence), his *yetzer* is greater than him."

Together with this, we have been promised that "according to the camel is the load," and each person is given the powers necessary for him to fulfill his own role. The matter depends only on his will to bring his ability and his powers into action.

A special, good, and beautiful point, in addition to what has been said, is that your influence is among military personnel, as you write that you are a religious sergeant in the army. Military personnel are in a unique framework, uprooted from their homes and usual environment, and are accustomed to accepting a yoke for most of the day and night, prioritizing *na'aseh* (we will do) over *nishma* (we will hear). This prepares them more for the conduct of the Jewish people in accepting our Torah and fulfilling its *mitzvos*, which are founded upon the acceptance of the yoke—*na'aseh* and then *nishma*. And even though in several points, on the contrary, the observance of Torah and *mitzvos* there is more difficult, as mentioned above, these difficulties should awaken an added strength in each of those in the army, and a manifold increase for those who stand over them regarding matters of religion. And there is nothing that can stand before the will.

It is my hope that you have fixed times for the study of Torah for yourself as well, in addition to the study with those you influence, and within that, also the study of the teachings of Chassidus. Surely the young *anash* in your vicinity will assist you in this as much as is necessary, if you establish a connection with them.

I await good news in all the above.

## 5192

B"H, 21 Adar Sheni, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

...Regarding your letter... Fortunate is his portion, that Divine Providence has placed him to engage in the education of the sons and daughters of Yisrael in accordance with the purity of holiness, in addition to the matter of [operating] within the framework of our Rebbes, the Nesi'im, may their merit protect us, which is permeated with the light and vitality of Chassidus. From this, it is also understood that the rectification for all of his personal matters—which is their completion and perfection—depends upon his avodah in this field as it ought to be, with an increase from time to time in the improvement of this avodah, in accordance with the command that we must always ascend in matters of holiness.

However, appropriate to certain matters about which you write, it is proper that you and your wife, may she live, set aside funds for tzedakah until the contributions of each of you combined reaches the sum of a *pidyon* for forty fasts (and according to what is written in the *Kovetz Lubavitch*, the measure for this is specified). It is understood that this can be done in a manner that will **not** burden your expenses, provided that the resolution to do so is accepted in the best possible way. The specific times for setting aside tzedakah are every weekday before morning davening, and regarding women, before lighting the candles on Erev Shabbos and Erev Yom Tov. It is understood that the above is in addition to the fixed daily study of the revealed Torah and the teachings of Chassidus, the keeping of the three well-known daily *shiurim* in Chumash, Tehillim, and Tanya, and, in general, participation in matters of Torah and mitzvos conducted in public, for the merit of the public assists one.

With blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned.

#### ***5192***

**The rectification:** For the sin of *niddah*. See also above, letter 473.

## 1893

B"H, 21 Adar Sheni, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

...It is certainly unnecessary to point out that precisely after the wedding, one must increase in matters of Torah and mitzvos. From the day of the chuppah, one is granted from Above an added measure of success in this, and since it is granted from Above, it is certainly intended that it be utilized properly and to the fullest extent. And just as in matters of mitzvos, *v’ahavta l’reiacha kamocha* is a *klal gadol baTorah*, especially toward those who are very close, may Hashem grant you success in your speaking with your brother. It is time to stop closing one's eyes and refusing to see the *chasdei Hashem Yisbarach* with him until now, and in the present as well. By stubbornly refusing to see this, not only does one make things more difficult for oneself, for one's surroundings, and for one's family, but one also creates a *levush* (covering) over the *chasdei Hashem*. For *is'arusa d'leila* is *k'ein is'arusa d'l'tata* (the arousal from Above corresponds to the arousal from below), and when one is stubborn below, insisting that "the meaning is not so, and the content is not so," etc., *ruach aisi ruach* (spirit brings spirit).

It goes without saying that merely seeing the *chasadim* is not enough; one must create *keilim tehorim* and *keilim rechavim* (pure and expansive vessels) to receive the *chasdei Hashem Yisbarach*. Sadly, at times he tries to convince himself that the world follows its own course and that this is merely "nature" [in the sense of the word *teva*, meaning sunken/submerged]. Consequently, this can lead, *chas v'shalom*, to the aforementioned results. And although *lo yidach mimenu nidach* (none shall be cast away from Him), the damage is that for the time being, he causes suffering to himself and his family, at a time when he could be an *oved Hashem* with *simchah*, and that his *gashmius* could also be with *simchah*—there is much to be happy about, even in the good that is visible and revealed to the physical eye. May it be His will that in this matter, too, you bring good tidings, and that this will include your brother feeling closer... participating in the *shiurim* that are studied in public, and in general, in the *peulos* of Chabad, and so on and so forth.

## 594

594

B"H, 21 Adar II, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and upright, engaged in communal needs, R' Dovid, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

...Regarding tefillin, I have not heard any specific instructions on this. It is possible that the elders of Anash in Eretz Yisroel, may it be rebuilt and established, are aware of the custom. My practice is that the height of the tefillin should be equal to their length and width; see Sefer HaEshkol, Darkei Moshe, and elsewhere.

The idea of compiling a book on the psychology of the soul according to Chabad—and primarily, according to the Rebbeim of Chabad and their teachings—for the schools in Eretz Yisroel is very correct indeed, and it would be worthwhile for you to consult with R' Avraham Chanoch Glitzenstein, may he live, regarding this.

In a separate package via regular mail, several documents belonging to the network that you sent here have been returned to you. You will surely confirm their receipt.

#### ***594***

Printed in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 21, p. 370, and completed based on a copy from the secretariat.

**R' Dovid, may he live:** Chanzin, Petach Tikvah. Additional letters to him appear above in 4762, and in the references noted there.

## 5295

B"H, 21 Adar Sheni, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the G-d-fearing, esteemed, and distinguished, who engages in the needs of the community, whose work is the work of Heaven,
R' Yosef, may he live.

Shalom u'Bracha!

Following the long interval, your letter from Motzaei Shabbos was received. Thank you for the good news regarding the improvement in the health of your wife, tichyeh. May it be His will that this improvement be a stepping stone, moving from one improvement to the next until a complete and speedy recovery, and may they lengthen their days and years in goodness and pleasantness.

It is understood that this also includes the success of the banquet you write about, which is being held in his honor at the school where he has been involved for eighteen years—success in both spirituality and physicality regarding the collection of funds, and in spirituality, that the concept of the necessity of proper education—and furthermore, education in the purity of holiness for every single son and daughter of Yisrael—will penetrate circles that are ever-widening and spreading, until they recognize that this is a vital necessity. Therefore, all the effort and exertion in this are worthwhile. And since you have merited that Divine Providence has placed you in this field of work, may He also merit you to share good news regarding progress in the aforementioned, until the prophecy is fulfilled: "They shall no longer teach each man his neighbor... for they shall all know Me, from the smallest to the greatest," and "the earth shall be full of the knowledge of Hashem."

With appreciation and blessing.

#### ***5295***

**R' Yosef:** Player, Chicago. Additional letters to him—above 4450, and in the references noted in the footnotes there.

## 5,296

B"H, 21 Adar Sheni, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed and distinguished individual, occupied with communal needs,
R' Chaim Zusia, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

Your letters, dated as listed below, were received in due time. It is surprising that since your last letter of 1 Adar, there have been no further letters from you (aside from those to which you have received a response), even though surely several details have developed during the intervening weeks, both regarding the matters about which you write and regarding new matters.

Surely you will fill this in at the next opportunity and with the necessary detail. May it be the will of Hashem that it be in a way that is good, both visibly and revealed, and this, of course, includes the proper health of yourself and your family, may they live.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,**

**A. Quint**
**Secretary**

P.S.

The letters received:
Dated 16 Teves, Motzaei Shabbos Va'era, Motzaei Shabbos Beshalach, 13 Shevat, 22 Shevat, 27 Shevat, 1 Adar.

Additionally, your letter of 17 Adar Sheni was received. It is understood that it is impossible for an educational institution with a mix of boys and girls to be within the Chabad framework. However, it is also understood that one must influence these institutions as well, and assist them in improving matters of yiras shamayim, Torah, and mitzvos, and the like. And to the wise, a word is sufficient.

Regarding the gathering of anash about which you write—it is understood that it is a correct idea, but it is essential to prepare a plan (at least a general one) in advance, to draw into this other active members of anash, etc., etc., and then one can hope for important and great results, with Hashem's help.

#### ***5,296***

**R' Chaim Zusia:** Vilmovsky, Tel Aviv. Additional letters to him—above 4,785, and in the references noted there.

## 5297

[21 Adar Sheni, 5717]

...Regarding what you wrote about the rash — the doubt in my words is not understood. It sometimes happens that certain types of food cause various symptoms in a particular person, such as a rash or the like. And by avoiding these types, the symptoms pass. The difficulty in the matter is to determine which types of food are the cause.

The simple (though long) way to handle this is to abstain from a specific type of food for a period of time and observe the results.

## 5,298

B"H, 22 Adar Sheni, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 18 Adar Sheni, in which you write that you are faced with the question of whether to accept a teaching position at a school affiliated with the Conservative movement, and you add that you received your education in Eretz Yisrael (may it be rebuilt and established), are a member of Agudas Yisrael, and serve as a counselor for Pirchei Agudas Yisrael.

It is understood and simple that if your accepting a position at such an institution could be interpreted by the environment as providing a stamp of approval for the Conservative ideology, the matter is **absolutely** out of the question, as the halachic ruling regarding these ideologies is well-known—that they constitute complete heresy.

- It is understood that this refers to the ideology itself, as opposed to the members of Conservative and Reform congregations, many of whom are believers in Hashem and His Torah as required, but who do not distinguish between light and darkness and between holiness and impurity, and so on. -

It is likewise understood and simple that even if there is no room for the aforementioned interpretation, if the content of the studies is not purely holy, the entire matter is also out of the question.

If there is no room for this concern at all—meaning that you would be the one in charge of the studies, and the school is separate and not connected to their prayer services, customs, etc.—then, on the contrary, there is room to strive to instill fear of Heaven there, and by educating the students in purity and holiness, to extract the precious from the vile. Although, I must say that such a case is extremely rare, and I have written this only for the sake of a complete analysis.

And surely for someone like you, it is superfluous to add that what was written above regarding the studies being purely holy refers not only to the specific subject you teach, but also to the other subjects that the children in your class will study before and after your lesson. Since they are all part of one package, the parents who entrust their children to study at the institution because they are satisfied with your teaching will automatically have these students study the other subjects as well.

Since it appears you have met with anash in Toronto, I hope that you have also acquired knowledge of the teachings of Chassidus and that you set fixed times for studying its teachings, which are called in this holy book the "soul of the Torah." May Hashem grant you success and bring you good tidings regarding all of the above.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

**Secretary**

## 599

B"H, 22 Adar II, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of the 17th of Adar II, in which you describe the sequence of events that led you to write to me.

It is understood that every connection between Yidden must be expressed through an increase in matters of Torah and character refinement. Since you are now living within the "four cubits" of Torah and have established a connection with me, it is proper and good that you immediately set aside time for the study of Chassidus Chabad, about which you have surely heard, as you have met with several of Anash. They will also instruct you on the order and methods of study. There is nothing that can stand before the will. May it be the will of the Giver of the Torah that you also merit to influence your friends to do the same, and the merit of the many will assist you.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,**

**Secretary**

## 500

B"H, 22 Adar II, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, learned, and distinguished R' Shlomo Zalman, may he live:

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter bringing the good news of your marriage, may it be in a good hour: May it be the will of Hashem that your home be built for good fortune, in a good and successful hour, and may you lengthen your days and years in goodness and pleasantness.

As you requested, I will mention you and all those you wrote about at the holy resting place of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

Surely you are aware of the three daily shiurim that are incumbent upon every soul, established by my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory—the well-known portions of Chumash, Tehillim, and Tanya—and that you keep them, or at the very least, will keep them from this point forward.

With blessings for a mazal tov.

P.S.
I noted with interest in your letter that you are a fifth-generation descendant of one of the Tzemach Tzedek’s students who would repeat his Chassidic discourses. I would be interested to know if any manuscripts or notes from his estate have been preserved.

It is surely unnecessary to remind you that since you are a descendant of one who disseminated the teachings of Chassidus to the masses, you too have the privilege and the obligation to make an effort in such matters; and there is nothing that can stand before the will.

#### ***500***

**R' Shlomo Zalman:** Glickman, Jerusalem.

## 151

B"H, 23 Adar Sheni, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

...Regarding what you wrote about traveling here for the upcoming month of Tishrei.

Although in general this is a correct thing to do, this applies only when the trip will bring benefit not only to him as an individual, but also to his surroundings through his influence upon them. It seems that some of the *avreichim* who visited here in the past did not conduct themselves as they should have, but merely to fulfill their obligation. In such a case, it is questionable whether the expenses incurred (expenditure of money, expenditure of time, loss of time from learning, etc.) were worthwhile. If he will take upon himself with a whole heart to conduct himself as he should, and not as mentioned above, and as preparation will increase his diligence in learning and his *hiddur* in the performance of *mitzvos*, may it be His will that his efforts in obtaining the papers and his visit here be successful.

With blessings.

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

#### ***151***

**Will bring benefit:** See also above, Letter 150, and the references in the footnotes there.

## 582

582

B”H, 23 Adar II, 5717
Brooklyn

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and learned, engaged in communal needs, R’ Yechiel Michel Yehuda Leib, may he live and be well,

Greetings and blessings!

Following the long interval, your letter from Shushan Purim was received, along with its enclosure. May it be the will of Hashem that you continue to hear good news, both in your personal matters and in communal affairs. Regarding the matter that the time calls for—the distribution of matzah shemurah, especially to those in the military—it is imperative that this year the number of people receiving it increases compared to previous years. After all, they have become acquainted and have established a connection over the past few months, and the *hisorerus* is also more vibrant this year than before, in addition to the general directive that one must always increase in matters of holiness. It is understood that my words here are for all those involved in this work, and you have the permission and the merit to convey this to everyone, for the time is short.

I was pleased to hear the initial reports regarding the Purim celebration in Kfar Chabad. Surely, as with all matters, you will maintain a constant, ongoing, and expanding connection with those who visited there. It will certainly be possible to utilize this connection for the dissemination and strengthening of Yiddishkeit, and in such a case, surely for them as well—at the very least, for a significant portion of them—one should provide a *kezayis* of matzah shemurah.

The *tzedakah* of *ShP"H* should be deposited into the network’s fund. It is understood that I am pleased with your involvement in this in appropriate ways—by which I mean in a manner where those involved do not distract one another by encroaching on each other’s territory, although in general there is no room for concern here, since the intention of all these individuals is for the sake of Heaven. This *tzedakah* is a most exalted matter, and its merit protects those who engage in it; it is simple that you will continue in this in the future, and may Hashem grant you success.

It is surprising that the number of participants in the *farbrengens* and in the learning among the yeshiva students is not increasing as it should. It would be appropriate for those involved in this to consult privately on how to rectify this matter.

With blessings for good news in all that has been mentioned.

#### ***582***

Printed in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 21, p. 290, and completed based on the secretariat’s copy.

**R’ Yechiel Michel Yehuda Leib, may he live and be well:** Zalmanov, Bnei Brak. Additional letters to him: above, 539, and the references in the notes there.

**The *hisorerus* is more vibrant:** See also above, letter 519, and the references in the notes there.

**The *tzedakah* of *ShP"H*:** *Keren HaShanah*. See also above, letter 524, and the references in the notes there.

## 563

B"H, 23 Adar Sheni, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of the 17th of Adar Sheni: May it be the will of Hashem that He fulfill the requests of your heart for good—for learning with consistency and diligence, for the performance of mitzvos with beauty, and for engagement in the avodah of tefillah. It is understood that all of the above must be done specifically with simchah, as it is written, "Serve Hashem with simchah," and more. Furthermore, the avodah of Hashem is not only during the times of tefillah, learning, and the performance of mitzvos, but "in all your ways," from which it is understood that the matter of simchah must be present always, as is explained in Tanya in many places; see also the end of Shulchan Aruch Orach Chayim.

And the merit of your engagement with others to bring their hearts closer to Chassidus and its matters will stand by you for your own success in all that was mentioned.

With blessings for good tidings in all that was mentioned,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

**Secretary**

#### ***563***

Printed in Likutei Sichos, Vol. 26, p. 522.

## H'dash

B"H, 23 Adar Rishon, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and learned, engaged in communal needs, R' Ben Zion [Shemtov]
May he live.

Greetings and Blessings,

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter regarding the meeting, as well as the previous ones from the 11th of the month, etc., and I await further good news regarding the practical outcomes. Surely the letters and telegrams sent from here will have their effect regarding the future as well, for ultimately, action is the main thing.

Thank you for the photograph enclosed in your letter. It is surprising that there are no letters from them, and that you yourself do not write details regarding what is being done with them; surely you will rectify this at the next opportunity, and thank you in advance. Likewise, regarding the utilization of the days of Purim for communal matters and also in a Chassidishe manner: because of the excessive *tumlenish* (commotion), there is a danger that everything will be forgotten. Therefore, one needs a *Chassidishe farbrengen* in the literal sense. It is understood that this has nothing to do with excessive drinking, G-d forbid, as discussed at this year’s Purim *farbrengen*: that the obligation of *d'basumi b'puraya ad d'lo yada* (to become intoxicated on Purim until one does not know) can perhaps be fulfilled with a single drop, provided it is done in a way that enables one to rise above one’s own *taam v'daas* (intellect and reason), as can be seen tangibly. So, when one truly desires it, one drop is enough; and if, G-d forbid, it is not so, even a *pessel* (barrel) will not help.

And since we have been commanded to increase in joy, may it be His will that the joy remove the *metzarim* (limitations) and boundaries of the animalistic body and the *nefesh habehamis*. *Bimila* (consequently), one will carry out the *kavanah* as required, according to the will of our holy Rebbes, with *harchavas hadaas*, joy, and proper health.

With blessings,

#### ***H'dash***

**R' Ben Zion:** Shemtov, London. Additional letters to him: see above, Vol. 12, p. 29, and the references in the footnotes there.

## 575

B"H, 23 Adar Rishon, 5717
Brooklyn.

Mrs. Z. Althaus, Mrs. G. Ginzburg,
Mrs. B. Zalmanov

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter from Rosh Chodesh Adar Rishon and the one preceding it, I sent a telegram today which reads: "The proposal to merge the vocational school for girls with the Slonim institution is good, with blessings."

It is not clear from your letter to what extent the age and other matters of the students from the mission [Hatzalah] are compatible (as certainly only female students are being considered for the merger; and if there will also be boys in the Slonim institution, they must, of course, be in a building at a distance, and it goes without saying that there should be no integration, even in the dining hall, etc.).

Regarding your question—since it is heard that the trade of sewing is not currently a viable means of livelihood according to the situation in Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established, whereas training in household management is, especially since this would also garner special support from the appropriate offices—if this is the case, this course of study should be implemented. Especially since it also includes training in sewing and the like, and it is always possible—should the situation change—to increase the hours of sewing instruction, etc., so that the students will have both options available.

With blessings for success in your most important and valuable work of establishing the vocational school, and to increase courage in this, with proper health and peace of mind.

With blessings for good tidings.

#### ***575***

**Mrs. ...:** Additional letters to her - see Vol. 13, p. 526, p. 622.

## 570

B"H, 24 Adar Sheni, 5717
Brooklyn.

Blessings and Peace!

In response to your letter from Monday, in which you write about your mood, your state of health, and also mention moving to a new apartment, viewing it as a difficult matter.

I am very surprised. Remembering what you once wrote about your current apartment, that should have convinced you that moving is good for you and your husband, *shiyichyeh*. Therefore, one should be satisfied with it. The difficulties you see regarding the furniture not being modern or not being good have no bearing on the benefit of moving out of your current section. Certainly, in time, you will get to know people there, and in time, you will also make friends.

In general, for a Jew—man or woman—finding oneself in the month of Adar and approaching Pesach, these two months are fortunate to the greatest degree for every Jew, wherever they may be. One must thank Hashem Yisbarach every day for the kindness He has shown until now, and be confident that He, *Baruch Hu*, will continue to show kindness, such that one will be privileged to see it with physical eyes as well, and in many ways.

Regarding what you write about loneliness, I hope that you are taking part in the work of the Ladies Auxiliaries and the like, which is connected with good work for Yiddishkeit and Jewish institutions. Certainly, Rabbi Hecht, *shiyichyeh*, can advise you on this.

I hope that the lines written above will be sufficient to convince you, and easily so, of the great kindnesses of Hashem Yisbarach: that you have had the merit to raise fine children and have *nachas* from them and from grandchildren, and that you are able to do for Yiddishkeit whenever you wish. From this, it is self-evident that not only is there no reason to be in a bad mood, but on the contrary, you should be *b'simcha* regarding the past, the present, and even more so regarding the future.

Enclosed is a check for $180 as a loan for an extended period. You should use the money for the expenses of the move and also for the expenses connected with Pesach. You do not need to rush to pay it back; it can take half a year, three-quarters of a year, a year, or more.

With blessings for a *kosher* Pesach and for good tidings regarding everything mentioned above.

P.S.
Self-evidently, when you visit a doctor, you must follow all his instructions. May Hashem Yisbarach grant that it be successful and that you report good news in this regard as well.

## 537

B"H, 24 Adar II, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

... It is understood that your activities in spreading the wellsprings outward will strengthen and increase the blessings of Hashem in general, and in the aforementioned matters in particular. However, it is also understood that the activities you mention are not sufficient **at all**, for we are truly in the *ikvesa d'Meshicha*, and every single moment is precious for making the preparation. The vessel for this is the spreading of the wellsprings as required, for then "the Master will come" (as in the well-known *Iggeres HaKodesh* of the Baal Shem Tov).

The concealments and obstructions—including the matters you write about—are merely an illusory phenomenon, as is explained in *Chassidus* regarding the distinction between a *nisayon* (trial) and a *birur* (refinement). This is especially true as we are in this very month and these very days of *v'nahafochu*, when all concealments and obstructions are transformed into revealed and manifest good.

With blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned.

## 508

B"H, 24 Adar Sheni, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 2 Tazria and the one preceding it, along with the enclosed English copy.

I was pleased to read in your last letter that the opposing side has decided not to establish a kindergarten, and consequently, they too acknowledge that a great thing has been accomplished in their camp for the benefit of proper chinuch. Although, naturally, it is regrettable that it seems that until now, the situation was such that signs and wonders were required to prove the necessity of proper chinuch, even in their own camp—whereas the necessity of the matter compels one to admit not only that it is possible in theory, but that it is also possible in actual practice, and it depends solely on those who need to engage in it.

May it be Hashem’s will that now that he has seen the sign and wonder, his perspective—as well as the perspective of his wife, may she live—will change regarding this, and they will view the state of Yiddishkeit... [realizing] that everything is within one’s ability and power. Furthermore, they are also given the strength to bring [these matters] from potential into actual, practical reality. It is only incumbent upon them to harness these strengths in the desired direction for the aforementioned activities. Then they will see that most of the concealments and hidden states are merely imagined, and the few that do exist will turn into aids—in accordance with the unique quality of these days of the month of Purim, regarding which the Torah says, "The month"—meaning all the days of the month—"which was turned for them, etc., into joy, etc., into a holiday."

And the concept of "turned" means that even matters which do not appear good in a revealed and manifest way, this month has the capacity to transform them into joy and into a holiday visible to the physical eye.

With blessings.

## 510

510

B"H, 24 Adar Sheni, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and learned, engaged in communal needs, R' Peretz, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

After a long interval, your letter of 22 Adar Sheni was received, containing the good news regarding your two sons, may they live and be well. May Hashem grant them success, and may you merit to share good news in all matters, especially regarding them, in a way that is good, visible, and revealed, until the Creator of the world and its Guide brings them out from the straits into the expanse, with kindness and mercy.

Based on what is explained in many places, that all of Klal Yisrael constitutes one complete stature, and especially parents and children, it is understood and simple that the more you increase your own avodah and the mitzvos in which you need to be particularly careful, the more this will serve as assistance regarding your sons, may they live and be well, to a much greater degree than sending packages and the like. And certainly, the mitzvah relevant to you in your current situation—your unique mitzvah—is the dissemination of the wellsprings outward in every place possible in your current environment. Even if there were concealments and hidden obstacles, the power of Purim is that "it was turned around" (*v'nahapoch hu*). All the more so, since the vast majority of these concealments are merely illusions, as is evident to the senses even with a slight amount of contemplation.

With blessings for good news in all the aforementioned.

#### ***510***

**R' Peretz:** Motzkin, Montreal. Additional letters to him: above, Vol. 12, p. 260, and in the references noted in the footnotes there.

**To his two sons:** Yosef and Shmuel, may they live, who at that time were still behind the Iron Curtain.

## 1510

B"H, 24 Adar Sheni, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

...Regarding what you write about the candlestick — it is simple that you should not pay any attention to this at all, for it is merely an indication that there is a lack in the matter of studying the "light of Torah," which is to say, the inner dimension of Torah. Surely, your husband is engaged in this to a degree that is not at all sufficient in proportion to his ability and what is required. Consequently, when he decides to change for the better in this regard and begins to implement this change in practice, the matter will be resolved correctly and will even be transformed into good, so that your home will be illuminated with the "candle of mitzvah, Torah of light," and the light of Torah that has been revealed in our generation through the understanding of Chassidus Chabad. May Hashem grant him success and may you share good news regarding all of the above.

With blessings for a kosher and happy Pesach.

P.S.
It is surprising that you do not mention your participation in the activities of N'shei U'Bnos Chabad. May it be His will that, at the very least, from here on out you will do everything necessary in this regard.

#### ***1510***

**Regarding the candlestick:** Which fell and broke while lighting the candles on Erev Shabbos Kodesh.

## 511

B”H, 25 Adar Sheni, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter:

1) Regarding what Rashi cites (Bereishis 28:13) that the Ribono shel Olam folded the entire Eretz Yisrael under Yaakov, you ask where Yitzchak was at that time.

I am surprised by your question. It is written earlier in the pasuk, "And he dreamed," which indicates that this all took place in a dream, as the Chizkuni emphasizes there.

2) At a time of favor, I will mention all those for whom you write that they are in need. However, you must write here each person’s name along with their mother’s name.

And since you are requesting a blessing for them from the Ribono shel Olam, you should ensure that you conduct yourself as the Ribono shel Olam has instructed in His holy Torah, and you should also influence your friends to do the same. If you truly desire this, you will be successful in it, and through this, your requests for those for whom you write will also be accepted Above.

With blessings.

## Letter 4612

B"H, 26 Adar Sheni, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letters from the 11th and 23rd of Adar Rishon, as well as those preceding and following them, you raise the question: What is the will of the Ribono shel Olam, and what must a person do to fulfill his obligation?

It has already been stated, "G-d made man upright, but they have sought many accounts" (Koheles 7:29). The "uprightness" of man is explained in the *Sefer HaYashar*—our holy Torah, both the Written Torah and the Oral Torah—in which the will of the Supreme One, blessed be He, is revealed. Within them are explained the counsels for clarifying doubts should they arise, as well as the guidance on how to discern what comes from the side of holiness, and so on. This latter matter, in particular, is explained in the teachings of Chassidus and in the *sichos* of our Rebbes, the *Nesi'im* (may their merits protect us).

Regarding you personally: since Divine Providence has placed you in a beautiful portion—to educate the sons and daughters of Yisrael with a kosher education, a task known as "the work of Heaven"—there is no room for doubt. Even though regarding specific actions and certain matters there may be room to argue one way or another [and it seems you are referring specifically to communal involvement in addition to your work in education, as well as your obligations toward your wife and children], regarding such things it is said: "If there is anxiety in a man's heart, let him speak of it to others." As is known from the saying of the Mitteler Rebbe, through this, two *nefesh elokis* stand against one *nefesh habahamis*. The main thing, as mentioned above, is not to "seek many accounts," but to walk securely and with integrity in your path. In the vast majority of cases, you will also find instruction in the *Shulchan Aruch* on how and what you should do.

Regarding the idea of moving to Kfar Chabad: it is understood that first and foremost, you must clarify the opinion of your wife (may she live and be well) on this matter. If she agrees to it wholeheartedly, it is a good thing. However, you should both visit the Kfar first and see the conditions on the spot, as there are various types of housing in Kfar Chabad. Likewise, you must prepare in advance for your livelihood, as is understood.

I am sure you are preparing for the upcoming days of Purim and considering how to utilize them within the framework of the school network and the framework of Tzeirei Agudas Chabad.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above.

#### ***Letter 4612***

**Note:** See also Letter 4590 above, and the references in the footnotes there.

## 563

563

B"H, 26 Adar Sheni, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of the 22nd of Adar Sheni.

I was alarmed to read therein that you are considering traveling on a ship where the sailors, the staff, and the captain are Israelis, and the ship remains at sea on Shabbos. This constitutes a public desecration of Shabbos, and the passengers share in this as well. Can it be believed that a bochur studying in Yeshivas Tomchei Temimim—a ben yeshiva in general—would have such a thought? And furthermore, that he would request my consent for this, and as you conclude, for the purpose of gaining benefit in Torah and yiras shamayim?

Regarding what you wrote about traveling to study in the yeshiva here, and bringing proof from other bochurim, students of Tomchei Temimim in France:

Surely you are aware of what was written to them—namely, that they should study with great diligence and persistence throughout the winter and summer months, until the administration of Tomchei Temimim can testify on their behalf. Only then should one consider traveling here at the end of the summer. Under these conditions, the matter applies to you as well, as you are among the first who came to study in the holy yeshiva of Tomchei Temimim. May it be His will that you study Torah with yiras shamayim—both the revealed Torah and the Torah of Chassidus—and that you be meticulous in the performance of mitzvos, and that you bring good tidings in all the aforementioned matters.

With blessings.

#### ***563***

**On a ship where the sailors... are Israelis:** See also above, letter 4,796, and the references in the footnotes there.

## 514

B.H., 26 Adar Sheni, 5717
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the women and daughters of the holy community Shuva Yisrael
in the city of Buenos Aires, Argentina
May Hashem grant them life

Blessings and peace!

I was pleased to receive news of your good work in strengthening Yiddishkeit within your community and disseminating matters of Yiddishkeit.

It is my hope that you are increasing in this from time to time, in accordance with the command of our holy Torah to "increase in holiness." It is understood that there is a special holy obligation and a holy privilege upon every daughter of Yisrael—daughters of Sarah, Rivkah, Rachel, and Leah—to conduct outreach and strive to strengthen the matters specific to women: taharas hamishpachah, the conduct of the home in general, the chinuch of sons and daughters, kashrus in particular, and much more. Since the Creator and Leader of the world demands this of the daughters of Yisrael, surely they have been granted the special kochos to bring all of the above to fruition in its entirety and perfection; the matter depends only upon their will.

Their reward is explained in the holy seforim, which is why they are also crowned with the title *akeres habayis*, which means the essence and foundation of the Jewish home.

This is especially relevant as we come from the days of Purim, when the miracle occurred through Esther, at a time when they stood with mesiras nefesh in matters of Yiddishkeit, during a time of *hester panim*, as our Sages of blessed memory stated regarding that era, that it is said, "I will surely hide [Hester Astir] My face."

And we are approaching the days of the festival of Pesach, through the merit of the righteous women of that generation, our forefathers were redeemed from Mitzrayim.

May it be the will that each and every one of you adds strength and courage in the holy avodah mentioned above, and may this be with peace of mind, breadth of spirit, and inner, true joy.

With blessings for a kosher and happy Pesach.

## 5,115

B"H, 26 Adar Rishon, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished individual, a man of many accomplishments,
R' Avraham Sender, may he live, Shochet u'Bodek

Shalom u'Bracha!

In response to your letter and the Pidyon Nefesh of the 25th of Adar Rishon, which will be read at an auspicious time at the holy resting place of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

Surely you will bring good tidings among us, especially as you write in your letter that the Yahrtzeit of my father-in-law, the Rebbe Rashab, of blessed memory, is approaching. For the righteous, the shepherds of Israel, just as they stand and serve in this world during their lifetime, so too do they stand on high and arouse great compassion to bestow the needs of all those who are connected to them—especially those who merited to hear their Torah in the manner of "your eyes shall behold your teachers," and who will continue to see, eye to eye, through their sefarim and the maamarim of Chassidus that they left with us. In these, their essence and the inwardness of their souls are present, in accordance with what is explained in Chassidus regarding the wondrous unity between the intellect, the one who intellectualizes, and the concept being intellectualized, and the rest is understood.

With blessings for a joyous and good new month, the month of Geulah, and may the promise soon be fulfilled: "He will have compassion upon us and redeem us from the 'hiding of the face' (hester astir)," in the merit of the faith that all of Israel possesses—for they are believers, the children of believers—and the first believer is Avraham, as it is written, "And he believed in Hashem, and He accounted it to him as righteousness."

With blessings for good tidings.

#### ***5,115***

R' Avraham Sender: Nemtzov, Long Beach. Additional letters to him appear above in Vol. 12, p. 410, and in the references noted in the footnotes there.

## 5716

B"H, 28 Adar II, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 24 Adar II, in which you write regarding the mezuzah that needed to be replaced; it is surprising that you do not mention anything about having the tefillin checked.

Regarding your question as to how much your mother should set aside for tzedakah before lighting the candles on Erev Shabbos and Erev Yom Tov:

It is understood that this depends on her ability. The point of this is that since it is an *eis ratzon* (a time of favor) for the acceptance of the requests of the women and daughters of Yisrael prior to the performance of the mitzvah of lighting the candles on Erev Shabbos and Erev Yom Tov, one should increase in blessings to Hashem through the giving of tzedakah.

As for what you write regarding the rectification of the mezuzah matter, she should learn by heart several chapters of Mishnayos and at least one chapter of Tanya, and when she is traveling or the like, she should review them, whether verbally or at the very least in her thoughts.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,**

**Secretary**

#### ***5716***

**Regarding the rectification of the mezuzah matter:** Which was *pasul* (invalid).

*) See also Zohar Vol. III, 186a.

**) Yevamos, Chapter 8, Halacha 3.

## 517

B"H, 28 Adar Sheni, 5717
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, G-d-fearing and diligent in communal needs,
R' Ephraim Eliezer Hakohen, may he live and be well,

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from the eve of the holy Shabbos, along with the enclosure, and the receipts are enclosed herewith.

May it be the will of Hashem that the commandment "This month shall be for you" be fulfilled in each and every one of us, specifically as emphasized in Shemos Rabbah on that verse, "for you" specifically. This is in accordance with what was explained in the maamar delivered at the farbrengen yesterday, based on the words of our Sages in the Yerushalmi : "A new bride—the halacha is renewed." This means that they should renew the concept of creation—which is *me'ayin l'yesh* (from nothing to something)—into the opposite concept: *yesh l'ayin* (from something to nothing), as explained by the Tzemach Tzedek in the aforementioned source.

Through this, the promise of "In Nissan they are destined to be redeemed" will also be fulfilled, as the Shemos Rabbah concludes on this matter: "For according to his opinion, Rabbi Yehuda says: 'Not with money shall you be redeemed'" (Sanhedrin 97b). This is explained in the *d'h* "HaChodesh" of the summer of 5717. Furthermore, this will be a miraculous event: even though "if a woman conceives and bears a male" (see Likutei Torah from last week, *d'h* "Sos Asis") implies an *is'arusa d'le'eila* (arousal from Above), nevertheless, "she shall bear a male" —[note the Yerushalmi, Berachos chapter 9, halacha 5, regarding one sitting on the birth stool, which differs from the Bavli there]—the future redemption, which is (in the language of the Sages) a "male" specifically (Pesachim 116b, in Tosafos).

With blessings.

## 510

B"H, 29 Adar Sheni, 5717
Brooklyn.

Tzeirei Agudas Chabad, Kfar Chabad Branch
May Hashem preserve them and keep them alive.

Greetings and blessings!

I have received your letter from the 21st of Adar Sheni, containing the main points of the Purim farbrengen. It is surprising that you abbreviate where you should have elaborated; even though you write that you have already written to me regarding this farbrengen, it is understood that this does not exempt the administration of Tzeirei Agudas Chabad from writing, as they are the ones who organized the farbrengen.

It is surely unnecessary to remind you that a farbrengen of this nature is merely plowing, or at most, sowing. It is essential that all the subsequent labor required for growth and fruit follows. In this specific case, the beginning of this process is to maintain a connection with the *panim chadashot* (new faces) with whom you came into contact during this farbrengen—though it is understood that the manner of connection is not the same for everyone. You should utilize the upcoming days of the Pesach holiday, specifically through the *mivtza* of distributing *matzah shemurah*.

With blessings for success in your holy work, and for a kosher and happy Pesach.

## 517

B"H, 29 Adar Sheni, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

Your letter was received some time ago, in which you write that although it is difficult, you will nevertheless continue to involve yourself in the affairs of N'shei U’Bnos Chabad, and may Hashem bring the salvation.

It gave me pleasure to read this, and my hope is strong that you will not only fulfill the beginning of your letter—that is, regarding your involvement—but that the conclusion will also be fulfilled: "may Hashem bring the salvation." May you see good fruit from your labor in this, which, in addition to the personal satisfaction one feels when seeing the success of one's efforts, will also lead to strengthening your influence on your friends and acquaintances so that they, too, will increase their activities within the framework of N'shei U’Bnos Chabad. This is the requirement and also the necessity of the hour, and whoever adds to this is praiseworthy.

This is especially true in these days, as we find ourselves between the days of Purim and Pesach, in both of which the righteous women of that time were the active participants.

With blessings for success in your avodah, with proper health and joy, and for good tidings.

## 520

B"H, 29 Adar II, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

...I was very surprised and distressed to read in the second part of your letter that the *otzar* of the *mikveh* has a rubber stopper. You have likely seen the letter from my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of righteous memory—the son of the Rebbe (Rashab), of righteous memory—advising against using rubber for such things. Therefore, even if it takes time to fulfill the other suggestions of Rabbi Telushkin, you must replace the rubber stopper with cement as soon as possible. It goes without saying that this requires that there be new, kosher water in the *otzar* afterward, and the sooner this is done, the more praiseworthy it is. For this reason, I am sending this letter express.

With blessings for good news regarding all of the above.

#### ***520***

Printed in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 21, p. 302.

**Rubber stopper:** See also above, Vol. 11, letter 3568, and the references in the notes there.

## 5221

B"H, 29 Adar Sheni, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter, in which you write that you are beginning to put on tefillin, as it is two months before your Bar Mitzvah.

It is appropriate that you study—whether by yourself or with someone—what is written in the Shulchan Aruch and quoted in the beginning of Chapter 41 of Tanya regarding the contemplation associated with the mitzvah of tefillin, until these matters are engraved in your memory. Then, they will certainly have their effect regarding the study of Torah, the fulfillment of mitzvos as they should be, and good conduct in general. It is understood that this also includes influencing your friends so that they, too, will do the same. Since the aforementioned conduct adds to the health of the soul, it will certainly also increase the health of the body.

Regarding what you write, that the children in the class disturb your studies: this depends only on your will. When you do not pay attention to these disturbances and continue to study, the disturbances will diminish of their own accord.

I trust that you recite the monthly Tehillim—as the Tehillim is divided for the days of the month—every day after morning davening. And it is good that a few times a week, and if possible every weekday, before morning davening, you set aside a few coins for tzedakah. May Hashem grant you success in taking upon yourself the yoke of Torah and the yoke of mitzvos as they should be.

With blessings,

**On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita**

**Secretary**

## 522

29 Adar II, 5717
Brooklyn, N.Y.

Greetings and Blessings!

I acknowledge receipt of your letter—which was undated—along with the enclosed article. I was pleased to read in your letter that you are in contact with your friends in […], and surely you will continue to strengthen this connection. Furthermore, the matters of Yiddishkeit in general, and those pertaining specifically to women, should be strengthened. It is a pity that the new mikveh has not yet been built; as in many places in America, a single push would perhaps set the matter into greater motion and bring it to a swifter conclusion. In such matters, one cannot overstate the necessity of completing it even one day sooner.

I assume that women from [...] likely come to New York from time to time. When you are in contact with them here, you could meet with them and warm them up to this aforementioned matter. At an appropriate opportunity, you could perhaps even bring them to a gathering of Nshei U’Bnos Chabad, which would hopefully make the proper impression upon them.

From what has been said above, it is also understandable that what you write in your letter about yourself—that you are in a joyless mood—is in contradiction to the beginning of your letter. For if Hashem Yisborach gives one the ability to accomplish for Yiddishkeit within an entire circle, it is proof that the strengths exist and that Hashem Yisborach is granting success in this. One must only see to utilizing these strengths to the maximum degree. This is an important function in life, and it provides content (*toichen*) even to those hours and days when one is not directly occupied with it, keeping in mind that in a short while, one will be able to act in the maintenance and dissemination of Yiddishkeit.

Especially since, through the unification of Nshei U’Bnos Chabad, there are great possibilities to accomplish—not only in matters of Chassidus, but unfortunately, there is still a lack in basic Yiddishkeit as well, due to ignorance. If one were to disseminate the laws and customs of Israel in a diplomatic manner, it would bring about an immediate, significant change for the good. Regarding what you write—that unfortunately the women there do not conduct themselves properly in this work, and consequently, it not only brings no benefit but can even cause harm—it is to be understood from this that this is yet another reason why one must perform this work with more warmth and more *koch*. Knowing that this work must certainly be done, since it is the demand of the Giver of the Torah and the Mitzvos, when one sees that the work is lacking or not being done sufficiently, this increases the necessity to act, even with the portion of work that should have been done by others. We have the promise of the Torah that the work we are required to fulfill is not beyond the strengths that Hashem Yisborach provides. One does not need to jump all at once, but rather step by step, and then, in the end, one will see great success.

Regarding what you write—that it seems to you as if you are walking on a ladder, and if one takes an incorrect step, not everyone has the ability to rectify it—I am surprised. How can a woman who was raised in a Chassidishe home even think and write such a thing, after the entire Chassidic train of thought is that **for everything**, teshuvah helps? And what is even more important to know is that, in the end, everyone does teshuvah; one must simply do it as early as possible, and thereby gain time. And teshuvah does not mean only to say *Ashamnu, Bagadnu*, etc., but rather that the deficiency which existed for a time should be filled in, so that no trace of it remains.

I hope that you have also read my *Michtav Klali* for this year’s Rosh Hashanah, in which the idea was raised—albeit briefly—as to how, from year to year and from day to day, the good in the world increases, and the not-good becomes ever less and less.

The point of all that has been written above is:

It is highly necessary and important to fulfill the instruction of "Serve Hashem with joy" (*Ivdu es Hashem b'simcha*) specifically. And this "serving Hashem" is not only during davening or when performing a mitzvah directly, but even when eating, drinking, and the like—one can serve Hashem Yisborach with this as well, as is explained in many places in the teachings of Chassidus.

May Hashem Yisborach help you and your husband—may he live—that your home should always be a joyful home, and that you raise all your children, may they live, to Torah, to the chuppah, and to good deeds, in the path of Chassidus.

With the blessing of a kosher and happy Pesach.

## 5323

B"H, 29 Adar Sheni, 5717
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

Following the interval, your letter from 24 Adar Sheni was received. It is surprising that you do not mention anything regarding your participation in Chabad activities or your involvement in the shiurim studied in public. For even though you study several shiurim in private, it is a great merit and a significant matter to participate in study conducted with a minyan of Jews—as is understood from Iggeres HaKodesh, Epistle 23, and see also the Kuntres Hechalotzu regarding these matters. My hope is that this is merely an omission in your writing, but not in actual practice.

Regarding what you wrote concerning the instruction of Rabbi Landau shlita regarding the placement of the mezuzah, that we follow the *heker tzir*—meaning, the direction in which the door opens—this is indeed our custom, except for a door that opens outward. It is understood that the rectification of this matter is also included in the general category of checking mezuzos.

In response to your question, there is an instruction from my father-in-law, the Rebbe, which I believe has already been published, regarding the three daily portions of Chitas: if for any reason one did not manage to study them during the day, one should complete them during the following night.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above, with peace of mind and joy.

#### ***5323***

**There is an instruction:** See also above, Vol. 5, Letter 1502, and the references in the footnotes there; Vol. 8, 2867.

## 524

524

*[Adar Sheni, 5717]*

At a time of favor, I will mention all those you wrote about at the gravesite of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

Regarding what you wrote concerning the interpretation of our Sages’ statement that in the time to come all the festivals will be nullified except for Purim—there is also an opinion that this excludes Chanukah and Yom Kippur as well.

See Responsa of the Rashba, 93; Sefer HaChaim (by the brother of the Maharal), Section on Parnassah, 87; Sefer HaIkkarim, Maamar 3, Chapter 10; Magen Avraham, end of Parshas Vayakhel; Additions to Torah Or by the Alter Rebbe, beginning of Megillas Esther; Shaarei Orah (by the Mitteler Rebbe), p. 113; and elsewhere.

#### ***524***

This letter is addressed to Rabbi Moshe Yamini and was written on the margin of his letter dated 12 Adar Sheni, 5717. Additional letters to him appear above in Vol. 12, p. 462, and in the references noted in the footnotes there.

## 525

525

*[Late Adar II, 5717]*

... "Know Him." See carefully the beginning of Chapter 5 of Hilchos Yesodei HaTorah by the Rambam, from which it is also understood that it is imperative not to suffice with the study of the revealed Torah alone, and *kal v'chomer* [it is understood].

N.B.

Regarding what you wrote about the questions concerning the lung [in kashrus], etc. — I have not heard of any specific instructions regarding this.

Regarding your comment on the explanation of "they journeyed from here," etc. — why deviate from the literal meaning (even according to this *drash* that they "journeyed from the attributes of the Holy One, Blessed be He," since the attributes of the Holy One, Blessed be He, are mercy, whereas they sold him, etc.)? It is known that one does not challenge a *drush* [homiletical interpretation], especially since it aligns with the commentary of the *Matnas Kehunah*. Nevertheless, to fulfill your request, I have desired to explain the difficulty.

It could be said that, on the contrary, according to the literal meaning, there was a great kindness in the sale of Yosef into slavery, similar to the concept of *teshuvas hamishkal* [a compensatory response] to rectify the sin of "Will you indeed reign over us?" and regarding his father, for the dream proves that these were the thoughts of his heart. And Binyamin was punished for the *machlokes* [dispute] (Yerushalmi, cited in Tosafos, Kiddushin 39b).

Regarding your comment on the verse concerning Yaakov’s dream, that one cannot say it was from the thoughts of his heart — see the *Kli Yakar*, who explains that the dream was in its proper time, etc., and this is explained more explicitly in *Bereishis Rabbah*, beginning of Parshas Vayeitzei.

#### ***525***

This letter is to Rabbi Yehuda Zarchia Mordechai Leib Chaim Segal, and it was written on the margin of his letter dated Wednesday, Parshas Tazria, 5717. Additional letters to him are found above, Vol. 13, 452, and in the references noted in the footnotes there.

**Regarding your comment:** In *Midrash Rabbah*, "they journeyed from here" refers to the attributes of the Holy One, Blessed be He. And it is explained in the sefer *Chanukas HaTorah* that the brothers judged him based on his ultimate outcome, that Yeravam ben Nevat would descend from him.
